```text
(1/(n+1)!) sum_prec r(prec)>=dim O_p-P_p.              (P22.25)
```

Target 22F2 implies Target 22C by averaging.  Unlike Target 22F1, it does not
require a monotone path or even a preferred order.  The strict `gt-average`
mode passed all `2,422` support-disjoint cases with at most seven factors,
labels and targets at most four, and total degree at most sixteen.  After the
signed-class quotient of Corollary 22F0B, both this mode and the independent
rank-threshold plateau mode passed all `3,201` cases at the same label and
factor bounds with total degree at most eighteen.  The exact replays and the
three obstruction outputs below are recorded in
`certificates/su2_gt_rank_average.log`.

Two tempting simplifications of `(P22.23)` are false.  First, the transports
`(P22.18)` do not define a symmetric-group action: for

```text
d=[1,1,1,1],                 alpha=[1,0,1,0],          (P22.26)
```

the two braid words at the first three positions give respectively
`[1,1,0,0]` and `[1,0,1,0]`.  Thus an averaged proof must work on the order
graph itself, not on path-independent Bender--Knuth orbits.  Second, replacing
the minimal relation count `o_alpha-1` by all odd pairs
`binomial(o_alpha,2)` is too expensive.  For

```text
remainder=[1^+,1^+,1^+,1^+,2^-],          target=0,   (P22.27)
```

one has `dim O_0=9`, `P_0=6`, and the sum of the ranks over all `6!=720`
orders is `2,160`, exactly the required average rank three.  But the sum of
the odd-pair collision counts is `7,488`, exceeding the available averaged
capacity `720*6=4,320`.  A proof of `(P22.23)` must therefore select a spanning
set of fibre relations rather than charge every colliding pair.

Nor can `(P22.24)` be proved separately for each raw `alpha` in the fixed
box slice of Corollary 22F0A.  The first exact obstruction is

```text
remainder=[1^-,1^-,2^+,3^+],             target=1,
alpha=[0,0,0,3,1].                                  (P22.28)
```

Across all `5!=120` physical orders this `alpha` carries fourteen excess odd
relations and no positive object with the same raw top vector.  The complete
case still satisfies `(AGT)`: `dim O_1=2`, `P_1=1`, and rank one suffices.
Thus an averaged proof must transfer capacity between different `alpha` as
well as between different orders.  Formula `(P22.18)` supplies exactly such
an adjacent transfer, even though `(P22.26)` shows it is path-dependent.

The path dependence can be retained rather than quotiented out.  Define the
Bender--Knuth state graph `K(d)` to have vertices

```text
(prec,alpha),                  alpha in B_prec(d),     (P22.29)
```

and join two vertices when their orders differ by one adjacent transposition
and their top vectors are related by `(P22.18)`.  Lemma 22F makes every edge
an involutive bijection.  For a state `x`, let `o(x)` and `e(x)` be its odd
and nonempty even admissible-cut counts.

**Target 22F3 (componentwise BK average).**  In the nontrivial sector
`dim O_p>P_p`, every connected component `C` of `K(d)` satisfies

```text
sum_(x in C)[o(x)-indicator_(o(x)>0)]
 <=sum_(x in C)e(x).                                  (P22.30)
```

Summing `(P22.30)` over the components gives Target 22F2.  Unlike the false
fixed-`alpha` statement, Target 22F3 allows exactly the cross-`alpha`
transfers generated by adjacent tableau switching; unlike an unrestricted
average, it presents a local graph on which a spanning forest or discrete
Morse matching could prove the inequality.  The strict component enumerator
passed every source-deficit instance occurring among the `1,001`
support-disjoint cases with at most five factors, labels and targets at most
four, and total degree at most fourteen.

There is a sharper signed form of the component target.  Include the empty
even cut and put

```text
delta(x)=1+e(x)-o(x),
rho(x)=delta(x)-indicator_(o(x)=0)
      =e(x)-o(x)+indicator_(o(x)>0).                 (P22.30E)
```

**Lemma 22F3A (signed-cut Euler form).**  Target 22F3 is equivalent, on
every component `C`, to either of the inequalities

```text
sum_(x in C) delta(x)
 >=#{x in C:o(x)=0},

sum_(x in C) rho(x)>=0.                              (P22.30R)
```

Moreover, if `F(x)` is the complete admissible-cut family, including the
empty cut, and the sign of a cut is the product of its vertex signs, then

```text
delta(x)=sum_(T in F(x)) sign(T).                    (P22.30S)
```

**Proof.**  The even members of `F(x)` are the empty cut and the `e(x)`
nonempty even cuts, whereas its odd members are the `o(x)` odd cuts.  This
proves `(P22.30S)` and the first equality in `(P22.30E)`.  Subtracting the
number of inactive states gives

```text
sum_C rho
 =sum_C e-sum_C[o-indicator_(o>0)],
```

which is nonnegative exactly when `(P22.30)` holds.  QED.

Thus the missing differential may be sought as a sign-reversing matching of
admissible cuts across a BK component, with one empty-cut survivor at every
inactive state.  The local residual `rho(x)` also gives an exact transport
diagnostic.  Give a state with `rho<0` demand `-rho` and a state with
`rho>0` capacity `rho`; join them when their BK graph distance is at most
`R`.  A capacitated matching saturating every demand proves `(P22.30R)`.
This is only a sufficient certificate for a chosen `R`, not a proof that
such a uniform radius exists.

There is a one-dimensional transfer formula for both statistics in
`(P22.30E)`.  It is useful because an adjacent BK switch changes only a
short local product.

Fix a state `x=(prec,alpha)`, write its ordered data as
`(alpha_j,beta_j,s_j)`, and let

```text
H_j=sum_(ell<j)(alpha_ell-beta_ell)                  (P22.30P)
```

be the total queue height immediately before vertex `j` consumes its bottom
tokens.  Index matrices by integers `0<=h<=H_j`, where `h` is the height of
the cut queue and `H_j-h` is the complementary height.  Define the local
signed transfer by

```text
K_j(h,h)=1_(H_j-h>=beta_j),

K_j(h,h+alpha_j-beta_j)
 +=s_j 1_(h>=beta_j),                                (P22.30Q)
```

with the second transition omitted at the distinguished target.  If both
transitions have the same endpoints, their weights are added.  Let
`K_j^+` be the same matrix with every `s_j` replaced by `+1`.

**Lemma 22F3A1 (signed two-queue transfer).**  If
`f(x)=1+e(x)+o(x)` is the total number of admissible cuts including the
empty cut, then

```text
f(x)=(product_j K_j^+)(0,0),
delta(x)=(product_j K_j)(0,0),                       (P22.30R1)

o(x)=[f(x)-delta(x)]/2,
e(x)+1=[f(x)+delta(x)]/2,
q(x):=max(0,-rho(x))=max(0,-delta(x)).               (P22.30R2)
```

**Proof.**  Immediately before job `j`, a partial cut has queue height `h`
and its complement has height `H_j-h`.  Putting `j` outside the cut is
legal exactly when the complementary queue can consume `beta_j`; this is
the first transition in `(P22.30Q)` and leaves `h` fixed.  Putting `j` in
the cut is legal exactly when `h>=beta_j`; it changes the cut height by
`alpha_j-beta_j` and multiplies the cut sign by `s_j`.  The target is forced
outside the cut.  Starting and ending at height zero therefore enumerates
exactly the admissible cuts, unsigned for `K_j^+` and signed for `K_j`.
This proves `(P22.30R1)`.  Even cuts contribute `1+e` and odd cuts
contribute `o`, giving the first two identities in `(P22.30R2)`.  If
`o=0`, then `delta=f=1+e>0` and `rho=e>=0`; if `o>0`, then
`rho=delta`.  The last identity follows.  QED.

There is an additional symmetry which is invisible if the target constraint
is imposed too early.  Give the target sign `+1`, put

```text
S=product_j s_j=(-1)^(number of minus factors),
```

and let `widehat f` and `widehat delta` be the unsigned and signed sums over
all feasible cuts, now allowing the target on either side.

**Lemma 22F3A3 (even-sign target removal).**  One has

```text
widehat f=2f.
```

If `S=+1`, then

```text
widehat delta=2delta.                                (P22.30R5)
```

If `S=-1`, then `widehat delta=0`; in that sector the target-free signed
sum contains no information about the target-forced `delta`.

**Proof.**  Complementation `T->T^c` is a fixed-point-free involution on
the feasible cuts: it merely exchanges the two queue colours.  Exactly one
member of every complementary pair excludes the target, proving the
unsigned identity.  Moreover,

```text
sign(T^c)=S sign(T).
```

For `S=+1`, the two members have the same sign and the target-forced member
contributes half their sum.  For `S=-1`, their signs cancel.  QED.

Thus the nontrivial even-minus descent problem can be studied with a
target-free, colour-symmetric transfer operator.  The odd-minus signed
integral is already zero by exchanging the two Haar variables, and should
not be forced through a stronger targetwise statement.

The two-job factor can be written without matrices.  Put
`Delta_a=alpha_a-beta_a` and `Delta_b=alpha_b-beta_b`, and let `H` and `h`
be respectively the total and cut queue heights before two consecutive jobs
`a,b`.  The four colour choices have the following complete transition
table:

```text
choice    condition on h
00        h<=H-beta_a,       h<=H+Delta_a-beta_b
11        h>=beta_a,         h>=beta_b-Delta_a
10        beta_a<=h<=H-beta_b
01        beta_b<=h<=H-beta_a,                         (P22.30R3)

choice    new cut height       signed weight
00        h                    1
11        h+Delta_a+Delta_b    s_a s_b
10        h+Delta_a            s_a
01        h+Delta_b            s_b.
```

A choice containing the target is omitted.  Under the BK switch, with

```text
z=max(0,beta_a+beta_b-H),
Delta'_b=Delta_b+2z,          beta'_b=beta_b-z,
Delta'_a=Delta_a-2z,          beta'_a=beta_a+z,        (P22.30R4)
```

the switched local transfer is the same table for the order `b,a` and the
primed data.

**Corollary 22F3A2 (interval form of a BK edge).**  The difference between
the signed or unsigned transfer operators on the two ends of a BK edge is
supported exactly on the threshold intervals obtained by subtracting the
two copies of `(P22.30R3)`, one with the original data and one with
`(P22.30R4)`.  In particular, it depends on the exterior state only through
the incoming total height `H`, the incoming cut height `h`, and the prefix
and suffix contractions in `(P22.30R1)`.

**Proof.**  For choice `00`, the complementary queue first pays `beta_a`,
changes by `Delta_a`, and then pays `beta_b`, giving the first line.  Choice
`11` is the identical calculation in the cut queue.  For `10`, the cut
queue pays `beta_a` while the unchanged complementary queue pays `beta_b`;
choice `01` is symmetric.  The new heights and sign weights are immediate.
Formula `(P22.30R4)` is `(P22.18)--(P22.19)` rewritten in terms of
`Delta`.  QED.

The local operator defect is not of bounded rank.  Let `T_(a,b;H)` denote
the target-free unsigned two-job transfer at incoming total height `H`.

**Lemma 22F3A4 (unbounded local transfer rank).**  For every `m>=1`, take

```text
H=2m-1,             a=(alpha,beta)=(0,m),
b=(alpha,beta)=(m,0).
```

The BK parameter is `z=0`, so the switch merely interchanges the two jobs,
and

```text
T_(a,b;H)-T_(b,a;H)=-I_(2m).                         (P22.30R6)
```

In particular, the unsigned and same-sign signed local defects have rank
`2m`; no height-independent finite-rank factorization is possible.

**Proof.**  Index incoming and outgoing cut heights by
`h=0,...,2m-1`.  In the order `a,b`, job `a` is forced outside the cut for
`h<m` and inside for `h>=m`.  Job `b` may then be placed on either side.
Thus row `h` is

```text
e_h+e_(h+m)             (h<m),
e_(h-m)+e_h             (h>=m).                       (P22.30R7)
```

In the order `b,a`, putting `b` outside reproduces one branch in
`(P22.30R7)`, while putting it inside reproduces the other branch and adds
one further legal transition to height `h`.  Hence the switched row is the
row in `(P22.30R7)` plus `e_h`, proving `(P22.30R6)`.  QED.

Although its rank is unbounded, the unsigned defect always has one
entrywise sign.  For ordered jobs `c,d`, define the single-queue threshold

```text
L(c,d)=max(beta_c,beta_d-(alpha_c-beta_c)).           (P22.30R8)
```

Thus `L(c,d)` is the minimum starting queue height from which that queue
can execute `c` and then `d`.

**Lemma 22F3A5 (unsigned interchange comparability).**  For a BK switch
`a,b -> b',a'` at incoming total height `H`, put

```text
L=L(a,b),                 L'=L(b',a').
```

Then the target-free unsigned local defect has the entrywise orientation

```text
T_(a,b;H)-T_(b',a';H) >= 0     if L<L',
T_(a,b;H)-T_(b',a';H)  = 0     if L=L',
T_(a,b;H)-T_(b',a';H) <= 0     if L>L'.              (P22.30R9)
```

**Proof.**  First consider the two mixed-colour choices `10,01`.  If
`z=0`, the switch only interchanges the jobs, and its `10` transition is
the original `01` transition while its `01` transition is the original
`10` transition.  Hence their unsigned contributions agree.  If `z>0`,
then

```text
H<beta_a+beta_b=beta'_b+beta'_a.
```

Neither order can pay the two bottom demands from two separate queues, so
all mixed-colour transitions are absent at both ends.

It remains to compare the two same-colour choices.  By `(P22.30R3)`,
choice `11` for an ordered pair `c,d` is feasible exactly when
`h>=L(c,d)`, and choice `00` is feasible exactly when
`h<=H-L(c,d)`.  Both endpoints have the same total increment, so
corresponding feasible choices also have the same output height.  The two
upper rays and the two lower rays are nested in the orientation stated in
`(P22.30R9)`.  QED.

**Corollary 22F3A6 (plateau support exclusion).**  Let `u` and `v` be
nonnegative prefix and suffix transfer vectors.  The unsigned scalar edge
defect `u(T-T')v` has the orientation in `(P22.30R9)`.  If this scalar
defect vanishes, then

```text
u_h v_k=0
```

for every nonzero defect entry `(T-T')_(h,k)`.

**Proof.**  Every summand `u_h(T-T')_(h,k)v_k` has the same weak sign.
Their sum can vanish only if every nonzero-defect summand has zero
prefix-suffix weight.  QED.

The matrix statement has an exact set-theoretic strengthening.  Regard a
cut as a subset of the named vertices, so the same subset can be compared
before and after an adjacent switch.  Let `Fhat(x)` be the complete
target-free feasible-cut family of a BK state.

**Lemma 22F3A7 (nested cut families and universal plateaux).**  For every
BK edge `x -> x'`, the two complete feasible-cut families are nested.  More
precisely, with the thresholds `L,L'` of Lemma 22F3A5,

```text
Fhat(x') subseteq Fhat(x)       if L<L',
Fhat(x') =        Fhat(x)       if L=L',
Fhat(x') supseteq Fhat(x)       if L>L'.              (P22.30R10)
```

The same orientation holds after requiring any fixed target to lie outside
the cut.  If the inclusion in `(P22.30R10)` is proper, then the
target-excluded inclusion is proper for every target.  Consequently the
following conditions on an edge are equivalent:

1. `f(x)=f(x')` for one target;
2. `f(x)=f(x')` for every target;
3. `Fhat(x)=Fhat(x')`.

On such an edge every target-forced signed sum `delta` is also unchanged,
for every assignment of vertex signs.  Thus the plateau subgraph depends
only on the unsigned BK states, not on the exchange target or the signs.
Its connected components are precisely the connected components induced
inside the fibres of the map `x -> Fhat(x)`.

**Proof.**  Fix a named subset and compare its two local colour choices.
When `z=0`, the two mixed choices are merely interchanged by the switch and
have identical local input and output heights.  When `z>0`, they are
infeasible at both endpoints because

```text
H<beta_a+beta_b=beta'_b+beta'_a.
```

For a same-colour choice, the input and output heights agree at the two
endpoints.  The choice with both jobs in the cut is feasible on the upper
ray `h>=L` or `h>=L'`; the choice with both jobs outside is feasible on the
lower ray `h<=H-L` or `h<=H-L'`.  These rays are nested in the common
orientation `(P22.30R10)`.  Prefix and suffix feasibility is unchanged, so
the local inclusion lifts to the complete named-cut families.

Intersecting nested families with the condition that a target lie outside
preserves the weak inclusion.  Each `Fhat` is closed under complementation.
Its difference from the smaller family is therefore also closed under
complementation.  If that difference is nonempty, exactly one cut in each
complementary pair excludes a specified target, proving strictness for
every target.  This proves the three equivalences.  When the families are
equal, the identity on named subsets preserves both target exclusion and
the product of the vertex signs, so every signed sum is equal as well.
The final assertions follow along paths of plateau edges.  QED.

For even total sign, strict edges admit a particularly simple valuation.
Suppose `Fhat_small` is properly contained in `Fhat_big` along a BK edge
and put

```text
E=Fhat_big\Fhat_small.
```

The shell `E` is complement-stable.  For a target `p`, let `E_p` contain
the unique member of each complementary pair which excludes `p`.

**Corollary 22F3A8 (edge-shell calculus).**  If the number of minus
vertices is even, then

```text
f_big-f_small=|E|/2=|E_p|,

delta_big-delta_small
  =sum_(T in E_p) sign(T)
  =(1/2)sum_(T in E) sign(T).                         (P22.30R11)
```

Both differences are independent of the target.  In particular, an edge
from a family `Fhat` to a larger family is a descent exactly when the shell
has positive signed sum.  An edge to a smaller family is a descent exactly
when the shell has negative signed sum, or when its signed sum is zero
(in which case `delta` is fixed and `f` decreases).

**Proof.**  Complementation preserves `E`, acts without fixed points, and,
under even total sign, gives the two members of every pair the same sign.
Exactly one member excludes `p`.  The two identities follow by subtracting
the unsigned and signed sums of the nested families.  The descent criterion
is the lexicographic definition following `(P22.30R2)`.  QED.

There is a useful word model for these fibres.  Replace an ordered job
`(alpha_j,beta_j)` by the parenthesis block

```text
B_j=)^beta_j (^alpha_j,
```

where a left parenthesis raises and a right parenthesis lowers the height.

**Lemma 22F3A9 (two-colour block-Dyck model).**  The concatenation

```text
W(x)=B_1 B_2 ... B_N
```

is a Dyck word.  A named subset `T` belongs to `Fhat(x)` exactly when
colouring every parenthesis in block `j` by the indicator of `j in T`
makes each of the two colour projections a Dyck word.

For adjacent blocks `a,b`, the BK move replaces

```text
)^beta_a (^alpha_a )^beta_b (^alpha_b
```

by

```text
)^(beta_b-z) (^(alpha_b+z)
)^(beta_a+z) (^(alpha_a-z),

z=max(0,beta_a+beta_b-H),                            (P22.30R12)
```

where `H` is the height before the first block.  Thus Target 22F3J is
equivalently a boundary theorem for two-colourings of a block-Dyck word:
inside one fixed colouring family, each adjacent block boundary need be
switched at most once before the final shell crossing.

**Proof.**  At the start of block `j`, the uncoloured height is `H_j`.
The block first lowers by `beta_j` and then raises by `alpha_j`; hence its
prefixes are nonnegative exactly when `H_j>=beta_j`.  The terminal height
is zero by the defining balance of a BK state.  After colouring, the two
heights are precisely the cut and complementary queue heights in Lemma
22F3A1.  Their two Dyck conditions are therefore exactly cut feasibility.
Formula `(P22.30R12)` is `(P22.30R4)` written as parenthesis
multiplicities.  QED.

Returns of the uncoloured Dyck word give an exact product decomposition.
Split `W(x)` at every internal zero of its height into primitive block
excursions `W_1,...,W_c`, and write `Fhat_r`, `fhat_r`, and
`deltahat_r` for their complete cut family and unsigned and signed sums.

**Lemma 22F3A10 (excursion factorization).**  One has

```text
Fhat(x)=Fhat_1 times ... times Fhat_c,
fhat(x)=product_r fhat_r,
deltahat(x)=product_r deltahat_r.                    (P22.30R13)
```

If the exchange target lies in excursion `r_0`, and `f_(r_0),delta_(r_0)`
denote the target-excluded sums in that excursion, then

```text
f(x)=f_(r_0) product_(r!=r_0) fhat_r,
delta(x)=delta_(r_0) product_(r!=r_0) deltahat_r.     (P22.30R14)
```

Consequently plateau motion and an exit shell supported inside one
excursion lift multiplicatively to the full state.  It is enough to prove
the fibre-boundary mechanism on primitive block-Dyck paths, while retaining
the signs of the other excursion factors in the exit orientation.

**Proof.**  At a zero of the total height, both nonnegative coloured
heights sum to zero, so both are zero.  A two-colouring is therefore
feasible exactly when its restrictions to all excursions are feasible.
This proves the family product and the unsigned identity.  Cut signs
multiply over the excursions, proving the signed identity.  Target
exclusion affects only `r_0`, which gives `(P22.30R14)`.  QED.

The adjacent move across an excursion boundary has a fixed orientation.

**Lemma 22F3A11 (return-boundary shell).**  Suppose an internal return lies
between adjacent jobs `a,b`, and let `H` be the positive height before the
last job `a` of the left excursion.  Then

```text
a=(alpha_a,beta_a)=(0,H),          beta_b=0,
z=0,

L(a,b)=H,       L(b,a)=max(0,H-alpha_b).              (P22.30R15)
```

Consequently switching the first job of the right excursion left across
the return boundary can only enlarge the complete feasible-cut family.  If
`alpha_b>0`, its local threshold interval is nonempty; the global inclusion
is strict exactly when that interval has a compatible prefix and suffix
completion.

**Proof.**  The last job returns the height to zero, so
`beta_a=H+alpha_a`.  Its validity also requires `beta_a<=H`; hence
`alpha_a=0` and `beta_a=H`.  The next excursion starts at height zero, so
validity forces `beta_b=0`.  Formula `(P22.30R12)` now gives `z=0`.
Substitution in `(P22.30R8)` gives the two thresholds in `(P22.30R15)`.
Lemma 22F3A7 supplies the family inclusion and its strictness criterion.
QED.

Thus every internal return has a canonical candidate edge: the
return-boundary switch adds a complement-stable shell.  By Corollary
22F3A8 it is an immediate descent exactly when that shell has positive
signed sum.  This gives one possible composite-state mechanism; the other
is a descent supported inside one primitive excursion, whose orientation
is multiplied by the signed sums of all other excursions in
`(P22.30R14)`.

The return shell has an explicit correlation formula.  Work target-free,
give the two boundary jobs signs `s_a,s_b`, and let `u_h` be the signed
prefix transfer coefficient immediately before `a`.  Let `v_k` be the
signed suffix coefficient from immediately after `b` to terminal height
zero.  Put `A=alpha_b` and `m=min(H,A)`.

**Lemma 22F3A12 (return-shell contraction).**  If `x'` is obtained by
switching the return-boundary pair `a,b`, then

```text
deltahat(x')-deltahat(x)
 =sum_(r=1)^m [
      u_r v_r+s_a s_b u_(H-r)v_(A-r)].                (P22.30R16)
```

The unsigned shell size is given by the same formula with unsigned prefix
and suffix coefficients and `s_a=s_b=+1`.  Under even total sign, the
target-excluded shell sum in Corollary 22F3A8 is one half of
`(P22.30R16)`.

More explicitly, complementation of a partial two-colouring gives

```text
u_(H-r)=S_P u_r,              v_(A-r)=S_S v_r,
```

where `S_P,S_S` are the products of the vertex signs in the prefix and
suffix.  Since even total sign says
`S_P s_a s_b S_S=+1`, `(P22.30R16)` reduces after target removal to

```text
f(x')-f(x)=sum_(r=1)^m u_r^+ v_r^+,
delta(x')-delta(x)=sum_(r=1)^m u_r v_r.               (P22.30R17)
```

Here `u^+,v^+` are the unsigned coefficients.  Thus strict enlargement is
equivalent to overlap of the two unsigned interior supports, while descent
is positivity of the signed interior dot product.

**Proof.**  Lemma 22F3A11 gives original threshold `H` and switched
threshold `H-m`.  For each `1<=r<=m`, the new `00` transition starts at
cut height `r`, ends at height `r`, and has weight one.  The complementary
new `11` transition starts at `H-r`, ends at `A-r`, and has weight
`s_a s_b`.  These are all threshold-interval transitions from Lemma
22F3A5.  Contracting them with the unchanged prefix and suffix gives
`(P22.30R16)`.  Complementing a prefix colouring exchanges heights `r`
and `H-r` and multiplies its sign by `S_P`; the suffix statement is
identical.  The even-total-sign identity makes the paired summands equal.
Target removal is Lemma 22F3A3, giving `(P22.30R17)`.  QED.

Thus the return-boundary mechanism is reduced to a finite cross-correlation
of the two adjacent primitive excursions.  It does not by itself cover
descents supported strictly inside one excursion.

The negative-state hypothesis also fixes the endpoint signs.  In the
notation of Lemma 22F3A12, the original return factors as

```text
deltahat(x)
 =(u_0+s_a u_H)(v_0+s_b v_A).                         (P22.30R18)
```

**Corollary 22F3A13 (opposite endpoint orientation).**  Under even total
sign, if `delta(x)<0`, then

```text
s_a S_P=s_b S_S=+1,             u_0 v_0<0.            (P22.30R19)
```

**Proof.**  Formula `(P22.30R18)` is the product of the signed sums of the
two excursion pieces separated at the return.  Complementation gives
`u_H=S_Pu_0` and `v_A=S_Sv_0`.  Each parenthesis in `(P22.30R18)` is
therefore either zero or twice its zero-height coefficient.  Since
`deltahat=2delta<0`, neither vanishes, both sign products equal `+1`, and
their zero-height coefficients have opposite signs.  QED.

For the return-boundary mechanism, two signed transfer sequences therefore
have oppositely oriented endpoints, and descent is positivity of the
interior dot product `(P22.30R17)`.  Any use of excursion factorization
must combine this with the internal-excursion mechanism from
`(P22.30R14)`.

The orientation of an internal move is also exact.

**Corollary 22F3A14 (excursion-magnitude descent).**  Suppose
`delta(x)<0`, and write its excursion factorization as

```text
delta(x)=C_j d_j,
```

where `d_j` is the signed sum of one excursion (target-excluded if it
contains the target) and `C_j` is the product of all other signed factors.
An internal plateau word which leaves the sign of `d_j` fixed and replaces
it by `d'_j` with

```text
|d'_j|<|d_j|
```

is a descent of the full state.  If `d'_j=d_j` but the corresponding local
unsigned count decreases, it is again a descent.

**Proof.**  Since `C_jd_j<0`, the signs of `C_j` and `d_j` are opposite.
Moving `d_j` toward zero therefore makes
`C_j(d'_j-d_j)>0`.  This increases the full `delta`.  If the signed factor
is fixed, `(P22.30R14)` shows that the full unsigned count is a positive
multiple of the local unsigned count, proving the second assertion.  QED.

Accordingly, the internal branch in `(P22.30R20)` is an
absolute-signed-sum reduction on one primitive excursion.  The return
branch is the positive correlation `(P22.30R17)`.  These are the two local
alternatives which the global `O>P` hypothesis must force.

Zero-transfer moves have a fixed scheduling orientation.  Call a job
`c=(alpha_c,beta_c)` productive when `alpha_c>=beta_c` and consumptive
otherwise.  Define a total preorder `preceq` by

```text
productive jobs precede consumptive jobs;
among productive jobs, beta is increasing;
among consumptive jobs, alpha is decreasing.          (P22.30R21)
```

**Lemma 22F3A15 (zero-transfer scheduling order).**  For two fixed jobs
`c,d`,

```text
              c preceq d  implies  L(c,d)<=L(d,c).     (P22.30R22)
```

Cross-class equality can occur, so the implication need not be strict.
Hence on a BK edge with `z=0`, swapping an adjacent pair into scheduling
order can only enlarge the complete feasible-cut family; swapping it out of
scheduling order can only shrink the family.

**Proof.**  Write

```text
L(c,d)=beta_c+max(0,beta_d-alpha_c).
```

If both jobs are productive, `beta_c<=beta_d` implies
`L(c,d)<=beta_d<=L(d,c)`, so increasing `beta` is optimal.  If both are
consumptive and `alpha_c>=alpha_d`, expanding the two maxima according as
`beta_d` exceeds `alpha_c` gives
`L(c,d)<=beta_c+beta_d-alpha_d=L(d,c)`; thus decreasing `alpha` is
optimal.  Finally, if `c` is productive and `d` consumptive, the same
two-case expansion according as `alpha_d>=beta_c` gives
`L(c,d)<=L(d,c)`.  For `z=0`, the switched jobs are the same fixed jobs in
reverse order, so Lemma 22F3A7 converts the threshold comparison into the
asserted family inclusion.  QED.

Excursion factorization also quantizes every composite deficit.

**Lemma 22F3A16 (excursion divisibility and minimal-deficit rigidity).**
Suppose the total number of minus vertices is even, the block-Dyck word has
`c` primitive excursions, and `delta(x)!=0`.  Then

```text
2^(c-1) divides delta(x).                              (P22.30R23)
```

More precisely, every excursion has total sign `+1`, and every excursion
not containing the target has a nonzero signed sum of the form

```text
deltahat_r=2d_r,              d_r in Z\{0}.           (P22.30R24)
```

Consequently a composite negative state has `delta<=-2`.  If that
composite state has `delta=-2`, then there are exactly two excursions, the
target excursion has signed factor `+1` or `-1`, and the other excursion
has the oppositely signed factor `-2` or `+2`.  In this minimal case

```text
q(x)>=2^(c-1),              f(x)=2 mod 4 if q(x)=2.  (P22.30R25)
```

**Proof.**  On any excursion `r`, complementation is a fixed-point-free
involution and multiplies cut sign by the excursion sign product `S_r`.
If `S_r=-1`, its two members cancel and `deltahat_r=0`.  The target-free
factorization `(P22.30R13)` and
`deltahat(x)=2delta(x)!=0` would then be impossible.  Hence every
`S_r=+1`.  On each non-target excursion the two complementary cuts have
equal sign, so their nonzero signed sum is twice an integer `d_r`.  There
are `c-1` such excursions, proving `(P22.30R23)`.

For a composite state with `delta=-2`, divisibility forces `c=2`.  The two
integer factors in `(P22.30R14)` then have absolute values one and two and
opposite signs.
For any target-forced cut family, its unsigned and signed sums have the
same parity.  Thus the unsigned count of the target excursion is odd, and
one half of the unsigned count of the other excursion is also odd.  Their
product in `(P22.30R14)` is therefore twice an odd number, proving
the congruence in `(P22.30R25)`.  The magnitude bound follows directly
from `(P22.30R14)` because every remaining nonzero integer factor has
absolute value at least one.  QED.

The minimal composite stratum has a canonical two-factor normalization.

**Lemma 22F3A17 (opposite unit-excursion normal form).**  In the composite
`delta=-2` case, let `(a,A)` be the signed and unsigned target-excluded
sums of the target excursion, and let `(b,B)` be one half of the
target-free signed and unsigned sums of the other excursion.  Then

```text
a,b in {+1,-1},       ab=-1,
A,B positive and odd,       f(x)=2AB.                (P22.30R27)
```

If `A=1` or `B=1`, the corresponding signed factor is `+1` and the other
factor is `-1`.  The equality `A=1` means that the target excursion has
only its empty target-excluded cut.  The equality `B=1` means that the
other excursion has only its empty/full complementary pair.

**Proof.**  Lemma 22F3A16 gives the two excursions and opposite unit signed
factors.  Unsigned and signed sums over the same finite family have equal
parity, so `A` and `B` are odd.  Excursion factorization gives `f=2AB`.
Every target-excluded family contains the positive empty cut.  Thus an
unsigned count one has signed sum `+1`; target-free complementation gives
the identical statement after dividing its unique empty/full pair by two.
The descriptions of the two rigid families are immediate.  QED.

Rigid positive excursions preserve the entire internal descent landscape.

**Lemma 22F3A18 (rigid-excursion embedding).**  Let `x` be any block-Dyck
state whose target lies among its jobs.  For `r>=1` and `s in {+1,-1}`,
append the two jobs

```text
R_(r,s):       (alpha,beta,s)=(r,0,s),(0,r,s).       (P22.30R27A)
```

The appended excursion has only the empty and full target-free cuts, and

```text
fhat(R_(r,s))=2,       deltahat(R_(r,s))=2.
```

Consequently

```text
(delta,f)(x concatenated R_(r,s))
       =(2delta(x),2f(x)).                            (P22.30R27B)
```

More generally, apply any BK word using only generators internal to `x`.
At every prefix of the word, the potential of the concatenated state is
twice the corresponding potential of `x`.  Hence the word is a plateau
word, and its final edge is a descent, for the concatenated state exactly
when the same statements hold for `x`.

**Proof.**  A feasible cut of the two-job excursion cannot contain exactly
one job: either singleton has nonzero terminal queue height.  Thus its only
cuts are the positive empty and full cuts; their product sign is
`s^2=+1`.  The first two identities follow.  Excursion factorization
`(P22.30R14)` gives `(P22.30R27B)`.  Internal BK moves do not alter the
rigid excursion or its return boundary, so the same factorization applies
at every word prefix.  Multiplication by the positive scalar two preserves
both equality of potentials and the lexicographic descent relation.  QED.

The full BK graph of the concatenation can still have shorter paths which
cross the new return boundary.  Lemma 22F3A18 therefore transports a
chosen internal escape word, not its global minimality.  Exact boundary
checks are needed when the construction is used as an obstruction.

**Corollary 22F3A18A (rigid-padding closure).**  Concatenate any finite
number of rigid excursions `(P22.30R27A)` before or after a target-containing
core state `x`.  If `x` has an escape word with square-free proper prefix
and at most one positive prefix transfer, then the concatenated state has
an escape word with the same two properties.  More precisely, after
shifting generator indices for rigid excursions placed before the core,
the identical internal word works, and every potential along it is
multiplied by `2^m`, where `m` is the number of rigid excursions.

**Proof.**  Apply Lemma 22F3A18 successively.  Generator-index shifts do not
change repetitions or transfer amounts inside the core.  Positive scaling
preserves every plateau and the final descent.  QED.

Thus Targets 22F3J--22F3M already hold on the unbounded class obtained by
rigidly padding any core state for which they hold.  The counterexample
`(P22.30R30)` lies in this class: it inherits the square-free,
zero-positive-transfer word `[2,5,4,3]` from its primitive core.

The same inheritance holds for every negative excursion, without
rigidity.

**Lemma 22F3A18B (negative-excursion inheritance).**  Let `x` have
nonzero negative global factor

```text
delta(x)=product_r d_r<0,                             (P22.30R27D)
```

where the target excursion uses its target-excluded signed sum and every
other `d_r` is its target-free signed sum.  If `d_j<0`, then

```text
C_j=product_(r!=j)d_r>0.                             (P22.30R27E)
```

Any plateau word supported inside excursion `j` whose final edge is a
descent for that excursion lifts to a plateau word and descent for `x`.
Square-freeness of the proper prefix and the number of positive-transfer
moves are preserved.

For a non-target excursion, the local potential may equivalently be
computed after forcing any one of its jobs outside: all its signs multiply
to `+1`, so target removal divides both its signed and unsigned sums by
two, and Lemmas 22F3A7--22F3A8 make plateau and exit orientation
independent of that auxiliary target.

**Proof.**  Equation `(P22.30R27E)` follows by dividing the negative global
product by the negative factor `d_j`.  Excursion factorization gives, at
every internal word prefix,

```text
delta_global=C_j delta_j,        f_global=F_j f_j,
```

with `C_j>0` and unsigned multiplier `F_j>0`.  Equality of the local
potential therefore gives equality globally; an increase of local
`delta_j`, or equality followed by a decrease of local `f_j`, is preserved
by the positive multipliers.  The word uses the same internal generators
and transfer amounts, proving the two normal-form assertions.  The final
paragraph is Lemma 22F3A3 applied within the excursion, together with
target independence from Lemmas 22F3A7--22F3A8.  QED.

Consequently a composite negative state needs a genuinely cross-excursion
argument only if every negative primitive excursion factor is locally
trapped.  This is the correct recursive replacement for False Target
22F3K: long primitive escapes may persist unchanged under padding, while
locally trapped negative factors must be released by a return-boundary
shell.

This gives the surviving composite reduction.

**Target 22F3P (negative-excursion release).**  Under the even-sign,
source-deficit hypotheses `O>P`, every composite state with `delta<0` has
a negative primitive excursion which admits a local escape word of length
at most its number of jobs, with square-free proper prefix and at most one
positive prefix transfer.

By Lemma 22F3A18B, Target 22F3P lifts that local word unchanged and proves
Targets 22F3J--22F3M for the composite state.  Hence the global
square-free/single-transfer problem splits cleanly into:

1. the primitive escape theorem;
2. Target 22F3P, excluding a composite made entirely from locally trapped
   negative factors.

The strict checker now performs this local factor audit independently.
In the complete source-filtered `(9,2)` domain, all `4,238` composite
negative states have at least one negative excursion with such a normal
local escape:

```text
source_composite_no_normal_negative_excursion=0.      (P22.30R27F)
```

The closed primitive obstruction `(P22.30N8)` provides the complementary
test.  Appending one compatible rigid excursion with every label at most
thirty and either allowed sign produces no `O>P` case: all `57` exact
embeddings remain in `O<=P`.  Repeated-padding sweeps likewise find no
source-deficit case among all `68` compatible choices with at most four
rigid pairs and label at most ten, or all `63` choices with at most three
pairs and label at most twelve.  Allowing two independently chosen signed
labels also leaves all `366` compatible paddings with labels at most
fifteen in `O<=P`; allowing three independently chosen signed labels
leaves all `850` compatible paddings with labels at most ten there as well.
This supports the role of the source filter in Target 22F3P but is bounded
evidence, not its proof.

An independent arbitrary-path random replay with twelve vertices and queue
parts at most six tested `822` source-deficit negative states.  It contained
`29` composite states, and every one had a normal escaping negative
excursion; globally there were `808` direct, `13` two-step, and one
three-step escape, with zero square-free-prefix or single-transfer
failures.  The same replay found no mixed strict scheduling direction, no
word with more than two strict scheduling moves, and maximum strict count
two.  This is deterministic-seed falsification evidence beyond the complete
boxes, not a uniform argument.

Excursion products also tensorize the component Euler inequality itself.

**Lemma 22F3A21 (product-cell Euler tensorization).**  For each excursion
`r`, let `C_r` be any finite set of its internal BK states.  Use the
target-excluded cut family on the excursion containing the global target
and the complete target-free cut family on every other excursion.  For
`y_r in C_r`, let

```text
delta_r(y_r)=signed cut sum,
i_r(y_r)=1 if that cut family has no negative cut, and 0 otherwise.
```

On the Cartesian product cell `C=product_r C_r`, the global residual sum
is

```text
sum_(y in C)rho(y)
 =product_r [sum_(y_r in C_r)delta_r(y_r)]
  -product_r [sum_(y_r in C_r)i_r(y_r)].             (P22.30R27G)
```

Consequently, if every factor cell satisfies

```text
sum_(C_r)delta_r >= sum_(C_r)i_r >=0,                (P22.30R27H)
```

then the product cell satisfies `sum_C rho>=0`.

**Proof.**  Excursion factorization makes the global signed sum the product
of the local signed sums.  Every local cut family contains its positive
empty cut.  A product cut family therefore has no negative cut exactly when
every local family has no negative cut, so the global inactivity indicator
is `product_r i_r`.  Summing both products over the Cartesian cell gives
`(P22.30R27G)`.  Under `(P22.30R27H)`, all factors in the first product are
nonnegative and dominate the corresponding factors in the second product,
proving the conclusion.  QED.

Thus primitive component inequalities automatically pay every composite
product cell on which all factor inequalities hold.  Cross-return analysis
is needed only for cells containing a locally negative Euler factor.  This
is the component-sum analogue of Target 22F3P and is the natural interface
with the later Hall-capacity argument.

Square-free BK words have an exact oriented-forest normal form, despite
the failure of braid relations.

**Lemma 22F3A19 (commutation heap of a square-free BK word).**  BK
generators `t_i,t_j` commute whenever `|i-j|>1`.  Let `w` be a word in
which every adjacent generator occurs at most once, and let `S` be its
support.  Orient the edge between `i` and `i+1` in the path induced by `S`
from the generator occurring earlier in `w` to the one occurring later.
Then:

1. the BK state `w x` depends only on `S` and this oriented forest;
2. two square-free words give the same commutation class exactly when they
   have the same support and oriented forest;
3. every linear extension of the oriented-forest order gives the same BK
   state.

In particular a full-support square-free prefix has at most `2^(N-2)`
commutation classes and length at most `N-1`.

**Proof.**  A BK switch at positions `i,i+1` changes only the two jobs in
those positions and preserves their total queue increment.  If
`|i-j|>1`, the two switched pairs are disjoint.  The earlier switch
therefore leaves both the jobs and the incoming total height used by the
later switch unchanged.  The two transfer amounts `z`, the final order,
and all top allocations are identical in either order, proving exact
commutation.

For a square-free word, the only pairs which cannot be interchanged by
these commutations are adjacent generators.  Their relative order is
recorded by the stated orientation.  Conversely, the transitive closure of
that orientation is a finite poset.  Any two of its linear extensions are
connected by swaps of adjacent incomparable elements; incomparable
generators cannot be adjacent in the Coxeter path and hence commute by the
first paragraph.  This proves all three claims.  A path on `N-1`
generators has `N-2` edges, each with two orientations, and square-free
support has at most `N-1` members.  QED.

Thus Target 22F3J asks for a descent on one oriented subforest of the
generator path.  Under Target 22F3M, all but at most one vertex of this
heap are zero-transfer swaps of fixed jobs governed by Lemma 22F3A15.  The
remaining primitive/composite distinction is irrelevant to this normal
form.

The single positive transfer creates only two exceptional named jobs.

**Lemma 22F3A20 (two-exception scheduling reduction).**  Consider a BK
word whose proper prefix is square-free and contains at most one move with
`z>0`.  Before that move, every prefix state is obtained by permuting the
original fixed jobs.  If the positive-transfer move switches named jobs
`a,b` with amount `z`, then after it the only changed job data are

```text
alpha_a'=alpha_a-z,          alpha_b'=alpha_b+z,
beta_a'=beta_a+z,            beta_b'=beta_b-z.        (P22.30R27C)
```

Every later proper-prefix move has `z=0` and merely permutes these two
modified jobs together with the unchanged jobs.  Consequently:

1. every later comparison between two jobs other than `a,b` is oriented by
   the original scheduling preorder `(P22.30R21)`;
2. only comparisons incident to `a` or `b` require a new threshold
   calculation;
3. at the positive-transfer edge itself both mixed-colour local choices
   are absent.

**Proof.**  A zero-transfer BK switch interchanges two jobs without
changing either top allocation, so the assertion before the exceptional
move follows inductively.  Formula `(P22.30R4)` gives `(P22.30R27C)`.
Every subsequent proper-prefix transfer is zero by hypothesis and hence
changes no job data.  Lemma 22F3A15 gives the fixed-preorder orientation
for every pair of unchanged jobs.  Finally `z>0` means
`H<beta_a+beta_b`; as in the proof of Lemma 22F3A5, two separate queues
cannot then pay the two bottom demands, so both mixed choices are absent.
QED.

Thus the combined Targets 22F3J--22F3M reduce to choosing an oriented
subforest and routing at most two exceptional jobs through it.  All other
vertices are controlled by one fixed weak order.  This is the surviving
local escape problem after the failure of the composite two-step targets.

The zero-transfer fibres are order-convex in that weak order.

**Lemma 22F3A22 (scheduling order-convexity).**  Fix the named job data and
orient every adjacent zero-transfer edge into the scheduling preorder
`(P22.30R21)`.  Along every directed path,

```text
Fhat(x_0) subseteq Fhat(x_1) subseteq ... subseteq Fhat(x_m).  (P22.30R27I)
```

Consequently, if `Fhat(x_0)=Fhat(x_m)`, every state on the path has that
same feasible-cut family and every edge is a universal plateau.  Equivalently,
each fibre of `x -> Fhat(x)` is convex along directed scheduling intervals.
The reverse orientation gives the identical statement with all inclusions
reversed.

**Proof.**  On a zero-transfer edge the two named jobs are unchanged.
Lemma 22F3A15 compares their two execution thresholds, and Lemma 22F3A7
turns that comparison into the family inclusion oriented into scheduling
order.  Composing the inclusions proves `(P22.30R27I)`.  If its endpoints
are equal, every intermediate nested family is equal as well; Lemma
22F3A7 then identifies every edge as a universal plateau.  Reversing the
path proves the last statement.  QED.

Thus, away from the two exceptional jobs of Lemma 22F3A20, Target 22F3J is
a boundary theorem for an order-convex fibre in a type-A weak order.  A
proof must show that some correctly signed boundary shell is visible from
a Coxeter-heap path of length at most `N-1`, not from an arbitrary
quadratic-length reduced word.

The equality cases in the scheduling comparison are explicit.  Call a
productive job neutral when `alpha=beta`.

**Lemma 22F3A23 (strict scheduling comparison).**  Suppose `c preceq d` in
the scheduling preorder `(P22.30R21)`.  Then

```text
L(c,d)=L(d,c)                                         (P22.30R27J)
```

holds exactly in the following cases:

1. `c,d` are productive and either `beta_c=beta_d` or `c` is neutral;
2. `c,d` are consumptive and `alpha_c=alpha_d`;
3. `c` is productive, `d` is consumptive, `c` is neutral, and
   `beta_c<=alpha_d`.

In every other case `L(c,d)<L(d,c)`.

**Proof.**  If both jobs are productive and `beta_c<=beta_d`, then

```text
L(d,c)=beta_d,
L(c,d)=beta_c+max(0,beta_d-alpha_c).
```

Equality holds if the two bottom demands agree, or, when
`beta_c<beta_d`, exactly when `alpha_c=beta_c`.

If both jobs are consumptive and `alpha_c>=alpha_d`, then

```text
L(d,c)=beta_d+beta_c-alpha_d.
```

When `beta_d>alpha_c`, subtraction from `L(c,d)` gives
`alpha_c-alpha_d`; when `beta_d<=alpha_c`, it gives the strictly positive
quantity `beta_d-alpha_d`.  Equality is therefore exactly
`alpha_c=alpha_d`.

Finally suppose `c` is productive and `d` consumptive.  Put

```text
x=alpha_c-beta_c>=0,       y=beta_d-alpha_d>0,
t=beta_c-alpha_d.
```

If `t>=0`, direct subtraction shows equality exactly when `x=t=0`.
If `t<0`, equality holds exactly when `x=0`.  These two alternatives say
precisely that `c` is neutral and `beta_c<=alpha_d`.  Lemma 22F3A15 gives
the weak inequality in all cases, so every unlisted case is strict.  QED.

For a zero-transfer swap, the cases `(P22.30R27J)` are local operator
identities and hence universal plateaux.  Every other scheduling-directed
plateau has a strictly oriented local shell which is absent only because
the compatible prefix/suffix support vanishes, as in Corollary 22F3A6.
Thus the unresolved zero-transfer heap consists precisely of locally
strict but globally unsupported inversions; threshold ties may be
contracted at the outset.

The contraction has an exact acyclic rank.  This does not yet choose the
correctly signed exit shell, but it removes directed recurrence from the
zero-transfer problem.

**Lemma 22F3A24 (strict-inversion rank).**  Fix a collection of named jobs,
so every edge under consideration has zero transfer.  Call an unordered
pair `{c,d}` strict when

```text
L(c,d) != L(d,c).
```

Lemma 22F3A15 then gives it a unique scheduling order.  For an ordering
`x` of the jobs, let `nu(x)` be the number of strict pairs which occur in
the reverse of that order.  On an adjacent zero-transfer switch:

1. an exact threshold-tie switch leaves `nu` fixed;
2. a strict switch into scheduling order decreases `nu` by one;
3. a strict switch out of scheduling order increases `nu` by one.

Consequently, contract every connected set of threshold-tie plateau edges
inside a fixed feasible-cut-family fibre and orient every remaining
plateau edge into scheduling order.  The resulting directed quotient is
acyclic.  Every quotient vertex reaches a sink by a path all of whose
strict moves point into scheduling order; reversing the orientation gives
the dual source statement.

**Proof.**  An adjacent switch changes the relative order of exactly its
two named jobs.  If their thresholds agree, their pair is omitted from
`nu`, while the order of every other pair is unchanged.  This proves the
first assertion.  If their thresholds differ, Lemma 22F3A15 and the
strict cases of Lemma 22F3A23 identify a unique scheduling order, so the
switch removes or creates exactly that one strict inversion.  Hence `nu`
strictly decreases along every quotient edge oriented into scheduling
order and is constant on every contracted tie edge.  A directed cycle is
therefore impossible.  Finiteness gives a directed path to a sink, and
reversing all arrows proves the last assertion.  QED.

**Corollary 22F3A24A (extremal-shell reduction).**  Start at any state and
restrict its plateau component to zero-transfer switches of the fixed
jobs.  After arbitrary threshold-tie transport, the state can reach:

1. a scheduling sink using only strict moves into scheduling order;
2. a scheduling source using only strict moves out of scheduling order.

If either extremal tie component has any descending boundary edge, the
initial state has an escape whose strict zero-transfer prefix is
unidirectional.  If neither does, then every strict shell which enlarges
the family from a sink has nonpositive signed sum, while every strict shell
which shrinks the family from a source has strictly positive signed sum.
Every positive-transfer boundary edge at either extremum is likewise
non-descending.

**Proof.**  The two paths are Lemma 22F3A24.  Threshold ties are universal
plateaux by Lemmas 22F3A7 and 22F3A23.  Append a descending boundary edge
to either path to obtain the stated escape.  At a sink, a strict move into
scheduling order enlarges the feasible family.  Corollary 22F3A8 says
that it is a descent exactly when its shell sum is positive, giving the
nonpositive obstruction.  Dually, at a source the move out of scheduling
order shrinks the family and is a descent when the shell sum is negative
or zero, giving the strictly positive obstruction.  A descending
positive-transfer edge would itself finish either unidirectional prefix,
so its absence is necessary for failure.  QED.

Thus the zero-transfer part of Target 22F3Q is reduced to excluding the
opposite shell polarizations in Corollary 22F3A24A under `O>P` and
`delta<0`.  With one positive transfer, Lemma 22F3A20 supplies two
acyclic ranks, one before and one after the exceptional event;
compatibility of their preferred orientations is the additional issue.

The exact escape words satisfy one further monotonicity.

**Target 22F3Q (unidirectional scheduling heap).**  Under the hypotheses of
Target 22F3G, the escape word may be chosen with square-free proper prefix
and at most one positive prefix transfer so that, after discarding
scheduling-preorder ties, every strict zero-transfer move in the proper
prefix has the same orientation: all move into scheduling order or all
move out of scheduling order.

Target 22F3Q strengthens Targets 22F3J--22F3M.  After contracting the
threshold ties classified by Lemma 22F3A23 and isolating the two exceptional
jobs of Lemma 22F3A20, its prefix lies on one directed scheduling interval.
Lemma 22F3A22 then supplies the order-convex plateau geometry.

The strict checker records scheduling-tie, increasing, decreasing, and
mixed-direction prefix counts.  Three complete source-filtered domains
give

```text
(N,max part)  hard states  tie steps  into steps  out steps  mixed states
(7,3)                  10         12           2          6             0
(8,2)                   8          7           2          5             0
(9,2)                  94         95          15         56             0.
                                                               (P22.30R27K)
```

The source-deficit composite obstruction `(P22.30R30)` also satisfies the
normal form: its three proper-prefix moves are all scheduling ties.  Thus
no tested hard word changes strict scheduling direction.  Target 22F3Q is
now the sharp primitive boundary target; the table is exact bounded
evidence, not a proof.

The strict part is smaller still.

**Target 22F3Q1 (two-strict-move core).**  In Target 22F3Q, the proper
prefix can be chosen with at most two strict zero-transfer scheduling
moves.

The same complete replays give

```text
(N,max part)   hard states   states with two strict moves   maximum strict
(7,3)                   10                              0                1
(8,2)                    8                              1                2
(9,2)                   94                              7                2.
                                                               (P22.30R27L)
```

Together with Target 22F3M, this leaves at most three non-tie prefix events:
two strict scheduling swaps and one positive transfer.  It does **not**
assert a constant raw escape radius.  Arbitrarily many threshold-tie swaps
may still be required to transport the exceptional jobs, and those tie
generators remain subject to the square-free linear bound.  Thus Target
22F3Q1 is compatible with the counterexamples to constant-depth Targets
22F3F and 22F3H.

The independent arbitrary-path replay with twelve vertices and queue parts
at most six supplies a larger-height check of both scheduling targets.  Its
`822` negative source-deficit states include one three-step escape; it has

```text
mixed_scheduling_direction_failures=0,
over_two_strict_scheduling_failures=0,
maximum_strict_scheduling_steps=2.                    (P22.30R27M)
```

This is again deterministic-seed evidence, not a proof of either target.

The positive-transfer exception can be removed in every complete domain
currently available.

**Target 22F3R (zero-transfer normal escape).**  Under the hypotheses of
Target 22F3G, an escape word can be chosen so that its proper prefix:

1. is square-free;
2. consists entirely of zero-transfer switches;
3. has one strict scheduling orientation after threshold ties are
   discarded;
4. contains at most two strict scheduling switches.

Target 22F3R simultaneously strengthens Targets 22F3J, 22F3M, 22F3Q, and
22F3Q1.  It still implies the sharp linear bound in Target 22F3G, because a
square-free proper prefix uses at most `N-1` generators and the final exit
adds one edge.  Lemma 22F3A20 is no longer needed inside the prefix; all
named job data remain fixed.

The strict checker now searches for such a word independently of its
unrestricted shortest escape.  In particular, a state passes only if some
normal zero-transfer word exists; it is not enough that the first
breadth-first word happens to have the desired form.  Three complete
source-filtered domains give

```text
(N,max part)  negative  direct  nondirect tested  normal failures
(7,3)          122026   118120             3906                0
(8,2)           15744    15378              366                0
(9,2)          201620   198064             3556                0.
                                                               (P22.30R27N)
```

Thus every nondirect negative `O>P` state in these domains has a
square-free, unidirectional, at-most-two-strict escape with no positive
prefix transfer.  This includes all one-plateau and longer escapes, not
only the hard states in `(P22.30R27K)--(P22.30R27L)`.

The twelve-vertex arbitrary-path replay independently tested all `14`
nondirect states among its `822` negative source-deficit samples.  All
`14` have the same zero-transfer normal form, with
`normal_zero_transfer_failures=0`.

The extremal-shell route is stronger in a different direction.

**Target 22F3R1 (source-shell sign).**  Under `O>P` and `delta<0`, a
scheduling source reachable by zero-transfer plateau moves out of
scheduling order, as in Corollary 22F3A24A, has a strict zero-transfer
boundary edge which moves out of scheduling order, shrinks the feasible-cut
family, and is a descent.  Equivalently, one of its removable strict shells
has signed sum at most zero.

This removes the two-phase compatibility issue left by a positive
transfer: a purely zero-transfer unidirectional prefix reaches the
correctly signed shell.  It does not by itself give the square-free or
two-strict compression in Target 22F3R.

On the hard states of the same three complete domains, the exact
zero-transfer plateau graphs give

```text
(N,max part)  hard  source shrink descents  sink descents  obstructions
(7,3)           10                      10             10             0
(8,2)            8                       8              8             0
(9,2)           94                      94             74             0.
                                                               (P22.30R27O)
```

The maximum zero-transfer component sizes in these rows are respectively
`144`, `80`, and `720`; the maximum threshold-tie quotient sizes are
`15`, `29`, and `169`.

The extrema also have positive-transfer plateau edges in respectively
`8`, `8`, and `84` of these hard states, but none needs to take one: every
case already has a descending removable source shell.  The sink count at
`(9,2)` shows that a dual sink-only formulation is too strong in the
bounded source-deficit domain.  Targets 22F3R and 22F3R1 are uniform proof
obligations; `(P22.30R27N)--(P22.30R27O)` are bounded exact evidence.

The source condition now enters through one explicit contrapositive.

**Lemma 22F3A25 (order-slice signed sum).**  Fix any vertex order and let
`X_prec=B_prec(d)` be its complete set of global top allocations.  Let `E`
be the empty-channel source dimension for the signed degree list.  Then

```text
|X_prec|=E,
sum_(x in X_prec)o(x)=O,
sum_(x in X_prec)e(x)=P,

sum_(x in X_prec)delta(x)=E+P-O.                       (P22.30R27P)
```

In particular

```text
O<=P
  iff sum_(x in X_prec)[delta(x)-1]>=0.                (P22.30R27Q)
```

**Proof.**  An empty cut at `x` is precisely the global tableau path with
top allocation `x`, so the empty-channel multiplicity is
`|B_prec(d)|`.  For a nonempty cut `T`, restricting the global tableau to
the two colours gives one tableau for `T` and one for its complement
together with the target.  Conversely, every such pair interleaves
uniquely in the fixed vertex order and recovers its global top allocation.
Thus summing the odd and nonempty even cut counts over all allocations
counts exactly the source spaces `O` and `P`.  Lemma 22F3A1 gives
`delta=1+e-o`; summing it proves `(P22.30R27P)`, and subtracting `E`
proves `(P22.30R27Q)`.  QED.

The strict checker verifies the slice identity independently on the closed
primitive obstruction `(P22.30N8)`.  Its fixed order has `59` allocations,
`8` negative states, and

```text
sum_x delta(x)=235=59+520-344.
```

Thus its trapped negative states are paid only after summing over the full
order slice, exactly as Lemma 22F3A25 predicts; a statewise nonnegative
bound would be false.

**Target 22F3R2 (source-shell capacity).**  Let `y` be a negative state in
a threshold-tie component which is a scheduling source after the
zero-transfer strict edges are oriented into scheduling order.  Suppose
every strict adjacent switch out of scheduling order which shrinks
`Fhat(y)` has strictly positive shell sum.  Then the order-independent
source dimensions satisfy

```text
O<=P.                                                   (P22.30R27R)
```

Targets 22F3R1 and 22F3R2 are equivalent.

Indeed, assume Target 22F3R2 and start from `O>P`, `delta<0`.  Lemma
22F3A24 reaches a scheduling source `y` on the same plateau.  If no
removable source shell is a descent, Corollary 22F3A8 says that every such
shell has strictly positive signed sum.  Target 22F3R2 would give `O<=P`,
a contradiction, so Target 22F3R1 follows.  Conversely, if the hypotheses
of Target 22F3R2 held with `O>P`, Target 22F3R1 applied at `y` would
produce a removable shell with nonpositive signed sum, again a
contradiction.  By Lemma 22F3A25, Target 22F3R2 asks precisely for the
order-slice lower bound

```text
sum_(x in X_prec)[delta(x)-1]>=0                       (P22.30R27S)
```

from positivity of the removable shells at `y`.  This is the exact
remaining global-capacity statement behind the local source-shell theorem.

Thus a plateau word with no positive transfer moves through a fixed weak
scheduling order on the jobs; only globally unsupported
threshold shells allow an inversion to cross without changing the cut
family.  Positive-transfer moves are the sole points where the job data
themselves change.

The strict C++ diagnostic
`character_ring_iter/analyze_su2_bk_local_operator.cpp` constructs both
local matrices independently and computes exact modular ranks.  With queue
parts at most five and incoming height at most twelve it finds maximum rank
ten, attained by the `m=5` instance of Lemma 22F3A4.  In the same `49,392`
local cases it finds no mixed-sign unsigned defect and no failure of the
threshold orientation `(P22.30R9)`.  The latter check is only a regression
test for Lemma 22F3A5, whose proof is uniform.  Therefore a proof of the
plateau targets must use the special prefix/suffix contractions or
cancellation among several switches, not low rank of an individual BK
edge.  Corollary 22F3A6 gives the first such restriction: an unsigned
plateau forces the prefix and suffix supports to avoid every local defect
entry.  The remaining task is to combine this support exclusion with the
signed defect along an escape word.

Lemma 22F3A7 sharpens that task.  Plateau navigation does not require
tracking signed transfers at all: it moves inside one fibre of the exact
feasible-cut-family map.  The signs and target enter only when choosing an
exit shell from that fibre.  Target 22F3I is therefore a
diameter-to-boundary statement for an unsigned fibre, followed by the shell
orientation test `(P22.30R11)` on its boundary.

For a switch at positions `i,i+1`, the prefix and suffix transfer vectors
in `(P22.30R1)` are unchanged; only the two local job factors and their
intermediate total height change according to `(P22.18)`.  For the
proposed two-step escape `(P22.30N3)`, only three consecutive job factors
change.  Thus the false Target 22F3F below would have reduced to a
three-factor comparison of the two scalar contractions in `(P22.30R1)`,
with arbitrary compatible prefix and suffix transfer vectors.  The
counterexamples `(P22.30N5)--(P22.30N6)` show that the actual plateau
descent cannot be truncated to this finite-width local queue problem.

The first exact cases show both locality and a real alternating-path
requirement.  For

```text
[1^-,1^-,2^+,3^+], target 1,
[1^-,1^-,2^+,2^+,3^+], target 1,
[2^+,2^+,3^-,4^+,5^+], target 2,
```

the minimum saturating radii are respectively `4,5,6`.  In the middle case
the unique component has `5,040` states, demand `1,032`, capacity `2,376`,
and slack `1,344`; the exact maximum flows at radii one through five are

```text
104, 304, 696, 1008, 1032.                          (P22.30T)
```

One deterministic sweep does not suffice.  Bubbling the first vertex
through the whole order partitions this same component into `840`
promotion cycles, of which `224` have negative residual sum; the minimum is
`-8`.  Adding canonical order reversal gives `420` dihedral orbits, still
with `112` negative sums and minimum `-16`.  The other two displayed cases
have the same obstruction.  Hence a proof of `(P22.30R)` must use
alternating paths among several adjacent-swap directions rather than sum
independent promotion or dihedral cycles.  The exact histograms, maximum
flows, and cycle decompositions are recorded in
`certificates/su2_gt_bk_euler_transport.log`.

The radius model has an exact Hall dual.  Put

```text
D_C={x in C:rho(x)<0},       S_C={y in C:rho(y)>0},
d(x)=-rho(x),                c(y)=rho(y),
N_R(A)={y in S_C:dist_K(y,A)<=R}.                    (P22.30H)
```

**Lemma 22F3B (weighted metric Hall criterion).**  The radius-`R`
transport saturates every negative residual if and only if

```text
sum_(x in A)d(x)<=sum_(y in N_R(A))c(y)
for every A subseteq D_C.                            (P22.30I)
```

If the maximum flow is deficient, the vertices reachable from the source in
the residual network give a set `A` for which the deficit in `(P22.30I)` is
exactly total demand minus maximum flow.

**Proof.**  Use the capacitated bipartite network with source capacities
`d(x)`, sink capacities `c(y)`, and an edge of capacity equal to the total
demand whenever `dist_K(x,y)<=R`.  A cut of value below the total demand
cannot cut one of the latter edges.  Its source-side demand set `A` must
therefore carry all of `N_R(A)`, and its value is

```text
total demand-sum_(x in A)d(x)+sum_(y in N_R(A))c(y).
```

The max-flow/min-cut theorem now gives both assertions.  QED.

The possible Hall obstructions have one exact irreducibility reduction.
For `A subseteq D_C`, put

```text
Phi_R(A)=sum_(x in A)d(x)-sum_(y in N_R(A))c(y).
```

**Lemma 22F3B1 (minimal Hall collision cluster).**  Suppose `A` is
inclusion-minimal with `Phi_R(A)>0`.  Join `x,x' in A` when their
radius-`R` positive neighborhoods overlap.  Then this overlap graph is
connected.  Moreover, for

```text
P_A(x)=N_R({x}) \ N_R(A\{x}),
```

the private positive capacity of `x`, one has

```text
d(x)-sum_(y in P_A(x))c(y)
 =Phi_R(A)-Phi_R(A\{x})
 >=Phi_R(A)>0.                                      (P22.30I1)
```

In particular every proper subset of `A` satisfies Hall, and a proof of
Target 22F3C need only exclude connected overlap clusters in which every
demand vertex already exceeds its private capacity by at least the common
cluster deficit.

**Proof.**  If the overlap graph had components `A_i`, their positive
neighborhoods would be pairwise disjoint, whence
`Phi_R(A)=sum_i Phi_R(A_i)`.  Some proper component would have positive
deficit, contradicting minimality.  Also

```text
N_R(A)=N_R(A\{x}) disjoint_union P_A(x).
```

Subtracting the two definitions of `Phi_R` proves the equality in
`(P22.30I1)`.  Minimality gives `Phi_R(A\{x})<=0`, proving the inequality.
QED.

There is an exact scalar measure of the remaining collision.  Define

```text
kappa_R(A)
 =sum_(x in A) sum_(y in N_R({x}))c(y)
  -sum_(y in N_R(A))c(y),                             (P22.30I2)

s_R(x)=sum_(y in N_R({x}))c(y)-d(x).
```

**Lemma 22F3B2 (overlap-congestion form).**  The Hall inequality for `A`
is equivalent to

```text
kappa_R(A)<=sum_(x in A)s_R(x).                       (P22.30I3)
```

**Proof.**  Subtract the right side of `(P22.30I3)` from the left.  The
singleton-neighborhood terms cancel, leaving

```text
sum_(x in A)d(x)-sum_(y in N_R(A))c(y)=Phi_R(A).
```

Thus the two inequalities are identical.  QED.

Consequently singleton expansion is insufficient: the sum of the
singleton slacks must also pay every repeated use of shared positive
capacity.  Exact enumeration in the complete five-factor bounded domain
finds genuine two-state minimal failures at the last failing radius.  The
first has signed labels

```text
[1^-,2^+,2^+,2^+,3^+],       target=2,       R=4,
```

with two demand-three states.  Each has private capacity two and the pair
shares capacity one.  Hence each singleton is tight, while

```text
kappa_4(A)=1,       sum_(x in A)s_4(x)=0,
Phi_4(A)=1.
```

This is at `R=N-2`, so it is not a counterexample to Target 22F3C.  It
does rule out reducing the proof to singleton ball inequalities; the
uniform task is precisely to prove `(P22.30I3)` for every connected
minimal overlap cluster at `R=N`.

The complete bounded classification contains `24` such multi-state
minimal failures, and all have the same incidence type: two demand-three
vertices, four private capacity-one vertices, and one shared capacity-one
vertex.  Thus their demand--capacity incidence graph is already a tree,
and

```text
kappa_R(A)=the weight of its unique demand-overlap edge=1.
```

Consequently acyclicity of the incidence hypergraph is also insufficient.
Even in the tree case, a proof must charge every overlap-edge weight to
singleton slack created by the remaining BK shells.  The two strict
instrumented replays of the `1,001`-case domain were byte-identical; this
is exact bounded evidence, not a uniform incidence-tree theorem.

This charging statement has an exact sufficient form.  Put

```text
Sigma_(R+1)(A)=N_(R+1)(A)\N_R(A),
```

and let `Q_(R+1)^A(x)` be the positive states in this new shell which lie
within radius `R+1` of `x` and of no other member of `A`.  The sets
`Q_(R+1)^A(x)` are pairwise disjoint.

**Lemma 22F3B3 (one-shell private repair).**  Let `A` be an
inclusion-minimal multi-state Hall failure at radius `R`.  If

```text
sum_(x in A) sum_(y in Q_(R+1)^A(x))c(y)>=kappa_R(A), (P22.30I4)
```

then `A` satisfies Hall at radius `R+1`.

**Proof.**  Every singleton is a proper subset of `A`, so minimality gives
`s_R(x)>=0`.  Lemma 22F3B2 therefore gives

```text
0<Phi_R(A)
 =kappa_R(A)-sum_(x in A)s_R(x)
 <=kappa_R(A).
```

The private sets in `(P22.30I4)` lie in `Sigma_(R+1)(A)`, so the new union
capacity is at least `kappa_R(A)`, hence at least `Phi_R(A)`.  Since

```text
Phi_(R+1)(A)
 =Phi_R(A)-sum_(y in Sigma_(R+1)(A))c(y),
```

it follows that `Phi_(R+1)(A)<=0`.  QED.

All `24` bounded two-state failures satisfy `(P22.30I4)` very strongly:
their old congestion is one, while the next shell contains private
capacities `11` and `10` and shared capacity `8`.  Their Hall slack jumps
from `-1` at radius four to `28` at radius five.  Thus the surviving
uniform question is whether terminal BK shells always provide a private
charge such as `(P22.30I4)`, together with the separate singleton
expansion needed for one-state minimal failures.

The singleton part is an exact terminal-shell statement.

**Target 22F3C0 (terminal singleton shell).**  For every negative state
`x` in the source-deficit sector,

```text
sum_(y in N_N({x})\N_(N-1)({x}))c(y)
 >=max(0,d(x)-sum_(y in N_(N-1)({x}))c(y)).           (P22.30I5)
```

This is necessary and sufficient for all singleton instances of Target
22F3C: the left side is precisely the new radius-`N` capacity, while the
right side is the radius-`N-1` singleton deficit.

The complete bounded radius audit contains `1,920` inclusion-minimal
singleton failures at their last failing radii.  Every one is repaired by
the next shell.  Their deficits are one, two, or three.  However a single
new positive state is too small to pay the deficit in `448` cases, and is
too small to pay the full demand in `816` cases.  Thus a proof of
`(P22.30I5)` must count aggregate shell capacity; it cannot select only one
dominant endpoint.  The two strict instrumented replays were byte-identical.
This is bounded evidence for Target 22F3C0, not a proof.

Among these failures, `272` are sharp: their next-shell capacity equals
their old deficit.  Every sharp case has zero old positive capacity and
the terminal shell consists of exactly `d(x)` distinct capacity-one
states.  The demand histogram is

```text
d=1:208,       d=2:48,       d=3:16.
```

The earlier count `252` was an extraction error, not a mathematical
subfamily: `20` `BK_SHARP` markers were concatenated to the preceding
component line because that diagnostic lacked a final newline, so a
line-anchored filter omitted them.  Counting marker occurrences gives
`272` in each byte-identical replay.

This sharp equality is not explained by one local word.  A demand-three
case needs three different unit exits, and its shortest words already
have three incompatible shapes.  Another sharp case has shortest word
`[0,1,2,3,2]`, refuting a palindromic or single local-braid rule.
Consequently a uniform equality proof must create `d(x)` distinct
terminal trace classes; neither one dominant endpoint nor one fixed
escape-word template can supply them.  Two full sharp-case sweeps were
byte-identical.  The unit-shell classification is exact bounded evidence,
not a proved equality theorem.

The scalar capacities admit a literal cut-ticket model.  Let `O_x` be the
odd admissible cuts at `x` and `E_x` the nonempty even admissible cuts.

**Lemma 22F3B4 (residual cut tickets).**  If `x` is negative, choose any
injection

```text
E_x disjoint_union {star} -> O_x
```

and let `U_x` be its unmatched odd cuts.  Then `|U_x|=d(x)`.  If `y` is
positive and `o(y)>0`, choose an injection

```text
O_y -> E_y disjoint_union {star}
```

and let `P_y` be the unmatched codomain tickets.  If `o(y)=0`, put
`P_y=E_y`.  In both cases `|P_y|=c(y)`.

**Proof.**  For negative `x`,
`d(x)=o(x)-e(x)-1>0`, so the first injection exists and leaves exactly
`d(x)` tickets.  For positive `y` with `o(y)>0`,
`c(y)=e(y)-o(y)+1>0`, proving the second assertion.  When `o(y)=0`,
`c(y)=e(y)`.  QED.

There is an exact way to remove the arbitrary choices from this ticket
test.  Consider a sharp case, so the terminal shell has `d=d(x)`
capacity-one states.  For such a terminal `y`, put

```text
L_y=E_y                         if o(y)=0,
L_y=E_y disjoint_union {star}   if o(y)>0.
```

**Lemma 22F3B5 (free-cancellation matching criterion).**  Fix relations
`R_y` between source odd cuts and tickets in `L_y`.  There are injections
allowed by Lemma 22F3B4, and a bijection from the resulting `U_x` to the
terminal states with `(u,p) in R_y`, if and only if the bipartite graph

```text
O_x -- {terminal states y},   u~y iff some p in L_y has (u,p) in R_y
```

has a matching which saturates all terminal states.

**Proof.**  A ticket bijection gives the stated matching.  Conversely, a
saturating matching selects `d` distinct cuts `u_y` in `O_x`.  Take these
as `U_x` and biject `E_x disjoint_union {star}` with their complement in
`O_x`.  At a terminal with `o(y)>0`, capacity one gives
`|L_y|=o(y)+1`; after choosing the witnessing `p_y`, biject `O_y` with
`L_y\{p_y}`.  The case `o(y)=0` is forced.  These are precisely the
required injections.  QED.

There is also a uniform parity obstruction.  Let `M` be the minus mask and
write

```text
pi(S)=|S intersect M| mod 2.
```

**Lemma 22F3B6 (parity obstruction to endpoint ticket rules).**  Every
source ticket `u in O_x` has `pi(u)=1`, while every allowable terminal
ticket `p in L_y` has `pi(p)=0`.  Hence a rule

```text
u=p symmetric_difference tau_y
```

can give an edge in Lemma 22F3B5 only if `pi(tau_y)=1`.

**Proof.**  The first two assertions are the definitions of odd and even
cuts; `star` is the empty even cut.  Add parities modulo two in the displayed
identity.  QED.

In particular, equality (`tau_y=empty`) is impossible for every nonzero
sharp demand, without computation.  Complement can work only when the
total number of minus factors is odd.  A touched-set rule leaves any
terminal with even minus-intersection completely isolated.

The named subsets still do not provide the missing transport rule, even
with this full freedom.  An exact replay of all `272` sharp cases applied
Lemma 22F3B5 to equality, complement in the non-target factor universe,
and symmetric difference with the set of factor identities touched by a
deterministic shortest BK word.  Equality and complement hold in `0`
cases; the shortest-word symmetric-difference rule holds in only `40`.
The first equality and complement failure has labels `[1,2,2,3]`,
target `2`, minus mask `1`, `d=1`,

```text
O_x={{0,1,3},{0,2,3}},   L_y={{1,2},star}.
```

The first symmetric-difference failure has labels `[1,1,2,3,4]`, target
`1`, minus mask `3`, and `d=3`; no three of its four source odd cuts
admit a perfect matching to its three terminal option sets.  Lemma 22F3B6
already explains this failure: two terminal touched sets are
`{0,1,2,3}` and `{2,4}`, whose intersections with the minus mask
`{0,1}` have even cardinality, so both terminal vertices are isolated.
Two strict full runs were byte-identical (SHA-256
`4f5f157fbcaa17c70156749a98b4d534825c00e0c3537a664b3d67ff244d13f2`).
Thus every literal rule tested here is ruled out even after optimizing
all cancellations.  A proof must instead construct a structural
path-compatible relation which is not determined solely by the endpoint
cut subsets and the set of factor identities touched by one shortest
word.

Two natural word traces are in fact endpoint invariants.  For an adjacent
swap word `gamma` from order `sigma` to order `sigma'`, let `I_gamma`
contain each non-target factor participating in an odd number of swaps,
and let `T_gamma` contain each factor crossing the target an odd number of
times.

**Lemma 22F3B7 (endpoint invariance of elementary traces).**

```text
I_gamma={i: position_sigma(i)-position_(sigma')(i) is odd},

T_gamma={i: the relative order of i and the target differs
             between sigma and sigma'}.
```

In particular these masks cannot be improved by choosing a different
shortest path.

**Proof.**  Every swap involving `i` changes its position by one, so the
parity of the number of such swaps is its endpoint displacement parity.
The relative order of `i` and the target changes exactly when that pair
is swapped.  QED.

An exact shortest-path-DAG audit tested these and six more ordered traces.
The extra traces use the identity moving left or right at each swap, or
retain swap endpoints only when the BK transfer is odd or positive.  For
every terminal it propagated all trace masks over all shortest words and
then used Lemma 22F3B5 with arbitrary cancellations.  There are `352`
terminal occurrences in the `272` sharp cases.  The results are

```text
trace                         passing cases    terminals with no odd pi
all swap incidences                 0/272                 156
target crossings                    0/272                 136
left movers                         4/272                 190
right movers                        4/272                 190
odd-transfer incidences             0/272                 240
odd-transfer left movers            0/272                 260
odd-transfer right movers           0/272                 260
positive-transfer incidences        0/272                 128.
```

Every propagated trace set happened to be a singleton in this bounded
audit.  This is proved uniformly only for the first two traces by Lemma
22F3B7.  The best rule, left movers, already fails for labels
`[1,2,2,3]`, target `2`, minus mask `1`, and `d=1`:

```text
O_x={{0,1,3},{0,2,3}},   L_y={{1,2},star},   tau_y={0,3}.
```
