The trace has the necessary odd minus parity, but neither allowed terminal
ticket becomes a source odd cut after symmetric difference.  Thus the
parity condition in Lemma 22F3B6 is far from sufficient.  Two optimized
strict runs were byte-identical (SHA-256
`53147cc64d22adf7ec9266f5bcdacff6b2276df64a9ad02069c785b96f5eb959`).
The missing trace must depend on the cut decomposition transported through
each BK move, not merely on the state path, its endpoint permutation, or
the scalar BK transfer.

That dependence has a precise local source.  Suppose a cut object
decomposes a state as

```text
(D,z)=(A,a)+(B,b),
```

where `(A,a)` and `(B,b)` are the two toric factors belonging to the two
sides of the cut.  At adjacent positions `r,r+1`, set

```text
ell_D(z)=(D_r-z_r)+(D_(r+1)-z_(r+1))
         -sum_(p<r)(2z_p-D_p),

t_D(z)=max(0,ell_D(z)).
```

Thus `t_D(z)` is exactly the amount transferred by the raw BK switch.

**Lemma 22F3B8 (local BK additivity defect).**  With the preceding
decomposition,

```text
ell_D(z)=ell_A(a)+ell_B(b)
```

and

```text
delta=t_A(a)+t_B(b)-t_D(z)
     =max(0,x)+max(0,y)-max(0,x+y)>=0,                (P22.30I6)
```

where `x=ell_A(a)` and `y=ell_B(b)`.  If `delta=0`, applying the BK switch
to the two cut factors separately gives a decomposition of the BK-switched
total state with the same named cut.  If `delta>0`, the sum of the two
separately switched tops differs from the switched total top by

```text
-delta at the first switched vertex,
+delta at the second switched vertex.                 (P22.30I7)
```

Moreover `delta>0` exactly when `x` and `y` have opposite strict signs,
and then `delta=min(|x|,|y|)`.

**Proof.**  The expression `ell` is linear in the degree/top pair, giving
the first identity.  The formula for `delta` and its sign follow from the
subadditivity of the positive part.  The raw BK formula subtracts its
transfer from the first switched top coordinate and adds it to the second,
which proves the last two assertions.  QED.

The scalar defect itself has a useful canonical normal form.  Write
`u_+=max(0,u)`.

**Lemma 22F3B8a (core--slack normal form).**  For integers `x,y`,

```text
delta=x_+ + y_+ - (x+y)_+
     =(|x|+|y|-|x+y|)/2.                              (P22.30I7a)
```

If `delta>0`, put `epsilon=sgn(x)=-sgn(y)` and `s=x+y`.  Then, uniquely,

```text
x= epsilon[delta+(epsilon s)_+],
y=-epsilon[delta+(-epsilon s)_+].                     (P22.30I7b)
```

Thus `(x,y)` is the sum of the balanced core
`(epsilon delta,-epsilon delta)` and a one-sided residual of defect zero.
The additivity mismatch in `(P22.30I7)` depends only on the core: it is
always `delta(e_(r+1)-e_r)`, independent of `epsilon` and the residual
slack.

**Proof.**  Formula `(P22.30I7a)` follows by checking whether `x,y` have
the same or opposite signs.  Positive defect forces opposite strict signs.
If `x` has sign `epsilon`, then `|x|=delta+(epsilon s)_+` and
`|y|=delta+(-epsilon s)_+`; these give `(P22.30I7b)` and uniqueness.
At most one of the two added terms is nonzero, so the residual is one-sided
and has zero defect.  Substitution in `(P22.30I7)` proves the last claim.
QED.

This lemma is only a scalar and top-update normalization.  It does not show
that the clipped residual is itself an admissible toric cut occurrence, nor
that the core can be peeled into `delta` multiplicity-preserving unit
Plucker/Temperley--Lieb moves.

Consequently every defect-zero edge gives canonical same-cut transport.
Such transport preserves cut parity and therefore cannot by itself take an
odd source ticket to an even terminal ticket.  The required parity change
must occur at a positive-defect edge, where `(P22.30I7)` demands a local
Plucker/Temperley--Lieb straightening.  This replaces the unsuccessful
path-mask search by a concrete next target: resolve each positive defect
into cut objects at the neighboring state, with multiplicities, and prove
that the resulting local boundaries satisfy the terminal Hall injection.

There is a canonical partial correspondence before any straightening.
Let `Omega_x` be the multiset of full cut occurrences `(S,a,b)` in a fixed
total-state fibre, including the empty cut and retaining multiplicities.

**Lemma 22F3B9 (defect-zero partial bijection).**  Across one BK edge
`x--y`, separately switching the two toric factors gives a parity-preserving
bijection

```text
{omega in Omega_x: delta_x(omega)=0}
 <->
{omega' in Omega_y: delta_y(omega')=0}.                (P22.30I8)
```

Every occurrence outside this partial bijection has positive defect in
the direction in which it is unmatched.

**Proof.**  Lemma 22F3B8 shows that a defect-zero separate switch sums to
the switched total state, so it is a cut occurrence at `y` with the same
named subset.  The BK switch on each toric factor is involutive.  Applying
the inverse separate switches therefore returns the original occurrence
and has zero reverse defect.  This proves both bijectivity and the last
assertion.  QED.

Call an unmatched occurrence at the source of an oriented edge a death,
and an unmatched occurrence at its target a birth.  If

```text
s(x)=1+e(x)-o(x)=|Omega_x^even|-|Omega_x^odd|,
```

then `(P22.30I8)` gives the exact event balance

```text
s(y)-s(x)
 =#odd deaths + #even births - #even deaths - #odd births. (P22.30I9)
```

The residual used in Hall transport is
`rho(x)=s(x)-1_(o(x)=0)`, so only the already explicit zero-odd correction
must be added when a path enters or leaves `o=0`.

The first sharp obstruction exhibits the mechanism literally.  For
labels `[1,2,2,3]`, target `2`, minus mask `1`, the path

```text
[1,2,3,2]: state 476 -> 332 -> 197 -> 333 -> 447
```

has one odd death at its second edge:

```text
S={0,2,3},       x=2,       y=-2,       delta=2.
```

It has no locally compatible occurrence at the next state.  At the final
edge the even occurrence `S={1,2}` is born; in the reverse direction its
data are `x=-2`, `y=2`, `delta=2`.  All other occurrences along the path
belong to the defect-zero partial bijection.  Thus the demand is not
resolved by a direct odd-to-even local map: an odd defect death and a later
even defect birth must be paired along the intervening defect-zero
transport.  Two strict replays of this full occurrence trace were
byte-identical (SHA-256
`a3a1a4664a05dba5673be90208e00f35ef1e64af02c1356eef46fcf938607b3b`).

This gives a nontrivial relation between endpoint tickets.  In the layered
shortest-path DAG from `x`, say that a source odd occurrence `u` bridges
an allowable even occurrence `p` at a positive terminal if

1. `u` follows defect-zero maps to an odd death;
2. `p` follows inverse defect-zero maps to an even birth;
3. the death endpoint reaches the birth source in the layered DAG; and
4. the two positive defects are equal.

**Lemma 22F3B10 (sharp event-bridge criterion).**  In a sharp case with
`d` capacity-one terminal states, injections allowed by Lemma 22F3B4 and
a bijection of the resulting `d` source tickets with bridged terminal
tickets exist if and only if the bipartite graph

```text
O_x -- {terminal states y},

u~y iff u bridges some allowable ticket p at y
```

has a matching saturating the `d` terminal states.

**Proof.**  A bridged ticket bijection gives the matching.  Conversely,
the matching selects `d` distinct source odd occurrences and one allowable
ticket at each terminal.  Lemma 22F3B5 constructs the source and terminal
cancellation injections leaving exactly those tickets.  QED.

The complete sharp audit now supports this criterion.  It constructs full
occurrence identities `(S,a,b)`, not just named masks, propagates over all
shortest paths, and retains multiplicities.  All `272/272` sharp cases
have a matching in Lemma 22F3B10.  Requiring equal death and birth defects
does not remove a case.  If the gap is the shortest DAG distance from a
death endpoint to its matched birth source, the minimum possible
bottleneck gap for a full matching has histogram

```text
gap 1: 144 cases,       gap 2: 80 cases,       gap 3: 48 cases.
```

Thus every bounded sharp case admits a full equal-defect bridge matching
with gap at most three.  Two strict optimized runs were byte-identical
(SHA-256
`f01cf21d31ae4d6cb21d3be3ab8115ba5a0d65845bf50c7e67874c436b00c6ba`).
This is exact bounded evidence, not a proof that the bridge matching exists
uniformly.

The same construction has a capacitated form which is no longer restricted
to sharp equality cases.

**Target 22F3C0a (defect-event ticket flow).**  For every negative source
state `x`, use the layered BK DAG through radius `N`.  Put a unit node at
each source odd occurrence and at each allowable positive ticket
occurrence.  Join them by the equal-defect bridge relation above, and give
the ticket occurrences belonging to a positive state `y` total capacity
`c(y)`.  The resulting capacitated bipartite flow has value `d(x)`.

Target 22F3C0a implies Target 22F3C0: an integral flow selects `d(x)`
distinct source odd occurrences and, at each `y`, at most `c(y)` distinct
allowable tickets.  The cancellation injections of Lemma 22F3B4 can leave
all selected tickets unmatched, so
`sum_(y in N_N(x))c(y)>=d(x)`.

Target 22F3C0a survives the complete bounded singleton census.  The exact
flow audit includes all `1,920` last-failing singleton instances, all
positive states through radius `R+1`, old neighborhood capacity, nonunit
state capacities, empty-ticket conventions, and full occurrence
multiplicities.  It requires equal positive defects at the odd death and
even birth.  Every instance has flow value `d(x)`.  The minimum possible
bottleneck gap of a full flow has histogram

```text
gap 0: 128,      gap 1: 1512,      gap 2: 224,      gap 3: 56.
```

Here gap zero means that the death endpoint is the source state of the
next birth edge; it does not identify death and birth on the same edge.
The `272/272` sharp equal-defect subcheck remains intact.  Two optimized
strict runs were byte-identical (SHA-256
`cf231e2753b8b61a7a21b4cde01b256ca5d92edd8236e41291ad1503e148d6bd`).
Thus no bounded singleton obstruction to Target 22F3C0a remains.  This is
still computation, not a uniform proof of the defect-event flow.

The observed radius is local enough to state a sharper target.

**Target 22F3C0b (three-gap defect bridge).**  The flow in Target 22F3C0a
can be chosen so that every matched odd death and even birth have equal
defect and the death endpoint reaches the birth source in at most three
distance-increasing BK edges.

Target 22F3C0b implies Target 22F3C0a.  Its complete bridge word, including
the death and birth edges, has length at most five.  The bounded histogram
above verifies Target 22F3C0b in all `1,920` singleton instances.  This
suggests a finite local proof program: classify the normalized generator
words of length at most five selected by a minimum-bottleneck flow, then
prove the corresponding equal-defect Plucker/Temperley--Lieb identities
and their compatibility under commuting and braid moves.

The resulting bounded classification is now canonical with respect to the
generator word: among equal-defect minimum-bottleneck witnesses, the audit
first minimizes the complete generator word and only then internal edge and
occurrence identifiers.  It selects `3,664` source--ticket pairs.  Their
word lengths, generator spans, and defects are

```text
length: 2:340, 3:3042, 4:226, 5:56,
span:   1:3088, 2:558, 3:18,
delta:  1:2508, 2:882, 3:274.                         (P22.30I10)
```

There are `20` translation-normalized generator words:

```text
[0,1]:52,          [0,1,0]:1516,       [0,1,0,1]:16,
[0,1,0,1,0]:4,     [0,1,2]:24,         [0,1,2,1]:84,
[0,1,2,1,0]:24,    [0,2]:134,          [1,0]:42,
[1,0,1]:1438,      [1,0,1,0]:16,       [1,0,1,0,1]:4,
[1,0,2,1]:24,      [2,0]:112,          [2,0,1]:16,
[2,0,1,2]:2,       [2,1,0]:30,         [2,1,0,1]:84,
[2,1,0,1,2]:24,    [3,0,1]:18.                        (P22.30I11)
```

Translation and reversal together leave `16` shapes.  Two strict C++20
runs were byte-identical: the log SHA-256 is
`b39b87e26104c0ee0b423158f34d1acebeb8f884b662d1ccb42801f6bc94c0e8`
and the complete-table SHA-256 is
`48f1935cc0e13a8b86e09b7d6f2973d9f03a58c97554cb6060cae5755f11566f`.
The flow remains `1,920/1,920`, including `272/272` sharp cases.  These
counts are exact for the bounded census, but they do not prove that the
same finite word list suffices at higher degree, more factors, or arbitrary
level.

Only two canonical shapes are exceptional relative to the connected
nearest-generator patterns.  The `18` occurrences of `[3,0,1]` all have
`delta=1`; Coxeter commutation gives

```text
[3,0,1] equivalent to [0,3,1] equivalent to [0,1,3],
```

so this is a connected `[0,1]` block with one commuting spectator.  The two
occurrences of `[2,0,1,2]` have `delta=2`, and

```text
[2,0,1,2] equivalent to [0,2,1,2]
            equivalent to [0,1,2,1]
```

by one commutation and one braid move.  These are identities of state words
only.  The first and last letters mark the death and birth events, so
reordering them does not yet prove an identity of occurrence bridges.
Indeed the generator-first audit would have selected the displayed
lexicographically smaller representatives if they were admissible marked
bridges for the same selected source--ticket pairs; it did not.  Directed
reachability or occurrence transport therefore obstructs the naive
reordering.  Precisely this missing naturality under commuting and braid
squares is part of the next target.

The exact next lifting problem is correspondingly sharper.

**False Target 22F3C0c (independent core--slack lifting).**  Every
positive-defect cut occurrence admits a multiplicity-preserving local
Plucker/Temperley--Lieb resolution into admissible occurrence traces in
which the balanced core of Lemma 22F3B8a supplies the defect event and the
one-sided scalar slack enters only through parity-preserving defect-zero
transport.  This resolution is natural under defect-zero BK transport and
the required commuting and braid moves, preserves distinct ticket
occurrences, and is compatible with resolving a defect-`delta` core into
`delta` unit cores.

Lemma 22F3B8a proves only the scalar normal form behind this assertion.
The following two obstructions show that it does not lift with the
spectator kept independent.

A literal same-cut version of this target is false.  For a positive-defect
occurrence put `t_L=x_+`, `t_R=y_+`; subtract `delta` from the unique positive
one and call the resulting transfers `hat(t)_L,hat(t)_R`.  Their sum is
`(x+y)_+`, so applying them separately always gives the correct *total* top
at the BK neighbor.  It is tempting to assert that both resulting component
tops are toric-admissible there and hence form a same-cut occurrence.  In
the complete bounded oriented-edge census, among `298,592` positive-defect
occurrences, this happens in only `7,232`.  Exact fibre membership and
separate component admissibility agree in every tested case.

The first failure is already

```text
labels=[1,1,2,3], target=1, minus mask=3,
generator=1, subset={0,1},
source order=[2,0,1,3,4], source top=[1,0,2,1,0],
neighbor order=[2,1,0,3,4], neighbor top=[1,0,2,1,0],
left top=[1,0,0,0,0], right top=[0,0,2,1,0],
x=1, y=-2, delta=1, clipped transfers=(0,0).
```

The total top is unchanged and is the correct neighbor top, but the left
component is not toric-admissible in the swapped order: its first positive
bottom demand occurs before any available top.  Thus scalar clipping cannot
be lifted occurrence-by-occurrence.

Allowing changed cut terms does not rescue an *independent* spectator.  Here
is an exact four-vertex counterexample.  In the order
`p<u<v<q=star`, take degrees `(1,2,2,1)`, total top `(1,2,0,0)`, cut
`S={u,v}`, left tableau `[uv]^2`, and right tableau `[pq]`.  At the `u,v`
switch,

```text
x=2,       y=-1,       delta=1.
```

The scalar unit core is `[uv][pq]` and the proposed spectator is the
remaining `[uv]`.  After total BK transfer one, the new order is
`p<v<u<q` and the total top is `(1,1,1,0)`.  The empty cut is its unique
admissible occurrence: the only other degree-compatible candidates are
`{u}`, `{v}`, and `{u,v}`, and in each case the first owned degree-two
vertex has one bottom unit but an empty prefix queue.  Hence both `u` and
`v` must change factors.  The spectator `[uv]` uses both vertices in the
old factor and cannot multiply any such resolved core term while remaining
independent.  If, for example, `u` is a minus vertex, recolouring the
spectator also changes its parity.  This disproves False Target 22F3C0c.

There is an exact replacement for the failed independence condition.  Let
`d^sp` be the spectator multidegree and let `S'` be the cut of a proposed
resolved core term.

**Lemma 22F3B8b (tableau core factorization).**  Let `u<v` be the adjacent
vertices of a positive-defect event.  Of the two cut factors, call the one
whose local form is positive `P` and the other `N`.  Then `P` owns both
`u,v`, while `N` owns neither.  In their two-row standard tableaux:

1. `P` contains exactly `ell_P` columns `[uv]`;
2. `N` contains exactly `-ell_N` columns `[pq]` with `p<u<v<q`.

Choose `delta` columns of each kind and call their product the core.  Deleting
these columns leaves admissible standard tableaux whose two local forms are

```text
(ell_P-delta, ell_N+delta),
```

so the remaining pair has defect zero.

**Proof.**  Let `Q` be the number of unmatched top entries immediately
before `u`.  If a factor owns at most one of `u,v`, the prefix inequalities
give `ell<=0`; hence the positive factor owns both and the other owns
neither.  For `P`, bottom entries at `u` first consume the old queue.  The
excess bottom entries at `v` which cannot use that queue must pair with top
entries at `u`; their number is

```text
(D_u-z_u)+(D_v-z_v)-Q=ell_P,
```

and these are exactly the `[uv]` columns.  For `N`, there are no entries at
`u,v`, so `ell_N=-Q`; its `Q` queued columns start before `u` and end after
`v`.  Removing columns preserves standardness.  A removed `[uv]` column
decreases the first form by one, while a removed gap-crossing column
increases the second by one.  Since
`delta=min(ell_P,-ell_N)`, one residual form is zero and the other has its
old sign, giving defect zero.  QED.

Lemma 22F3B8b is an exact factorization of the bracket monomial, not an
independent occurrence-level lifting: the core and spectator can share
vertices, and resolving the core may change their factor colours.

**Lemma 22F3B8c (spectator support compatibility).**  A defect-zero
spectator with original cut `S` can multiply the resolved core term of cut
`S'` without recolouring if and only if

```text
support(d^sp) intersect (S symmetric_difference S')=empty. (P22.30I7c)
```

Assuming the resolved core tops are admissible and their sum with the
transported spectator is the required target top, this condition is also
sufficient to produce a target-fibre occurrence.

**Proof.**  A vertex in the displayed intersection has positive spectator
degree but different factor colours under `S` and `S'`, proving necessity.
If the intersection is empty, every spectator-supported vertex retains its
factor colour.  Transport the spectator by its defect-zero BK map and
multiply it factorwise by the resolved core term.  Toric-top balance and
prefix inequalities are closed under addition, so both products are
admissible; the assumed sum gives the target top.  QED.

At unit defect the actual colored Plucker block is explicit.  Let
`p<u<v<q`, let colour `P` own `u,v`, and let colour `N` own `p,q`.

**Lemma 22F3B8d (colored unit block).**  In the ordinary bracket gauge put

```text
C=[uv]_P[pq]_N,
A=[pv]_P[uq]_N,
B=[pu]_P[vq]_N.
```

Channel multiplication into the uncoloured invariant ring gives

```text
mu_S(C)-mu_(S_A)(A)+mu_(S_B)(B)=0,                   (P22.30I7d)

S_A=S symmetric_difference {p,u},
S_B=S symmetric_difference {p,v}.
```

The terms `C,A` have total top `{p,u}`, while `B` has the BK-switched total
top `{p,v}`.  A common defect-zero spectator can multiply `A` only if its
support avoids `{p,u}`, can multiply `B` only if its support avoids
`{p,v}`, and can multiply the whole three-term block only if it avoids
`{p,u,v}`.

**Proof.**  Equation `(P22.30I7d)` is the oriented Plucker identity

```text
[uv][pq]=[pv][uq]-[pu][vq].
```

Reading the `P` endpoints gives the two new cuts.  Reading the top endpoint
of each ordered bracket gives the two stated total tops.  The spectator
conditions are Lemma 22F3B8c applied to `S_A` and `S_B`.  QED.

This block is triangular between two BK states, not a map from one death
directly to two target-fibre tickets.  Its channel parities also need not
match its algebraic signs.  If `epsilon_i` is the exchange sign of vertex
`i`, then

```text
sigma_(S_A)=sigma_S epsilon_p epsilon_u,
sigma_(S_B)=sigma_S epsilon_p epsilon_v.              (P22.30I7e)
```

At higher defect, unit blocks do not iterate inside the cut category.

**Lemma 22F3B8e (mixed-colour divided-power obstruction).**  For
`delta>1`, expand the product of the `delta` unit relations associated to
crossing columns `[p_iq_i]`.  Every mixed product containing at least one
`A_i` and one `B_j` assigns positive degree at each of `u,v` to both
colours, so it is not an occurrence for any vertex cut.  Only the two pure
extreme products define cuts.

**Proof.**  In an `A_i` term, `v` belongs to the `P` bracket and `u` to the
`N` bracket.  In a `B_i` term the assignments are reversed.  A mixed
product therefore uses each switched vertex in both factors, whereas a cut
colours the whole degree of every vertex once.  QED.

Thus the core in Target 22F3C0d cannot be obtained by a naive divided power
of `(P22.30I7d)`.  A JW-symmetrized or higher-channel block must repackage
the mixed products, and its rank is an additional theorem.  In the
one-fundamental-negative strand the unit block is the lowest face of the
relative Plucker differential behind Target 22H3; the previously recorded
higher-row-ideal obstructions show that these quadratic blocks alone do not
span the global kernel.

Even unitary recoupling does not turn the unit block into a ticket map.  For
four fundamental strands in `TLJ_k`, `k>=2`, the normalized `F`-matrix on
intermediate labels `0,2` is, up to row and column gauge signs,

```text
       1      [ 1          sqrt([3]) ]
F = -------  [                        ],
      [2]     [ sqrt([3])      -1     ]

[3]=[2]^2-1.
```

Only the label-zero branch is a cut factorization; the label-two branch is
an entangled four-vertex channel.  Re-expanding it through another pairing
recovers the three-term Plucker block.  Hence `F`-unitarity proves local
invertibility in the full channel space, but it leaks outside the direct
sum of cut tickets.  At higher defect the corresponding `q`-Racah block has
all intermediate channels, precisely repackaging the forbidden mixed terms
of Lemma 22F3B8e.  Derived descent back to cut channels remains the missing
step.

The coincident-endpoint divided-power model makes the rank loss exact.

**Lemma 22F3B8f (q-Racah cut projection).**  In `TLJ_k`, replace each of
the four endpoint packets by `V_delta` and put

```text
r=min(delta,k-delta),       J={0,2,...,2r}.
```

Then

```text
H_(delta,k)=Hom(1,V_delta^(tensor 4))
```

has dimension `r+1`.  Its three pairing bases are indexed by `c in J`, and
the recoupling matrix is the unitary `q`-Racah matrix.  From the channel-zero
line,

```text
|F_(0,e)|=sqrt([e+1])/[delta+1],       e in J.         (P22.30I7f)
```

Every admissible intermediate channel therefore occurs.  Projection onto
the two channel-zero lines belonging to the two other vertex-cut pairings
has rank two when `0<delta<k`, and rank one for the trivial/simple-current
cases `delta=0,k`.

**Proof.**  The fusion rule for `V_delta tensor V_delta` gives the basis and
dimension.  The channel-zero row of the normalized `q`-Racah matrix has the
displayed quantum-dimension form; its norm is one because

```text
sum_(e in J)[e+1]=[delta+1]^2.
```

Two normalized channel-zero pairing lines have overlap of modulus
`1/[delta+1]`.  Their Gram determinant is
`1-1/[delta+1]^2`, positive exactly when `0<delta<k`.  QED.

Thus even in the maximally symmetrized four-packet model, cut projection
retains only two directions from an `(r+1)`-dimensional local channel space.
The source cut line itself supplies only one vector.  No quantum-dimension
minor covers the omitted channels.  Moreover an actual FIFO core can have
distinct crossing endpoints `[p_iq_i]`, so it is an iterated fundamental
recoupling problem rather than this single four-`V_delta` block.  Lemma
22F3B8f is a uniform diagnostic, not the missing general resolution.

The ordinary bracket model gives the exact loss for arbitrary repeated
outer endpoints.  For each crossing column put

```text
A_i=[p_i v][u q_i],       B_i=[p_i u][v q_i],
```

and let `G` be the bipartite multigraph with edge `i=(p_i,q_i)`.  For
`S subset E(G)` put

```text
M_S=product_(i in S) A_i product_(i notin S) B_i,
W_G=span{M_S:S subset E(G)}.
```

**Lemma 22F3B8g (exact outer-graph channel rank).**  Two channel products
coincide exactly when their selected-edge degree sequences coincide:

```text
M_S=M_T
 iff deg_S(w)=deg_T(w) for every outer vertex w.       (P22.30I7g)
```

Consequently

```text
dim W_G
 = number of distinct degree sequences of subgraphs S subset E(G).
                                                               (P22.30I7h)
```

For `delta=|E(G)|>0`, coordinate projection in this monomial basis onto the
two pure products `M_emptyset,M_E` has rank two and kernel dimension
`dim(W_G)-2`.  In particular,

```text
G a forest:                    dim W_G=2^delta,
delta parallel edges:          dim W_G=delta+1.        (P22.30I7i)
```

**Proof.**  Orient every bracket consistently and specialize
`u=(1,0),v=(0,1)`.  Writing an outer vector as `w=(x_w,y_w)` gives, up to
fixed orientation signs,

```text
A_i=x_(p_i)y_(q_i),       B_i=y_(p_i)x_(q_i).
```

The exponent vector of the resulting coordinate monomial is the selected
degree at every outer vertex together with its complementary degree.
Equal degree sequences already give literally the same product of bracket
factors before specialization.  Distinct degree sequences specialize to
distinct coordinate monomials, hence are linearly independent.  This proves
`(P22.30I7g)`--`(P22.30I7h)`.  The two extreme degree sequences are distinct
when `delta>0`.  The unsigned incidence map has no nonzero kernel on a
forest, while for parallel edges its value records only `|S|`; this proves
`(P22.30I7i)`.  QED.

Thus the nonsymmetrized loss is usually larger, not smaller, than the
`delta-1` omitted directions in Lemma 22F3B8f.  For a forest it is exactly
`2^delta-2`.  There is also no uniform auxiliary channel `SL_2` action after
outer-endpoint multiplication.  In `K_(2,2)` the two opposite-edge subsets
have the same degree sequence and hence the same `M_S`.  Applying the
labelled-channel raising operation `B -> A` before multiplication produces
the four three-edge subsets; their degree sequences are distinct, so their
images are linearly independent.  The multiplication kernel is therefore
not invariant under that operation.  The physical diagonal `SL_2` cannot
repair this: every bracket is already invariant.

Lemmas 22F3B8e--22F3B8g close the proposed local two-extreme route.  For
`delta>1`, no proof using only the two vertex-cut extremes can recover the
mixed channel space, even before spectator compatibility and global Hall
collisions.  The omitted directions must instead be carried through the
row-ideal resolution and descended globally to available positive tickets.
This is exactly the capacity/derived-contraction content of Target 22H3 and,
in the general strand, Target 22F3C0d; another local divided-power identity
cannot substitute for it.

Thus a local core must absorb every spectator-supported vertex which its
resolution recolours.  This motivates the corrected target.

**Target 22F3C0d (vertex-compatible core resolution).**  Every relevant
odd death admits a multiplicity-preserving Plucker/Temperley--Lieb
resolution whose core is enlarged until every term satisfies
`(P22.30I7c)`, and these resolutions can be paired with equal-defect even
births while preserving ticket injectivity and naturality under directed
commuting squares and braid hexagons.

The deterministic minimal core from Lemma 22F3B8b is usually insufficient.
An exact FIFO-tableau audit reconstructed its columns for all `3,664`
canonical selected deaths and tested every exact occurrence in the immediate
BK target fibre against `(P22.30I7c)`.  Only `1,690` deaths have any
compatible occurrence, and only `134` have a compatible even occurrence
(equivalently, positive ticket capacity).  The exact histograms are

```text
compatible occurrences:       0:1974, 1:1580, 2:110,
compatible even occurrences:  0:3530, 1:134,
delta:                         1:2508, 2:882, 3:274,
minimal core support size:     4:3018, 5:644, 6:2,
spectator support size:        0:32, 2:594, 3:618,
                               4:1442, 5:796, 6:182.   (P22.30I12)
```

Every compatible cut has multiplicity one in this census.  The first death
with no compatible target occurrence has labels `[1,1,2,2,3]`, target `1`,
minus mask `3`, cut mask `25`, `(x,y)=(-1,1)`, and `delta=1`.  Its core
columns are `[2,5]` and `[0,4]`, while its spectator multidegree is
`[0,1,1,2,2,0]`.  Two strict runs were byte-identical: the log SHA-256 is
`5c905579b56a8613989c6092ca5590848072ba237ff4b5aa11e297af5d608748`
and the table SHA-256 is
`2322027996695b26d8dfef44237c6ae9d3d8f29f3aff9d4453c2765eb154dfa7`.

This is a necessary immediate-fibre support audit for one deterministic
minimal core, not a Plucker resolution.  Its failures do not refute Target
22F3C0d; they prove that the core must generally be enlarged or chosen
differently.  The fact that direct compatible even terms occur in only
`134/3,664` selected deaths also confirms that the observed death--birth
bridges cannot usually be replaced by one-edge parity flips.

Allowing arbitrary enlargement restores endpoint compatibility but exposes
why that condition alone is too weak.  A second exact audit enumerated
subsets of all remaining FIFO columns.  It required the residual spectator
to have zero defect, transport admissibly, satisfy `(P22.30I7c)`, and
subtract factorwise from a target occurrence leaving admissible core
degrees and tops.  Every one of the `3,664` deaths then has both some
compatible endpoint and an even compatible endpoint.  The minimum numbers
of additionally absorbed columns are

```text
                    0     1     2     3     4     5
any endpoint:     1666  1076   386   334   106    96
even endpoint:     134   400  1122  1146   446   416. (P22.30I13)
```

For the even optimum, `1,730` cases absorb the entire spectator, and every
optimized even endpoint is the universal empty cut.  Two strict runs were
byte-identical (log SHA-256
`da75fd6ded8a4f6509f0715a47a85b34672408dfecc3371124ab88d8b6f34caf`,
table SHA-256
`f7a3c1ad536ad9dfa177e37bcfdace10b66217d48abc137c82b2b4e5c05e04e5`).

This proves only bounded endpoint factorizations.  Universal even existence
is partly tautological: absorb the full occurrence into the core and use
the empty cut.  It supplies no Plucker coefficient, differential, trace,
or ticket injection.  Therefore endpoint compatibility, even after optimal
enlargement, is not the missing lemma.  The next local object must retain
the actual colored Plucker/TL relation matrix and its rank.

The first minimal-core failure shows this distinction exactly.  In case
`161`, use the ordered vertices `p=0<u=2<v=5<q=4`.  The source core and
remaining columns are

```text
core:       [2,5]_R [0,4]_L,
remainder:  [1,2]_R [4,3]_L^2.
```

Absorb `[1,2]_R` into the core.  The `A` branch of Lemma 22F3B8d is

```text
[0,5][2,4][1,2],
```

and gives an actual target-core factorization at cut `30` with coefficient
`+1`; multiplying the unchanged `[4,3]_L^2` spectator gives a full target
occurrence.  By contrast, the endpoint optimizer selected cut `29`.
After target-order straightening, that target core receives `+1` from the
`A` branch and `-1` from the `B` branch, so its total coefficient is zero.
Thus even an exact support-compatible endpoint may be absent from the
colored relation.

For `delta` labelled unit cores, the unambiguous free algebraic closure is
the tensor row ideal generated by the relation vector
`r=C-A+B` in each tensor position.  Its dimension is

```text
3^delta-2^delta,
```

while `C^delta=product_i(A_i-B_i)` has `2^delta` mixed branches.  Projection
to cut channels becomes meaningful only after specifying the admissible
parity, support, and actual target-core subspaces.  If every legal cut is
allowed, the empty cut makes the endpoint test trivial.  Hence a future
rank audit must fix the positive ticket space dictated by the global
capacity problem and retain every branch coefficient through
straightening; there is no canonical bare ``legal-cut rank'' to test.

Unlike False Target 22F3C0c, the necessary core may grow with the labels;
Lemma 22F3B8a alone therefore gives neither a finite unit-core list nor a
uniform three-gap theorem.  Target 22F3C0d, Target 22F3C0b, and the
collision-free assembly remain separate missing assertions.

Thus Target 22F3C0 would follow from a terminal trace injection: cancel as
many tickets of `U_x` as possible against the disjoint union of `P_y` for
`y in N_(N-1)({x})`, and inject the remainder into the `P_y` for
`y in N_N({x})\N_(N-1)({x})`.  The missing SU(2)-specific assertion is
that these pairings can be chosen compatibly with BK words and that two
cut traces which merge under Plucker or Temperley--Lieb straightening can
be separated into distinct terminal tickets.  Local nested-shell
identities give reachability but not this collision resolution.

This suggests a clean strengthening of the missing component theorem.
Write `N` for the number of enlarged vertices, including the exchange
target.

**Target 22F3C (linear-radius Hall expansion).**  In the source-deficit
sector, every BK component satisfies `(P22.30I)` with `R=N`.

Target 22F3C implies Target 22F3 by matching all negative residual to
positive residual and summing capacities.  It is strictly more informative
than the unmetric component sum, and unlike a chosen maximum flow it is a
canonical family of inequalities.

The strict `gt-bk-radius` mode verifies Target 22F3C in all `1,001`
support-disjoint cases with at most five factors, labels and targets at most
four, and total degree at most fourteen.  The last failed cuts in the three
displayed benchmark components are very small:

```text
case                         R       A states   d(A)   c(N_R(A))
[1^-,1^-,2^+,3^+],1         3          8         8        0
[1^-,1^-,2^+,2^+,3^+],1     4         16        48       24
[2^+,2^+,3^-,4^+,5^+],2     5          4         4        0.   (P22.30J)
```

In the middle case all sixteen demand states have `(o,e)=(4,0)`.  Enlarging
the radius from four to five expands their positive neighborhood capacity
from `24` to `184`; in the other two cases the final shell likewise removes
the exact Hall obstruction.  Thus the observed radius is not caused by a
diffuse global shortage: it is the length needed to escape a small,
zero-positive odd cluster.  A proof of Target 22F3C should establish
boundary expansion for such clusters, presumably by the short adjacent
Bender--Knuth braid moves visible in the successful paths.

The escape paths admit a further directed restriction.  Define the local
deficit

```text
q(x)=max(0,-rho(x)),                                  (P22.30M)
```

and orient a BK edge `x--y` from `x` to `y` whenever `q(y)<=q(x)`.  Let
`N_R^down(A)` be the positive-residual states reachable from `A` by at most
`R` oriented edges.

**Target 22F3D (deficit-gradient Hall expansion).**  In the source-deficit
sector, every BK component satisfies

```text
sum_(x in A)q(x)
 <=sum_(y in N_N^down(A))rho(y)
for every A subseteq D_C.                            (P22.30N)
```

This implies Target 22F3C, hence Target 22F3.  Unlike unrestricted metric
transport, it says that an alternating path never has to enter a state with
a worse local deficit.  The strict `gt-bk-radius-deficit` mode passes all
`1,001` support-disjoint cases with at most five factors, labels and targets
at most four, and total degree at most fourteen.

The bounded data supports a sharper orientation.  While `q(x)>0`, allow
`x->y` only when

```text
(q(y),o(y)) <=lex (q(x),o(x));                        (P22.30N1)
```

once `q=0`, allow every edge which stays in `q=0`.  Thus a path may move
inside a fixed positive-deficit plateau without increasing `o`, must
eventually descend in `q`, and may redistribute freely only after reaching
the zero-deficit reservoir.

**Target 22F3E (two-stage gradient Hall expansion).**  Target 22F3D remains
true when `N_N^down(A)` is replaced by reachability in the sharper
orientation `(P22.30N1)`.

The strict `gt-bk-radius-gradient` mode passes the same complete
`1,001`-case domain.  It identifies a possible negative descent phase
controlled by `(q,o)` and a separate zero-deficit redistribution phase.
The following initially plausible constant-radius strengthening is false.
Call a neighbor lower when its `(q,o)` pair is lexicographically smaller.

**False Target 22F3F (two-move plateau escape).**  Every state `x` with `q(x)>0`
either has a lower BK neighbor, or has a neighbor `x'` with

```text
(q(x'),o(x'))=(q(x),o(x))
```

which itself has a lower neighbor obtained by the *adjacent* swap generator:
if `x'=t_i x`, the lower state may be chosen as

```text
t_(i-1)t_i x   or   t_(i+1)t_i x.                    (P22.30N3)
```

This assertion does hold throughout the old `1,001`-case domain.  In the
four hard components displayed above and below, there are respectively

```text
2, 64, 28, 14
```

positive-deficit plateaux; none is closed and the maximum internal distance
to a lower boundary vertex is one.  Among states without a direct lower
neighbor, the exact two-step counts are `4,32,36,36`; all use adjacent
generator pairs and none uses commuting generators.  These bounds do not
persist after adding one vertex.

The two-step states are confined to the first few excess types in these
benchmarks:

```text
case 1:       (q,o)=(1,2) x4,
case 2:       (q,o)=(1,2) x16,  (1,3) x16,
case 3:       (q,o)=(1,2) x36,
case (P22.30O):
              (q,o)=(1,2) x28,  (2,3) x8.            (P22.30N4)
```

The first exact counterexample to Target 22F3F has seven vertices.  In
ordered queue notation it is

```text
alpha=[1,2,2,1,0,0,0],
beta =[0,1,0,0,1,2,2],
sign =[+,+,0,+,+,-,-],                               (P22.30N5)
```

where `0` is the forced-outside target.  Thus

```text
d=[1,3,2,1,1,2,2],       delta=-2,       f=6,
(q,o,e)=(2,4,1).
```

Direct subset enumeration and the transfer product `(P22.30R1)` agree.
No neighbor is lower.  The neighbor contractions `(delta,f)`, in generator
order, are

```text
(-2,6), (-3,9), (-2,6), (-3,5), (-2,6), (-2,6).
```

The shortest descent staying on the plateau before its final edge is the
zero-based braid word `[2,3,2]`: two plateau edges are required before the
exit.  This example has two minus factors and support-disjoint signs, but its
global source totals are

```text
O=30,                 P=31.
```

It therefore refutes only the unrestricted every-state wording, not the
source-deficit version actually needed for Target 22F3.

The first counterexample in the source-deficit sector has eight vertices:

```text
alpha=[2,1,1,0,2,0,1,0],
beta =[0,0,0,2,1,1,1,2],
sign =[+,-,-,+,+,0,+,+],                             (P22.30N6)
d    =[2,1,1,2,3,1,2,2].
```

Here the target is at position five,

```text
delta=-1,       f=7,       (q,o,e)=(1,4,2),
empty=42,       O=144,     P=129.
```

Thus the signed source integer is `42+129-144=27>0`, but the Plucker
relation sector is genuinely source-deficient.  The adjacent contractions
are

```text
(-1,7), (-1,7), (-2,6), (-1,11),
(-1,7), (-5,11), (-1,7).
```

No one- or two-move descent exists.  The shortest plateau descent is the
zero-based monotone word `[0,1,2]`.  Therefore the two-move Target 22F3F is
false even under all relevant parity, support, and source-deficit
hypotheses, and no comparison confined to three consecutive jobs can prove
the gradient target.

The viable replacement retains the linear radius already present in Target
22F3C.

**Target 22F3G (source-deficit linear plateau escape).**  Suppose the number
of minus factors is even and the global case satisfies `O>P`.  For every
state `x` with `q(x)>0`, there is a word of at most `N` adjacent BK
generators such that every proper-prefix state has the same `(q,o)` as `x`
and the final state is lower.

The standalone strict C++ checker
`character_ring_iter/search_su2_bk_local_escape.cpp` enumerates valid queue
paths directly, assigns signs by degree so that the supports remain
disjoint, checks every target position, and computes `O,P` independently
from the ordinary Clebsch--Gordan recursion before classifying a long
escape.  The following complete domains pass Target 22F3G:

```text
(N,max part)     signed states   negative states   source-deficit >2   source max
(6,2)                 60,060            1,432                0          2
(7,2)                529,593           19,918                0          2
(8,2)              4,520,948          217,916               16          3
(9,2)             37,763,456        2,349,240              172          3
(6,3)              1,485,028           19,184                0          2
(7,3)             27,021,430          629,370               16          3.
```

In particular, all `172` nine-vertex source-deficit states which evade the
two-move rule still reach a lower state within three moves; no
source-deficit plateau is closed.  A larger-height sweep shows that the
constant three-move strengthening is nevertheless false.

**False Target 22F3H (source-deficit three-move escape).**  Under the hypotheses of
Target 22F3G, the escaping word may always be chosen to have length at most
three.

The first counterexample occurs at eight vertices with queue parts at most
three:

```text
alpha=[2,3,3,2,0,0,0,0],
beta =[0,1,0,0,1,2,3,3],
sign =[+,+,0,+,+,+,-,-],                             (P22.30N7)
d    =[2,4,3,2,1,2,3,3].
```

The target is at position two and

```text
delta=-1,       f=7,       (q,o,e)=(1,4,2),
empty=129,      O=200,     P=183.
```

All adjacent contractions are `(-1,7)` except generator one, which gives
`(-2,10)`.  The plateau has no exit within three moves; its shortest descent
is the zero-based word `[2,5,4,3]`.  Direct subset enumeration independently
gives the same `(delta,f)`.

A source-filtered deterministic 24-shard sweep of all `6,204,388` valid
eight-vertex queue paths with parts at most three tested `36,289,940` signed
states and `2,161,376` negative states.  Exactly `840` negative states
needed more than two moves, none had a closed plateau, and the maximum
escape length was four.  Thus Target 22F3G survives while every constant
bound currently suggested by the smaller domains has failed.  The proof
roadmap therefore retains the honest linear `N` radius.

The successful words have a stronger Coxeter-support property which explains
that radius without imposing a constant depth.

**Target 22F3I (almost-square-free escape word).**  Under the hypotheses of
Target 22F3G, the escape word can be chosen so that every adjacent generator
occurs at most once, except that one generator may occur twice.

Target 22F3I implies Target 22F3G immediately: there are only `N-1`
adjacent generators, so such a word has length at most `N`.  Every
source-deficit shortest word in the complete domains above has this
property.  It includes the braid word `[i,i+1,i]`, monotone runs such as
`[0,1,2]`, separated activations such as `[2,5]`, and the first four-step
word `[2,5,4,3]`.  The C++ checker records any violation separately as
`source_forest_word_failures`.  A further source-filtered, dynamically
sharded sweep with seven vertices and queue parts at most four covered

```text
4,981,977 queue paths,       61,209,516 signed O>P states,
1,347,306 negative states,  274 states needing more than two moves.
```

Its maximum escape length was four and
`source_forest_word_failures=0`.  This is now the concrete descent
subtarget: prove that a repeated generator can be deleted or that its first
repetition already exposes a lower boundary, before addressing merging
multiplicities.

The exact words satisfy a stronger normal form.

**Target 22F3J (square-free plateau prefix).**  Under the hypotheses of
Target 22F3G, an escape word

```text
[i_1,...,i_r,j]
```

can be chosen so that the proper-prefix generators `i_1,...,i_r` are
pairwise distinct.  The exit generator `j` may be new or may repeat one of
them.

Target 22F3J implies Target 22F3I and gives the same sharp bound
`r+1<=N`.  It is a cleaner fibre statement in the language of Lemma
22F3A7: before crossing an oriented boundary shell, one visits at most one
edge direction of the fixed feasible-cut-family fibre.

The strict C++ checker now records violations separately as
`source_prefix_forest_word_failures`.  Complete source-filtered replays give

```text
(N,max part)   negative states   prefix failures   maximum escape
(8,3)               2,161,376                 0                4
(7,4)               1,347,306                 0                4.
```

The first run used twenty-nine shards and the second twenty-four, chosen
from the live CPU headroom rather than fixed in the source.  Thus every
previously tested source-deficit escape also satisfies Target 22F3J.
Unlike a Coxeter reduction, this statement cannot be obtained by imposing
braid relations: exact plateau braid faces already have distinct endpoints.
The proof must instead use the nested-family fibre and the signed boundary
shell criterion `(P22.30R11)`.

The positive-transfer part is even more localized.

**Target 22F3M (single-transfer scheduling escape).**  In Target 22F3J,
the square-free proper prefix can be chosen so that at most one of its BK
moves has `z>0`.

All remaining proper-prefix moves have `z=0`, so Lemma 22F3A15 orients them
by one fixed scheduling preorder on the unchanged jobs.  Target 22F3M
therefore separates the proof into a weak-order navigation and at most one
genuine change of job data.

The checker records states and steps with `z>0`, and states with more than
one such step, separately.  The complete hard source-deficit domains give

```text
(N,max part)   states with z>0   z>0 steps   multiple-z states
(9,2)                        20          20                   0
(8,3)                        48          48                   0
(7,4)                         0           0                   0.
```

Thus every tested difficult escape satisfies Target 22F3M.  The equality
of the state and step counts in the first two rows means that each affected
word has exactly one positive-transfer prefix move.

Lemma 22F3A10 initially suggested a further preliminary target.

**False Target 22F3K (composite-path two-step escape).**  Under the hypotheses of
Target 22F3G, if the block-Dyck word has an internal return and `q(x)>0`,
then a descent exists after at most one plateau move.

This would make every state requiring the genuine square-free-prefix
argument a primitive block-Dyck excursion.  The C++ checker records
nonprimitive source-deficit states requiring more than two moves as
`source_nonprimitive_beyond_two_steps`.  Complete replays give

```text
(N,max part)   source deficit >2   nonprimitive >2
(9,2)                          94                 0
(8,3)                         840                 0
(7,4)                         274                 0.
```

Thus all `1,208` difficult states in these domains are primitive.  This
bounded pattern does not persist.  Append the rigid two-job excursion
`(alpha,beta)=(3,0),(0,3)`, with both signs negative, to the primitive
four-step state `(P22.30N7)`.  The resulting exact state is

```text
alpha=[2,3,3,2,0,0,0,0,3,0],
beta =[0,1,0,0,1,2,3,3,0,3],
sign =[+,+,0,+,+,+,-,-,-,-],                        (P22.30R30)
```

with target position two and

```text
delta=-2,       f=14,       O=6864>P=6778,
(a,A;b,B)=(-1,7;+1,1).
```

Direct subset enumeration and the signed transfer product agree.  There is
no descent in one or two moves.  The shortest plateau escape is

```text
[2,5,4,3],
```

with successive potentials

```text
(-2,14), (-2,14), (-2,14), (2,10).                  (P22.30R31)
```

All three proper-prefix moves have `z=0`.  Hence `(P22.30R30)` satisfies
every parity, support-disjointness, and source-deficit hypothesis and
refutes Target 22F3K.  Composite states cannot be discarded before the
square-free scheduling argument: Targets 22F3J--22F3M must cover primitive
and composite paths uniformly.

The return-shell formula does not eliminate the internal branch.

**False Target 22F3K1 (return-shell-only two-step escape).**  Every
nonprimitive source-deficit negative state has a direct descent across an
excursion return, or one plateau move exposes such a return-shell descent.

In the complete source-filtered `(9,2)` domain there are `4,238`
nonprimitive negative states.  Only `310` descend directly across a return
shell and `580` do so after one plateau move; the remaining `3,348` fail
Target 22F3K1 even though every one has a generic descent within two moves.
Those descents are supported inside an excursion.  Therefore a proof of
any composite descent theorem would have to establish the dichotomy

```text
internal excursion descent, scaled by (P22.30R14),
or
return-shell descent, measured by (P22.30R17).         (P22.30R20)
```

Neither branch alone is sufficient.

Lemma 22F3A16 nevertheless separates composite deficits into two rigid
strata and explains why the bounded data looked simpler.

**False Target 22F3K2 (quantized composite escape).**

1. Every even-sign composite state with `delta=-2`, without assuming
   `O>P`, has a direct descent or one plateau move followed by a descent.
2. Under the source-deficit hypothesis `O>P`, if `delta<=-4`, there is a
   direct descent.

These two assertions would imply Target 22F3K because a nonzero composite
deficit is an even integer.  The complete source-filtered `(9,2)` replay supports the
stronger split exactly.  Among its `4,238` composite negative states,
`4,148` descend directly and `90` need one plateau move.  The potential
histogram of precisely those `90` delayed states is

```text
(delta,f)=(-2,6)  x66,
          (-2,14) x20,
          (-2,22) x4.                                (P22.30R26)
```

In particular no state with `delta<=-4` needs the plateau move.  A separate
forced-composite randomized C++ stress test beyond the exhaustive box
sampled `273` source-deficit negative states with ten vertices and queue
parts at most six.  Of these, `269` descended directly and `4` after one
plateau move; all four delayed states again had `delta=-2`.  This random
test is only falsification evidence.  A second forced-composite test dropped
the source filter and retained only the quantized `delta=-2` stratum.  With
ten vertices, queue parts at most eight, and `50,000` generated paths, it
tested `2,298` negative states: `2,252` descended directly and `46` after
one plateau move, with no failure.  The exact state `(P22.30R30)` shows
that this evidence was misleading: it has `delta=-2` and `O>P` but needs
four moves, refuting assertion 1 even after restoring the source filter.
Assertion 2 remains a possible large-deficit statement, but by itself no
longer yields a composite reduction.

Lemma 22F3A17 suggested two still sharper assertions.

**False Target 22F3K3 (unit-excursion rigidity dichotomy).**  In the notation of
Lemma 22F3A17:

1. if `A>1` and `B>1`, the concatenated state has a direct descent;
2. if `min(A,B)=1`, it has a direct descent or one plateau move followed
   by a descent.

The complete source-filtered `(9,2)` replay classifies all `90` states
without a direct descent as

```text
(a,A;b,B)=(-1,3;+1,1)   x66,
          (-1,7;+1,1)   x20,
          (-1,11;+1,1)  x4.                          (P22.30R28)
```

Thus every delayed state has exactly the rigid positive factor predicted
by the second branch.  In the unconditional ten-vertex random replay, the
`46` delayed states have factor histogram

```text
(-1,3;+1,1) x37,       (-1,7;+1,1) x1,
(-1,9;+1,1) x1,        (+1,1;-1,3) x7.               (P22.30R29)
```

This includes both possible locations of the rigid factor and no
nonrigid-pair obstruction.  Nevertheless `(P22.30R30)` has
`(a,A;b,B)=(-1,7;+1,1)` and requires four moves, so it directly refutes
branch 2.  Branch 1 remains unrefuted but cannot eliminate the rigid
composite states that actually inherit the long primitive escape.

One more tempting simplification is false.  The proper plateau prefix need
not keep the raw top allocation fixed: the checker finds `20` hard states
with a nonzero BK transfer `z` in the `(9,2)` domain and `48` in the `(8,3)`
domain.  Hence a general proof must allow the full block replacement
`(P22.30R12)`, not merely permutations of fixed blocks.

The source filter still excludes additional obstructions.  The first
unrestricted nonprimitive state requiring more than two moves is

```text
alpha=[1,0,1,2,2,1,0,0,0],
beta =[0,1,0,1,0,0,1,2,2],
sign =[+,+,+,+,+,+,+,-,-],                           (P22.30N9)
```

with target position four.  Its Dyck path returns to zero after the first
two jobs, and

```text
delta=-4,       f=12,       empty=59,
O=400,          P=464.
```

No one- or two-move descent exists; the shortest plateau escape is
`[4,5,4]`.  Thus excursion factorization by itself does not give a
two-step theorem.  This state is excluded exactly because `O<P`.  In the
complete unrestricted `(9,2)` domain there are twelve nonprimitive states
requiring more than two moves, whereas the source-filtered count in that
bounded domain remains zero.  State `(P22.30R30)` shows that the filter
does not rescue the two-step theorem at larger bounds.

The global `O>P` hypothesis is essential.  The even-minus state

```text
alpha=[1,1,1,1,1,2,0,0,0],
beta =[0,0,0,0,0,1,2,2,2],
sign =[0,+,+,+,+,-,-,-,-],                          (P22.30N8)
```

has `delta=-14`, `f=46`, and a closed plateau, but its global totals are
`O=344<P=520`.  Such a case already has enough positive capacity before any
Plucker relations are imposed and is deliberately excluded from Target
22F3.  Odd-minus closed plateaux also occur, consistently with the
target-removal cancellation in Lemma 22F3A3.

Target 22F3G supplies reachability but not the weighted Hall capacity in
`(P22.30N)`.  The remaining global task is still to control collisions when
descent paths merge and then redistribute capacity inside `q=0`.

A scalar maximum principle does not explain the escape.  If

```text
(Delta q)(x)=(N-1)q(x)-sum_(y adjacent x)q(y),
```

then the middle and all-degree-at-least-two benchmarks contain respectively
`144` and `56` negative states with `Delta q<0`, and the minimum is `-2`.
Replacing `q` by the signed count `delta` does not repair the maximum
principle: the corresponding subharmonic inequality fails at respectively
`128` and `48` negative states, again with minimum Laplacian `-2`.
Nor does the one-parameter correction

```text
V_lambda(x)=q(x)(1+lambda o(x))
```

make every negative state superharmonic.  In the middle benchmark the
necessary constraints require simultaneously

```text
lambda>=-3/16,              lambda<=-1/3,             (P22.30N2)
```

and in the all-degree-at-least-two benchmark they require
`lambda>=-3/16` and `lambda<=-1/5`.  Hence a proof of the surviving Target
22F3G must use the directed plateau graph itself, not an averaging argument
based on one scalar linear Lyapunov correction.

The apparently stronger rule obtained by requiring `o(y)<=o(x)` is false.
Its first exact failure in the same bounded sweep is

```text
remainder=[1^+,2^+,3^+,3^+,4^-],       target=1.      (P22.30O)
```

Here `N=6`, the total deficit is `640`, unrestricted radius-six transport
nonincreasing-deficit transport, and the two-stage gradient transport all
saturate `640`, but transport along globally nonincreasing-`o` paths reaches
only `628`.  The unrestricted radius flows are

```text
12,64,320,528,632,640
```

at radii one through six.  Thus the correct gradient is the deficit `q`,
not the raw number of odd cuts: a valid path may temporarily increase `o`
provided that enough even capacity appears to keep `q` from increasing.

The metric does not rescue a pairwise XOR differential.  Give an odd-pair
edge in a state access to every positive object in its own fibre and to every
positive object in its symmetric-difference channel at a state of BK
distance at most `N`.  Choose a minimum-radius spanning forest in every odd
fibre and then solve the exact transversal matching problem for the chosen
edges.  In the middle benchmark this matches `2,192` of the `2,256`
relations.  The remaining `64` fibres all have

```text
(o,e)=(2,0),                                             (P22.30K)
```

and their two odd cuts have no usable XOR edge.  There are `384` odd-pair
occurrences in this component whose XOR channel is absent globally, so no
larger BK radius can repair them.  The deficit `64` is exactly four times
the signed-class quotient deficit `16` behind `(P22.30C)`.  In the
all-degree-at-least-two benchmark the analogous forest matches only
`1,384/1,704`; all `320` disconnected fibres again have `(o,e)=(2,0)`,
and all `448` missing odd-pair channels are globally absent.  Hence the
obstruction is not failure to move a pairwise resolution far enough: it is
a genuine higher syzygy.

Shortest escapes from the last Hall witnesses show how that syzygy must
propagate.  Writing a path as its sequence of `(o,e,rho)` triples, the
deterministic nearest-positive paths include

```text
(2,0,-1),(2,0,-1),(1,0,0),(1,0,0),(1,1,1),

(4,0,-3),(4,0,-3),(2,0,-1),(2,0,-1),(2,2,1),

(2,0,-1),(2,0,-1),(2,0,-1),(2,0,-1),
          (1,0,0),(1,0,0),(1,1,1).                    (P22.30L)
```

Thus the successful mechanism does not send an isolated odd relation
directly to an even cut.  It transports the relation through other
zero-positive odd fibres, reduces the odd fibre rank along the way, and only
then reaches positive surplus.  The next differential target should
therefore be an alternating relation--state complex whose internal arrows
are adjacent BK switches and whose terminal arrows are local positive
objects.  A construction confined to one pair of cuts, even with arbitrary
transport radius, is now ruled out exactly.

There is a useful exact matroid formulation of what a pairwise proof would
have to select.  For every state `x` with odd-cut set `O(x)`, form the
complete graph on `O(x)`.  A spanning tree has `o(x)-1` edges, exactly the
relation contribution of that fibre in `(P22.23)`.  Take the direct sum of
these graphic matroids over all states and orders.  On the other side, make
one resource for every nonempty even object `(x,S)`.  Any specified rule
which declares which resources may pay an edge gives a transversal matroid
on the same edge set.

**Lemma 22F4 (graphic--transversal certificate).**  If the graphic and
transversal matroids have a common independent set of size

```text
R_rel=sum_(prec,x)(o(x)-indicator_(o(x)>0)),          (P22.30A)
```

then Target 22F2 holds.

**Proof.**  The rank of the graphic summand at `x` is `o(x)-1` when
`o(x)>0` and zero otherwise.  Equality with the sum of all these ranks
forces the selected edges to contain a spanning tree in every nontrivial
odd fibre.  Transversal independence matches the selected edges to distinct
positive resources.  Hence `R_rel` is at most the total number of positive
resources, which is the right side of `(P22.24)`.  QED.

This formulation sharply separates the missing issue from the already false
all-pairs bound.  Unfortunately, even a generous pair-local resource rule is
still too narrow.  Let an edge `{A,B}` use either any positive object in its
own fibre or any positive channel which is a parity-even union of the four
Boolean atoms

```text
A intersect B,  A\B,  B\A,  and  (A union B)^c.       (P22.30B)
```

The strict augmenting-path matroid-intersection enumerator finds the first
support-disjoint failure at

```text
remainder=[1^-,1^-,2^+,2^+,3^+],       target=1.      (P22.30C)
```

On the signed-class quotient there are `180` orders.  The odd source has
dimension eight per order, the rank sum is `876`, and therefore the exact
relation demand is `180*8-876=564`.  The positive supply is `180*5=900`, so
Target 22F2 has slack `336`.  Nevertheless XOR-only spanning trees have
maximum common rank `484`, and allowing both local objects and every
parity-even Boolean resolution in `(P22.30B)` raises it only to `548<564`.
Even replacing the two-cut Boolean algebra by the algebra of membership
atoms generated by the *entire odd fibre* leaves the same maximum `548`.
The full physical BK graph for this case is connected and has `2,256`
relations, `3,600` positive objects, and slack `1,344`, exactly four times
the quotient counts.  Thus the surviving component inequality cannot be
proved by a differential whose destination depends only on one colliding
pair of cuts and its Boolean regions; merely seeing all Boolean membership
atoms of the fibre is still insufficient.  It must use a non-Boolean
higher syzygy or genuine transport through other BK states.

The component picture itself is nontrivial.  For degree vector `[2,2,2,2]`,
`K(d)` has components of sizes `48` and `24`; only the first contains a
nontrivial admissible cut.  For `[2,2,3,3]` the same sizes occur, showing that
the gcd of the row multiplicities is not a complete component invariant.
For the first all-degree-at-least-two source deficit,

```text
remainder=[2^+,2^+,3^-,4^+,5^+],       target=2,
```

the two component sizes are `15,120` and `720`, and again only the first is
loaded.  The strict `gt-bk-single-loaded` sweep passed all `1,868`
support-disjoint source-deficit cases with at most six factors, labels and
targets at most four, and total degree at most sixteen.  This property is
special to the relevant signed sector: the unsigned degree vector
`[2,2,2,2,2,2]` already has two different components containing admissible
cuts.  Hence a proof may use the source-deficit and support-disjoint sign
hypotheses, but cannot assume a universal unique decomposable orbit.

The fibres also admit a matching-theoretic model which exposes where a
non-Boolean syzygy could live.  For a state `(prec,alpha)`, make
`alpha_v` top tokens and `beta_v=d_v-alpha_v` bottom tokens at every vertex.
Join a top token at `u` to a bottom token at `v` precisely when `u` precedes
`v` in `prec`.  This is a Ferrers bipartite graph `H_(prec,alpha)`.

**Lemma 22F5 (Ferrers separation form).**  A cut `T` is admissible for
`(prec,alpha)` if and only if both vertex-induced bipartite graphs

```text
H[T]  and  H[T^c]
```

have perfect matchings, where the target belongs to `T^c`.

**Proof.**  List the top and bottom tokens of one induced graph in
nondecreasing vertex order.  Because its adjacency matrix is Ferrers, it has
a perfect matching if and only if the `j`-th top token precedes the `j`-th
bottom token for every `j`; when this holds, pairing equal ranks is already
a perfect matching.  Equality of the two token counts is the terminal queue
condition, and the rank-by-rank inequalities are exactly the prefix queue
conditions in `(P22.16)`.  Apply this separately to `T` and `T^c`.  QED.

Thus Target 22F3 is an averaged signed uncrossing theorem for balanced
vertex separations of Ferrers graphs which remain perfectly matchable on
both sides.  The failure of the whole-fibre Boolean rule above says that the
standard lattice of membership atoms alone does not prove the required
uncrossing; a successful differential must also use alternating matching
paths, or equivalently the adjacent state transport `(P22.18)`.

The queue formulation has an exact finite-level analogue.  For an ordered
list `d_1,...,d_m` and a level-`k` fusion path

```text
h_0=0,h_1,...,h_m=0,
```

put

```text
alpha_i=(d_i+h_i-h_(i-1))/2,       beta_i=d_i-alpha_i. (P22.31)
```

**Lemma 22G (two-sided level queue).**  Level-`k` invariant fusion paths are
in bijection with integer vectors in `(P22.31)` satisfying, at every step,

```text
0<=alpha_i,beta_i<=d_i,
beta_i<=h_(i-1),                 alpha_i<=k-h_(i-1),
h_i=h_(i-1)-beta_i+alpha_i.                       (P22.32)
```

Thus `beta_i` consumes the lower queue while `alpha_i` consumes the upper
hole queue.  Omitting the upper inequality recovers the ordinary queue in
Lemma 22F.

**Proof.**  The ordinary Clebsch--Gordan conditions for
`V_(h_(i-1)) tensor V_(d_i)` are equivalent to the integrality in `(P22.31)`,
`0<=alpha_i,beta_i<=d_i`, and `beta_i<=h_(i-1)`.  The extra affine-wall
condition is

```text
h_(i-1)+d_i+h_i<=2k.
```

Substituting `h_i=h_(i-1)+d_i-2beta_i` turns it into
`h_(i-1)+alpha_i<=k`, which is the upper inequality in `(P22.32)`.
Every transformation is reversible.  QED.

Lemma 22G identifies the uniform finite target: construct the required
odd-to-positive matching on pairs of two-sided queue paths using moves that
preserve both inequalities in `(P22.32)`.  Such a matching would prove
`(PCP_k)` simultaneously for every `k`; dropping the upper queue would give
the ordinary matching studied in Targets 22C--22F.

Thus the remaining proof cannot be an order-independent polymatroid
exchange, an order-independent pairwise XOR injection, or a fibrewise
parity theorem.  The surviving statement is the adaptive cumulative Hall
matching of Target 22C2, or the weaker rank-plateau statement Target 22F1.

Two still stronger fixed-degeneration shortcuts are also false.  In the squarefree
Stanley--Reisner degeneration of the Plucker ring, crossing products vanish.
For remainder labels `[1,1,1,1]`, target `2`, and minus mask `3`, the odd
source dimension is four, its degenerate rank is one, and the nonempty even
supply is two.  Thus the special-fibre kernel has dimension three and is too
large by one.  In the domain Gelfand--Tsetlin degeneration a fixed vertex
order can also lose too much rank: for support-disjoint labels
`[1,1,2,2,3]`, target `1`, and minus mask `3`, the odd source dimension,
degenerate rank, and nonempty even supply are respectively `8,2,5`.
Reordering the six vertices as `[0,2,4,1,3,5]` raises the rank to six and
makes every tableau fibre satisfy `(P22.9)`.  Hence neither standard fixed
degeneration proves even the aggregate estimate automatically.

The strict exact C++ diagnostic
`character_ring_iter/analyze_su2_plucker_sr_parity.cpp` constructs both
degenerations and the tableau fibres.  Its order-adapted support-disjoint
fibre-order mode passed all 505 cases with at most six factors, remainder labels at most
three, targets at most three, and total degree at most fourteen.  A wider
replay passed all 2,422 support-disjoint cases with at most seven factors,
labels and targets at most four, and total degree at most sixteen.  Proposition
22B first occurs beyond that replay's total-degree cutoff.  Outside the
support-disjoint sector the fibrewise claim itself can fail for every vertex
order: six label-two factors, target two, and three minus copies have a fibre
with six odd and four even cuts.  Proposition 5 removes precisely this type
of mixed-sign equal-label case before Target 22B is invoked.
The earlier bounded replay is recorded in
`certificates/su2_plucker_gt_sd_best_4_7_4_16.log`.

The divided-difference factorization used earlier for the all-minus search
has a useful categorical and finite-level form.  In the ordinary
representation ring, or in `R_k` with `q<=k`, put

```text
Delta=e_1 tensor e_0-e_0 tensor e_1,
S_q=e_q tensor e_0+e_0 tensor e_q,
D_q=sum_(j=0)^(q-1) e_(q-1-j) tensor e_j.               (P23.1)
```

**Proposition 23 (fundamental-difference normal form).**  One has

```text
e_q tensor e_0-e_0 tensor e_q=Delta D_q.                (P23.2)
```

Consequently every signed remainder with `m` minus factors has the exact
form

```text
R=Delta^m H,
H=product_(i minus)D_(q_i) product_(i plus)S_(q_i).      (P23.3)
```

Here `H` is exchange-symmetric and belongs to the nonnegative tensor-product
basis cone.  Thus `(PCP)` and `(PCP_k)` reduce to the single rank-one target

```text
(id tensor tau)(Delta^m H) belongs to the nonnegative cone              (FDC)
```

for `H` in the multiplicative cone generated by the `D_q` and `S_q`.

**Proof.**  Write `e_q=U_q(e_1/2)`.  The divided-difference identity

```text
[U_q(X/2)-U_q(Y/2)]/(X-Y)
   =sum_(j=0)^(q-1)U_(q-1-j)(X/2)U_j(Y/2)               (P23.4)
```

follows by telescoping the recurrence
`XU_j(X/2)=U_(j+1)(X/2)+U_(j-1)(X/2)`.  Substituting
`X=e_1 tensor e_0` and `Y=e_0 tensor e_1` proves `(P23.2)`.
All indices in `D_q` are at most `q-1`; hence the same telescoping uses no
affine wall when `q<=k`, and the identity remains exact in every `R_k`.
Multiplying `(P23.2)` over the minus factors gives `(P23.3)`.  Every `D_q`
and `S_q` is symmetric and coefficientwise nonnegative, proving the final
assertions.  QED.

After the principal specialization used in Proposition 18, `(P23.2)` reads

```text
P_q(t)-t^(q/2)chi_q(y)
 =[1+t-t^(1/2)chi_1(y)]
   sum_(j=0)^(q-1)t^(j/2)P_(q-1-j)(t)chi_j(y).           (P23.5)
```

There is an exact supermodule behind both factors in `(P23.5)`.  Put
`a=t^(1/2)`, let `V=V_1`, and grade

```text
E=exterior^bullet(aV)=1 direct-sum aV direct-sum a^2 1.  (P23.6)
```

Here the displayed expression is the ordinary graded character; its
supercharacter is

```text
B(a,y)=1-a chi_1(y)+a^2.                                (P23.7)
```

**Lemma 23A (exterior--symmetric-power form).**  For every `q>=1`,

```text
Q_q(a,y)=sum_(j=0)^(q-1) a^j P_(q-1-j)(a^2) chi_j(y)
        =character Sym^(q-1)(E),                         (P23.8)

P_q(a^2)-a^q chi_q(y)=B(a,y)Q_q(a,y).                   (P23.9)
```

Consequently the outer polynomial `A_R` is the invariant super-Hilbert
polynomial of the genuine graded supermodule

```text
K_R=
 tensor_(i minus)[exterior^bullet(aV) tensor Sym^(q_i-1)(E)]
 tensor_(i plus)[(direct-sum_(j=0)^q_i a^(2j)1)
                  direct-sum a^(q_i)V_(q_i)],           (P23.10)
```

where parity occurs only in the first exterior factor of each minus
position.

**Proof.**  If `z,z^(-1)` are the weights of `V`, then

```text
sum_(n>=0) character Sym^n(E) u^n
 =1/[(1-u)(1-a^2u)(1-azu)(1-az^(-1)u)].                 (P23.11)
```

The first two denominator factors have coefficient `P_l(a^2)` in degree
`l`, and the last two have coefficient `a^j chi_j(y)` in degree `j`.
Convolution gives `(P23.8)`, while `(P23.9)` is `(P23.5)`.  Multiplying the
supercharacters of the factors in `(P23.10)` and extracting `SU(2)`
invariants gives exactly `(P18.3)`.  QED.

The minus-factor supermodule has a natural Koszul differential, and its
homology is completely explicit.  Write

```text
E=kx direct-sum V direct-sum kz
```

with `a`-degrees `0,1,2`.  For `v in V`, let `A_v(x)=v` and let `A_v`
vanish on `V direct-sum kz`.  The operators `A_v` commute, and they induce
commuting derivations `D_v` on `Sym^n(E)`.  Use the invariant symplectic
form to identify `V` with its dual.

**Lemma 23A1 (factorwise Koszul resolution).**  For `n=q-1`, the operator

```text
d_q=sum_i iota_(v_i) tensor D_(v^i)
```

is an odd `SU(2)`-equivariant differential on

```text
exterior^bullet(aV) tensor Sym^n(E).
```

It preserves total `a`-degree, and its homology is

```text
H_0=direct-sum_(j=0)^(q-1) a^(2j) 1,
H_1=direct-sum_(j=0)^(q-1) a^(q+j) V_(q-j),
H_2=direct-sum_(j=0)^(q-1) a^(q+1+j) V_(q-1-j).       (P23.11a)
```

Consequently its homology Euler character is

```text
P_q(a^2)-a^q chi_q=B(a,y)Q_q(a,y).                   (P23.11b)
```

**Proof.**  The contractions anticommute and the `D_v` commute, so
`d_q^2=0`.  Contraction lowers exterior degree by one, while `D_v` replaces
`x` by a vector of `a`-degree one, so total `a`-degree is preserved.  In
coordinates `y_1,y_2` on `V`, one has `D_i=y_i partial_x`.  Decomposition
first by the power `z^j` and then by
`deg_x-exterior_degree` gives homogeneous strands of the Koszul complex
for the regular sequence `(y_1,y_2)`.  All interiors are exact.  In degree
zero only `x^(n-j)z^j` survives.  In degree two the `x`-degree-zero endpoint
is `z^j Sym^(n-j)(V) tensor exterior^2(V)`.  In degree one, quotienting the
`x`-degree-zero endpoint by the incoming Koszul image leaves
`z^j V_(n-j+1)`.  These are exactly `(P23.11a)`.  The terms `H_1(j)` for
`1<=j<=q-1` equal the graded
characters `H_2(j-1)`.  The unpaired terms are `a^qV_q` in odd degree and
`a^(2q)1` in even degree, which with `H_0` give `(P23.11b)`.  QED.

This factorwise resolution does not make invariant homology even in the
outer range.

**Lemma 23A2 (factorwise-homology obstruction).**  Give every minus factor
of `(P23.10)` its differential `d_q`, give the plus factors zero
differential, and take the tensor-product differential.  For

```text
R=[3^-,3^-,1^+,2^+,2^+],                 p=5,
```

its homology has a nonzero odd invariant in the outer grade `a^6`.

**Proof.**  Here the label sum of `R` is `11`, so the coefficient of
`V_5` occurs in grade `a^(11-5)=a^6`.  Choose the essential odd class
`a^3V_3` from one minus factor, the degree-zero scalar from the other,
`aV_1` from the plus label one, `a^2V_2` from one plus label two, and the
degree-zero scalar from the other.  Their tensor product has grade six and

```text
dim Hom_SU(2)(1,V_3 tensor V_1 tensor V_2)=1,
```

because `V_1 tensor V_2` contains `V_3`.  The indexed choices give at least
four such odd classes.  Kunneth's theorem makes them genuine homology
classes of the factorwise differential.  QED.

The obstruction in Lemma 23A2 has a unique lowest-degree binary correction,
but its local chain lift is curved.

**Lemma 23A3 (first cross-factor transvectant and curvature).**  For the
outer class in Lemma 23A2, the only lowest-degree binary
`SU(2)`-equivariant channel which can map it to an even invariant is

```text
V_3 tensor V_1 -> V_2.                               (P23.11c)
```

It sends the odd `a^3V_3` class of a minus factor, together with the
`aV_1` state of the plus-one factor, to the even `a^4V_2=H_2(j=0)` class.
No binary channel using a plus-two state works.

On chains, exterior multiplication by the plus vector `w` is the natural
order-zero lift:

```text
delta_w(alpha tensor f tensor w)
 =(w wedge alpha) tensor f tensor 1.
```

It satisfies

```text
delta_w^2=0,             {d,delta_w}=D_w.              (P23.11d)
```

In particular it does not extend the factorwise differential to a
bicomplex.  This curvature is unavoidable among order-zero binary lifts.

**Proof.**  Clebsch--Gordan gives
`V_3 tensor V_1=V_2 direct-sum V_4`, and the required even homology target
is `V_2`; hence `(P23.11c)` is unique up to scalar.  In contrast,
`V_3 tensor V_2=V_1 direct-sum V_3 direct-sum V_5` contains no `V_2`.
The Cartan identity between contraction and exterior multiplication gives
`(P23.11d)`.  It is nonzero already on
`x Sym^(q-2)(E)`.  Finally, the relevant equivariant Hom-space into
`exterior^2(V)` is one-dimensional, so every order-zero lift is a scalar
multiple of this one and has the same curvature.  QED.

There is an exact nonlocal lift for this first obstruction.  Choose the
`SU(2)`-invariant orthogonal deformation retract `(p,i,h)` of the
factorwise complex onto its homology, let `delta_H` be the map
`V_3 tensor V_1 -> V_2` from `H_1(j=0)` to `H_2(j=0)`, and put

```text
tilde_delta=i delta_H p.                              (P23.11e)
```

Then `{d,tilde_delta}=0` and `tilde_delta^2=0`.  Summing `(P23.11e)` over
the two minus factors in Lemma 23A2 pairs all four indexed odd invariants
with the corresponding even `V_2 tensor V_2` invariants at the reduced
essential-source level.  This does not yet remove them in the full homology
complex, because the same `H_2` classes are paired characterwise with
`H_1(j=1)` classes; Lemma 23A6B below gives the resulting Schur-complement
condition.  What remains is to redistribute those paired odd columns while
making the harmonic projections compatible across arbitrary factors and
grades, or to cancel the curvature `D_w` by higher-order terms satisfying
the resulting Maurer--Cartan equations.

The characterwise cancellations inside `(P23.11a)` cannot be performed
before constructing the cross-factor arrows.  If they are, the effective
minus and plus factors are

```text
M_q^eff=(direct-sum_(i=0)^q a^(2i)1)
          direct-sum Pi(a^q V_q),

P_r=(direct-sum_(j=0)^r a^(2j)1) direct-sum a^r V_r.  (P23.11f)
```

Here `Pi` denotes odd parity.

**Lemma 23A4 (effective binary-map classification).**  Every
degree-preserving equivariant binary odd map from the odd summand of
`M_q^eff tensor P_r` to its even part is a linear combination of the
following channels:

```text
a^qV_q tensor a^(2s)1 -> a^(2s)1 tensor a^rV_r,
    possible exactly when q=r;

a^qV_q tensor a^rV_r -> a^(2i)1 tensor a^(2t)1,
    possible exactly when q=r and i+t=q;

a^qV_q tensor a^rV_r -> a^q1 tensor a^rV_r,
    possible exactly when q is even and q<=2r.         (P23.11g)
```

In particular, if `q` is odd and support-disjoint from the plus labels,
every such effective binary map is zero.

**Proof.**  The target in the minus coordinate is scalar, while the only
plus-coordinate types are `1` and `V_r`.  Thus the relevant Hom-spaces are

```text
Hom(V_q,V_r),       Hom(V_q tensor V_r,1),
Hom(V_q tensor V_r,V_r).
```

The first two are nonzero exactly when `q=r`.  Clebsch--Gordan makes the
third one-dimensional exactly when `q` is even and `q<=2r`.  Equality of
the displayed `a`-degrees forces respectively `i=s`, `i+t=q`, and
`i=q/2`, giving `(P23.11g)`.  QED.

For the word of Lemma 23A2, all effective binary channels vanish:
`q=3` is odd and differs from the plus labels `1,2`.  The required
invariant is already ternary,

```text
V_3 tensor V_1 tensor V_2 -> 1.                       (P23.11h)
```

Retaining the characterwise cancellable `a^4V_2=H_2(j=0)` carrier instead
allows the binary transvectant `(P23.11c)`.  Therefore the paired
`H_1/H_2` summands are essential Hall capacity, despite cancelling in the
Euler character.  A derived proof must either retain them and solve the
overlapping-arrow relations, or use genuinely higher-arity operations on
the minimal effective object.

The higher-arity evaluation maps on the minimal object have a simple
classification and no square-zero obstruction.  For an odd minus summand
`Pi(a^qV_q)` and two nontrivial plus summands `a^rV_r,a^sV_s`, put

```text
alpha=(q+r-s)/2,       beta=(r+s-q)/2,
gamma=(s+q-r)/2.
```

**Lemma 23A5 (ternary evaluation differential).**  An invariant evaluation

```text
V_q tensor V_r tensor V_s -> 1                      (P23.11i)
```

exists exactly when

```text
|r-s|<=q<=r+s,                 q+r+s even.
```

It is unique up to scalar.  For every

```text
0<=i<=q, 0<=j<=r, 0<=l<=s,
i+j+l=(q+r+s)/2,
```

it gives a degree-preserving odd map

```text
Pi(a^qV_q) tensor a^rV_r tensor a^sV_s
 -> a^(2i)1 tensor a^(2j)1 tensor a^(2l)1.            (P23.11j)
```

The allocation `(i,j,l)=(alpha,beta,gamma)` is always available.
Any linear combination of such indexed ternary evaluations, extended by
the identity on untouched factors with Koszul signs, squares to zero.

**Proof.**  Clebsch--Gordan gives the stated admissibility and
one-dimensional Hom-space.  Equivalently, `(P23.11i)` contracts
`alpha,beta,gamma` pairs of fundamental indices between the three symmetric
powers.  Degree equality is exactly the displayed sum condition.  If two
