pair `(0,4)` supplies the latter contribution, and every other endpoint
is stronger.  Hence

```text
m_2(B)>=8,                         m_4(B)>=9.
```

If `m_4(A)>=2`, then `N>=3+2*8+2*9=37>36`.  If
`m_4(A)=1`, then `A={1,1,2}`, its equal-plus pair contributes
`m_2(B)>=8`, and

```text
N+P_eq>=3+2*8+9+8=36=12d.                          (P23.11z5zzc7)
```

Now let `d=2`, so `K={s,s+2}` with `s>=1`.  Proposition 23A9Z gives
`m_2(B),m_4(B)>=5`.  It also gives

```text
m_6(B)>=3.                                         (P23.11z5zzc8)
```

Indeed, if `s=1`, the pair `(3,3)` and the forced upper-extension pairs
`(5,1),(5,3)` contribute to `m_6(B)`.  If `s=2`, the three pairs
`(2,4),(4,2),(4,4)` contribute; and if `s>=3`, all four pairs inside
`K` contribute.  Moreover,

```text
m_4(A)>=2  implies  m_6(A)>=1.                     (P23.11z5zzc9)
```

To see this, choose two adjacent channels `r,r+2` counted by `m_4(A)`.
The upper one satisfies

```text
r+2>=|z-4|+2>=|z-6|,
```

where `z` is the largest triple label, and it is at most `z+4`; hence it
is also a `V_6` channel.  Thus if `m_4(A)>=2`,

```text
N>=2+2*5+2*5+3=25>24.
```

It remains in this case to take `m_4(A)=1`, so
`A={1,1,2}`.  Its equal-plus pair contributes `m_2(B)`, and therefore

```text
N+P_eq>=2+3m_2(B)+m_4(B).                           (P23.11z5zzc10)
```

This is at least `24` unless `m_2(B)=5` and `m_4(B)<=6`.  In that endpoint
there is exactly one extension side beyond `K`.  A one-step extension
comes from a label pair containing `2`.  Two or more lower-extension
steps give `m_4(B)>=7`; two or more upper-extension steps do likewise
unless `s=1`, when the unextended interval `K={1,3}` itself comes from
the pair `{1,2}`.  Thus `B` contains a label `2` in every remaining case.
If it contains two, disjoint support already fails.  Otherwise its unique
label `2` must occupy the remaining minus position, so `q=a=2`.

The first summand of `P_eq` then supplies `m(P)>=2`.  Explicitly, writing
the five plus labels as `{1,1}` and the remaining triple `R`, the active
cut has

```text
m(P)=sum_t m_t(1,1)m_t(R)>=m_2(R)=m(2,R)=d=2.
```

Adding this to `(P23.11z5zzc10)` gives at least `24`.

Finally suppose `d=1`.  Proposition 23A9Z gives
`m_2(B)>=3,m_4(B)>=2`, and the pair `(s,s+2)` used there also gives
`m_6(B)>=1`.  If `m_4(A)>=2`, `(P23.11z5zzc9)` yields

```text
N>=1+2*3+2*2+1=12.
```

If `m_4(A)=1`, the equal-plus reservoir contributes at least three while
`N>=9`, again giving `N+P_eq>=12`.

Thus `N+P_eq>=12d` in every case.  Since `T<=12d`, this proves
`(P23.11z5zzc4)`.  QED.

The remaining dense layer has only even labels.

**Corollary 23A9ZC (parity localization of the dense layer).**  Under the
hypotheses of Proposition 23A9S, if at least thirteen indexed oriented
summands in `T` are nonzero, then

```text
q,a,p_1,...,p_5 are all even.                       (P23.11z5zzc11)
```

Consequently Corollary 23A9ZB reduces the complete unresolved ordinary
seven-factor two-minus layer to the all-even, or `SO(3)`, sector with
between thirteen and twenty active negative cuts.

**Proof.**  Let `o` of the five plus labels be odd and put `e=5-o`.
For one minus label `r`, an indexed plus pair can support an invariant
triple with `r` only if its label sum has the same parity as `r`.  Thus an
even `r` has at most

```text
S=binom(e,2)+binom(o,2)
```

candidate pairs, while an odd `r` has at most

```text
O=eo.                                               (P23.11z5zzc12)
```

The complementary fourfold invariant can only decrease these counts.
Since at least one summand is active, the total sum
`q+a+sum_i p_i` is even.

(i) If `q,a` have opposite parity, then `o` is odd and the two orientations
together have at most

```text
S+O=binom(5,2)=10
```

active summands.

(ii) If `q,a` are both odd, then `o` is even and there are at most `2O`
active summands.  For `o=0,2,4`, these bounds are respectively
`0,12,8`.

(iii) If `q,a` are both even, then `o` is even and there are at most `2S`
active summands.  For `o=0,2,4`, these bounds are respectively
`20,8,12`.

Therefore thirteen active summands force the last case with `o=0`, proving
`(P23.11z5zzc11)`.  QED.

The all-even sector has enough neighboring-channel multiplicity to pay
all twenty cuts directly.

**Corollary 23A9ZD (complete ordinary seven-factor two-minus theorem).**
Under `(P23.11z5zv)`, in the ordinary representation category,

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                          (P23.11z5zzd1)
```

Consequently every ordinary seven-factor contraction with two minus
labels is nonnegative.

**Proof.**  By Corollaries 23A9ZB and 23A9ZC it remains only to assume
that all seven labels are even and at least thirteen cuts are active.
Choose a maximal active contribution `d`, its invariant triple `A`, and
its four-label complement `B` as in Proposition 23A9Z.

Write the three labels of `A` as `2x<=2y<=2z` and put

```text
L={|x-y|,|x-y|+1,...,x+y}.
```

Then

```text
m_(2j)(A)=|L intersection
                 {|z-j|,|z-j|+1,...,z+j}|.          (P23.11z5zzd2)
```

If `x+y=z`, positivity gives the profile

```text
(m_0(A),m_2(A),m_4(A),m_6(A))>=(1,2,3,2).          (P23.11z5zzd3)
```

Indeed, `L` contains `z-2,z-1,z`; the last three comparison intervals
contain respectively at least `z-1,z`, then all three entries, then
`z-1,z`.  If `x+y>=z+1`, then `L` contains
`z-1,z,z+1`.  The case `z=1` would make all three original labels equal
to two, contrary to disjoint support between the minus position and the
two plus positions in `A`.  Hence

```text
(m_0(A),m_2(A),m_4(A),m_6(A))>=(1,3,3,3).          (P23.11z5zzd4)
```

Pair the four labels in `B` and retain the intervals `I,J` and their
intersection `K` from Proposition 23A9Z.  Because the four input labels
are positive and even, both `I,J` have at least three entries.  Moreover
`K` begins at an even value `s>=2`: parity makes its entries even, while
Proposition 23A9Z excludes zero.

Suppose first that `d>=3`.  The two intervals cannot both equal `K`,
since equality would make the four labels of `B` two equal pairs and
force its unique minus label to occur also at a plus position.  Thus one
interval has an entry adjacent to an endpoint of `K` that the other does
not have.  Counting pairs inside `K` and then this extension gives

```text
m_2(B)>=3d-1,        m_4(B)>=5d-5,
m_6(B)>=7d-12.                                     (P23.11z5zzd5)
```

For the first two bounds, the pairs in `K` whose indices differ by at
most one and two steps number `3d-2` and `5d-6`; all satisfy the lower
triangle inequality because `s>=2`.  For output six, the corresponding
three-step count is `7d-12`, with only `(2,2)` possibly failing.  The
extension replaces that possible loss: a lower extension at `s=2`
contributes through `(0,6)`, while every other lower or upper extension
has a nearer admissible pair.

The weaker profile `(P23.11z5zzd3)` now gives

```text
N>=d+2(3d-1)+3(5d-5)+2(7d-12)
  =36d-41>=20d,                                   (P23.11z5zzd6)
```

where the last inequality uses `d>=3`.  The nonsaturated profile
`(P23.11z5zzd4)` is stronger.

Let `d=2`, so `K={s,s+2}`.  Since both intervals have at least three
entries and their intersection is exactly `K`, after exchanging them one
contains `s-2` and the other contains `s+4`.  The resulting three-by-three
rectangle contains respectively at least

```text
m_2(B)>=6,                 m_4(B)>=7,
m_6(B)>=6.                                         (P23.11z5zzd7)
```

For example, the six output-two pairs are

```text
(s-2,s), (s,s), (s,s+2),
(s+2,s), (s+2,s+2), (s+2,s+4).
```

For output four use the seven pairs

```text
(s-2,s+2),
(s,s), (s,s+2), (s,s+4),
(s+2,s), (s+2,s+2), (s+2,s+4);
```

The following six pairs are admissible for output six:

```text
(s-2,s+4), (s,s+2), (s,s+4),
(s+2,s), (s+2,s+2), (s+2,s+4).
```

Therefore even the saturated profile gives

```text
N>=2+2*6+3*7+2*6=47>40=20d.                        (P23.11z5zzd8)
```

Finally let `d=1`, with `K={s}`.  Singleton intersection and the
three-entry minimum for both intervals force, after exchange,

```text
{s-4,s-2,s} subset I,       {s,s+2,s+4} subset J,
```

so in particular `s>=4`.  Direct pair counting gives

```text
m_2(B)>=3,                 m_4(B)>=6,
m_6(B)>=7.                                         (P23.11z5zzd9)
```

For output six one may use

```text
(s-4,s+2),
(s-2,s), (s-2,s+2), (s-2,s+4),
(s,s), (s,s+2), (s,s+4);
```

the output-two and output-four counts are the evident one-step and
two-step collections

```text
(s-2,s), (s,s), (s,s+2)
```

and

```text
(s-4,s),
(s-2,s), (s-2,s+2),
(s,s), (s,s+2), (s,s+4).
```

Hence

```text
N>=1+2*3+3*6+2*7=39>20=20d.                        (P23.11z5zzd10)
```

There are at most twenty active indexed oriented summands, each at most
`d`.  Thus `T<=20d<=N` throughout the dense all-even sector.  Corollary
23A9ZB handles every smaller active-cut count, proving
`(P23.11z5zzd1)`.  Proposition 23A9S proves the contraction claim under
disjoint support; the support-overlap reduction cited in Proposition
23A9W reduces the complementary cases to the already proved six-factor
theorem.  QED.

The strict exact C++ cross-check
`character_ring_iter/search_su2_all_even_two_minus_dense.cpp` exhausts the
remaining all-even dense chamber through even label forty.  It passed all
`2,071,772` cases with at least thirteen active cuts; the minimum raw
margin was `N-T=10` at minus labels `[4,4]` and plus labels
`[2,2,2,2,2]`.  Its source SHA-256 is
`7789206c2ae0841a1c33a6622d91d450981f33faf3a9c7781908f4db4d165164`,
and the strict replay is
`certificates/su2_all_even_two_minus_dense.log`.  This bounded run is only
an independent check; Corollary 23A9ZD is the unbounded proof.

The ordinary proof survives through the affine wall on a box much larger
than the stable total-degree chamber.

**Proposition 23A9ZE (finite half-level box).**  Let `k>=3` and put

```text
h=floor((k-3)/2).
```

In `SU(2)_k`, assume that all seven nontrivial labels satisfy

```text
q,a,p_1,...,p_5<=h.                                (P23.11z5zze1)
```

Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq,                                         (P23.11z5zze2)
```

and hence every seven-factor contraction with two minus labels is
nonnegative on this box.  The conclusion is uniform in `k` and does not
use the positive-cut term `U`.

**Proof.**  Under support overlap, Proposition 5 and Proposition 25D4
reduce the claim to the finite six-factor theorem, so assume
`(P23.11z5zv)`.  If `k<=6`, then `h<=1`; all nontrivial labels in the box
would equal one, contradicting disjoint support.  We may therefore assume
`k>=7`.  We verify that every channel used in Propositions
23A9Z and Corollaries 23A9ZA--23A9ZD is unchanged by affine truncation.

For any two labels in `(P23.11z5zze1)`,

```text
b+c<=2h<=k-3.
```

Their finite Clebsch--Gordan interval is therefore the ordinary interval,
and every one of its entries is at most `k-3`.  If `u,v` are entries of
two such intervals and `r<=6`, then

```text
u+v+r<=2(k-3)+6=2k.                                (P23.11z5zze3)
```

Thus `N_(u,v)^r` is also the ordinary Clebsch--Gordan coefficient.  It
follows that for every four-label block `B`, after either pairing,

```text
m_r^(k)(B)=m_r^(infinity)(B),        r=0,2,4,6.     (P23.11z5zze4)
```

Only equality for the displayed low channels is asserted; higher
channels may still meet the wall.

The same check applies to an active triple `A`.  Fuse two of its labels
first.  The intermediate channel is at most `2h`, and the third label is
at most `h`, so for `r<=6`,

```text
u+c+r<=3h+6<=2k.
```

Hence

```text
m_r^(k)(A)=m_r^(infinity)(A),        r=0,2,4,6.     (P23.11z5zze5)
```

The numerical inequality uses
`3h+6<=3(k-3)/2+6<=2k`, valid for `k>=3`.

Now choose a maximal active cut contribution
`d=m_0^(k)(B)`.  Formula `(P23.11z5zze4)` identifies its paired interval
description and all low-channel lower bounds with those in the ordinary
proof.  Orthogonality in the finite fusion category gives

```text
N=sum_(r=0)^k m_r^(k)(A)m_r^(k)(B),
```

so discarding the unlisted nonnegative channels reproduces every bound in
Proposition 23A9Z and Corollaries 23A9ZA--23A9ZD.  The parity localization
is unchanged, since finite fusion has the same parity rule.  Therefore
`T<=N+P_eq` exactly as in `(P23.11z5zzd1)`.  Proposition 23A9S, now with
the additional nonnegative term `U`, proves the signed contraction.
QED.

There is also a wall-touching chamber in which the affine loss in the
first three neighboring channels is bounded independently of the level.

**Proposition 23A9ZF (finite large-cut even chamber).**  Work in
`SU(2)_k`, with `k>=6`, under the disjoint-support hypothesis
`(P23.11z5zv)`, and assume that all seven labels are positive and even.
Choose an active indexed negative cut `A|B` whose contribution

```text
d=m_0(B)
```

is maximal.  Suppose that

```text
d>=11,                                                (P23.11z5zzf1)
```

that the invariant triple `A` contains no label `k`, and, when `k` is
odd, that its label multiset is not

```text
{2,k-1,k-1}.                                         (P23.11z5zzf2)
```

Then

```text
T<=N.                                                (P23.11z5zzf3)
```

In particular, the finite seven-factor two-minus contraction is
nonnegative in this chamber.  No claim is made here for the excluded
simple-current and odd-level endpoint triples.

**Proof.**  We first establish the low-channel profile of `A`.  Order its
labels as `a<=b<=c` and use the variables of `(P25.29e3)`:

```text
x=(b+c-a)/2, y=(a+c-b)/2, z=(a+b-c)/2,
w=k-(a+b+c)/2.
```

Thus `x,y>0`, `z,w>=0`, and the evenness of `a,b,c` makes `x,y,z`
have the same parity.  Formula `(P25.29e3)` gives

```text
m_(2j)(A)=#(Z intersection [L_j,U_j]).
```

If `z,w>0`, its endpoint formulas give two entries for `j=1,2` and one
for `j=3`, except that `a=b=c=2` is the sole smallest case in the
`j=2` check.  That triple is impossible here because its minus label
would equal its two plus labels.  If `z=0`, then `x,y` are positive even
integers; the same endpoint check gives the required entries.  If `w=0`,
the check can fail only when `z=1` and one of `x,y` equals one.  In that
case the labels are exactly `{2,k-1,k-1}`, excluded by
`(P23.11z5zzf2)`.  Finally `z=w=0` would make `c=k`, which is excluded
separately.  Consequently

```text
m_0(A)=1,       m_2(A)>=2,       m_4(A)>=2,
m_6(A)>=1.                                          (P23.11z5zzf4)
```

Pair the four labels of `B`, choosing one pair to contain its unique
minus position, and let `I,J` be the two finite Clebsch--Gordan
intervals.  Put `K=I intersection J`; then `|K|=d`.  If `0 in K`, the
minus-plus pair would have equal labels, contrary to disjoint support.
Thus `0 notin K`.  All labels are even, so write

```text
K={s,s+2,...,s+2(d-1)},             s>=2.            (P23.11z5zzf5)
```

Before imposing the affine upper triangle inequality, the ordered pairs
in `K times K` whose indices differ by at most one, two, and three steps
number

```text
3d-2,                    5d-6,                    7d-12.
```

They are the candidate contributions to `m_2(B),m_4(B),m_6(B)`.
The lower triangle inequality removes none for outputs two and four, and
can remove only `(2,2)` for output six.

It remains to count the upper-wall losses from

```text
u+v+r<=2k.
```

For even `k`, the possible lost pairs are contained in

```text
r=2: (k,k);

r=4: (k,k), (k,k-2), (k-2,k);

r=6: (k,k), (k,k-2), (k-2,k),
     (k,k-4), (k-4,k), (k-2,k-2).
```

For odd `k`, the even interval `K` ends at most at `k-1`; the
corresponding lists have respectively zero, one, and three entries:

```text
r=4: (k-1,k-1);

r=6: (k-1,k-1), (k-1,k-3), (k-3,k-1).
```

These lists follow directly by writing
`u=k-2alpha,v=k-2beta` in the even-level case and
`u=k-1-2alpha,v=k-1-2beta` in the odd-level case.  Hence, uniformly,

```text
m_2(B)>=3d-3,       m_4(B)>=5d-9,
m_6(B)>=7d-19.                                      (P23.11z5zzf6)
```

Finite fusion orthogonality across `A|B`, followed by
`(P23.11z5zzf4)`--`(P23.11z5zzf6)`, now gives

```text
N>=d+2(3d-3)+2(5d-9)+(7d-19)
  =24d-43.                                          (P23.11z5zzf7)
```

For `d>=11`, this is at least `20d`.  There are only twenty indexed
oriented negative cuts, and maximality of `d` gives

```text
T<=20d<=N.
```

Proposition 23A9S finishes the contraction inequality.  QED.

The two triples excluded from Proposition 23A9ZF admit the following
endpoint reduction.  Both branches close completely in the large-cut
range by combining affine band counts with active-cut structure.

**Corollary 23A9ZG (finite maximal-triple endpoint reductions).**  Keep
the all-even, disjoint-support, maximal-cut notation of Proposition
23A9ZF, including `d>=11`.

(i) Suppose that `A` contains `k`.  Then

```text
A={k,u,k-u}.
```

Put `t=min(u,k-u)`.  Then

```text
T<=N.                                                (P23.11z5zzg1)
```

(ii) Suppose that `k` is odd and

```text
A={2,k-1,k-1}.
```

Then the number `c` of active indexed oriented negative cuts satisfies

```text
c<=10,                                             (P23.11z5zzg2)
```

and

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                          (P23.11z5zzg3)
```

**Proof.**  (i) The simple-current identity gives

```text
V_k star_k V_u=V_(k-u),
V_(k-u) star_k V_(k-u)=sum_(h=0)^t V_(2h).
```

Consequently

```text
m_(2h)(A)=1 for 0<=h<=t,             m_r(A)=0 otherwise. (P23.11z5zzg4)
```

Here `t` is a positive even integer, because all three labels of `A` are
even.  Thus `t>=4` means either `t=4` or `t>=6`.

We need a band estimate for `B`.  Divide its even intermediate channels
by two, so that `K` becomes a consecutive integer interval of length
`d`, beginning at least at one.  For `h>=1` and `d>=h+1`, the ordered
pairs in `K times K` at distance at most `h` number

```text
(2h+1)d-h(h+1).
```

Among these, the lower triangle inequality for output `2h` removes at
most

```text
(h-2)(h-1)/2
```

pairs, obtained when the interval begins at one.  The affine upper
triangle inequality removes at most

```text
h(h+1)/2
```

pairs, obtained when the interval ends at `k/2`.  Therefore

```text
m_(2h)(B)>=(2h+1)d-2h^2-1,             h>=1,        (P23.11z5zzg5)
```

while `m_0(B)=d`.  Summing `(P23.11z5zzg5)` through `h=s`, where
`s<=min(t,d-1)`, gives

```text
N>=sum_(h=0)^s m_(2h)(B)
 >=(s+1)^2d-{s(s+1)(2s+1)/3+s}.                     (P23.11z5zzg6)
```

If `t>=6`, take `s=6`.  Since `d>=11`,

```text
N>=49d-188>20d>=T.
```

Let `t=4`.  Taking `s=4` gives

```text
N>=25d-64>=19d.                                     (P23.11z5zzg7)
```

Thus only twenty active cuts could escape this bound.  If the label `k`
is a minus label, activity of all ten pairs in that orientation forces
every indexed plus pair to sum to `k`.  Hence all five plus labels equal
`k/2`; because `t=4`, one has `k=8`, and every fourfold invariant
multiplicity is at most `k+1=9<d`, a contradiction.

If instead `k` is a plus label, activity of all four pairs containing it
forces the other four plus labels to equal `k-q` in the `q` orientation
and `k-a` in the `a` orientation.  Hence `q=a=s`, and the plus multiset
is `{k,(k-s)^4}`.  Across the selected cut, pair `B` as

```text
(s,k-s) | (k-s,k-s).
```

Both finite Clebsch--Gordan intervals have at most
`min(s,k-s)+1=t+1=5` entries, so `d<=5`, again a contradiction.  Thus
there are at most nineteen active cuts and `(P23.11z5zzg7)` proves
`(P23.11z5zzg1)` when `t=4`.

It remains to take `t=2`.  The exact profile `(P23.11z5zzg4)` and the
first two cases of the wall-loss count `(P23.11z5zzg5)` give

```text
N>=d+(3d-3)+(5d-9)=9d-12.                         (P23.11z5zzg7a)
```

First suppose that `k` is a minus label.  Put a graph on the five indexed
plus positions, joining two positions when their labels sum to `k`, and
let `g` be its number of edges.  A negative cut in the `k` orientation
can be active only on such an edge, and its contribution is at most `d`.
Every cut in the other minus orientation has `k` in its fourfold
complement.  For any other label `x` in that complement, the
simple-current identity gives

```text
m(k,x,y,z)=m(k-x,y,z)<=1,                           (P23.11z5zzg7b)
```

because a threefold invariant multiplicity is zero or one.  Thus these
ten cuts contribute at most ten in total.

The selected triple supplies the edge with labels `2,k-2`.  Since
`k>=6`, these are distinct and lie in one non-fixed complementary-value
component `K_(p,q)` of the graph.  If that component uses `n` of the five
vertices, its edge count is at most `floor(n^2/4)`.  The remaining
`5-n` vertices, including any possible fixed-value clique at `k/2` and
all other complementary components, have at most `binom(5-n,2)` edges.
For `n=2,3,4,5`, the resulting bounds are respectively

```text
1+3,                  2+1,                  4+0,       6+0.
```

Hence `g<=6`, and therefore

```text
T<=6d+10<=9d-12<=N,                    d>=11.       (P23.11z5zzg7c)
```

Now suppose that `k` is a plus label.  For a minus orientation `r`, a cut
whose plus pair contains `k` can be active only when the other plus label
is `k-r`.  Every other cut has `k` in its fourfold complement and hence
contributes at most one by `(P23.11z5zzg7b)`.  Among the four non-`k`
plus positions, write `n_v` for the number having label `v`, and put

```text
h=n_(k-q)+n_(k-a).
```

The `h` exceptional oriented cuts contribute at most `hd`, while all
others contribute at most one.  If `q!=a`, the two counted labels are
distinct, so `h<=4`.  If `q=a`, then `h=2n_(k-q)`.  The only way to have
`h=8` is for all four non-`k` plus labels to equal `k-q`.  In that case
the selected complement is

```text
B={q,k-q,k-q,k-q}.
```

Pairing it as `(q,k-q)|(k-q,k-q)` shows that each finite
Clebsch--Gordan interval has `t+1=3` entries, and hence `d<=3`, contrary
to `d>=11`.  Thus `h<=6`, and

```text
T<=hd+(20-h)<=6d+14<=9d-12<=N,         d>=11.       (P23.11z5zzg7d)
```

This proves `(P23.11z5zzg1)` also for `t=2`, completing (i).

(ii) Disjoint support forces the label two in `A` to be the minus label
and the two copies of `k-1` to be plus labels.  Simple-current reflection
of both copies of `k-1` gives the exact profile

```text
m_0(A)=1,                    m_2(A)=2,
m_4(A)=1.                                           (P23.11z5zzg8)
```

Deleting the equal plus pair contributes exactly `m_2(B)` to `P_eq`.
At odd level the even interval `K` has no affine loss in the output-two
band, while only `(k-1,k-1)` can be lost in the output-four band.  Since
`0 notin K`,

```text
m_2(B)>=3d-2,                  m_4(B)>=5d-7.         (P23.11z5zzg9)
```

Orthogonality and the displayed equal-pair reservoir give

```text
N+P_eq>=d+3m_2(B)+m_4(B)>=15d-13.                  (P23.11z5zzg10)
```

It remains to bound `c`; this is where the large value of the selected
fourfold multiplicity is used.  Write the labels of `B` as

```text
{a,r_1,r_2,r_3}.
```

For any label `ell` in this set, pair it with one of the other three
labels, say `x`.  The fourfold invariant multiplicity is the cardinality
of an intersection of two fusion intervals, and hence

```text
d<=|ell star_k x|<=min(ell,k-ell)+1.                (P23.11z5zzg11)
```

Since `d>=11`, every label in `B` therefore lies in

```text
10<=a,r_i<=k-10.                                   (P23.11z5zzg12)
```

Put `e=k-1`, so the five plus labels are
`{e,e,r_1,r_2,r_3}`.  In the orientation whose minus label is two, an
active selected pair must have equal labels or labels differing by two.
No pair `{e,r_i}` can qualify by `(P23.11z5zzg12)`.  Thus only the pair
`{e,e}` and the three pairs among the `r_i` can be active, for at most
four cuts.

In the other orientation, the pair `{e,e}` cannot be active because

```text
e star_k e={0,2},                   a>=10.
```

There are at most six indexed pairs `{e,r_i}`.  Finally, if a selected
pair consists of two `r_i`, its fourfold complement has labels
`{2,e,e,r_j}`.  Since

```text
e star_k e={0,2},
```

that complement is invariant only if

```text
r_j in {2,4};
```

this follows equivalently from
`N_(2,r_j)^0+N_(2,r_j)^2>0`.  It is impossible by
`(P23.11z5zzg12)`.  Hence at most six cuts occur in the second
orientation and `c<=4+6=10`, proving `(P23.11z5zzg2)`.

Maximality gives `T<=cd<=10d`, while `(P23.11z5zzg10)` and `d>=11`
give

```text
N+P_eq>=15d-13>=10d.
```

This proves `(P23.11z5zzg3)`.  QED.

Combining the generic and endpoint estimates removes every large-cut
configuration in the dense parity sector.

**Corollary 23A9ZG1 (complete finite large-cut even chamber).**  Under
the all-even, disjoint-support hypotheses of Proposition 23A9ZF, if a
maximal active cut has

```text
d>=11,
```

then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.
```

Hence the finite seven-factor two-minus contraction is nonnegative
throughout this chamber.

**Proof.**  If the maximal triple contains neither `k` nor, at odd level,
the exceptional multiset `{2,k-1,k-1}`, Proposition 23A9ZF gives the
stronger inequality `T<=N`.  Corollary 23A9ZG(i) handles every maximal
triple containing `k`, again with `T<=N`, and part (ii) handles the sole
remaining odd-level triple with `T<=N+P_eq`.  These cases are exhaustive.
QED.

The simple-current branch admits a stronger weighted-cut comparison below
the uniform large-cut threshold.

**Corollary 23A9ZG2 (finite simple-current bounded-cut closure).**  Keep
the all-even, disjoint-support, maximal-cut notation and suppose

```text
A={k,u,k-u},                    t=min(u,k-u),
d=m_0(B).
```

Then `T<=N` in each of the following chambers:

```text
t>=4 and d>=5;
t=2 and d>=6.
```

At `d=5`, the `t=2` chamber is also closed unless `k` is a plus label
and the two minus labels are distinct.

**Proof.**  Retain the exact profile `(P23.11z5zzg4)` and the band sum
`(P23.11z5zzg6)`.  If `t>=4` and `d>=5`, take `s=4` to obtain

```text
N>=25d-64.                                          (P23.11z5zzg2a)
```

If `k` is a minus label, at most all ten plus pairs are active in its
orientation, each with contribution at most `d`; every cut in the other
orientation has `k` in its complementary fourfold block and contributes
at most one.  Hence `T<=10d+10`.  If `k` is a plus label, only the four
pairs containing it in each minus orientation can avoid leaving `k` in
the complementary block.  Thus at most eight cuts contribute `d`, and
the other twelve contribute at most one, giving `T<=8d+12`.  Both bounds
are at most `(P23.11z5zzg2a)` for `d>=5`.

Now take `t=2`.  The first three exact bands give

```text
N>=9d-12.                                           (P23.11z5zzg2b)
```

Suppose first that `k` is a minus label.  Its selected plus labels are
`2,k-2`.  A fourfold invariant containing either of these labels has
multiplicity at most three, so `d>=5` excludes both values from the
three other plus positions `R_1,R_2,R_3`.  In the `k`-minus orientation,
activity requires the two plus labels to sum to `k`.  There is the
selected edge and at most three edges among the `R_i`; hence this
orientation contributes at most `4d`.  The other orientation contributes
at most ten because every complementary block contains `k`.  Therefore

```text
T<=4d+10<=9d-12,                    d>=5.           (P23.11z5zzg2c)
```

Finally suppose that `k` is a plus label.  Denote the selected minus
label by `q in {2,k-2}`, the other minus label by `a`, and put

```text
h=n_(k-q)+n_(k-a)
```

among the four non-`k` plus positions.  Exactly as in
`(P23.11z5zzg7d)`, the `h` pairs containing `k` contribute at most
`hd`, while every other cut contributes at most one:

```text
T<=hd+(20-h).                                       (P23.11z5zzg2d)
```

If `q!=a`, the counted labels are distinct, so `h<=4`, and
`T<=4d+16<=9d-12` for `d>=6`.  If `q=a`, write
`x=k-q` and `h=2n_x`.  The selected triple uses one copy of `x`.
If a second copy occurred, the selected complement `B` would contain
the pair `q,x`, whose finite fusion interval has exactly `t+1=3`
channels; this would force `d<=3`.  Thus `d>=5` gives `n_x=1`,
`h=2`, and

```text
T<=2d+18<=9d-12.
```

This proves all the stated chambers.  QED.

The only bounded-cut placement omitted above also closes after retaining
the three-channel cap of its repeated complementary pair.

**Corollary 23A9ZG3 (complete simple-current `d>=5` chamber).**  Under
the simple-current hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZG2, if `d>=5`, then

```text
T<=N.
```

**Proof.**  Corollary 23A9ZG2 leaves only

```text
d=5,                    t=2,
```

with `k` a plus label and the two minus labels `q,a` distinct.  Put

```text
x=k-q,                    y=k-a.
```

Among the four non-`k` plus positions, the selected label `x` occurs
exactly once.  Indeed, a second copy would put the pair `(q,x)` inside
the selected fourfold complement; its fusion interval has
`t+1=3` entries, contrary to `d=5`.

If `y` occurs at most twice, the exceptional-pair count in
`(P23.11z5zzg2d)` has `h<=3`, and hence

```text
T<=3d+17=32<33=9d-12<=N.
```

It remains that all three other non-`k` plus positions equal `y`.
The selected `q`-oriented pair `{k,x}` contributes five.  There are
three `a`-oriented pairs `{k,y}`.  Each has fourfold complement

```text
{q,x,y,y};
```

pairing `(q,x)` first caps its invariant multiplicity by three.  Of the
other sixteen oriented cuts, the three `q`-oriented pairs `{k,y}` and
the `a`-oriented pair `{k,x}` are inactive because `x!=y`; every
remaining cut has `k` in its fourfold complement and contributes at
most one.  Therefore

```text
T<=5+3*3+16=30<33<=N.
```

This closes the last placement.  QED.

At a four-point cut, parity rules out the only complementary cliques
which could saturate the preceding weighted estimates.

**Corollary 23A9ZG4 (complete simple-current `d>=4` chamber).**  Under
the simple-current hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZG2, if `d=4`, then

```text
T<=N.
```

Consequently the simple-current chamber is closed for every `d>=4`.

**Proof.**  First record the four-point band count.  In half-channel
coordinates the positive even interval `K` has four consecutive
entries.  The output-two, -four, and -six candidate counts are
respectively

```text
10,                         14,                         16.
```

The wall-loss lists in the proof of Proposition 23A9ZF remove at most
one, three, and seven entries, respectively.  At output eight all
sixteen ordered pairs are distance-admissible; the lower wall removes
at most three and the upper wall at most ten.  Thus

```text
m_0(B)>=4,       m_2(B)>=9,       m_4(B)>=11,
m_6(B)>=9,       m_8(B)>=3.                         (P23.11z5zzg2e)
```

The exact simple-current profile `(P23.11z5zzg4)` consequently gives

```text
N>=24 if t=2,                    N>=36 if t>=4.       (P23.11z5zzg2f)
```

We repeatedly use one further consequence of `d=4`: every label `r`
of the selected complementary block `B` satisfies

```text
min(r,k-r)+1>=4.                                  (P23.11z5zzg2g)
```

Indeed, pair `r` with any other label of `B`; the fourfold intersection
has size four and is no larger than the fusion interval containing
`r`.

Suppose first that `k` is a minus label.  Put a graph on the five plus
positions, joining complementary labels whose sum is `k`, and let `g`
be its number of indexed edges.  The `k` orientation contributes at
most `4g`.  Every cut in the other minus orientation has `k` in its
fourfold complement and contributes at most one, so

```text
T<=4g+10.                                          (P23.11z5zzg2h)
```

When `t=2`, the selected complementary pair has labels `2,k-2`.
Neither label can occur among the other three plus positions by
`(P23.11z5zzg2g)`.  Hence `g` is at most the selected edge plus the
three edges among those remaining positions.  Equality would make all
three remaining labels equal to `k/2`.  The selected complementary
block would then be

```text
B={a,k/2,k/2,k/2}.
```

Pairing `(a,k/2)|(k/2,k/2)` gives

```text
d=min(a,k-a)+1.
```

The equality `d=4` would force `min(a,k-a)=3`, impossible because `a`
and `k` are even.  Thus `g<=3`, and `(P23.11z5zzg2h)` gives
`T<=22<=N`.

Let `t>=4`.  If the selected labels `u,k-u` are distinct, the
five-vertex complementary-value graph estimate used in
`(P23.11z5zzg7c)` gives `g<=6`.  If `u=k-u=k/2`, let `n` be the number
of `k/2` positions.  For `n<=4`, the fixed-value clique together with
all remaining complementary components again has at most six edges.
The case `n=5` produces the same block
`{a,k/2,k/2,k/2}` as above and the same parity contradiction.
Therefore `g<=6`, so `T<=34<=36<=N`.

Now suppose that `k` is a plus label.  Denote the selected minus label
by `q`, the other one by `a`, and put

```text
x=k-q,                         y=k-a.
```

There are exactly twelve oriented plus pairs avoiding the indexed
position of `k`; their complements contain `k`, so their total
contribution is at most twelve.  A pair containing `k` is active only
when its other label is `x` in the `q` orientation or `y` in the `a`
orientation.

For `t=2`, `(P23.11z5zzg2g)` shows that the selected copy of `x` is
the only copy among the four non-`k` plus positions.  Let `n` be the
number of copies of `y` among the other three positions.  The selected
pair `{k,x}` contributes four.  Each pair `{k,y}` has a fourfold
complement containing `(q,x)`, whose fusion interval has `t+1=3`
entries, and hence contributes at most three.  Thus

```text
T<=12+4+3n.                                        (P23.11z5zzg2i)
```

If `n<=2`, this is at most `22`.  Suppose that `n=3`, so
`B={a,y,y,y}` with `a+y=k`.  Put `v=min(a,y)`.  The two paired
intervals in `(a,y)|(y,y)` are

```text
I={k-2v,k-2v+2,...,k},       J={0,2,...,2v}.
```

Write `K={L,L+2,L+4,U}`; then `U=L+6` and `k=L+U`.
Disjoint support gives `a!=y`, hence `L>=2`, so both intervals extend
past opposite ends of `K`.  All ten output-two pairs inside
`K times K` are admissible, as are the two extension pairs
`(L,L-2)` and `(U+2,U)` in `I times J`.  Hence
`m_2(B)>=12`.  Together with `m_0(B)=4`, `m_4(B)>=11`, and the
`t=2` profile, this gives

```text
N>=4+12+11=27>25>=T.
```

Finally let `t>=4`.  If `q!=a`, then `x!=y`, so among the four
non-`k` plus positions there are at most four active pairs containing
`k`.  They and the twelve remaining cuts give

```text
T<=4*4+12=28<36<=N.
```

If `q=a`, write `n` for the number of copies of `x=y`.  The two minus
orientations give at most `2n` active pairs containing `k`, whence

```text
T<=8n+12.
```

For `n<=3`, this is at most `36`.  If `n=4`, then
`B={q,x,x,x}` with `q+x=k`, so the preceding two-sided interval
description applies.  In addition to `m_2(B)>=12`, all fourteen
output-four pairs inside `K times K` and the two next-to-end extension
pairs `(L+2,L-2)` and `(U+2,U-2)` are admissible, giving
`m_4(B)>=16`.  Retaining the uniform
output-six and -eight bands yields

```text
N>=4+12+16+9+3=44=T.
```

This closes all placements.  QED.

The first simple-current chamber below four-point overlap need not satisfy
the stronger inequality `T<=N`.  Its fixed-point saturation is instead
paid exactly by indexed equal pairs.

**Corollary 23A9ZG5 (simple-current fixed-point packet at `d=3`).**
Let

```text
k=4r>=8,                         h=k/2,
```

and work under the all-even disjoint-support hypotheses of Proposition
23A9ZF.  Suppose that the two minus labels and five plus labels are

```text
{k,a} minus,                     {h,h,h,h,h} plus,
```

and that the maximal active contribution is

```text
d=m_0(a,h,h,h)=3.
```

Then

```text
a in {2,k-2},                    T=40,
P_eq=30,                         T<N+P_eq.           (P23.11z5zzg2j)
```

Consequently the signed contraction is positive throughout this
unbounded `d=3` family.

**Proof.**  Pair the selected complementary block as
`(a,h)|(h,h)`.  Since

```text
h star_k h={0,2,...,k},
```

the other fusion interval lies inside this one and has
`min(a,k-a)+1` entries.  Therefore

```text
d=min(a,k-a)+1=3,
```

which proves `a in {2,k-2}`.  The condition `k>=8` is necessary here:
at level four it would give `a=h=2`, contrary to disjoint support.

In the minus-`k` orientation every one of the ten indexed plus pairs is
`{h,h}`.  Its complementary block is `{a,h,h,h}` and contributes
`d=3`, for a total of thirty.  In the minus-`a` orientation every pair
is again `{h,h}` and is active because `a` is even and belongs to
`h star_k h`.  Its complement is `{k,h,h,h}` and has multiplicity one:
the simple-current identity gives `k star_k h={h}`, while
`N_(h,h)^h=1` because `h` is even.  Thus

```text
T=10*3+10*1=40.                                    (P23.11z5zzg2k)
```

All ten indexed plus pairs are equal.  Deleting any one of them gives
the `P_eq` summand

```text
m(k,a,h,h,h)=m(a,h,h,h)=3,
```

where the first equality again moves the simple current across the
fixed label `h`.  Hence

```text
P_eq=10*3=30.                                      (P23.11z5zzg2l)
```

Finally the selected triple `{k,h,h}` has
`m_0=m_2=m_4=1`.  The first two band bounds in
`(P23.11z5zzf6)`, together with `m_0(B)=3`, give

```text
N>=3+(3*3-3)+(5*3-9)=15.
```

Therefore `N+P_eq>=45>40=T`.  Proposition 23A9S finishes the signed
contraction inequality.  QED.

The other raw-deficit simple-current orbit at overlap three is a single
level-eight endpoint and is likewise paid by indexed equal pairs.

**Corollary 23A9ZG6 (level-eight equal-minus endpoint packet).**  In
`SU(2)_8`, let `b in {2,6}`, put `x=8-b`, and take

```text
{b,b} minus,                    {8,x,x,4,4} plus.    (P23.11z5zzg2m)
```

Then the maximal active contribution is three and

```text
N=19,                    T=22,                    P_eq=7.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzg2n)
```

In particular `T<N+P_eq`, so the signed contraction is positive.

**Proof.**  Select the triple `{b,8,x}`.  Its complement has label
multiset

```text
B={2,6,4,4}.
```

Pairing `(2,6)|(4,4)` gives the intervals

```text
{4,6,8}                    and                    {0,2,4,6,8}.
```

Directly counting output zero, two, and four gives

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4)(B)=(3,7,9).
```

The simple-current triple has one copy of each of these three outputs,
and no others, because `min(b,8-b)=2`.  Orthogonality therefore gives

```text
N=3+7+9=19.                                        (P23.11z5zzg2o)
```

For one indexed minus orientation, the nonzero cut contributions are as
follows:

```text
b=2:
selected plus-pair type       indexed count       complement rank
{8,6}                              2                     3
{6,6}                              1                     1
{6,4}                              4                     1

b=6:
selected plus-pair type       indexed count       complement rank
{8,2}                              2                     3
{2,4}                              4                     1
{4,4}                              1                     1.
```

Every omitted pair type is inactive or has zero complementary
multiplicity.  Thus each minus orientation contributes eleven, proving
`T=2*11=22`; the displayed rank-three entries also show that the
maximal contribution is three.

Finally the equal-minus term in `P_eq` is three.  Deleting the equal
pair `{x,x}` contributes three, while deleting `{4,4}` contributes one.
Explicitly, for either `b=2` or `6`, the three required level-eight
intersection counts are

```text
m(8,x,x,4,4)=3,
m(b,b,8,4,4)=3,
m(b,b,8,x,x)=1.
```

These are the only indexed equal-plus pairs, so

```text
P_eq=3+3+1=7.                                      (P23.11z5zzg2p)
```

Hence `N+P_eq=26>22=T`, and Proposition 23A9S proves the claim.  QED.

The same wall-loss count also resolves a precise collection of smaller
maximal-cut chambers.  This is only a parameter reduction; the cases not
listed as proved remain open here.

**Corollary 23A9ZH (finite small-cut active-count refinement).**  Work
under the all-even, disjoint-support hypotheses of Proposition 23A9ZF,
with `k>=6`.  Let `A|B` be a maximal active cut, put

```text
d=m_0(B),                    c=number of active oriented cuts,
```

and assume `d>=3`.  Suppose that `A` contains no label `k` and, when `k`
is odd, that

```text
A!={2,k-1,k-1}.
```

Then the contraction is nonnegative whenever

```text
(24-c)d>=43.                                      (P23.11z5zzh1)
```

Equivalently, the following chambers are proved:

```text
d=3:       c<=9,
d=4:       c<=13,
d=5:       c<=15,
d=6:       c<=16,
d=7:       c<=17,
d=8:       c<=18,
d=9,10:    c<=19,
d>=11:     c<=20.                                  (P23.11z5zzh2)
```

Thus, within these nonexceptional hypotheses and with `3<=d<=10`, the
only parameter pairs not covered by this corollary are

```text
d=3,       10<=c<=20;
d=4,       14<=c<=20;
d=5,       16<=c<=20;
d=6,       17<=c<=20;
d=7,       18<=c<=20;
d=8,       19<=c<=20;
d=9,10,    c=20.                                   (P23.11z5zzh3)
```

**Proof.**  The proof of Proposition 23A9ZF uses `d>=11` only in its
final comparison with twenty maximal contributions.  For every `d>=3`,
the same finite triple profile `(P23.11z5zzf4)` and the same uniform
lower- and upper-wall loss count `(P23.11z5zzf6)` give

```text
N>=24d-43.                                         (P23.11z5zzh4)
```

Maximality of `d` gives `T<=cd`.  Condition `(P23.11z5zzh1)` is exactly
`cd<=24d-43`, and Proposition 23A9S then proves the contraction
inequality.  Evaluating this condition for the displayed integral values
of `d` gives `(P23.11z5zzh2)` and its complementary list
`(P23.11z5zzh3)`.  QED.

Retaining one more output channel removes every higher-profile residual
from `d=6` upward.

**Corollary 23A9ZH1 (output-eight bounded-cut refinement).**  Under the
hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZH, assume `d>=5`.  Then

```text
N>=24d-43+m_8(A)(9d-33).                           (P23.11z5zzh5)
```

Consequently `T<=N` whenever `d>=6` and `m_8(A)>=1`.  At `d=5`,
`m_8(A)>=1` closes every chamber with `c<=17`, and `m_8(A)>=2`
closes every chamber.

**Proof.**  Apply the band count `(P23.11z5zzg5)` with `h=4`; it gives

```text
m_8(B)>=9d-33.
```

Adding the product `m_8(A)m_8(B)` to `(P23.11z5zzh4)` proves
`(P23.11z5zzh5)`.  If `d>=6` and `m_8(A)>=1`, its right side is at
least

```text
33d-76>=20d>=T.
```

At `d=5`, the baseline is `77` and the output-eight contribution is
at least `12m_8(A)`.  Thus one copy pays `c<=17`, while two copies give
`N>=101>=20d`.  QED.

The zero output-eight profile itself consists of only two near-wall
families, and their fourfold ranks are too small to support the residual
cut load.

**Corollary 23A9ZH2 (complete nonexceptional closure for `d>=6`).**
Under the hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZH, if `d>=5` and `m_8(A)=0`,
then

```text
T<=N.
```

Consequently every nonexceptional all-even chamber of Corollary 23A9ZH
with `d>=6` is closed.  At `d=5`, the only cases not closed by
Corollaries 23A9ZH--23A9ZH2 have

```text
m_8(A)=1,                    18<=c<=20.             (P23.11z5zzh6)
```

**Proof.**  Order the labels of `A` as `a<=b<=c` and use the variables
`x,y,z,w` of `(P25.29e3)`.  Thus `x,y>0`, `z,w>=0`,
`z<=min(x,y)`, and `x,y,z` have the same parity.  At `j=4`, the
interval in `(P25.29e3)` is nonempty exactly when

```text
x+y+z>=4,                    x+y+w>=4,
x+y+z+w=k>=8,                min(x,y)+z+w>=4.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh7)
```

Indeed, these are precisely the nonautomatic comparisons of the three
lower endpoints with the four upper endpoints.

Here `d>=5` forces `k>=8`.  The first inequality can fail only for
`x=y=z=1`, which gives `A={2,2,2}` and violates plus/minus disjoint
support.  The second follows from `k>=8` and
`z<=min(x,y)`.  Hence `m_8(A)=0` forces

```text
min(x,y)+z+w<=3.                                   (P23.11z5zzh8)
```

If `x,y,z` are even, `(P23.11z5zzh8)` and `z,w` not both zero force

```text
min(x,y)=2,                    z=0, w=1,
```

and therefore

```text
A={2,k-3,k-1}.                                    (P23.11z5zzh9)
```

If they are odd, the possibilities are

```text
min(x,y)=z=1,                    w in {0,1}.
```

The case `w=0` is exactly the excluded triple `{2,k-1,k-1}`; the case
`w=1` gives

```text
A={2,k-2,k-2}.                                    (P23.11z5zzh10)
```

It remains to pay these two families.  A fourfold invariant containing
a label `r` has multiplicity at most `min(r,k-r)+1`.  For
`(P23.11z5zzh9)`, use the label `k-1`, whose cap is two.  If it is a
plus label, then in either minus orientation the four pairs containing
its indexed position contribute at most `d`, while the other six leave
it in the complementary block and contribute at most two.  Thus

```text
T<=2(4d+12)=8d+24.
```

If `k-1` is the selected minus label, its orientation contributes at
most `10d`, while all ten cuts in the other orientation leave it in
the complementary block and contribute at most two.  Thus

```text
T<=10d+20.
```

Both bounds are at most `24d-43<=N` for `d>=5`.

For `(P23.11z5zzh10)`, disjoint support forces the two copies of `k-2`
to be plus labels and the label two to be the selected minus label.
The fourfold cap of `k-2` is three.  Choosing one indexed copy and
counting as above gives

```text
T<=2(4d+18)=8d+36<=24d-43,             d>=5.
```

This proves the first assertion.  Corollary 23A9ZH1 handles
`m_8(A)>=1` for `d>=6`; at `d=5`, it handles `m_8(A)>=2` and
`m_8(A)=1,c<=17`.  Together with the present `m_8(A)=0` case, this
leaves exactly `(P23.11z5zzh6)`.  QED.

The remaining single-output chamber also has a structural payment.

**Corollary 23A9ZH3 (complete nonexceptional closure for `d>=5`).**
Under the hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZH, if

```text
d=5,                         m_8(A)=1,
```

then `T<=N`.  Consequently every nonexceptional all-even chamber of
Corollary 23A9ZH with `d>=5` is closed.

**Proof.**  Order the labels of `A` and retain the variables
`x,y,z,w` of `(P25.29e3)`.  The fourfold-rank caps supplied by its
three labels are

```text
C_a=min(y+z,x+w)+1,
C_b=min(x+z,y+w)+1,
C_c=min(x+y,z+w)+1.                                (P23.11z5zzh11)
```

We claim that one of these caps is at most three.  Suppose instead that
all three are at least four.  Then

```text
y+z,x+w,x+z,y+w,x+y,z+w >=3.                       (P23.11z5zzh12)
```

At output eight, `(P25.29e3)` reads

```text
L_4=max(-4,4-x-y,-min(x,y)),
U_4=min(4,z,w,z+w-4),                              (P23.11z5zzh13)
```

and `m_8(A)=1` says `L_4=U_4`.

First suppose that `x,y,z` are even.  If `z=0`, then
`(P23.11z5zzh12)` gives `x,y>=4` and `w>=3`, so

```text
L_4=-4,                    U_4=min(0,w-4) in {-1,0},
```

a contradiction.  If `z=2`, then `w>=1`; writing
`m=min(x,y)`, the two endpoints are

```text
L_4 = 0  if x=y=2,   -2 if m=2 and (x,y)!=(2,2),
       -4 if m>=4,
U_4 = -1,0,1,2 according as w=1,2,3, or w>=4.
```

Thus equality is possible only for `x=y=2,w=2`.  If `z>=4`, then
`z<=min(x,y)` gives `L_4=-4`, whereas `U_4>=0`.

Now suppose that `x,y,z` are odd.  The case `min(x,y)=1` would force
`z=1`, making one of `x+z,y+z` equal to two, contrary to
`(P23.11z5zzh12)`.  Hence `min(x,y)>=3`.  When the minimum is three,
`L_4=-2` if `x=y=3` and `L_4=-3` otherwise, while
`z+w>=3` gives `U_4>=-1`.  When the minimum is at least five,
`L_4=-4` and again `U_4>=-1`.  Therefore the only possible profile
under the contrary assumption is

```text
x=y=z=w=2.
```

It has `k=x+y+z+w=8` and `A={4,4,4}`.  An active triple contains
labels of both signs, so this profile violates disjoint plus/minus
support.  This proves the cap claim.

Choose a label of `A` with cap at most three.  If it is a plus label,
then in each minus orientation the four cuts containing its indexed
position contribute at most `d`, while the other six contribute at
most three.  Hence

```text
T<=2(4d+6*3)=76.
```

If it is a minus label, the ten cuts in its orientation contribute at
most `d`, and the ten cuts in the other orientation contribute at most
three.  Hence

```text
T<=10d+10*3=80.
```

Corollary 23A9ZH1 gives

```text
N>=24*5-43+(9*5-33)=89,
```

so either estimate proves `T<N`.  Together with Corollaries
23A9ZH1--23A9ZH2, this closes every listed `d=5` case.  QED.

The four-point band retains enough weight to close the next cut size as
well.

**Corollary 23A9ZH4 (complete nonexceptional closure for `d>=4`).**
Under the hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZH, if `d=4`, then

```text
T<=N.
```

Consequently every nonexceptional all-even chamber of Corollary
23A9ZH with `d>=4` is closed.

**Proof.**  Four positive even channels in `K` force `k>=8`.  The
four-point band count `(P23.11z5zzg2e)` gives

```text
N>=4m_0(A)+9m_2(A)+11m_4(A)+9m_6(A)+3m_8(A).
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh14)
```

Order the labels of `A`, retain `x,y,z,w` from `(P25.29e3)`, and put

```text
s=x+y,                         m=min(x,y).
```

Suppose first that `m_8(A)>=1`, equivalently `L_4<=U_4`.  The endpoint
formula then gives

```text
4-s<=z,w,                     m+z+w>=4.             (P23.11z5zzh15)
```

Together with `z,w>=0`, `z+w>=1`, and `k=s+z+w>=8`, these inequalities
show that every defining lower endpoint of `L_3`, after adding one, is
at most every defining upper endpoint of `U_3`.  Hence

```text
m_6(A)>=2.                                          (P23.11z5zzh16)
```

For completeness, the only comparisons not automatic from
nonnegativity are

```text
4-s<=z,w,                    4-s<=z+w-3,
1-m<=z,w,                    1-m<=z+w-3;
```

they are respectively `(P23.11z5zzh15)`, `k>=7`, positivity of `m`,
and `m+z+w>=4`.

Use the three label caps from `(P23.11z5zzh11)`.  If all are at least
three, the six sums occurring there are at least two.  Comparing every
defining term of `L_2`, after adding two, with every defining term of
`U_2` now gives

```text
m_4(A)>=3.                                          (P23.11z5zzh17)
```

Indeed, the new comparisons are `z+w>=2`,
`m+z,m+w>=2`; the comparisons involving `4-s` and
`m+z+w` follow from `(P23.11z5zzh15)`, and the final total-sum
comparison follows from `k>=8`.

If all three caps are at least four, the same comparison at `j=3`,
now adding two to every lower endpoint, gives

```text
m_6(A)>=3.                                          (P23.11z5zzh18)
```

Here the six cap sums are at least three.  Parity gives
`s+z>=5`; it also gives `s+w>=5`, except for
`(x,y,z,w)=(1,1,1,2)`, whose level is five.  Finally,

```text
2(m+z+w)=(m+z)+(m+w)+(z+w)>=9
```

gives `m+z+w>=5`; these are exactly the nonautomatic endpoint
comparisons.

The basic profile `(P23.11z5zzf4)`, strengthened by
`(P23.11z5zzh16)`, and `(P23.11z5zzh14)` now give

```text
N>=65.
```

If some label cap is at most two, choosing that indexed label in the
weighted-cut count gives

```text
T<=56 if it is plus,             T<=60 if it is minus.
```

If every cap is at least three but one equals three,
`(P23.11z5zzh17)` gives `N>=76`, while the corresponding cut bounds
are at most `68` and `70`.  If every cap is at least four, then
`(P23.11z5zzh17)--(P23.11z5zzh18)` give

```text
N>=4+2*9+3*11+3*9+3=85,
```

whereas maximality alone gives `T<=20d=80`.  Thus every
`m_8(A)>=1` case closes.

It remains that `m_8(A)=0`.  Since `k>=8`, the endpoint classification
in the proof of Corollary 23A9ZH2 applies unchanged.  Apart from the
forbidden triple `{2,2,2}` and the separately excluded odd triple
`{2,k-1,k-1}`, it leaves

```text
A={2,k-3,k-1}                 or
A={2,k-2,k-2}.                                      (P23.11z5zzh19)
```

For the first family, `k` is odd and the exact low profile of `A` is

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)(A)=(1,2,2,1).
```

At odd level the four-point band loses zero, one, and at most four
entries at outputs two, four, and six, respectively: the candidate
counts `10,14,16` therefore give

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)(B)>=(4,10,13,12).
```

This is the source of the extra nine beyond the uniform bound:

```text
N>=4+2*10+2*13+12=62.                              (P23.11z5zzh20)
```

The label `k-1` has cap two.  The weighted-cut bounds are `56` if it is
plus and `60` if it is minus, so this family closes.

For the second family, `k` is even and

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)(A)=(1,3,2,1).
```

The uniform four-point band gives

```text
N>=4+3*9+2*11+9=62.                                (P23.11z5zzh21)
```

Disjoint support makes the label two the selected minus label and the
two indexed copies of `e=k-2` plus labels.  In the label-two
orientation, the selected pair `{e,e}` contributes four and every
other plus pair leaves at least one copy of `e` in its complement, so
the remaining nine cuts contribute at most three each.  In the other
minus orientation every complement contains the label two and hence
has rank at most three.  Therefore

```text
T<=4+9*3+10*3=61<62<=N.
```

This exhausts `(P23.11z5zzh19)` and proves the corollary.  QED.

At overlap three, the exact parity of the level improves the first five
bands and removes all sparse active-cut layers.

**Corollary 23A9ZH5 (nonexceptional `d=3` band reduction).**  Under the
hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZH, assume `d=3`.  If `k` is even and
`k>=8`, then

```text
N>=30+m_8(A),                                      (P23.11z5zzh22)
```

so `T<=N` whenever `c<=10`.  If `k=7`, then `N>=38`, so `T<=N`
whenever `c<=12`.  If `k` is odd and `k>=9`, then

```text
N>=39+3m_8(A),                                     (P23.11z5zzh23)
```

so `T<=N` whenever

```text
c<=13+m_8(A).                                      (P23.11z5zzh24)
```

Thus the only nonexceptional `d=3` chambers left by this reduction have

```text
k even:                 c>=11;
k=7:                    c>=13;
k odd, k>=9:            c>=14+m_8(A).              (P23.11z5zzh25)
```

The level-six chamber retains the earlier bound `N>=29` and `c<=9`
from Corollary 23A9ZH.

**Proof.**  Divide the three positive even channels of `K` by two and
write

```text
K={s,s+1,s+2},                    s>=1.
```

For output `2h`, a pair `(p,q)` in `K times K` is admissible exactly
when

```text
|p-q|<=h,                    h<=p+q<=k-h.           (P23.11z5zzh26)
```

Suppose first that `k` is even and `k>=8`.  At `h=1`, the three
diagonal and four adjacent pairs are candidates; only the top diagonal
can cross the upper wall.  At `h=2`, all nine pairs are
distance-admissible, and the first three sum-layers, of cardinalities
`1,2,3`, lie below the upper wall.  At `h=3`, if `s=1` only the
bottom diagonal fails the lower wall, while the next two sum-layers
already supply five admissible pairs; if `s>=2`, the first two
sum-layers, of cardinalities `1,2`, are admissible.  Finally, at `h=4`
the sum interval

```text
[2s,2s+4] intersection [4,k-4]
```

is nonempty because `2s<=k-4`.  Hence

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6,m_8)(B)>=(3,6,6,3,1).             (P23.11z5zzh27)
```

Combining this with `(P23.11z5zzf4)` proves

```text
N>=3+2*6+2*6+3+m_8(A)=30+m_8(A).
```

Now let `k` be odd.  The top even half-channel is `(k-1)/2`.  No
output-two pair crosses the upper wall, so all seven candidates survive.
At output four only the top diagonal can be lost, leaving eight.  At
output six the bottom wall can remove one pair and the top wall at most
three; this leaves five when `k=7` and at least six for `k>=9`.  For
odd `k>=9` and `s>=2`, the first two sum-layers at output eight survive
and have cardinalities `1+2=3`; when `s=1`, the sum-four layer alone
has cardinality three and survives.  Therefore

```text
k=7:       (m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)(B)>=(3,7,8,5);
k>=9:      (m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6,m_8)(B)>=(3,7,8,6,3).
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh28)
```

The basic triple profile gives respectively `N>=38` and
`N>=39+3m_8(A)`.  Finally maximality yields `T<=3c`, so the displayed
active-count comparisons prove all assertions.  QED.

The zero-output-eight part of the residual has only the two endpoint
families already visible at larger overlap.  One of them carries its own
indexed equal-pair payment.

**Corollary 23A9ZH6 (nonexceptional `d=3`, `m_8=0` reduction).**  Under
the hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZH5, suppose `k>=8` and `m_8(A)=0`.
Then exactly one of the following holds:

```text
k odd:       A={2,k-3,k-1};
k even:      A={2,k-2,k-2}.                         (P23.11z5zzh29)
```

For the odd family, `k>=9` and

```text
N>=39.                                                (P23.11z5zzh30)
```

For the even family, `k>=8` and

```text
N+P_eq>=42.                                          (P23.11z5zzh31)
```

Consequently the only cases in these two families not closed by
maximality have

```text
{2,k-3,k-1}:       c>=14;
{2,k-2,k-2}:       c>=15.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh32)
```

**Proof.**  The endpoint argument in Corollary 23A9ZH2 uses only
`k>=8`; it gives the two families in `(P23.11z5zzh29)`, besides the
forbidden `{2,2,2}` and the separately excluded exceptional triple.

For the odd family the exact triple profile is

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)(A)=(1,2,2,1).
```

The odd three-point bands `(P23.11z5zzh28)` therefore give

```text
N>=3+2*7+2*8+6=39.
```

For the even family, put `e=k-2`.  Its exact profile is

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)(A)=(1,3,2,1).
```

At `k>=8`, `(P23.11z5zzh27)` gives

```text
N>=3+3*6+2*6+3=36.
```

Disjoint support forces the two indexed copies of `e` to be plus labels
and the label two to be the selected minus label.  Deleting the equal
pair `{e,e}` leaves `V_2 tensor B`, so its indexed term in `P_eq` is
exactly `m_2(B)>=6`.  Hence `N+P_eq>=42`.

Finally `T<=3c`; comparison with the two displayed supplies proves
`(P23.11z5zzh32)`.  QED.

The low levels have no further symbolic families: overlap three is empty
through level six, while level seven has only four support multisets.

**Corollary 23A9ZH7 (complete all-even `d=3` closure through level
seven).**  Under the all-even disjoint-support hypotheses of Proposition
23A9ZF, no maximal active contribution has `d=3` when `k<=6`.  At
`k=7`, exactly four signed label multisets have maximal contribution
three:

```text
minus          plus                 N       P_eq       T       c
{2,2}          {4,4,4,6,6}         28        21       36      20
{2,2}          {4,4,4,4,6}         43        37       48      20
{2,2}          {4,4,4,4,4}         66        90       60      20
{2,6}          {4,4,4,4,4}         49        60       50      20.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh33)
```

The first row contains the excluded exceptional triple `{2,6,6}` and
is already closed by Corollary 23A9ZM.  Each of the other three rows
satisfies `T<N+P_eq`.  Consequently every all-even `d=3` contraction
through level seven is nonnegative.

**Proof.**  For `k<=3` there is only one positive even label, so
disjoint support is impossible.  At levels four and five, any fourfold
block of rank three would have to use only labels whose individual cap
`min(r,k-r)+1` is at least three.  This leaves only label two, again
contradicting disjoint support.

At level six the positive even fusion intervals are

```text
2 star 2={0,2,4},       2 star 4={2,4,6},
2 star 6={4},           4 star 4={0,2,4},
4 star 6={2},           6 star 6={0}.               (P23.11z5zzh34)
```

Pair a fourfold complement so that its unique minus position is paired
with one plus position.  Disjoint support makes the two labels distinct.
The only resulting interval of size three is
`2 star 4={2,4,6}`.  If the minus label is two, the other plus pair uses
only labels four and six, whose interval meets `{2,4,6}` in at most two
entries.  If the minus label is four, the other plus pair uses only two
and six and again meets it in at most two entries.  Every pair involving
the minus label six has size one.  Thus every fourfold complement has
rank at most two, proving emptiness at `k=6`.

At level seven the corresponding fusion table is

```text
2 star 2={0,2,4},       2 star 4={2,4,6},
2 star 6={4,6},         4 star 4={0,2,4,6},
4 star 6={2,4},         6 star 6={0,2}.              (P23.11z5zzh35)
```

The same pairing argument shows that a rank-three complement must use
`2 star 4={2,4,6}` for the pair containing its minus position.  The
other plus pair must have an interval containing all three channels;
by `(P23.11z5zzh35)` it is either `{2,4}` or `{4,4}`.  Disjoint support
rules out the former and also rules out putting label four at the minus
position.  Hence every rank-three complement is exactly

```text
{2,4,4,4}.                                         (P23.11z5zzh36)
```

There are twenty-one disjoint signed support multisets at level seven:
an equal-minus choice leaves two plus-label types and hence six
five-element multisets, giving `3*6=18`; a distinct-minus choice leaves
one plus-label type, giving three more.  Condition
`(P23.11z5zzh36)` reduces these twenty-one possibilities as follows.
If both minus labels are two, the selected plus pair can be
`{6,6}`, `{4,6}`, or `{4,4}`, producing the first three rows of
`(P23.11z5zzh33)`.  If the other minus label is six, all five plus
labels must be four, producing the last row.  No other minus support is
compatible with `(P23.11z5zzh36)`.

Finally, successive multiplication using the six explicit intervals in
`(P23.11z5zzh35)`, with indexed equal pairs retained as in Proposition
23A9S, gives the four displayed values of `N,P_eq,T,c`.  Each row has a
rank-three cut, and the same table bounds every cut by three, so the
maximal contribution is exactly three.  The first row is Corollary
23A9ZM; direct comparison closes the remaining three.  QED.

At level eight raw domination fails in the simple-current branch, but the
full all-even support classification is still small enough for an exact
fusion-table proof.

**Corollary 23A9ZH8 (complete all-even `d=3` closure at level eight).**
In `SU(2)_8`, under the all-even disjoint-support hypotheses of
Proposition 23A9ZF, exactly the following sixteen signed label multisets
have maximal contribution three:

```text
minus       plus                    N      P_eq      T      c
{2,2}       {4^2,6^2,8}            19       7       22     14
{2,2}       {4^2,6^3}              49      31       44     20
{2,2}       {4^3,6,8}              23       9       18     14
{2,2}       {4^3,6^2}              61      36       54     20
{2,2}       {4^4,6}                75      53       60     20
{2,2}       {4^5}                  93     103       60     20
{2,6}       {4^5}                  93      90       60     20
{2,8}       {4^4,6}                29      18       28     16
{2,8}       {4^5}                  35      30       40     20
{6,6}       {4^5}                  93     103       60     20
{6,6}       {2,4^3,8}              23       9       18     14
{6,6}       {2,4^4}                75      53       60     20
{6,6}       {2^2,4^2,8}            19       7       22     14
{6,6}       {2^2,4^3}              61      36       48     18
{6,8}       {4^5}                  35      30       40     20
{6,8}       {2,4^4}                29      18       28     16.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh37)
```

The first and thirteenth rows are Corollary 23A9ZG6.  The ninth and
fifteenth rows are Corollary 23A9ZG5.  Every other row satisfies
`T<=N`.  Hence every all-even `d=3` contraction at level eight is
nonnegative.

**Proof.**  A label in a rank-three fourfold block has cap at least
three, so every label of a maximal complement `B` lies in `{2,4,6}`.
The level-eight even fusion intervals are

```text
2 star 2={0,2,4},       2 star 4={2,4,6},
2 star 6={4,6,8},       4 star 4={0,2,4,6,8},
4 star 6={2,4,6},       6 star 6={0,2,4}.            (P23.11z5zzh38)
```

The reflection `2<->6` reduces the interval intersections to four
disjoint signed complement forms:

```text
minus position in B       three plus positions in B
2                         {4,4,4}
6                         {4,4,4}
2                         {4,4,6}
6                         {2,4,4}.                   (P23.11z5zzh39)
```

Indeed, the rank-three unsigned multisets obtained from
`(P23.11z5zzh38)` are

```text
{2^4}, {6^4}, {2^2,4^2}, {2^2,6^2},
{2,4^3}, {2,4^2,6}, {4^3,6}, {4^2,6^2}.
```

Every form without a singleton forces the minus label to occur also at
a plus position and violates disjoint support; the two constant forms do
so as well.  The four surviving singleton placements are precisely
`(P23.11z5zzh39)`.

For each of these four complements, choose the other minus label and an
invariant selected plus pair, again using `(P23.11z5zzh38)`.
Disjoint support leaves exactly the sixteen global multisets in
`(P23.11z5zzh37)`.  This is an interval enumeration of four complement
forms, not a search over unrestricted words.  Successive multiplication
by the six displayed fusion intervals gives the listed values of
`N,T,c`; retaining the equal-minus term and every indexed equal-plus
deletion gives the listed `P_eq`.  The same intervals show that each row
has a rank-three cut and no cut of larger rank.  The comparisons stated
after the table now prove the corollary.  QED.

At level nine the cap reduction gives six signed complement forms.  Raw
domination is already strict in every one of them.

**Corollary 23A9ZH9 (complete all-even `d=3` closure at level nine).**
In `SU(2)_9`, under the all-even disjoint-support hypotheses of
Proposition 23A9ZF, every maximal-cut chamber with `d=3` satisfies

```text
T<N.
```

Hence every all-even `d=3` contraction at level nine is nonnegative.

**Proof.**  Every label in a rank-three complement has cap at least
three, so its even labels lie in `{2,4,6}`.  Their fusion intervals are

```text
2 star 2={0,2,4},         2 star 4={2,4,6},
2 star 6={4,6,8},         4 star 4={0,2,4,6,8},
4 star 6={2,4,6,8},       6 star 6={0,2,4,6}.       (P23.11z5zzh40)
```

The unsigned four-label multisets of rank three are

```text
{2^4}, {2^2,4^2}, {2^2,6^2},
{2,4^3}, {2,4^2,6}, {2,4,6^2}, {4,6^3}.
```

Disjoint support removes the three forms without a singleton and leaves
exactly six signed complements:

```text
2|{4,4,4},       2|{4,4,6},       2|{4,6,6},
4|{2,6,6},       4|{6,6,6},       6|{2,4,4}.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh41)
```

For each form, choose the other minus label and an invariant selected
plus pair using `(P23.11z5zzh40)`.  After disjoint support there are
thirty distinct global signed multisets.  Some admit more than one
maximal complement form; counting those incidences separately gives the
following exact audit:

```text
signed B       compatible rows       min(N-T)       attaining row
2|{4,4,4}             8                  21          {2,2}|{4^3,8^2}
2|{4,4,6}             7                  12          {2,2}|{4^2,6,8^2}
2|{4,6,6}             7                   5          {2,2}|{4,6^2,8^2}
4|{2,6,6}             5                  16          {4,8}|{2^2,6^3}
4|{6,6,6}             8                   9          {2,4}|{6^3,8^2}
6|{2,4,4}             3                  11          {6,6}|{2^2,4^2,8}.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh42)
```

Every entry is obtained by successive multiplication with the six
explicit intervals in `(P23.11z5zzh40)` and the definitions of `N` and
`T` in Proposition 23A9S.  Thus the table is a finite interval
calculation over the six symbolic forms `(P23.11z5zzh41)`.  Its minimum
margin is five, proving `T<N` in all thirty rows.  QED.

The same complement-form audit remains finite at level ten.  Three forms
are sharp up to one unit, but none needs an equal-pair payment.

**Corollary 23A9ZH10 (complete all-even `d=3` closure at level ten).**
In `SU(2)_10`, under the all-even disjoint-support hypotheses of
Proposition 23A9ZF, every maximal-cut chamber with `d=3` satisfies

```text
T<N.
```

**Proof.**  The rank cap restricts a maximal complement to
`{2,4,6,8}`.  The ten even fusion intervals are

```text
2 star 2={0,2,4},          2 star 4={2,4,6},
2 star 6={4,6,8},          2 star 8={6,8,10},
4 star 4={0,2,4,6,8},      4 star 6={2,4,6,8,10},
4 star 8={4,6,8},          6 star 6={0,2,4,6,8},
6 star 8={2,4,6},          8 star 8={0,2,4}.        (P23.11z5zzh43)
```

Intersecting these intervals and imposing the singleton condition from
disjoint support gives sixteen signed rank-three complement forms.
Choosing the other minus label and an invariant selected pair produces
eighty-nine distinct global signed multisets.  The exact interval audit,
with multiple maximal-complement incidences counted separately, is

```text
signed B       compatible rows       min(N-T)       attaining row
2|{4,4,4}             12                 12          {2,2}|{4^3,8,10}
2|{4,4,6}             10                 11          {2,2}|{4^2,6,8,10}
2|{4,6,6}             10                 12          {2,2}|{4,6^2,8,10}
2|{4,6,8}              7                  1          {2,2}|{4,6,8^2,10}
2|{6,6,6}             11                 11          {2,2}|{6^3,8,10}
4|{2,6,6}              7                  8          {4,8}|{2^2,6^2,10}
4|{2,6,8}              7                  3          {4,10}|{2^2,6,8^2}
4|{6,6,8}              7                  9          {2,4}|{6^2,8^2,10}
6|{2,4,4}              6                  9          {6,8}|{2^2,4^2,10}
6|{2,4,8}              5                  1          {6,10}|{2^2,4,8^2}
6|{4,4,8}              7                  8          {2,6}|{4^2,8^2,10}
8|{2,4,6}              6                  1          {8,8}|{2^2,4,6,10}
8|{4,4,4}             11                 11          {8,8}|{2,4^3,10}
8|{4,4,6}              9                 12          {8,8}|{2,4^2,6,10}
8|{4,6,6}              9                 11          {8,8}|{2,4,6^2,10}
8|{6,6,6}             11                 12          {8,8}|{2,6^3,10}.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh44)
```

As at level nine, every entry follows by successive multiplication using
the explicit intervals `(P23.11z5zzh43)`.  The cap and singleton
conditions prove that the sixteen rows of `(P23.11z5zzh44)` exhaust the
symbolic complement forms; the selected-pair enumeration within them is
finite and gives the stated eighty-nine global rows.  Since every class
has positive minimum margin, `T<N` throughout.  QED.

**Referee status.**  Lemma 23A9ZH11 and Corollaries
23A9ZH12--23A9ZH14 below have passed a cutoff-free symbolic audit.
In particular, the load-bearing endpoint classifications in
`(P23.11z5zzh65)--(P23.11z5zzh66)` are derived from their normalized
integer offsets.  Bounded computations used to discover those rows are
evidence only.

The rank-three fourfold block has a stronger profile than the coarse
internal-band estimate used in Corollary 23A9ZH5.  The missing units
come either from a strict interval extension or, at even level, from
the exact reflected-pair profile.

**Lemma 23A9ZH11 (uniform rank-three even fourfold profile).**  Let
`B` consist of one minus label and three plus labels, all positive and
even, with disjoint plus/minus support, and suppose

```text
m_0(B)=3.
```

If `k` is odd and `k>=11`, then

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6,m_8,m_10)(B)
 >=(3,8,10,9,6,3).                                (P23.11z5zzh45)
```

For odd `k>=13`, one also has `m_12(B)>=1`.

If `k` is even and `k>=12`, then

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6,m_8,m_10,m_12)(B)
 >=(3,7,9,8,6,3,1).                               (P23.11z5zzh46)
```

**Proof.**  Pair the minus label `a` with one plus label `p`, pair the
other two plus labels `r,s`, and denote the two finite fusion intervals
by `I,J`.  Divide their even channels by two.  Their intersection is

```text
K={u,u+1,u+2},                    u>=1.             (P23.11z5zzh47)
```

The inequality `u>=1` follows because zero in the first interval would
give `a=p`, contrary to support disjointness.  An output `2h` receives
one contribution from an ordered pair `(x,y)` precisely when

```text
|x-y|<=h,                    h<=x+y<=k-h.           (P23.11z5zzh48)
```

For even labels `b,c`, the half-channel interval is

```text
F(b,c)=[|b-c|/2,(k-|k-b-c|)/2] intersection Z,
|F(b,c)|=min(b,c,k-b,k-c)+1.                       (P23.11z5zzh49)
```

Equality of the lower and upper endpoints gives

```text
F(b,c)=F(v,w)
 iff {b,c}={v,w} or {b,c}={k-v,k-w}.               (P23.11z5zzh50)
```

Suppose first that `I=J`.  The first alternative in
`(P23.11z5zzh50)` contradicts support disjointness.  The second is
impossible for odd `k`, because the reflection of an even label is then
odd.  At even level, equality and `|K|=3` give, up to permutation,

```text
B={2,b,k-2,k-b},                    4<=b<=k-4,
K={b/2-1,b/2,b/2+1}.                              (P23.11z5zzh50a)
```

The nine pairs in `K times K`, inserted in `(P23.11z5zzh48)`, give

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6,m_8,m_10,m_12)(B)
 >=(3,7,9,8,6,3,1).                               (P23.11z5zzh50b)
```

Now suppose `I!=J`.  Both intervals contain `K`.  At odd level at
least one contains an adjacent extra point.  At even level every
interval has odd cardinality by `(P23.11z5zzh49)`; since the two
intervals are unequal, one contains at least two adjacent extra points.
Thus `I times J` contains `K times E`, where `E` is `K` plus one
adjacent point in the odd case and two nearest adjacent points in the
even case.

Here is the complete endpoint count.  Write `k=2q+epsilon`,
`epsilon in {0,1}`, put

```text
x=u+i,                    y=u+j,
d=u,                      e=q-u-2.
```

Condition `(P23.11z5zzh48)` becomes

```text
|i-j|<=h,              h-2d<=i+j<=2e+4+epsilon-h.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh50c)
```

Increasing `d` or `e` only relaxes one of these two endpoint
inequalities.  It therefore suffices to count the displayed finite
offset rectangles at their boundary values.  Listing their twelve or
fifteen pairs gives

```text
parity     offsets j of E       endpoint range       h=0,1,2,3,4,5,6

odd        -1,0,1,2             k=11                 3,8,10,9,6,3,0
odd        -1,0,1,2             k>=13                3,8,10,9,6,3,1
odd         0,1,2,3             k=11                 3,8,11,11,9,5,0
odd         0,1,2,3             k>=13                3,8,11,11,9,6,3

even        0,1,2,3,4           all k>=12            3,8,12,13,12,8,3
even       -1,0,1,2,3           all k>=12            3,9,12,12,9,6,2
even       -2,-1,0,1,2          all k>=12            3,7,9,8,6,3,1.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh50d)
```

In the even rows the feasibility conditions are respectively

```text
d>=max(1,ell),              e>=2-ell,
d+e>=4,                    ell=0,1,2,
```

where `ell` is the number of extra points below `K`; these conditions
are exactly the availability of the listed offsets.  The odd rows have
the analogous one-extra conditions.  Hence no unavailable offset was
used in the table.  Taking the componentwise minima of
`(P23.11z5zzh50b)--(P23.11z5zzh50d)` proves
`(P23.11z5zzh45)--(P23.11z5zzh46)`.  QED.

The stronger profile closes every unbounded nonexceptional rank-three
chamber.  The only sharp row is paid by its repeated near-wall pair.

**Corollary 23A9ZH12 (complete nonexceptional `d=3` closure for
`k>=11`).**  Under the hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZH5, suppose

```text
d=3,                    k>=11.
```

Then `T<=N+P_eq`.

**Proof.**  Put

```text
a_j=m_(2j)(A).
```

The tetrahedral formula `(P25.29e3)` writes `a_j=U_j-L_j+1`, where
`L_j` is a maximum of affine functions and `U_j` is a minimum of
affine functions.  Hence the successive differences of `a_j` are
nonincreasing until the profile vanishes.  In particular,

```text
a_3>=3  ==> a_2>=3.                                (P23.11z5zzh51)
```

Using the componentwise weaker of
`(P23.11z5zzh45)--(P23.11z5zzh46)`, this gives

```text
N>=3+2*7+3*9+3*8=68>60>=T.                         (P23.11z5zzh52)
```

If `a_3=2` and `a_4>=2`, then

```text
N>=3+2*7+2*9+2*8+2*6=63>60.                       (P23.11z5zzh53)
```

It remains that `a_3<=2,a_4<=1`.  If `a_4=1`, concavity and the
basic bound `a_2>=2` force `a_3=2`: if `a_3=1`, the drop from
`a_2` to `a_3` is at most minus one, so the next drop is no larger
and `a_4=0`.  For `(a_3,a_4)=(2,1)`, the endpoint interval
`(P25.29e3)` gives

```text
k even:       a_2>=3;
k odd:        a_2>=2.                               (P23.11z5zzh54)
```

For the even assertion, `x,y,z,w` have one common parity.  If that
parity were odd, then every defining term of both `L_3` and `U_3`
would be odd, so `U_3-L_3` could not equal one.  Hence the common
parity is even.  Every defining term of `L_2,U_2` is then even, so
`a_2=U_2-L_2+1` cannot equal two.  The basic profile gives
`a_2>=2`, and therefore `a_2>=3`.  At odd level concavity already
gives `a_2>=2`.
Thus `(P23.11z5zzh45)--(P23.11z5zzh46)` yield respectively

```text
k even:       N>=3+2*7+3*9+2*8+6=66;
k odd:        N>=3+2*8+2*10+2*9+6=63.              (P23.11z5zzh54a)
```

Both are raw.  Finally let `a_4=0`.  Corollary 23A9ZH6 gives exactly

```text
k odd:       A={2,k-3,k-1};
k even:      A={2,k-2,k-2}.
```

For the odd row,
`(P23.11z5zzh45)` gives

```text
N>=3+2*8+2*10+9=48.                                (P23.11z5zzh55)
```

Let `H=k-1`.  If `H` is a plus label, at most eight indexed cuts remove
its position; every other active complement contains `H` and has rank
at most two.  Thus `T<=2*20+8=48`.  If `H` is the minus label, the
other orientation has total contribution at most twenty.  Its own
orientation cannot have all ten pairs active: the selected plus labels
are `2,k-3`, and a third label fusing with `H` against both would have
to equal `k-3`, but two copies of `k-3` do not fuse to `H` for
`k>=11`.  Hence

```text
T<=9*3+10*2=47<48.                                 (P23.11z5zzh56)
```

For the even `(1,0)` row, put `e=k-2`.  Its exact profile and
`(P23.11z5zzh46)` give

```text
N>=3+3*7+2*9+8=50.                                 (P23.11z5zzh57)
```

Disjoint support makes the label two the selected minus label and the
two copies of `e` plus labels.  Deleting their indexed equal pair
contributes `m_2(B)>=7` to `P_eq`.
Moreover not all twenty cuts can be active.  If they were, activity in
the minus-two orientation would force every plus label to lie in

```text
{k-4,k-2,k},
```

with at most one copy of `k` and with `k-4` and `k` not both present.
Activity of the pair `{e,e}` in the other orientation would force the
other minus label into `{2,4}`.  If the plus support is contained in
`{e,k}`, at least four copies of `e` occur; delete two of them in the
other-minus orientation.  The resulting complement is `{2,e,e,e}` or
`{2,e,e,k}`, both of multiplicity zero.  Otherwise put `f=k-4`; the
plus support is contained in `{f,e}`.  Write `n` for the number of
copies of `e`, so `2<=n<=5`.  For `n=4,5`, select two copies of `e`;
for `n=2,3`, select two copies of `f`.  The resulting fourfold
complement is

```text
n=3,5:       {2,e,e,e};
n=2,4:       {2,f,e,e},
```

and both have multiplicity zero, since

```text
e star_k e={0,2,4},
2 star_k e={k-4,k-2,k},
2 star_k f={k-6,k-4,k-2},
```

with `k>=12`; also `m(2,e,e,k)=m(2,2,e)=0`.  This contradicts
twenty-fold activity.  Consequently
`c<=19`, and

```text
T<=57<=N+P_eq.                                     (P23.11z5zzh58)
```

This exhausts the nonexceptional maximal triples.  QED.

The same profile also closes the simple-current rank-three branch.  At
the two-channel endpoint the complete demand is smaller than the first
three neighboring bands.

**Corollary 23A9ZH13 (complete simple-current `d=3` closure for
`k>=12`).**  Under the all-even disjoint-support hypotheses of
Proposition 23A9ZF, suppose a maximal active triple is

```text
A={k,u,k-u},                    d=3.
```

Then `T<=N+P_eq`.

**Proof.**  Put `t=min(u,k-u)`.  The exact profile is

```text
m_(2j)(A)=1                    for 0<=j<=t.
```
