
If `t>=4`, Lemma 23A9ZH11 gives

```text
N>=3+7+9+8+6=33.                                  (P23.11z5zzh59)
```

Suppose first that `k` is a minus label, and let `g` be the number of
active plus pairs in that orientation.  They form the complementary
value graph `x+y=k`.  Every cut in the other orientation has rank at
most one, so

```text
T<=20+2g.
```

If `g<=6`, this is at most thirty-two.  A five-vertex complementary
graph with at least seven edges is necessarily the fixed clique `K_5`;
thus all five plus labels equal `k/2`.  The rank-three condition then
forces the other minus label to be `2` or `k-2`, and this is exactly
Corollary 23A9ZG5.

If instead `k` is a plus label, let `h` count the active cuts, in both
orientations, whose pair contains its indexed position.  Again
`T<=20+2h`.  The inequality `h>=7` forces equal minus labels and four
copies of their common complementary label.  The selected complement
then has rank `t+1`, so rank three would force `t=2`.  Hence `h<=6`
when `t>=4`, and this branch is raw.

It remains that `t=2`.  Put `e=k-2`.  The first three bands in
Lemma 23A9ZH11 give

```text
N=m_0(B)+m_2(B)+m_4(B)>=3+7+9=19.                  (P23.11z5zzh60)
```

We record the endpoint allocation special to this row.  Write
`Jx=k-x` and `F(x,y)=x star_k y`.  Suppose first that `k` is a minus
label, let `a` be the other minus label, and write the residual plus
labels as `R={r_1,r_2,r_3}`.  Put `n_2,n_e` for the multiplicities of
`2,e` in `R`.  The number `g` of distinguished cuts is

```text
g=(n_2+1)(n_e+1)+w,                                (P23.11z5zzh61)
```

where `w` counts complementary pairs among the other residual
positions.  With three residual vertices this is at most four except
possibly for `(n_2,n_e)=(2,1)` or `(1,2)`, when it is six.  In the
first exceptional multiplicity pattern,

```text
3=m(a,2,2,e)=|F(a,2) intersection F(2,e)|
```

forces equality of the two three-entry intervals.  The equality
criterion `(P23.11z5zzh50)` gives `a=e`, contrary to support
disjointness.  Reflection gives `a=2` in the other pattern.  Hence

```text
g<=4.                                               (P23.11z5zzh62)
```

Every cut in the other orientation has rank at most one.  Its exact
number is

```text
ell=delta+C+L+H,

delta=[a in F(2,e)][m(k,R)>0],
C=sum_i [r_i in {2,4}][a in F(r_j,r_l)],
L=sum_i [a in F(2,r_i)][2 in F(r_j,r_l)],
H=sum_i [a in F(e,r_i)][e in F(r_j,r_l)],          (P23.11z5zzh63)
```

where `{i,j,l}={1,2,3}` cyclically.  Disjoint support reduces the last
two indicator types to

```text
L_i=[|r_i-a|=2][|r_j-r_l|<=2],
H_i=[|r_i-Ja|<=2][|r_j+r_l-k|<=2].                 (P23.11z5zzh64)
```

Clearly `C<=3`.  We first solve the last two sums without a level
cutoff.  Put

```text
k=2h,                 alpha=a-h,                 s_i=r_i-h.
```

All four quantities `alpha,s_1,s_2,s_3` are even when `k=0 mod 4`
and odd when `k=2 mod 4`.  Formula `(P23.11z5zzh64)` becomes

```text
L_i=[|s_i-alpha|=2][|s_j-s_l|<=2],
H_i=[|s_i+alpha|<=2][|s_j+s_l|<=2].               (P23.11z5zzh64a)
```

If `L+H>=4`, inclusion-exclusion among the three indices gives an
index belonging to both sums.  Its two first-factor conditions force
`|alpha|<=2`.  When `alpha=0`, its two second-factor conditions force
the other two offsets to have both sum and difference of absolute
value at most two.  Disjoint support excludes offset zero, and the
remaining offsets `+-2` cannot satisfy both conditions.  Thus
`alpha!=0`.

If `alpha=-2`, a common index has offset zero.  The same two
second-factor conditions put the other offsets in `{-2,0,2}`;
disjoint support excludes `-2`.  Directly in `(P23.11z5zzh64a)`,
the triples with at least four indicators are then
`(0,0,0)` and `(0,0,2)`.  Reflection gives the two
`alpha=2` rows.  If `alpha=-1`, the common index has offset one,
and the two other odd offsets must both equal one; reflection handles
`alpha=1`.  Consequently `L+H>=4` is possible only in the following
rows:

```text
k=0 mod 4:
(a;R)=(h-2;h,h,h),       (h-2;h,h,h+2),
      (h+2;h-2,h,h),     (h+2;h,h,h);

k=2 mod 4:
(a;R)=(h-1;(h+1)^3),     (h+1;(h-1)^3).           (P23.11z5zzh65)
```

Pairing the repeated central labels shows this rank directly.  The
relevant interval intersections are, up to reflection,

```text
[2,2h-2]_2,
[4,2h]_2 intersection [0,2h]_2,
[2,2h]_2 intersection [0,2h-2]_2,
```

where `[u,v]_2={u,u+2,...,v}`; each has `h-1` entries.  Hence every
row has `m(a,R)=h-1>=5`, contradicting the selected rank three.
Thus `L+H<=3`.  If `delta=0`, this gives `ell<=6`.  If
`delta=1`, then `F(2,e)={k-4,e,k}`.  Support excludes `a=e`, while
`a=k` would give `m(a,R)=m(k,R)<=1`; hence `a=k-4`.

It remains to solve `C+L+H>=4` under this last value of `a`.
Here

```text
L_i>0 ==> r_i in {k-6,e},
H_i>0 ==> r_i in {2,4,6}.                          (P23.11z5zzh65a)
```

Two `L`-indices cannot occur.  If their first labels are `k-6,e`,
their remaining label is the forbidden midpoint `a`.  If both are
`k-6`, the remaining label is `k-8` or `k-6` and the selected rank is
five.  If both are `e`, the remaining label is `e` and the selected
rank is two.  Hence `L<=1`.

Likewise `H<=2`.  Three `H`-indices would put all residual labels in
`{2,4,6}` and every residual pair would have sum at least `k-2`.
This is impossible for `k>=16`; at `k=14` it forces `R={6,6,6}`,
and at `k=12` it forces `R={4,6,6}` or `{6,6,6}`.  Each displayed
low-level row has selected rank five.

If `C=3`, all three residual labels lie in `{2,4}`.  The three
opposite pairs can reach `a=k-4` only at `k=12,R={4,4,4}`, where
`C+L+H=3`.  Thus a row with total at least four has `C<=2`.
For `C=2`, the two indexed low labels and the possibilities for the
third label are

```text
low pair       third label(s)          selected ranks
{2,2}          k-6,e                   2,2
{2,4}          k-6,e                   3,3
{4,4}          k-8,k-6,e               3,4,3.       (P23.11z5zzh65b)
```

The rank-three row `{4,4,k-8}` has `C+L+H=2`.  The remaining
rank-three rows with total at least four are exactly

```text
R={2,4,k-6},       {2,4,e},       {4,4,e},
(C,L,H)=(2,1,1),  (2,1,2),       (2,1,2),          (P23.11z5zzh66)
```

with the level-twelve collision `R={4,4,4}` having total three.

For completeness, the only other numerical possibility under
`C<=2,L<=1,H<=2` is `(C,L,H)=(1,1,2)`.  An `H`-index labelled two
or four is automatically a `C`-index: for label two this follows from
the selected rank-three intersection with
`F(a,2)={k-6,a,e}`, and for label four it follows already from the
simple-current condition in `delta`.  Thus the two `H`-labels would
be `{2,6}` or `{4,6}`.  The first pair forces the third label to be
the forbidden `a`; the second forces it to be `k-6`, but
`m(a,4,6,k-6)=5`.  This excludes the last possibility.

Therefore `C+L+H<=5` and again `ell<=6`.

Now suppose that `k` is a plus label.  Let `q in {2,e}` be the
selected minus label, put `x=Jq`, and retain `a` for the other minus
label.  Among the four non-`k` positions, the distinguished number is

```text
h=n_x+n_(Ja).
```

If `a!=q`, the two labels are distinct and `h<=4`.  If `a=q`, then
`h=2n_x`.  For `n_x>=3`, the selected complement contains two copies
of `x`; pairing it as `F(q,x) intersection F(x,r)`, rank three forces
equality of the two intervals, and `(P23.11z5zzh50)` gives `r=q`,
again violating support disjointness.  Thus `h<=4`.

It remains to count the rank-one cuts omitting `k`.  Partition the four
non-`k` positions into unordered pairs `E,F`; there are three such
partitions.  For a fixed partition with `E={x,y}`, the four possible
oriented cuts are

```text
[q in F(E)][Ja in F(F)],       [a in F(E)][x in F(F)],
[q in F(F)][Ja in F(E)],       [a in F(F)][x in F(E)].
                                                               (P23.11z5zzh67)
```

At most two can hold.  By reflection take `q=2,x=e`.  If the first,
second, and fourth, or the first, third, and fourth indicators held,
then `F(e,y)` would contain both `2,e`, forcing simultaneously
`y in {k-4,e,k}` and `y=4`, impossible for `k>=12`.  If the first
three held, `F(e,y)` would contain `2,a,Ja`; the endpoint conditions
force `k=12,a=6,y=8`.  Then `F(F)` would contain `{2,6,10}`, but its
lower endpoint at most two and upper endpoint at least ten would force
the two positive even labels of `F` to differ by at most two and have
sum between ten and fourteen, impossible while avoiding the minus
labels two and six.  The last three indicators similarly force
`y=4,k=12,a=6` and the same contradiction.  Hence each partition
contributes at most two cuts, for at most six rank-one cuts in total.

Therefore

```text
T<=4*3+6=18<19<=N.                                 (P23.11z5zzh68)
```

This closes the `t=2` endpoint and proves the corollary.  QED.

Combining the nonexceptional, exceptional, and simple-current branches
gives the uniform closure sought above.

**Corollary 23A9ZH14 (complete all-even `d=3` chamber).**  Under the
all-even disjoint-support hypotheses of Proposition 23A9ZF, if a maximal
active cut has `d=3`, then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.
```

**Proof.**  Corollaries 23A9ZH7--23A9ZH10 handle `k<=10`.
At `k>=11`, Corollary 23A9ZH12 handles every nonexceptional triple,
Corollary 23A9ZM handles `{2,k-1,k-1}`, and
Corollary 23A9ZH13 handles every triple containing `k`.  These cases
are exhaustive; at odd level a triple of even labels cannot contain
`k`, so the lower threshold `k>=12` in Corollary 23A9ZH13 causes no
gap.  QED.

There is a complementary mixed-parity chamber in which the exceptional
triple reaches the top channel.  Here parity itself reduces the number of
negative cuts before the affine losses are counted.

**Corollary 23A9ZI (top-channel exceptional-triple chamber).**  Work in
`SU(2)_k`, with `k>=4`, under the disjoint-support hypothesis
`(P23.11z5zv)`.  Suppose that a maximal active cut `A|B` has

```text
A={2,k-1,k-1},
```

where `2` is the minus label and the two copies of `k-1` are plus labels.
Pair the four labels of `B`, let `I,J` be the resulting finite
Clebsch--Gordan intervals, and put

```text
K=I intersection J,                   d=|K|=m_0(B).
```

If

```text
k in K,                              d>=5,           (P23.11z5zzi1)
```

then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                            (P23.11z5zzi2)
```

Consequently the finite seven-factor two-minus contraction is
nonnegative in this chamber without using `U`.

**Proof.**  Since `k` lies in both fusion intervals, each of the two
paired label sums equals `k`.  Indeed,

```text
N_(u,v)^k=1
```

forces simultaneously `u+v>=k` and
`2k-u-v>=k`.  Thus both intervals end at `k`, and

```text
K={s,s+2,...,k}.                                    (P23.11z5zzi3)
```

The two intervals cannot coincide.  An interval ending at `k` determines
its unordered input pair from its lower endpoint: the pair is

```text
{(k-s)/2,(k+s)/2}.
```

If `I=J`, the pair containing the unique minus position of `B` would
therefore repeat that minus label in the all-plus pair, contrary to
disjoint support.  Hence one interval contains `s-2` and the other is
`K`.  In particular `s>=2`.

Count first the ordered pairs in `K times K`.  For output two there are
`3d-2` pairs at distance at most one step.  The affine upper triangle
inequality removes only `(k,k)`, and the additional pair
`(s-2,s)` is admissible.  Hence

```text
m_2(B)>=3d-2.                                       (P23.11z5zzi4)
```

For output four the `K times K` band has `5d-6` pairs.  Its only affine
upper-wall losses are

```text
(k,k), (k,k-2), (k-2,k),
```

and its lower triangle inequality removes none because `s>=2`.  The
extension pair `(s-2,s+2)` is admissible; here `d>=5` gives
`s+2<=k-6`, so the upper triangle inequality is strict.  Therefore

```text
m_4(B)>=5d-8.                                       (P23.11z5zzi5)
```

As in `(P23.11z5zzg8)`, the exact triple profile is

```text
m_0(A)=1,                 m_2(A)=2,
m_4(A)=1.
```

Moreover deleting the equal plus pair contributes exactly `m_2(B)` to
`P_eq`.  Orthogonality across the selected cut and
`(P23.11z5zzi4)--(P23.11z5zzi5)` give

```text
N+P_eq
 >=d+3m_2(B)+m_4(B)
 >=15d-14
 >=12d.                                               (P23.11z5zzi6)
```

It remains to justify the factor twelve.  The finite parity rule is the
same as the ordinary one, so the counting proof of Corollary 23A9ZC
applies unchanged: thirteen active oriented cuts would force all seven
labels to be even.  If `k` is even, the two labels `k-1` already rule
this out.  If `k` is odd, either paired block of `B` has label sum `k`
and therefore contains an odd label, again ruling it out.  Hence there
are at most twelve active cuts.  Maximality of `d` now yields

```text
T<=12d<=N+P_eq,
```

which proves `(P23.11z5zzi2)`.  QED.

The interval-size argument in Corollary 23A9ZG(ii) also closes the
exceptional all-even branch well below the large-cut threshold.

**Corollary 23A9ZJ (odd exceptional triple for `d>=5`).**  Work under
the all-even, disjoint-support hypotheses of Proposition 23A9ZF, with
`k` odd.  Suppose that a maximal active cut has

```text
A={2,k-1,k-1},                    d=m_0(B)>=5.       (P23.11z5zzj1)
```

Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                            (P23.11z5zzj2)
```

Consequently the finite seven-factor two-minus contraction is
nonnegative throughout this exceptional chamber.  Only `d<=4` remains
from this triple type.

**Proof.**  Corollary 23A9ZG(ii) used only the triple profile and
finite endpoint bands, not `d>=11`, to prove

```text
N+P_eq>=15d-13.                                     (P23.11z5zzj3)
```

Write the five plus labels as

```text
{e,e,r_1,r_2,r_3},                    e=k-1.
```

For every label `ell` of `B`, the interval-size argument
`(P23.11z5zzg11)` gives

```text
d<=min(ell,k-ell)+1.                                (P23.11z5zzj4)
```

Suppose first that `d>=6`.  Then every label of `B` lies between `5`
and `k-5`.  Since all those labels are even and `k` is odd, they in
fact lie between `6` and `k-5`.  In the minus-two orientation no pair
`{e,r_i}` can be active, so there are at most the pair `{e,e}` and the
three pairs among the `r_i`.  In the other orientation the pair
`{e,e}` cannot be active, there are at most six indexed pairs
`{e,r_i}`, and a pair of `r_i` cannot be active because its complement
`{2,e,e,r_j}` is invariant only for `r_j in {2,4}`.  Thus there are at
most ten active cuts.  Maximality and `(P23.11z5zzj3)` give

```text
T<=10d<=15d-13.
```

Now let `d=5`.  Formula `(P23.11z5zzj4)` and parity give

```text
4<=a,r_i<=k-5.                                      (P23.11z5zzj5)
```

The minus-two orientation again has at most four active cuts.  The other
orientation has at most six pairs `{e,r_i}`.  A selected pair among the
`r_i` can be active only when its unused label equals four, because its
complement is `{2,e,e,r_j}`.  If `z` of the three `r_i` equal four,
there are at most `z` such cuts.  Hence

```text
c<=10+z<=13.                                       (P23.11z5zzj6)
```

Equality `c=13` would force equality throughout this count.  In
particular,

```text
r_1=r_2=r_3=4,
```

and all six pairs `{e,4}` would have to support the minus label `a`.
Since

```text
e star_k 4={k-5,k-3}
```

and `(P23.11z5zzj5)` gives `a<=k-5`, this forces `a=k-5`.
Pairing the selected complement as

```text
(k-5,4)|(4,4)
```

shows that its two fusion intervals are

```text
{k-9,k-7,k-5,k-3,k-1},             {0,2,4,6,8}.
```

Their intersection can have five entries only for `k=9`.  But then
`a=4` is also a plus label, contradicting disjoint support.  Therefore
`c<=12`.  Finally

```text
T<=12d=60<62=15d-13<=N+P_eq,
```

which proves the `d=5` case and `(P23.11z5zzj2)`.  QED.

One more endpoint layer closes by retaining the actual sizes of the
fourfold complements instead of bounding every active cut by `d`.

**Corollary 23A9ZK (odd exceptional triple for `d=4`).**  Under the
hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZJ, replace `(P23.11z5zzj1)` by

```text
A={2,k-1,k-1},                    d=m_0(B)=4.        (P23.11z5zzk1)
```

Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                            (P23.11z5zzk2)
```

Thus only `d<=3` remains from the all-even exceptional triple.

**Proof.**  Put `e=k-1` and write the other three plus labels as
`r_1,r_2,r_3`.  Formula `(P23.11z5zzj4)`, parity, and disjoint support
give

```text
4<=a,r_i<=k-3.                                      (P23.11z5zzk3)
```

Let

```text
n=#{i:r_i=k-3},                    z=#{i:r_i=4}.
```

We bound the two minus orientations separately.  In the minus-two
orientation, the selected pair `{e,e}` contributes exactly `d=4`.
A pair `{e,r_i}` can be active only for `r_i=k-3`; there are `2n`
indexed choices and maximality bounds each contribution by four.  For
a pair of `r_i`, the fourfold complement is `{a,e,e,r_j}`.  Since

```text
e star_k e={0,2},
```

its invariant multiplicity is

```text
indicator_(a=r_j)+N_(2,r_j)^a<=2.
```

There are three such pairs.  Hence this orientation contributes at most

```text
4+8n+6=10+8n.                                      (P23.11z5zzk4)
```

In the minus-`a` orientation, the pair `{e,e}` is inactive by
`a>=4`.  For each of the at most six indexed pairs `{e,r_i}`, its
fourfold complement is `{2,e,r_j,r_l}`.  Pairing `2` with `e` shows
that its invariant multiplicity is at most

```text
|2 star_k e|=2.
```

Finally, a pair of `r_i` can be active only if its complement
`{2,e,e,r_j}` is invariant.  As in Corollary 23A9ZG(ii), this forces
`r_j in {2,4}`; by `(P23.11z5zzk3)` only `r_j=4` is possible, and the
complement then has invariant multiplicity one.  Thus the second
orientation contributes at most

```text
12+z.                                               (P23.11z5zzk5)
```

If `k>=9`, the values `k-3` and four are distinct, so `n+z<=3`.
Combining `(P23.11z5zzk4)--(P23.11z5zzk5)` gives

```text
T<=22+8n+z<=46.                                    (P23.11z5zzk6)
```

At `k=7`, the two values coincide.  If `n=z<=2`, the same count gives
`T<=40`.  If `n=z=3`, then all `r_i=4`.  Disjoint support leaves only
`a=2` among the positive even labels at level seven not already used
on the plus side.  But

```text
m_0(2,4,4,4)
 =|{2,4,6} intersection {0,2,4,6}|=3,
```

contrary to `d=4`.  Thus `(P23.11z5zzk6)` also holds at level seven.

Finally `(P23.11z5zzj3)` at `d=4` gives

```text
N+P_eq>=47>46>=T,
```

which proves `(P23.11z5zzk2)`.  QED.

The mixed-parity sector has a wall-uniform chamber obtained solely from
pairs inside the common fusion interval.  No interval extension is used:
at finite level distinct reflected input pairs can define the same
Clebsch--Gordan interval.

**Proposition 23A9ZL (finite mixed-parity large-cut chamber).**  Work in
`SU(2)_k` under `(P23.11z5zv)`, with all labels positive but not all
even.  Choose a maximal active cut `A|B` and put

```text
d=m_0(B),                    c=number of active oriented cuts.
```

Assume that no label of the invariant triple `A` equals `k`.  Then

```text
N>=12d-16.                                          (P23.11z5zzl1)
```

Consequently the contraction is nonnegative whenever

```text
(12-c)d>=16.                                       (P23.11z5zzl2)
```

If in addition

```text
m_6(A)>=1,                                         (P23.11z5zzl3)
```

then

```text
N>=19d-37.                                         (P23.11z5zzl4)
```

In particular, because parity gives `c<=12` in the non-all-even sector,
condition `(P23.11z5zzl3)` closes every such chamber with `d>=6`.

**Proof.**  Proposition 25D3, specifically `(P25.29e3)--(P25.29e4)`,
gives the finite triple profile

```text
m_0(A)=1,                    m_2(A)>=2,
m_4(A)>=1.                                          (P23.11z5zzl5)
```

Pair the four labels of `B`, taking one pair to contain its unique minus
position, and let `K` be the intersection of the two finite
Clebsch--Gordan intervals.  Then `|K|=d`.  Disjoint support excludes
`0 in K`, since the minus-plus pair would otherwise have equal labels.

For `d=1`, `(P23.11z5zzl1)` follows already from `N>=d`; assume
`d>=2` in the two-step band count below.

Before the affine upper triangle inequality is imposed, the ordered
pairs in `K times K` at distance at most one and two steps number
`3d-2` and `5d-6`.  For output two there is no lower loss and at most
the single upper loss `(k,k)`.  For output four the sole possible lower
loss is `(1,1)`, while the upper wall removes at most

```text
(k,k),                    (k,k-2), (k-2,k)
```

when these channels have the parity of `K`; the opposite parity has
fewer losses.  Hence

```text
m_2(B)>=3d-3,                    m_4(B)>=5d-10.      (P23.11z5zzl6)
```

Finite orthogonality across `A|B` and `(P23.11z5zzl5)` now give

```text
N>=d+2(3d-3)+(5d-10)=12d-16,
```

proving `(P23.11z5zzl1)`.  Maximality gives `T<=cd`, so
`(P23.11z5zzl2)` proves `T<=N`; Proposition 23A9S finishes the
contraction inequality.

For the stronger branch, if `d<=2` then `(P23.11z5zzl4)` follows from
`N>=d` and `(P23.11z5zzl1)`.  Assume `d>=3`.  The three-step band in
`K times K` has
`7d-12` ordered pairs.  Its lower triangle inequality can remove only

```text
(1,1),                    (1,3), (3,1),
```

and its upper triangle inequality removes at most the six pairs

```text
(k,k), (k,k-2), (k-2,k),
(k,k-4), (k-4,k), (k-2,k-2),
```

again with fewer losses in the opposite parity.  Therefore

```text
m_6(B)>=7d-21.                                      (P23.11z5zzl7)
```

Retaining the additional product
`m_6(A)m_6(B)` proves `(P23.11z5zzl4)`.

Finally the parity count in Corollary 23A9ZC uses only the fusion parity
rule and therefore applies unchanged at finite level: a non-all-even
configuration has `c<=12`.  For `d>=6`,

```text
19d-37>=12d,
```

which completes the last assertion.  QED.

At overlap three, two near-wall plus labels force a stronger fourfold
profile; three such labels also create three additional equal-pair
reservoirs.

**Corollary 23A9ZM (odd exceptional triple for `d=3`).**  Under the
hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZJ, assume

```text
A={2,k-1,k-1},                    d=m_0(B)=3.        (P23.11z5zzm1)
```

Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                            (P23.11z5zzm2)
```

Thus only `d<=2` remains from the all-even exceptional triple.

**Proof.**  Again put `e=k-1`, write the other plus labels as
`r_1,r_2,r_3`, and set

```text
n=#{i:r_i=k-3},                    z=#{i:r_i=4}.
```

The interval-size bound `(P23.11z5zzj4)`, parity, and disjoint support
give

```text
2<=a<=k-3,                    4<=r_i<=k-3.          (P23.11z5zzm3a)
```

The weighted cut count in Corollary 23A9ZK changes only as follows.
The selected `{e,e}` cut contributes three; every `{e,r_i}` cut in the
minus-two orientation contributes at most three; and the three
`r_i`-pair complements still contribute at most two each.  In the other
orientation, the pair `{e,e}` can now contribute only when `a=2`, and
its complement has multiplicity at most three.  The six `{e,r_i}`
complements still have multiplicity at most two, while an `r_i`-pair
complement contributes one only when its unused label is four.  Therefore

```text
T<=24+6n+z.                                         (P23.11z5zzm3)
```

If `n=0`, the right side is at most `27`; if `n=1`, it is at most `32`.
Both are bounded by

```text
N+P_eq>=15d-13=32                                  (P23.11z5zzm4)
```

from `(P23.11z5zzj3)`.  It remains to take `n>=2`.

Assume first that `k>=9` and put `h=k-3`.  Pair the two copies of `h`
inside the selected complement `B`.  Simple-current reflection gives

```text
h star_k h={0,2,4,6}.
```

The other paired fusion interval has intersection of size three with
this set.  It cannot contain zero, by disjoint support, and hence it
contains all three channels `2,4,6`.  Counting only these channels
against `{0,2,4,6}` gives

```text
m_2(B)>=8,                         m_4(B)>=10.       (P23.11z5zzm5)
```

Indeed, for output two the four rows `0,2,4,6` contribute respectively
`1,2,3,2` pairs, and for output four they contribute `1,3,3,3`.
All these pairs satisfy the affine upper triangle inequality for
`k>=9`.

The exceptional triple profile and the equal pair `{e,e}` now give

```text
N+P_eq>=d+3m_2(B)+m_4(B)>=37.                      (P23.11z5zzm6)
```

If `n=2`, then `z<=1`, so `(P23.11z5zzm3)` is at most `37`.  If
`n=3`, there are also three equal pairs among the `r_i`.  Deleting any
one of them leaves the plus labels `{e,e,h}`.  The corresponding
`P_eq` summand is

```text
m(2,a,e,e,h)
 =m(2,a,h)+m(2,a,2,h)>=1+2=3,                      (P23.11z5zzm7)
```

because `a star_k h` contains `2,4,6`.  The three summands add at least
nine to `(P23.11z5zzm6)`, whereas `(P23.11z5zzm3)` is at most `42`.
Thus this case is also paid.

It remains to take `k=7`.  The interval-size bound
`d<=min(r_i,k-r_i)+1`, parity, and plus/minus disjointness force every
`r_i=4`; hence `n=3`.  Disjoint support then gives `a=2`.
Direct fusion at level seven gives

```text
N=28,                    P_eq=4+8+3*3=21,
T=2(3+6*2+3*1)=36.                              (P23.11z5zzm8)
```

Here the three terms of `P_eq` are respectively the equal-minus pair,
the pair `{e,e}`, and the three pairs among the copies of four; the
three terms in one orientation of `T` correspond to pair types
`{e,e}`, `{e,4}`, and `{4,4}`.  Hence `T=36<49=N+P_eq`.
This proves `(P23.11z5zzm2)`.  QED.

The missing output-six channel in Proposition 23A9ZL has only three
unbounded endpoint forms.

**Corollary 23A9ZN (classification of the mixed-parity `m_6=0`
residual).**  Keep the hypotheses and notation of Proposition 23A9ZL,
assume `d>=6`, and suppose

```text
m_6(A)=0.
```

Then the label multiset of `A` is one of

```text
{1,1,2},                 {1,k-2,k-1},
{2,k-1,k-1}.                                       (P23.11z5zzn1)
```

For either repeated-label multiset in `(P23.11z5zzn1)`,

```text
N+P_eq>=15d-19.                                    (P23.11z5zzn2)
```

For the distinct multiset, according as the minus label in `A` is
`1`, `k-2`, or `k-1`,

```text
T<=2d+42,                    T<=2d+36,
T<=2d+38.                                           (P23.11z5zzn2a)
```

Consequently every case in `(P23.11z5zzn1)` is nonnegative for `d>=6`;
in particular, every `m_6(A)=0` case under the hypotheses of the
corollary is closed.

**Proof.**  First note that `K` is a nonempty step-two interval avoiding
zero.  At level `k`, either parity therefore has at most `ceil(k/2)`
available nonzero channels.  Hence `d>=6` implies `k>=11`.

Order the labels of `A` as in `(P25.29e3)` and retain its variables
`x,y,z,w`.  Put

```text
L=max(-3,3-x-y,-min(x,y)),
U=min(3,z,w,z+w-3),
```

so that

```text
m_6(A)=#(Z intersection [L,U]).                    (P23.11z5zzn3)
```

The largest-label convention gives `x,y>0`, `z,w>=0`, and
`z<=min(x,y)`.  Also `z,w` are not both zero, because no label of `A`
equals `k`.

If `z,w>0`, then `U>=-1`.  When `x+y>=4`, one has `L<=-1`; when
`x+y=2`, one has `x=y=z=1`, `w=k-3>=8`, and `L=U=1`.
When `x+y=3`, one has `{x,y}={1,2}`, `z=1`, `w=k-4>=7`,
and `L=0<=1=U`.  Thus this case cannot have `m_6(A)=0`.

Let `z=0`.  Then

```text
U=min(0,w,w-3).
```

If `w>=3`, the inequality `L>U=0` forces `x=y=1`, giving
`A={1,1,2}`.  Since `k=x+y+w>=11`, the case `w=2` has
`x+y>=9` and gives `L<=U`.  If `w=1`, then `x+y=k-1`; the inequality
`L>-2=U` forces `min(x,y)=1`, giving
`A={1,k-2,k-1}`.

Finally let `w=0`.  If `z>=2`, then `z<=min(x,y)` gives `L<=U`.
If `z=1`, the inequality `L>-2=U` forces
`min(x,y)=1`, and the labels are `{2,k-1,k-1}`.  This proves
`(P23.11z5zzn1)`.

For `A={1,1,2}`, disjoint support forces the two copies of one to be
plus labels and the label two to be the minus label.  The same argument
puts the equal copies of `k-1` on the plus side for
`A={2,k-1,k-1}`.  Deleting that equal pair contributes `m_2(B)` to
`P_eq`.  The exact profiles of both triples are

```text
m_0(A)=1,                    m_2(A)=2,
m_4(A)=1.
```

Using `(P23.11z5zzl6)` therefore gives

```text
N+P_eq
 >=d+3m_2(B)+m_4(B)
 >=d+3(3d-3)+(5d-10)
 =15d-19,
```

proving `(P23.11z5zzn2)`.  The finite parity count gives `c<=12`, so
for `d>=7`,

```text
T<=12d<=15d-19<=N+P_eq.
```

It remains to close the repeated-label endpoint `d=6`.  Pair `B` as
`(a,p)|(r,s)`, with the minus label `a` in the first pair, and call the
two finite fusion intervals `I,J`.

Suppose first that `I!=J`.  One interval has a nearest endpoint entry
beyond `K`.  The internal output-two band has sixteen pairs.  If `K`
does not end at `k`, none is lost at the upper wall.  If it does end at
`k`, only `(k,k)` is lost and the lower endpoint extension restores one
admissible pair.  Thus

```text
m_2(B)>=16.
```

The internal output-four band has twenty-four pairs.  When `K` ends at
`k`, its three upper-wall losses are

```text
(k,k),                    (k,k-2), (k-2,k),
```

and the lower extension restores at least one output-four pair; the
lower endpoint cannot be one, since then no such extension would exist.
This leaves at least twenty-two.  If `K` does not end at `k`, there is
at most one upper loss and the sole possible lower loss is `(1,1)`, so
again at least twenty-two remain.  Hence

```text
m_4(B)>=22.
```

The equal-plus reservoir therefore gives

```text
N+P_eq>=6+3*16+22=76>72>=T.                        (P23.11z5zzn4)
```

Now let `I=J`.  A finite fusion interval determines its input pair up to
simultaneous reflection by the simple current.  Direct equality of the
two unordered input pairs would repeat the minus label on the all-plus
pair, so the two pairs are reflected.

If `K` does not end at `k`, the internal bands give

```text
m_2(B)>=16,                    m_4(B)>=20,
```

and hence `N+P_eq>=74`.  If `K` ends at `k`, then
`m_2(B)=15`.  Its length is six, so its lower endpoint is `k-10`.
For `k>11` there is no lower output-four loss, and only the three upper
losses displayed above occur.  Thus `m_4(B)>=21` and

```text
N+P_eq>=6+3*15+21=72>=T.
```

At the sole remaining endpoint `k=11`,

```text
K={1,3,5,7,9,11}.
```

An interval with these endpoints comes from the unordered pair
`{5,6}`, which is fixed as an unordered pair by simple-current
reflection.  Hence both pairs in `B` are `{5,6}`.  Whichever member is
the unique minus label occurs again in the all-plus pair, contradicting
disjoint support.  This excludes the last deficient endpoint.

It remains to prove `(P23.11z5zzn2a)`.  Put

```text
H_1=k-1,                    H_2=k-2.
```

Any finite fourfold invariant containing `H_1` has multiplicity at
most two, and one containing `H_2` has multiplicity at most three.
Indeed, pair that label first.  Its finite Clebsch--Gordan interval
has respectively at most

```text
min(H_1,k-H_1)+1=2,         min(H_2,k-H_2)+1=3
```

channels, and the fourfold multiplicity is the size of its intersection
with the interval from the other pair.

First suppose that the minus label in
`A={1,H_2,H_1}` is one.  Write the other three plus labels as
`R_1,R_2,R_3`.  In either minus orientation, the plus pair
`{H_1,H_2}` contributes at most `d` by maximality.  The three pairs
`{H_1,R_i}` leave `H_2` in the complementary fourfold block and
contribute at most three each; the three pairs `{H_2,R_i}` leave
`H_1` and contribute at most two each; and the three pairs among the
`R_i` also leave `H_1` and contribute at most two each.  Thus each
orientation contributes at most

```text
d+3*3+3*2+3*2=d+21,
```

which proves `T<=2d+42`.

Next suppose that the minus label in `A` is `H_2`; its two selected
plus labels are `1,H_1`.  Among the four non-`H_1` plus positions,
let `n` be the number whose label is one or three.  Since

```text
H_2 star_k H_1=V_1+V_3,
```

a triple `{H_2,H_1,p}` is invariant only for `p in {1,3}`.  In the
`H_2`-minus orientation, only those `n` pairs containing `H_1` can
be active, and each contributes at most `d`; each of the six pairs not
containing `H_1` leaves `H_1` in the complementary block and
contributes at most two.  This orientation is therefore at most
`nd+12`.  In the other-minus orientation, the four pairs containing
`H_1` leave `H_2` and contribute at most three each, while the six
remaining pairs leave both `H_1` and `H_2` and contribute at most two
each.  That orientation is at most `12+12=24`.

Here `n<=2`.  Otherwise, besides the selected plus label one, at least
two of the three `R_i` would lie in `{1,3}`.  Pairing those two labels
inside the selected fourfold complement `B` would bound its multiplicity
`d` by the size of their finite fusion interval, which is at most four.
This contradicts `d>=6`.  Hence

```text
T<=2d+36.
```

Finally suppose that the minus label in `A` is `H_1`; its selected plus
labels are `1,H_2`.  Define `n` analogously among the four non-`H_2`
plus positions.  In the `H_1`-minus orientation, the `n` active pairs
containing `H_2` contribute at most `nd`, and the other six pairs
leave `H_2` and contribute at most three each.  This gives `nd+18`.
In the other-minus orientation, the four pairs containing `H_2` leave
`H_1` and contribute at most two each; the other six pairs also leave
`H_1` and contribute at most two each.  This gives `8+12=20`.
The preceding two-`R_i` argument again gives `n<=2`, and therefore

```text
T<=2d+38.
```

For `d>=6`, all three bounds in `(P23.11z5zzn2a)` are at most
`12d-16<=N` by Proposition 23A9ZL.

Proposition 23A9S finishes the contraction inequality.  QED.

Combining the two branches above removes the non-simple-current
large-cut chamber completely.

**Corollary 23A9ZO (mixed-parity non-top large-cut closure).**  In the
finite mixed-parity, disjoint-support setting of Proposition 23A9ZL,
let `A|B` be a maximal active cut, assume that no label of `A` equals
`k`, and put `d=m_0(B)`.  If `d>=6`, then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.
```

**Proof.**  If `m_6(A)>=1`, Proposition 23A9ZL gives `T<=N`.  If
`m_6(A)=0`, Corollary 23A9ZN gives the same conclusion, with the
equal-pair reservoir included where needed.  QED.

The remaining top-label chamber has a uniform four-band estimate.

**Corollary 23A9ZP (mixed-parity top-label large-cut reduction).**  In
the same setting, suppose instead that

```text
A={k,u,k-u},                    t=min(u,k-u),
d=m_0(B)>=9.
```

Then

```text
T<=N.
```

Thus every mixed-parity top-label chamber with `d>=9` is closed.

**Proof.**  The simple-current rule gives the exact profile

```text
m_(2h)(A)=1 for 0<=h<=t,             m_r(A)=0 otherwise.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzp1)
```

Indeed, `V_k star_k V_(2h)=V_(k-2h)`, and the finite fusion interval
of `u` and `k-u` contains `k-2h` exactly when `0<=h<=t`.

Let `K` be the nonzero step-two intersection interval for the selected
fourfold block `B`.  For `d>=h+1`, the ordered pairs in `K times K`
at distance at most `h` steps number

```text
(2h+1)d-h(h+1).
```

The lower triangle inequality for output `2h` removes at most
`h(h-1)/2` pairs, and the affine upper triangle inequality removes at
most `h(h+1)/2`.  The first maximum occurs when the lower endpoint is
one, and the second when the upper endpoint is `k`; intervals of the
opposite parity lose fewer pairs.  Consequently

```text
m_(2h)(B)>=(2h+1)(d-h).                            (P23.11z5zzp2)
```

If `t>=3`, sum `(P23.11z5zzp2)` for `0<=h<=3`.  Orthogonality across
the selected cut and `(P23.11z5zzp1)` give

```text
N>=16d-34.
```

The mixed-parity active-cut count is at most twelve, so

```text
T<=12d<=16d-34<=N,                    d>=9.         (P23.11z5zzp3)
```

It remains to take `t=2`.  The first three bands give

```text
N>=d+(3d-3)+(5d-10)=9d-13.                         (P23.11z5zzp4)
```

Suppose first that `k` is a minus label.  Put a graph on the five
indexed plus positions, joining two when their labels sum to `k`, and
let `g` be its number of edges.  Only those edges can be active in the
`k`-minus orientation, and each contributes at most `d`.  Every cut in
the other orientation has `k` in its fourfold complement and hence
contributes at most one, since

```text
m(k,x,y,z)=m(k-x,y,z)<=1.
```

The selected labels `2,k-2` form a non-fixed complementary pair.  The
component count in the proof of Corollary 23A9ZG gives `g<=6`;
therefore

```text
T<=6d+10<=9d-13.                                   (P23.11z5zzp5)
```

Finally suppose that `k` is a plus label.  For a minus orientation
`r`, a pair containing `k` is active only when its other label is
`k-r`; every other cut again contributes at most one.  With the two
minus labels denoted by `q,a`, let

```text
h=n_(k-q)+n_(k-a)
```

among the four non-`k` plus positions.  If `q!=a`, then `h<=4`.  If
`q=a`, the sole possibility `h=8` would make all four positions equal
to `k-q`; pairing the selected complement as

```text
(q,k-q) | (k-q,k-q)
```

would give `d<=t+1=3`, contrary to `d>=9`.  Hence `h<=6`, and

```text
T<=hd+(20-h)<=6d+14<=9d-13,             d>=9.      (P23.11z5zzp6)
```

It remains to take `t=1`.  Put `H=k-1`.  The first two bands in
`(P23.11z5zzp1)--(P23.11z5zzp2)` give

```text
N>=d+(3d-3)=4d-3.                                  (P23.11z5zzp7)
```

Recall that a finite fourfold invariant containing a label `x` has
multiplicity at most `min(x,k-x)+1`: pair `x` first and bound the
intersection by the size of its fusion interval.  Thus labels `1,H`
cap such a multiplicity by two, and label `k` caps it by one.  Write
the three plus labels outside the selected triple as `R_1,R_2,R_3`.
Since they lie in the selected fourfold block `B`, the hypothesis
`d>=9` implies

```text
R_i notin {1,H,k}.                                  (P23.11z5zzp8)
```

Suppose first that `k` is the minus label of `A`; its selected plus
pair is `{1,H}`.  In the `k`-minus orientation a pair is active only
when its labels sum to `k`.  The selected pair contributes `d`.
It is the only such pair incident to `1` or `H` by
`(P23.11z5zzp8)`.  There are at most three pairs among the `R_i`, and
each leaves both `1,H` in the complementary block, so each contributes
at most two.  The other minus orientation has `k` in every complementary
fourfold block, so all ten of its cuts contribute at most one.  Hence

```text
T<=d+3*2+10=d+16<=4d-3.                            (P23.11z5zzp9)
```

Finally suppose that `k` is a plus label.  If the minus label of `A`
is one, its other selected plus label is `H`.  Since

```text
V_1 star_k V_k=V_H,
```

the selected pair `{k,H}` is the only active pair containing `k` in
that minus orientation.  It contributes `d`; each of the remaining six
pairs not containing `k` leaves `k` in the complementary block and
contributes at most one.  This orientation contributes at most `d+6`.
In the other-minus orientation, the four pairs containing `k` leave
the label one and contribute at most two each, while the other six
pairs leave `k` and contribute at most one each.  It contributes at
most fourteen.  Therefore

```text
T<=d+20<=4d-3,                         d>=8.        (P23.11z5zzp10)
```

When the minus label of `A` is `H`, interchange `1` and `H`; the
identity `V_H star_k V_k=V_1` gives exactly the same count.  Together
with `(P23.11z5zzp7)`, this proves `T<=N` in every `t=1` placement.

This proves the claim.  QED.

The same proof closes most of the bounded-cut top-label chamber once
the fourth band and the placement-sensitive cut estimates are retained.

**Corollary 23A9ZP1 (mixed-parity top-label bounded-cut refinement).**
Keep the hypotheses and notation of Corollary 23A9ZP, but assume

```text
6<=d<=8.
```

Then

```text
T<=N.                                               (P23.11z5zzp11)
```

Consequently, together with Corollary 23A9ZP, every finite
mixed-parity top-label chamber with `d>=6` is closed.

**Proof.**  Suppose first that `t>=4`.  In addition to the first four
bands used in `(P23.11z5zzp3)`, retain output eight.  The unrestricted
four-step band in `K times K` has

```text
9d-20
```

ordered pairs.  Its lower triangle inequality can remove at most the
six pairs

```text
(1,1),
(1,3),(3,1),
(1,5),(5,1),(3,3),
```

and the affine upper triangle inequality removes at most ten pairs,
the reflected four-step triangle at the upper endpoint.  Therefore

```text
m_8(B)>=9d-36.                                      (P23.11z5zzp13)
```

Together with the first three positive bands from the proof of
Corollary 23A9ZP, this gives

```text
N>=d+(3d-3)+(5d-10)+(7d-21)+(9d-36)
  =25d-70
  >=12d,                              d>=6.         (P23.11z5zzp14)
```

Mixed parity gives at most twelve active cuts, and maximality gives
`T<=12d`; hence all `t>=4` cases are closed.

Now let `t=3`.  Formula `(P23.11z5zzp3)` still gives

```text
N>=16d-34.                                          (P23.11z5zzp15)
```

If `k` is a minus label, use the complementary-value graph from
`(P23.11z5zzp5)`.  The selected pair is `{3,k-3}`.  Unless `k=6`,
it lies in a non-fixed component, so the same five-vertex component
count gives at most six active edges in this orientation.  Every cut in
the other orientation has `k` in its fourfold complement and contributes
at most one.  Thus

```text
T<=6d+10.                                           (P23.11z5zzp16)
```

At `k=6`, every finite Clebsch--Gordan interval has at most

```text
floor(k/2)+1=4
```

entries.  Pairing any fourfold block shows `d<=4`, contrary to `d>=6`.
Thus no fixed-component exception remains.

If `k` is a plus label, the multiplicity count `h` from
`(P23.11z5zzp6)` again satisfies `h<=6`.  Indeed, the only excluded
case `h=8` would pair the selected complement through an interval of
size at most `t+1=4`, contradicting `d>=6`.  Hence

```text
T<=6d+14.                                           (P23.11z5zzp17)
```

For `d>=6`, both `(P23.11z5zzp16)` and
`(P23.11z5zzp17)` are at most `16d-34`.  This closes `t=3`.

It remains to improve the placement bounds for `t=2,1`.  Every label
`ell` in the selected fourfold block `B` satisfies

```text
d<=min(ell,k-ell)+1.
```

Thus `d>=6` forces

```text
5<=ell<=k-5                              for ell in B. (P23.11z5zzp18)
```

For `t=2`, retain

```text
N>=9d-13.                                           (P23.11z5zzp19)
```

Suppose that `k` is a minus label.  The selected plus labels are
`2,k-2`.  By `(P23.11z5zzp18)`, none of the other three plus labels
equals either one.  In the complementary-value graph the selected edge
is therefore isolated from those three vertices, which span at most
three further edges.  Hence the `k`-minus orientation contributes at
most `4d`.  Every cut in the other orientation has `k` in its fourfold
complement and contributes at most one.  Therefore

```text
T<=4d+10<=9d-13,                       d>=6.        (P23.11z5zzp20)
```

Now suppose that `k` is a plus label, and denote the minus label in the
selected triple by `q`.  Among the four non-`k` plus positions, the
label `k-q` occurs exactly once: it is the other selected plus label,
and `(P23.11z5zzp18)` excludes another copy in `B`.  The label `k-a`
for the other minus position can occur at most three further times.
Thus the multiplicity count in `(P23.11z5zzp6)` satisfies `h<=4`.
The remaining `20-h` cuts have `k` in their fourfold complements, so

```text
T<=4d+16<=9d-13,                       d>=6.        (P23.11z5zzp21)
```

This closes `t=2`.

Finally let `t=1`, and retain

```text
N>=4d-3.                                            (P23.11z5zzp22)
```

If `k` is a minus label, its selected plus pair is `{1,k-1}`.
Condition `(P23.11z5zzp18)` makes this complementary edge isolated
from the other three plus positions.  Let `g` be the number of active
cuts in this orientation.  Then `g<=4`; the selected cut contributes
`d`, and the other `g-1` cuts leave both labels `1,k-1` in their
fourfold complements and contribute at most two each.  The opposite
orientation has at most `12-g` active cuts by mixed parity, each with
`k` in its complement and hence of rank at most one.  Consequently

```text
T<=d+2(g-1)+(12-g)
  =d+g+10
  <=d+14
  <=4d-3,                              d>=6.        (P23.11z5zzp23)
```

If `k` is a plus label, let `q` be the minus label in `A`; it is one
of `1,k-1`, and the other is the selected plus label.  In the
`q`-orientation, the selected pair containing `k` contributes `d`.
If `g` is the number of active cuts in this orientation, its other
`g-1` cuts have `k` in their complements and contribute at most one,
so this orientation contributes at most `d+g-1`.

In the other-minus orientation, let `x` active cuts use a pair
containing `k` and let `y` use a pair not containing `k`.  The first
kind leaves `q` in its complement and contributes at most two; the
second leaves `k` and contributes at most one.  Since

```text
x<=4,                    x+y<=12-g,
```

the total contribution is at most

```text
d+g-1+2x+y
 <=d+11+min(4,12-g)
 <=d+15
 <=4d-3,                              d>=6.        (P23.11z5zzp24)
```

This closes `t=1`.  Since positive labels in
`A={k,u,k-u}` give `t>=1`, all cases are exhausted.  QED.

**Corollary 23A9ZQ (complete finite mixed-parity large-cut chamber).**
Every finite mixed-parity maximal-cut case with `d>=6` is closed.
Together with Corollary 23A9ZG1, every parity sector is closed whenever
`d>=11`.

**Proof.**  In mixed parity, Corollary 23A9ZO handles triples without
`k`, while Corollaries 23A9ZP and 23A9ZP1 handle triples containing
`k`.  The all-even statement is Corollary 23A9ZG1.  QED.

The last two overlap sizes in the odd exceptional triple close completely
when the two minus labels coincide.

**Corollary 23A9ZR (equal-minus odd exceptional endpoint).**  Work in
`SU(2)_k` under the all-even, disjoint-support hypotheses, with `k` odd.
Suppose that a maximal active cut has

```text
A={2,k-1,k-1},                    d=m_0(B) in {1,2},
```

and that both minus labels equal two.  Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                           (P23.11z5zzr1)
```

Consequently only the case in which the other minus label is at least
four remains from this exceptional `d<=2` chamber.

**Proof.**  Put

```text
e=k-1,                    h=k-3,
R={r_1,r_2,r_3},          x_s=m_s(R),
H=#{i:r_i in {e,h}},      z=#{i:r_i=4}.
```

The selected complement is `{2} union R`, so

```text
d=x_2.
```

For each `i` counted by `H`, put

```text
c_i=m(2,e,R minus {r_i}).
```

In one minus orientation the selected pair `{e,e}` contributes `d`.
A pair `{e,r_i}` is active only for `r_i in {e,h}`; its two indexed
choices contribute `2c_i`.  A pair among the `r_i` contributes only
when its unused label is four, because

```text
m(2,e,e,r)=m(2,r)+m(2,2,r),
```

and then its contribution is one.  The two minus orientations coincide,
including their indexed multiplicities.  Hence the exact demand is

```text
T=2d+4 sum_(i counted by H)c_i+2z.                  (P23.11z5zzr2)
```

For `k>=7`, fuse

```text
V_2 star_k V_2=V_0+V_2+V_4,
V_e star_k V_e=V_0+V_2.
```

The global invariant, the equal-minus term of `P_eq`, and the selected
equal-`e` term of `P_eq` therefore sum to

```text
(2x_0+4x_2+3x_4+x_6)
 +(x_0+x_2)+(x_0+x_2+x_4)
 =4x_0+6d+4x_4+x_6.                                (P23.11z5zzr3)
```

All other equal-pair terms are additional nonnegative supply.

Suppose first that `H<=1`.  Maximality gives `c_i<=d`, so

```text
T<=6d+2z.
```

If `z>0`, associate one label four last.  Every channel counted by
`x_2` is one of `2,4,6` in the other paired block, and each of those
channels also fuses with four to output four.  Thus

```text
x_4>=x_2=d.                                         (P23.11z5zzr4)
```

The extra `4x_4` in `(P23.11z5zzr3)` pays `2z`, except potentially
when `d=1,z=3`.  In that endpoint `R={4,4,4}` has `x_2>1` for every
`k>=7`; at `k=5` one has `e=4` and hence `H=3`.  Thus the endpoint
cannot occur here, and `(P23.11z5zzr1)` follows for `H<=1`.

Let `H=2`, and write `t` for the third label of `R`.  The three possible
high pairs have exact fusion intervals

```text
e star_k e={0,2},
e star_k h={2,4},
h star_k h={0,2,4,6}.
```

Intersecting these with the channels which fuse `t` to output two, and
using `d in {1,2}`, leaves exactly

```text
(e,e,4), d=1;             (e,h,4), d=2;
(e,h,6), d=1;             (h,h,6), d=2;
(h,h,8), d=1.                                     (P23.11z5zzr5)
```

Cases in which the third displayed label equals `e` or `h` belong to
`H=3` below.  Direct substitution in `(P23.11z5zzr2)--(P23.11z5zzr3)`
gives, in the order displayed,

```text
(base supply,T)=(15,12),(26,18),(16,6),(32,12),(17,2).
                                                               (P23.11z5zzr6)
```

Thus every `H=2` case is paid without using any further equal pair.

Finally let `H=3`.  The same three short fusion intervals show that the
complete list is finite:

```text
k   R             d       N       P_eq       T
5   (4,4,4)       1       10      31         20
7   (4,6,6)       1       11      19         12
7   (4,4,6)       2       18      19         24
9   (6,6,8)       1       12      10          6
9   (6,6,6)       2       23      12         16
11  (8,8,8)       1       13       4          2.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzr7)
```

Each row follows by fusing the displayed high pair first; all occurring
fusion intervals have at most four entries.  The table includes every
equal pair in `P_eq`, and every row satisfies `T<=N+P_eq`.  This proves
`(P23.11z5zzr1)`.  QED.

The unequal-minus endpoint is controlled by retaining the actual ranks
of the two types of fourfold complements.

**Corollary 23A9ZS (complete odd exceptional endpoint).**  Keep the
hypotheses of Corollary 23A9ZR, except that the other minus label is now
an even label `a>=4`.  Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                           (P23.11z5zzs1)
```

Together with Corollaries 23A9ZJ, 23A9ZK, 23A9ZM, and 23A9ZR, this
closes the exceptional maximal triple `{2,k-1,k-1}` for every maximal
cut size `d>=1`.

**Proof.**  Put

```text
e=k-1,                    h=k-3,
H=#{i:r_i in {e,h}},
M=#{i:r_i in {k-1-a,k+1-a}},
Y=#{i:r_i in {a-2,a+2}},
Z=#{i:r_i=4}.
```

The selected `{e,e}` cut contributes `d`.  In the minus-two orientation,
the two indexed pairs `{e,r_i}` occur only for the `H` positions and
contribute at most `d` each.  A pair among the `r_i` has complement
`{a,e,e,r_j}`; disjoint support removes the scalar channel, so its
rank is at most one and it can be active only for
`r_j in {a-2,a+2}`.  Thus this orientation contributes at most

```text
d+2Hd+Y.
```

In the minus-`a` orientation the pair `{e,e}` is inactive because
`e star_k e={0,2}`.  A pair `{e,r_i}` is possible only at the `M`
positions.  Its complement contains `{2,e}` and hence has rank at most
two.  Finally a pair among the `r_i` has complement `{2,e,e,r_j}`;
it has rank one only for `r_j=4`.  Therefore

```text
T<=d+2Hd+Y+4M+Z.                                    (P23.11z5zzs2)
```

We record the required endpoint supply.  The exceptional triple has
profile `(m_0,m_2,m_4)=(1,2,1)`, and the selected equal pair `{e,e}`
contributes `m_2(B)` to `P_eq`.  If `d=2` and both paired intervals
for `B` have length at least three, their two-entry intersection forces
opposite endpoint extensions.  The resulting `3 times 3` block gives

```text
m_2(B)>=6,                    m_4(B)>=6,
```

including the possible lower and reflected-upper losses.  Hence
`N+P_eq>=26`.  If one paired interval has length two, its all-even
inputs at odd level must include `e=k-1`.  Thus `B` contains an
additional plus label `e`, and the full plus list has at least three
copies of `e`.  Removing any indexed equal-`e` pair leaves the same
label multiset `B`, so at least three terms of `P_eq` contribute
`m_2(B)`.  The two channels inside the common interval give

```text
m_2(B)>=4,                    m_4(B)>=3,
```

and therefore

```text
N+P_eq>=2+5m_2(B)+m_4(B)>=25.                      (P23.11z5zzs2a)
```

If `d=1`, positive even inputs at odd level rule out a one-entry fusion
interval.  The singleton intersection therefore has an endpoint
extension on each side.  Its `2 times 2` block gives

```text
m_2(B)>=3,                    m_4(B)>=2,
```

so the selected equal pair already yields

```text
N+P_eq>=1+3m_2(B)+m_4(B)>=12.                      (P23.11z5zzs2b)
```

Suppose first that `a>=6`.  The two value sets counted by `H` and `M`
are disjoint: their only possible intersection under `a>=4` is `h`,
and that intersection forces `a=4`.  Hence `H+M<=3`, while
`Y,Z<=3`.

For `d=2`, formula `(P23.11z5zzs2)` gives

```text
T<=2+4(H+M)+Y+Z<=20<25.
```

For `d=1`, the corresponding endpoint-extension argument gives
`N+P_eq>=12`, while

```text
T<=1+2(H+M)+Y+Z<=13.
```

Equality in the last bound would require `H+M=3` and `Y=Z=3`.
Thus `R={4,4,4}` and `a=6`.  Requiring four to lie in one of the
`H` or `M` value classes restricts `k` to `7,9,11`; direct interval
intersection gives

```text
m_0(6,4,4,4)=2,4,4
```

at those three levels, respectively.  None has `d=1`.  Hence
`T<=12<=N+P_eq` also for `d=1`.

It remains to take `a=4`.  Disjoint support gives `Z=0`.  Put

```text
g=k-5,                    c_i=m(2,e,R minus {r_i}).
```

Now the exact weighted count is

```text
T=d
 +2 sum_(r_i in {e,h})c_i
 +2 sum_(r_i in {h,g})c_i
 +Y.                                                     (P23.11z5zzs3)
```

Let `p` be the number of entries of `R` equal to `h`.  For `d=2`,
maximality gives `c_i<=2`.  Away from `h`, the two displayed value
classes are disjoint.  Thus `p=0,1,2` give respectively

```text
T<=17,21,25,
```

all bounded by `N+P_eq>=25`.  If `p=3`, the condition
`m_2(h,h,h)=2` forces `k=9` and `h=6`.  Each `c_i=1` and `Y=3`, so

```text
T=2+3*4+3=17.
```

Finally take `d=1`.  If fewer than two entries of `R` lie in

```text
{e,h,g},
```

then `(P23.11z5zzs3)` gives `T<=8`.  If at least two do, intersecting
their short near-wall fusion interval with the channels that can fuse
the third label to output two leaves only

```text
(e,h,6),        (h,h,8),        (e,g,8),
(h,g,10),       (g,g,12).                              (P23.11z5zzs4)
```

The apparent sixth case `(e,e,4)` is forbidden because four is the
minus label.  Directly intersecting

```text
2 star_k e={h,e}
```

with the remaining pair in each of the five displayed families gives,
in the same order,

```text
T<=5,5,4,4,3.                                      (P23.11z5zzs5)
```

For example, the only contribution exceeding the selected cut comes
from an occurrence of `h` or `e` in the two-channel intersection; the
endpoint coincidences at levels `9,11,13,15` give the displayed maxima.
Thus coincident labels add only the already counted membership.  Since
the endpoint-extension bound gives
`N+P_eq>=12`, every `d=1` case is paid.  This proves
`(P23.11z5zzs1)`.  QED.

The first unresolved mixed-parity overlap has only eight low-profile
maximal triples.

**Proposition 23A9ZT (exact mixed-parity `d=5` profile reduction).**
Work in the finite mixed-parity, disjoint-support setting of
Proposition 23A9ZL.  Let `A|B` be a maximal active cut, assume that no
label of `A` equals `k`, and put

```text
d=m_0(B)=5.
```

If either

```text
m_6(A)>=2,
```

or

```text
m_6(A)=1,                    m_8(A)>=1,             (P23.11z5zzt1)
```

then

```text
T<=N.                                               (P23.11z5zzt2)
```

In every case not covered by `(P23.11z5zzt1)`, the label multiset of
`A` belongs to the following exact list:

```text
m_6(A)=0:
 {1,1,2},            {1,k-2,k-1},       {2,k-1,k-1};

m_6(A)=1, m_8(A)=0:
 {1,2,3},            {1,k-3,k-2},       {2,k-2,k-2},
 {2,k-3,k-1},        {3,k-2,k-1}.                    (P23.11z5zzt3)
```

Thus the complete non-top mixed-parity `d=5` problem is reduced to the
eight displayed endpoint families.

**Proof.**  The first four band estimates from Proposition 23A9ZL and
Corollary 23A9ZP1 give, at `d=5`,

```text
m_2(B)>=12,       m_4(B)>=15,
m_6(B)>=14,       m_8(B)>=9.                       (P23.11z5zzt4)
```

Every invariant triple has

```text
m_0(A)=1,                    m_2(A)>=2,
m_4(A)>=1.
```

Hence `m_6(A)>=2` gives

```text
N>=5+2*12+15+2*14=72,
```

while the second condition in `(P23.11z5zzt1)` gives

```text
N>=5+2*12+15+14+9=67.                              (P23.11z5zzt5)
```

Mixed parity permits at most twelve active cuts, and maximality gives
`T<=12d=60`.  This proves `(P23.11z5zzt2)`.

It remains to classify the profiles with `m_6(A)=0` or with
`m_6(A)=1,m_8(A)=0`.  Since the common interval `K` has five nonzero
entries of one parity, necessarily `k>=9`.  The proof of Corollary
23A9ZN, with `d>=6` replaced by this observation, applies verbatim to
`m_6(A)=0` and gives the first row of `(P23.11z5zzt3)`.

For the second row, order the triple and use the tetrahedral variables
from `(P25.29e3)`.  After exchanging `x,y` if necessary, assume

```text
1<=x<=y,                 0<=z<=x,                 w>=0,
```

with `z,w` not both zero.  Put

```text
L_j=max(-j,j-x-y,-x),
U_j=min(j,z,w,z+w-j).
```

The required profile is exactly

```text
L_3=U_3,                    L_4>U_4.                (P23.11z5zzt6)
```

Separating the three exhaustive cases `z,w>0`, `z=0`, and `w=0`,
the two endpoint equalities in `(P23.11z5zzt6)` give:

```text
z,w>0:
 (x,y,z,w)=(1,1,1,w), w>=3;
             (1,2,1,2);
             (1,y,1,1), y>=3;
             (2,2,1,1);

z=0:
 (x,y,z,w)=(1,2,0,w), w>=3;
             (1,y,0,2), y>=3;
             (2,2,0,2);
             (2,y,0,1), y>=3;

w=0:
 (x,y,z,w)=(2,2,2,0);
             (2,y,1,0), y>=3.                     (P23.11z5zzt7)
```

For completeness, if `x>=3,z>=2,w>=2`, then

```text
L_4<=-2<0<=U_4,
```

contrary to `(P23.11z5zzt6)`.  Hence at least one of
`x<=2,z<=1,w<=1` holds.  On the first branch substitute `x=1,2`.
On its complement, substitute `z=0,1` or `w=0,1`; the equality
`L_3=U_3` then forces `x<=2` as well.  Applying the same substitutions
to `L_4>U_4` leaves precisely the three blocks displayed in
`(P23.11z5zzt7)`; the only free variable is `y`, with the lower bounds
shown there.  This exhausts the indicated union of cases.
Conversely direct substitution verifies both inequalities in
`(P23.11z5zzt6)` for every displayed row.

The first row of `(P23.11z5zzt7)` gives the disallowed repeated triple
`{2,2,2}`.  Four rows have `k=6`; they cannot occur because every
finite Clebsch--Gordan interval at level six has at most four entries,
whereas `d=5`.  Translating the remaining rows through

```text
{a,b,c}={y+z,x+z,x+y},              k=x+y+z+w
```

gives exactly the five multisets in the second row of
`(P23.11z5zzt3)`.  This proves the classification.  QED.

Six of the eight endpoint families are paid by endpoint rank caps and
parity saturation.

**Corollary 23A9ZU (six mixed-parity `d=5` endpoint families).**  Under
the hypotheses of Proposition 23A9ZT,

```text
T<=N+P_eq                                            (P23.11z5zzu1)
```

whenever the maximal triple has one of the multisets

```text
{1,1,2},            {1,k-2,k-1},       {2,k-1,k-1},
{1,2,3},            {2,k-2,k-2},       {2,k-3,k-1}.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzu2)
```

Thus only

```text
{1,k-3,k-2},                    {3,k-2,k-1}         (P23.11z5zzu3)
```

remain from the non-top mixed-parity `d=5` profile reduction.

**Proof.**  We first record a consequence of maximality that will be
used throughout.  Pairing any label `ell` of `B` first gives

```text
5=d<=min(ell,k-ell)+1,
```

and hence

```text
4<=ell<=k-4                    for every ell in B.  (P23.11z5zzu4)
```

Consider the two repeated low-profile families `{1,1,2}` and
`{2,k-1,k-1}`.  Disjoint support places the repeated pair on the plus
side.  Their exact profile is `(1,2,1)`, and deleting that pair adds
`m_2(B)` to `P_eq`.  Therefore `(P23.11z5zzl6)` gives

```text
N+P_eq>=5+3*12+15=56.                              (P23.11z5zzu5)
```

There cannot be twelve active cuts.  For `{1,1,2}`, twelve-cut parity
saturation would put both minus labels at two and make all four odd
plus positions lie in `{1,3}`.  The selected complement would then
contain a pair with label two, so its fourfold multiplicity would be
at most

```text
|2 star_k s|<=3,
```

contrary to `d=5`.  For `{2,k-1,k-1}`, odd `k` is incompatible with
the required parity count, while even `k` again forces both minus
labels to be two; the same rank-three cap applies.  Hence at most
eleven cuts are active, and `T<=55<=N+P_eq`.

Now take the distinct family `{1,k-2,k-1}`.  Put

```text
H_2=k-2,                    H_1=k-1.
```

A fourfold invariant containing `1` or `H_1` has rank at most two,
and one containing `H_2` has rank at most three.  Condition
`(P23.11z5zzu4)` also makes every nonselected pair involving a boundary
plus label inactive in the orientation of the displayed maximal
triple.  Counting the selected cut, the three pairs among the other
plus labels, and then the opposite orientation gives:

```text
minus label in A     selected orientation   opposite orientation

1                         5+3*2=11                 20
H_2                       5+3*2=11                 21
H_1                       5+3*2=11                 20.             (P23.11z5zzu6)
```

For example, in the first row every nonselected boundary pair would
require a label in `{1,3,k-3,k-1}`, excluded by
`(P23.11z5zzu4)`.  In the opposite orientation the selected boundary
pair and each of the other nine pairs leave either `1` or `H_1` in
the complementary block, giving the rank-two total `20`.  The other
rows use the same count, with the selected opposite block capped by
three in the middle row.  Thus `T<=32`.  The first three bands give

```text
N>=5+2*12+15=44,
```

which proves this family.

It remains to treat the three families in the second row of
`(P23.11z5zzu2)`.  Each has `m_6(A)=1`, so

```text
N>=5+2*12+15+14=58.                                (P23.11z5zzu7)
```

We show that twelve active cuts would contradict `(P23.11z5zzu4)` or
`d=5`.

For `{1,2,3}`, if the minus label is one, parity saturation forces the
other odd plus label into `{1,3}`, contrary to
`(P23.11z5zzu4)`.  If the minus label is two, the other minus label is
also two and the selected complement has rank at most three.  If the
minus label is three, saturation forces its selected complement to
have the form

```text
{a,5,4,4}.
```

The interval `4 star_k 4` has at most five entries and contains zero,
while `a star_k 5` cannot contain zero by disjoint support.  Their
intersection therefore has at most four entries.

For `{2,k-2,k-2}`, disjoint support makes two the minus label.  At even
`k` the repeated plus pair has the wrong parity for a twelve-cut
configuration.  At odd `k`, saturation forces the selected complement
to have the form

```text
{4,k-4,k-4,s}.
```

The interval `(k-4) star_k (k-4)` has exactly five possible low
entries including zero, while `4 star_k s` omits zero; again `d<=4`.

Finally consider `{2,k-3,k-1}`.  At odd `k` its all-even selected
triple is incompatible with mixed twelve-cut parity.  At even `k`,
according as the minus label is `2,k-3`, or `k-1`, parity saturation
forces the selected complement into one of the forms

```text
{4,k-3,k-3,s},       {a,k-5,4,4},       {a,k-3,4,4}.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzu8)
```

The first has rank at most four because
`|(k-3) star_k (k-3)|=4`; in the other two, the interval
`4 star_k 4` contains zero while the other paired interval omits it,
again leaving at most four common entries.  Thus twelve cuts are
impossible in all three families.  Hence `T<=11d=55<58<=N`, proving
`(P23.11z5zzu1)`.  QED.

The first of the two residual endpoint families is ruled out by the
same saturation mechanism, but the reflected parity must be kept.

**Corollary 23A9ZV (seventh mixed-parity `d=5` endpoint family).**
Under the hypotheses of Proposition 23A9ZT, if the maximal triple is

```text
{1,k-3,k-2},
```

then

```text
T<=N.                                               (P23.11z5zzv1)
```

Consequently only `{3,k-2,k-1}` remains from the non-top mixed-parity
`d=5` profile reduction.

**Proof.**  Put

```text
L=1,                    M=k-3,                    H=k-2.
```

This triple has `m_6=1`, so `(P23.11z5zzu7)` gives `N>=58`.  It is
therefore enough to rule out twelve active cuts.  By
`(P23.11z5zzu4)`, every label in the selected complement lies in
`[4,k-4]`.

Suppose first that `k` is even.  The labels `L,M` are odd and `H` is
even.  If `H` is the selected minus label, parity saturation forces the
selected complement into

```text
{k-4,M,M,s}.
```

But `M star_k M` has only four entries, so the cut has rank at most
four.  If `L` is the selected minus label, saturation instead gives

```text
{5,M,k-4,k-4},
```

and the same multiset occurs with the first two entries interchanged
when `M` is the selected minus label.  The interval
`(k-4) star_k (k-4)` has five entries and contains zero, whereas
`5 star_k M` omits zero by disjoint support.  Their intersection
therefore has at most four entries.

Now suppose that `k` is odd.  The labels `L,H` are odd and `M` is
even.  According as the selected minus label is `M,L`, or `H`,
saturation forces the selected complement into

```text
{M,k-4,k-4,s},       {5,k-4,M,M},       {k-4,5,M,M}.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzv2)
```

In the first case `(k-4) star_k (k-4)` has five entries including
zero, while `M star_k s` omits zero.  In the other two cases
`M star_k M` has only four entries.  Thus every putative twelve-cut
configuration has selected rank at most four, contradicting `d=5`.
Hence at most eleven cuts are active and

```text
T<=11d=55<58<=N.
```

This proves `(P23.11z5zzv1)`.  QED.

The last family is excluded by the equality case of the parity count.

**Corollary 23A9ZW (complete non-top mixed-parity `d=5` layer).**
Under the hypotheses of Proposition 23A9ZT, if the maximal triple is

```text
{3,k-2,k-1},
```

then

```text
T<=N.                                               (P23.11z5zzw1)
```

Together with Corollaries 23A9ZU and 23A9ZV, this closes every non-top
mixed-parity maximal cut with `d=5`.

**Proof.**  Put

```text
L=3,                    H_2=k-2,                  H_1=k-1.
```

Again `m_6(A)=1`, so `(P23.11z5zzu7)` gives `N>=58`.  Suppose for a
contradiction that all twelve allowed cuts are active.  The equality
case of the parity count in Corollary 23A9ZC says precisely:

```text
both minus labels even:
  four plus labels are odd, one is even, and every odd--odd pair
  is active in both orientations;

both minus labels odd:
  two plus labels are odd, three are even, and every odd--even pair
  is active in both orientations.                  (P23.11z5zzw2)
```

The opposite-parity-minus case has at most ten cuts.  The all-even
case is excluded by the mixed-parity hypothesis.  Also every label in
the selected complement lies in `[4,k-4]` by `(P23.11z5zzu4)`.

Assume first that `k` is even.  Then `L,H_1` are odd and `H_2` is
even.  If the selected minus label is `H_2`, the first line of
`(P23.11z5zzw2)` supplies another odd plus label `O` in the complement
and makes the pair `{H_1,O}` active in the `H_2` orientation.  Hence

```text
O in H_1 star_k H_2={1,3},
```

contrary to `O>=4`.  If the selected minus label is `L`, the second
line of `(P23.11z5zzw2)` makes the selected pair `{H_2,H_1}` active
also in the orientation of the other minus label `a`.  Thus

```text
a in H_2 star_k H_1={1,3},
```

again contradicting `(P23.11z5zzu4)`.  Finally, if the selected minus
label is `H_1`, the second line supplies another odd plus label `O`
and makes `{O,H_2}` active in the `H_1` orientation.  The same
two-entry fusion interval forces `O in {1,3}`.

Now assume that `k` is odd.  Then `L,H_2` are odd and `H_1` is even.
If the selected minus label is `H_1`, the first line of
`(P23.11z5zzw2)` supplies another odd plus label `O`; activity of
`{H_2,O}` forces `O in H_1 star_k H_2={1,3}`.  If the selected minus
label is `L`, the selected pair `{H_2,H_1}` must also be active in the
other-minus orientation, forcing that minus label into `{1,3}`.  If
the selected minus label is `H_2`, the remaining odd plus label `O`
must pair actively with `H_1`, again forcing `O in {1,3}`.

Thus twelve active cuts are impossible in every case.  Therefore

```text
T<=11d=55<58<=N,
```

which proves `(P23.11z5zzw1)`.  QED.

The top-label chamber at the same cut size is paid by coupling the
number of active cuts to their actual boundary ranks.

**Corollary 23A9ZX (complete mixed-parity top-label `d=5` layer).**
Keep the hypotheses and notation of Corollary 23A9ZP, and assume

```text
d=5.
```

Then

```text
T<=N.                                               (P23.11z5zzx1)
```

Together with Corollaries 23A9ZP and 23A9ZP1, this closes every
mixed-parity top-label chamber with `d>=5`.

**Proof.**  Recall that there are at most twelve active cuts.  If a
cut uses a plus pair not containing `k`, then its fourfold complement
contains `k` and has rank at most one.

Suppose first that `t>=4`.  Formula `(P23.11z5zzp14)` gives

```text
N>=25d-70=55.
```

If `k` is a minus label, let `g` be the number of active cuts in its
orientation.  Trivially `g<=10`; the other orientation has at most
`12-g` active cuts, all of rank at most one.  Hence

```text
T<=5g+(12-g)<=52<55.
```

If `k` is a plus label, let `h` be the total number of active cuts in
both orientations whose selected pair contains `k`.  There are four
such indexed pairs in each orientation, so `h<=8`.  All other active
cuts have rank at most one, and therefore

```text
T<=5h+(12-h)<=44<55.                               (P23.11z5zzx2)
```

Now let `t=3`.  Formula `(P23.11z5zzp15)` gives `N>=46`.  The proofs
of `(P23.11z5zzp16)--(P23.11z5zzp17)` remain valid at `d=5`: the
fixed component would require `k=6`, where every fusion interval has
at most four entries, and the case `h=8` would make the selected
fourfold rank at most `t+1=4`.  Thus

```text
k a minus label:       T<=6d+10=40,
k a plus label:        T<=6d+14=44,
```

both strictly below `N`.

Take `t=2`.  The first three bands give

```text
N>=9d-13=32.                                         (P23.11z5zzx3)
```

Every label `ell` in the selected complement satisfies

```text
4<=ell<=k-4.                                        (P23.11z5zzx4)
```

If `k` is a minus label, its selected plus pair is `{2,k-2}`.
Condition `(P23.11z5zzx4)` isolates this edge from the other three plus
positions, which span at most three further complementary edges.
Thus at most four active cuts occur in the `k` orientation.  Coupling
this with the rank-one opposite orientation gives

```text
T<=4*5+(12-4)=28<32.
```

If `k` is a plus label, let `q` be the selected minus label.  Among the
four non-`k` plus positions, the boundary label `k-q` occurs only at
the selected position.  The other minus label `a` belongs to the
selected complement, so `(P23.11z5zzx4)` implies `a!=q`; consequently
`k-a` can occur only among its three remaining plus positions.  Hence
at most four active cuts in total use a pair containing `k`.  As above,

```text
T<=4*5+(12-4)=28<32.                                (P23.11z5zzx5)
```

It remains to take `t=1`.  Put `H=k-1`.  The first two bands give

```text
N>=4d-3=17.                                         (P23.11z5zzx6)
```

First suppose that `k` is a minus label.  Write the other three plus
labels as `R_1,R_2,R_3`; all lie in `[4,k-4]`.  Besides the selected
edge `{1,H}`, an active cut in the `k` orientation must use an indexed
pair among the `R_i` whose labels sum to `k`.  Let `r` be the number
of these active edges and `W` the sum of their fourfold ranks.  Such
an edge, with unused label `s`, has complement

```text
{a,1,H,s}.
```

Since

```text
1 star_k H={k-2,k},
```

this complement has rank two only if `a+s=k`.

The complementary-value graph on three indexed vertices gives the
following exhaustive bounds:

```text
r=0: W=0;       r=1: W<=2;       r=2: W<=2;
r=3: W<=3.                                         (P23.11z5zzx7)
```

Indeed, two edges share a vertex and force the two unused labels to
equal the complement of that vertex.  Rank two would then force `a`
to equal the shared plus label, contrary to disjoint support.  Three
edges force all three labels to equal `k/2`, and rank two would force
`a=k/2`, with the same contradiction.  The opposite orientation has
at most `12-(1+r)` active cuts, each of rank one.  Therefore the four
values `r=0,1,2,3` give respectively

```text
T<=16,17,16,16.                                     (P23.11z5zzx8)
```

Finally suppose that `k` is a plus label.  The selected minus label
`q` is one of `1,H`, and put `x=k-q`, the other one.  The selected
pair `{k,x}` contributes five.  It is the only pair containing `k`
that is active in the `q` orientation, because the three labels in
the selected complement satisfy `(P23.11z5zzx4)`; every other active
cut in that orientation has rank one.

The other minus label `a` also satisfies `(P23.11z5zzx4)`, so `a!=q`.
Put `y=k-a`.  A cut using `{k,y}` in the `a` orientation has
complement

```text
{q,x,R_i,R_j}.
```

It has rank two only if `R_i+R_j=k`.  If two of the three residual
plus positions equal `y`, this condition for either indexed cut forces
the third residual label to equal `a`, forbidden by disjoint support.
If all three equal `y`, it forces `y=a=k/2`, again forbidden.
Thus among all the `a`-oriented cuts containing `k`, at most one has
rank two; all the others have rank at most one.  Since at most twelve
cuts are active altogether,

```text
T<=5+11+1=17<=N.                                   (P23.11z5zzx9)
```

All positive top triples have `t>=1`, so the proof is complete.  QED.

One more descent is possible because at overlap four the boundary cuts
can be charged directly to the output-two band.

**Corollary 23A9ZY (complete mixed-parity top-label `d=4` layer).**
Keep the hypotheses and notation of Corollary 23A9ZP, and assume

```text
d=4.
```

Then

```text
T<=N.                                               (P23.11z5zzy1)
```

Together with Corollary 23A9ZX, every mixed-parity top-label chamber
with `d>=4` is closed.

**Proof.**  Every label `ell` in the selected complement satisfies

```text
3<=ell<=k-3.                                        (P23.11z5zzy2)
```

There are at most twelve active cuts, and a cut whose selected plus
pair omits `k` has rank at most one when `k` lies in its complement.

Suppose first that `t>=4`.  The first four top bands give

```text
N>= d+(3d-3)+(5d-10)+(7d-21)=30.                  (P23.11z5zzy3)
```

If `k` is a minus label and its selected complementary-value edge is
not fixed, the component count used in `(P23.11z5zzp16)` gives at most
six active cuts in that orientation.  Coupling the two orientations
therefore gives

```text
T<=6*4+(12-6)=30.
```

If the selected edge is fixed, put `x=k/2`.  The same bound applies
unless the complementary-value graph has at least seven edges.  Since
two of its five indexed vertices already equal `x`, an elementary
component count shows that this can happen only when all five plus
labels equal `x`.  The selected complement is then `{a,x,x,x}`, and

```text
4=m_0(a,x,x,x)=min(a,k-a,x)+1.
```

Fusion parity and disjoint support force

```text
x odd,       x>=5,       a in {3,k-3}.
```

The opposite-minus orientation is parity-inactive, so `T=10*4=40`.
Pairing `a` with one copy of `x` and the other two copies together
gives, for outputs `0,2,4,6`,

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)(a,x,x,x)>=(4,12,16,16).
                                                               (P23.11z5zzy4)
```

This is the direct four-band count for
`3 star_k x={x-3,x-1,x+1,x+3}` and
`x star_k x={0,2,...,k}`; replacing three by `k-3` gives the reflected
same count.  The top triple contains each of these four channels, so
`N>=48>40`.

Now let `k` be a plus label, and let `h` count the active selected
pairs containing it in both minus orientations.  Unless `h=8`, the
multiplicity argument from `(P23.11z5zzp17)` gives `h<=6`, and hence

```text
T<=6*4+(12-6)=30.
```

If `h=8`, the two minus labels are equal to a label `q`, and all four
non-`k` plus labels equal `x=k-q`.  Put `t=min(q,x)`.  Pairing the
selected complement `{q,x,x,x}` as `(q,x)|(x,x)` shows

```text
4=2t-k/2+1,                 k=4t-6.                (P23.11z5zzy5)
```

Disjoint support excludes `t=3`; mixed parity forces `t` odd, so
`t>=5`.  All non-`k` plus pairs are parity-inactive, whence `T=8*4=32`.
The two intervals

```text
q star_k x={2t-6,2t-4,...,k},
x star_k x={0,2,...,2t}
```

give directly

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4)(q,x,x,x)=(4,12,20).                 (P23.11z5zzy6)
```

The top triple contains these three channels, so `N>=36>32`.

Next take `t=3`.  Bound `(P23.11z5zzy3)` still gives `N>=30`.
For `k` in a minus position the nonfixed complementary graph has at
most six edges.  Its fixed exception has `k=6`; then
`(P23.11z5zzy2)` would put every label of the selected complement at
three, violating plus/minus disjoint support.  For `k` in a plus
position, `h<=6` unless `h=8`; formula `(P23.11z5zzy5)` would again
give `k=6` and `q=x=3`, also forbidden by disjoint support.  Thus in
both placements

```text
T<=6*4+(12-6)=30<=N.                              (P23.11z5zzy7)
```

Let `t=2`.  The first three bands give

```text
N>=9d-13=23.                                       (P23.11z5zzy8)
```

If `k` is a minus label, its selected edge `{2,k-2}` is isolated by
`(P23.11z5zzy2)`.  Let `r` be the number of active complementary
edges among the other three plus positions and `W` their total rank.
Their complements contain `{2,k-2}` and an unused pair `{a,s}`.
Since

```text
2 star_k (k-2)={k-4,k-2,k},
```

rank three requires `a+s=k`.  Exactly as for the three-vertex graph
in `(P23.11z5zzx7)`, disjoint support gives

```text
r=0: W=0;       r=1: W<=3;       r=2: W<=4;
r=3: W<=6.                                         (P23.11z5zzy9)
```

The opposite orientation has at most `11-r` active rank-one cuts.
Thus `T<=18<23`.

If `k` is a plus label, write `q` for the selected minus label and
`x=k-q`; these are `2,k-2` in some order.  The selected pair `{k,x}`
contributes four and is the only active `k`-pair in the `q`
orientation.  Put `y=k-a` for the other minus label.  A cut `{k,y}`
in the `a` orientation has complement `{q,x,R_i,R_j}` and can have
rank three only if `R_i+R_j=k`.  If `y` occurs at least twice among
the three residual positions, this condition forces a plus label to
equal `a`, or forces `y=a=k/2`, both forbidden.  Hence at most one
such cut has rank three.  Every other nonselected cut has rank at most
one, and

```text
T<=4+11+2=17<23.                                   (P23.11z5zzy10)
```

It remains to take `t=1`.  Put `H=k-1`.  The exact top profile gives

```text
N=4+m_2(B),                    m_2(B)>=3d-3=9.      (P23.11z5zzy11)
```

First suppose that `k` is a minus label.  Besides the selected
`{1,H}` cut, only complementary pairs among the three residual plus
labels occur in this orientation.  With `r,W` as in
`(P23.11z5zzx7)`, one has

```text
(r,W)=(0,0),(1,at most 2),(2,at most 2),(3,at most 3).
```

In the other-minus orientation, a pair among the three residual plus
labels has complement `{k,1,H,s}` and vanishes because

```text
m(k,1,H,s)=m(H,H,s)=0,                 3<=s<=k-3.
```

Only the six pairs incident to `1` or `H` can remain, and each has
rank one.  Thus the total demand beyond the selected cut is at most

```text
W+6<=9<=m_2(B).                                    (P23.11z5zzy12)
```

Finally let `k` be a plus label.  First take the selected minus label
to be `q=1` and the other selected plus label to be `H`; the case
`q=H` is the identical calculation with the two boundary roles
interchanged.  In the `q` orientation no pair `{H,R_i}` is active by
