`(P23.11z5zzy2)`, and only the three pairs among the residual labels
can contribute beyond the selected cut; each has rank one.

In the other-minus orientation, pairs among the residual labels vanish
because their complements are `{k,1,H,s}`.  At most the three pairs
`{H,R_i}` contribute rank one.  Put `y=k-a` and let `n` be its
multiplicity among the residual plus positions.  The `n` pairs
`{k,y}` have rank at most two.  If `n=2`, rank two would force the
remaining residual label to equal `a`; if `n=3`, it would force
`y=a=k/2`.  Disjoint support excludes both events.  Consequently the
total demand beyond the selected cut is at most

```text
n=0: 6;       n=1: 8;       n=2: 8;       n=3: 9.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzy13)
```

It is again bounded by `m_2(B)`.  Hence `T<=4+m_2(B)=N`, completing
all placements and all `t>=1`.  QED.

The non-top chamber has the same eight endpoint profiles as at overlap
five, but its interior labels improve all three low output bands.

**Corollary 23A9ZZ (complete mixed-parity non-top `d=4` layer).**
Keep the hypotheses of Proposition 23A9ZL and assume

```text
d=4.
```

Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                         (P23.11z5zzz1)
```

Together with Corollary 23A9ZY, every finite mixed-parity maximal-cut
case with `d>=4` is closed.

**Proof.**  Every label in the selected fourfold block `B` belongs to
`[3,k-3]`.  Pair `B` as `(a,p)|(r,s)`, with the minus label in the
first pair, and let `I,J` be the two finite fusion intervals.  Their
intersection `K` has four nonzero entries.

The internal output-two, output-four, and output-six bands in
`K times K` have respectively

```text
10,                    14,                    16
```

ordered pairs before wall losses.  If `K` does not end at `k`, output
two has no loss.  If it does, only `(k,k)` is lost.  The intervals
`I,J` cannot then coincide: a finite interval ending at `k` determines
its unordered input pair from its lower endpoint, and equality would
repeat the minus label on the all-plus pair.  One interval therefore
extends below `K`.  Writing the lower endpoint of `K` as `s`, its
nearest new entry is `s-2`, and `(s-2,s)` restores the lost
output-two pair.  Hence

```text
m_2(B)>=10.                                         (P23.11z5zzz2)
```

For output four, a lower endpoint at one removes only `(1,1)`, leaving
thirteen internal pairs.  An upper endpoint at `k` removes three,
leaving eleven; the strict extension above then restores
`(s-2,s+2)`, giving twelve.  The two wall types cannot occur together:
that would make the four-entry interval run from one to `k`, forcing
`k=7`; but then `B` has labels only in `{3,4}`, and plus/minus
disjointness makes its total parity odd.  Thus

```text
m_4(B)>=12.                                         (P23.11z5zzz3)
```

Finally output six starts with sixteen internal pairs.  Away from the
upper wall, the lower triangle removes at most three.  If `K` ends at
`k`, the reflected upper triangle removes six.  There is no simultaneous
lower loss unless `K={2,4,6,8}` at `k=8`; in that case `(2,2)` is also
lost, but the strict extension contains zero and `(0,6)` restores one
unit.  The odd interval from one to `k` would be the excluded level-seven
case.  Hence

```text
m_6(B)>=10.                                         (P23.11z5zzz4)
```

We shall also use

```text
m_8(B)>=3.                                          (P23.11z5zzz4a)
```

If `K={s,s+2,s+4,s+6}` does not end at `k`, the four ordered pairs

```text
(s,s+6), (s+2,s+4), (s+4,s+2), (s+6,s)
```

fuse to output eight.  If `K` ends at `k`, the three pairs

```text
(k-6,k-2), (k-4,k-4), (k-2,k-6)
```

do so.  The unavailable level-seven endpoint was excluded above.

Using the universal triple profile

```text
m_0(A)=1,                    m_2(A)>=2,
m_4(A)>=1,
```

gives

```text
N>=4+2*10+12=36.                                   (P23.11z5zzz5)
```

If `m_6(A)>=2`, then `(P23.11z5zzz4)` improves this to

```text
N>=56>48>=T.                                       (P23.11z5zzz6)
```

If `m_6(A)=1,m_8(A)>=1`, the fourth band gives instead

```text
N>=36+10+3=49>48.
```

It remains to classify `m_6(A)=0`, and
`m_6(A)=1,m_8(A)=0`.  Since `K` has four nonzero
step-two entries, `k>=7`.  For `m_6(A)=0`, the tetrahedral endpoint
argument in Corollary 23A9ZN applies with this lower bound on `k`.
The only formerly unused subcase is `z=0,w=2`; here
`x+y=k-2>=5`, which gives `L<=U` and therefore cannot have
`m_6(A)=0`.  The result is

```text
{1,1,2},             {1,k-2,k-1},       {2,k-1,k-1}.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzz7)
```

Now suppose `m_6(A)=1,m_8(A)=0`.  In the tetrahedral notation of
Proposition 23A9ZT this is exactly `L_3=U_3,L_4>U_4`, so the complete
table `(P23.11z5zzt7)` applies.
The repeated triple `{2,2,2}` violates plus/minus disjointness, while
the four level-six rows cannot support a four-entry nonzero `K`.
The remaining triples are

```text
{1,2,3},             {1,k-3,k-2},       {2,k-2,k-2},
{2,k-3,k-1},         {3,k-2,k-1}.       (P23.11z5zzz8)
```

We now pay the eight endpoint families.  For the repeated
`m_6=0` triples, disjoint support puts the repeated wall-distance-one
pair on the plus side.  Deleting it contributes `m_2(B)` to `P_eq`,
so

```text
N+P_eq>=36+10=46.                                  (P23.11z5zzz9)
```

If at most eleven cuts are active, this is already larger than
`11d=44`.  If twelve are active, the parity count is saturated and
each orientation contains six active cuts.  Besides the selected cut,
the other five active cuts in that orientation cannot all remove both
indexed copies of the repeated wall-distance-one label.  Any one that
leaves a copy in its complement has rank at most two.  Thus the rank
deficit relative to `12d=48` is at least two, proving `T<=46`.

For the distinct `m_6=0` triple `{1,k-2,k-1}`, the boundary count
`(P23.11z5zzu6)` remains valid with the selected rank changed from five
to four.  According as the selected minus label is `1,k-2`, or `k-1`,
it gives

```text
T<=30,                    T<=31,                    T<=30.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzz10)
```

All three are below `N>=36`.

Finally take one of the five triples in `(P23.11z5zzz8)`.  It has
`m_6(A)=1`, so

```text
N>=36+10=46.                                      (P23.11z5zzz11)
```

Again only the twelve-cut equality case remains.  Four of the five
triples

```text
{1,2,3},       {1,k-3,k-2},
{2,k-3,k-1},  {3,k-2,k-1}
```

contain a wall-distance-one label.  If that label is a minus label,
all six cuts in the other orientation leave it in their complements
and have rank at most two.  If it is a plus label, its degree among
the six active parity-compatible edges is at most four; at least two
active cuts therefore omit its indexed position and have rank at most
two.  In either event the rank deficit is at least two, and
`T<=48-2=46`.

The remaining triple is `{2,k-2,k-2}`.  Disjoint support forces two to
be its minus label.  In the twelve-cut equality case all six cuts in
the other-minus orientation leave this label in their fourfold
complements, so each has rank at most three.  In particular the total
rank deficit is at least two and again `T<=46`.  This exhausts
`(P23.11z5zzz7)--(P23.11z5zzz8)` and proves
`(P23.11z5zzz1)`.  QED.

At overlap three the same endpoint-extension argument gives a sharp
parameter reduction, but not yet a payment of every dense low-profile
case.

**Proposition 23A9ZZ1 (mixed-parity non-top `d=3` parameter reduction).**
Work in the finite mixed-parity, disjoint-support setting of
Proposition 23A9ZL.  Let `A|B` be a maximal active cut, assume that no
label of `A` equals `k`, and put

```text
d=m_0(B)=3.
```

Write `c` for the number of active oriented cuts and
`r=m_6(A)`.  Then

```text
m_2(B)>=7,             m_4(B)>=7,             m_6(B)>=3,
                                                               (P23.11z5zzza1)
N>=24+3r.                                           (P23.11z5zzza2)
```

Since mixed parity gives `c<=12`, raw domination `T<=N` holds in all
of the following chambers:

```text
r>=4;
r=3, c<=11;
r=2, c<=10;
r=1, c<=9;
r=0, c<=8.                                         (P23.11z5zzza3)
```

Thus the exact parameter layers left by these band estimates are

```text
(r,c)=(3,12);
       (2,11),(2,12);
       (1,10),(1,11),(1,12);
       (0,9),(0,10),(0,11),(0,12).                 (P23.11z5zzza4)
```

Top-label triples are not included in this proposition and require a
separate boundary-rank analysis.  The tetrahedral endpoint triples
inside `(P23.11z5zzza4)` still require their compensating `P_eq` and
`U` payments.

**Proof.**  Pair `B` as `(a,p)|(s,t)`, with the minus label in the
first pair, and call the two fusion intervals `I,J`.  Pairing any label
`ell` of `B` first gives

```text
3<=min(ell,k-ell)+1,
```

so

```text
2<=ell<=k-2.                                        (P23.11z5zzza5)
```

Their intersection is a three-entry nonzero step-two interval

```text
K={L,L+2,L+4}.
```

The level-five possibility in which `K` runs simultaneously from one
to `k` does not occur.  Indeed, `(P23.11z5zzza5)` would put all labels
of `B` in `{2,3}`; plus/minus disjointness then makes `B` either
`{2,3,3,3}` or `{3,2,2,2}`, both of odd total parity.

For output two, the internal band `K times K` contains seven ordered
pairs.  There is no lower loss.  If `K` does not end at `k`, there is
no upper loss either.  If it does, only `(k,k)` is lost.  The intervals
`I,J` cannot coincide in this case, since an interval ending at `k`
determines its unordered input pair and equality would repeat the
minus label on the all-plus pair.  One interval therefore contains
`L-2`, and `(L-2,L)` restores the lost output-two pair.  Hence
`m_2(B)>=7`.

For output four, the internal band has nine pairs.  If `K` begins at
one, only `(1,1)` is lost, leaving eight.  If `K` ends at `k`, the
three reflected upper losses leave six, while the same strict extension
adds the admissible pair `(L-2,L+2)`, leaving seven.  Away from both
walls all nine pairs survive.  This proves `m_4(B)>=7`.

For output six, the internal band again has nine pairs.  Away from the
upper wall, a lower endpoint at one removes the three pairs

```text
(1,1),                    (1,3),(3,1),
```

and the even lower endpoint two removes only `(2,2)`.  If `K` ends at
`k`, the reflected upper triangle removes six.  A simultaneous lower
loss can occur only for `K={2,4,6}` at level six; then the strict
extension contains zero and `(0,6)` restores the lost unit.  The odd
simultaneous endpoint is the excluded level-five case.  Therefore
`m_6(B)>=3`, completing
`(P23.11z5zzza1)`.

The universal invariant-triple profile gives

```text
m_0(A)=1,                    m_2(A)>=2,
m_4(A)>=1.
```

Orthogonality across the selected cut now yields

```text
N>=3+2*7+7+3r=24+3r.
```

Maximality gives `T<=3c`, and the parity count of Corollary 23A9ZC
gives `c<=12`.  Comparing `3c` with `24+3r` proves
`(P23.11z5zzza3)` and leaves precisely
`(P23.11z5zzza4)`.  QED.

The same three-entry band count gives the exact raw supply needed in the
top-label chamber.  There the high-rank cuts are confined to one
distinguished complementary graph.

**Corollary 23A9ZZ2 (mixed-parity top-label `d=3`, low-repetition
chambers).**  Keep the hypotheses and notation of Corollary 23A9ZP, and
assume

```text
d=3.
```

If `k` is a minus label, let `g` be the number of active indexed cuts in
the `k`-minus orientation.  Equivalently, these are the active indexed
plus pairs whose labels sum to `k`.  If `k` is a plus label, let `h` be
the number of active indexed cuts, in both minus orientations, whose plus
pair contains that indexed copy of `k`.  Then

```text
t>=3 and g<=4,       or       t>=3 and h<=4,
t=2  and g<=2,       or       t=2  and h<=2          (P23.11z5zzzb1)
```

implies

```text
T<=N.                                                (P23.11z5zzzb2)
```

**Proof.**  Write the selected top triple as

```text
A={k,u,k-u},                    t=min(u,k-u).
```

Its exact profile `(P23.11z5zzp1)` is

```text
m_(2j)(A)=1                         (0<=j<=t).
```

The proof of Proposition 23A9ZZ1 applies verbatim to the selected
fourfold complement `B`: it uses only `m_0(B)=3`, support disjointness,
and the fact that the configuration is not all even.  In particular,

```text
m_0(B)=3,       m_2(B)>=7,       m_4(B)>=7,
m_6(B)>=3.                                         (P23.11z5zzzb3)
```

Orthogonality across `A|B` therefore gives

```text
t=2:                  N>=3+7+7=17,
t>=3:                 N>=3+7+7+3=20.               (P23.11z5zzzb4)
```

Suppose first that `k` is a minus label.  A triple containing that
minus label is invariant precisely when its two plus labels sum to
`k`; these are the `g` distinguished cuts.  Every other active cut is
in the opposite-minus orientation, and its fourfold complement contains
`k`, so the simple-current rule bounds its rank by one.  Maximality
bounds each distinguished cut by three.  Consequently

```text
T<=3g+(c-g)=c+2g.                                  (P23.11z5zzzb5)
```

If instead `k` is a plus label, every cut whose plus pair omits `k`
leaves it in the fourfold complement and again has rank at most one.
Only the `h` cuts whose pair contains `k` can have rank up to three, so

```text
T<=c+2h.                                           (P23.11z5zzzb6)
```

Mixed parity gives `c<=12`.  Under the first line of
`(P23.11z5zzzb1)`, either weighted bound is at most twenty; under the
second line it is at most sixteen.  Comparison with
`(P23.11z5zzzb4)` proves `(P23.11z5zzzb2)`.  QED.

The repetition excluded from the first line of Corollary 23A9ZZ2 creates
an indexed equal-pair reservoir larger than the entire remaining rank
deficit.

**Corollary 23A9ZZ3 (complete mixed-parity top-label `d=3`, `t>=3`
layer).**  Under the hypotheses and notation of Corollary 23A9ZZ2,
assume `t>=3`.  Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                          (P23.11z5zzzc1)
```

**Proof.**  By Corollary 23A9ZZ2 it remains only to treat `g>=5` when
`k` is a minus label and `h>=5` when `k` is a plus label.

First let `k` be a minus label.  On the five indexed plus positions,
join two vertices when their labels are complementary under
`x mapsto k-x`.  A nonfixed component with multiplicities `r,s` is
the complete bipartite graph `K_(r,s)`, while the fixed value `k/2`
gives a clique.  Partitioning five vertices among these components
shows that a graph with at least five edges is one of

```text
K_(2,3),              K_4 plus an isolated vertex,
K_5.                                                   (P23.11z5zzzc2)
```

Here the last two graphs are fixed-point cliques.  Indeed, a nonfixed
component on `n` vertices has at most `floor(n^2/4)` edges; two or more
nontrivial components have at most four edges in total, and a fixed
clique on at most three vertices together with the remaining vertices
also has at most four.

The fixed `K_5` cannot occur.  In that case `k=2x`, all five plus labels
equal `x`, and the selected complement is `{a,x,x,x}`.  Directly
intersecting `a star_k x` with `x star_k x` gives

```text
3=m_0(a,x,x,x)=min(a,k-a,x)+1.                    (P23.11z5zzzc3)
```

If `x` is odd, invariant parity makes `a` odd, whereas the minimum in
`(P23.11z5zzzc3)` would have to equal two.  If `x` is even, invariant
parity makes `a` even and all seven labels are even.  The first
alternative is impossible and the second is outside the mixed-parity
chamber.

Suppose the graph is `K_(2,3)`, with complementary labels `x,y` and
the label occurring three times denoted by `y`.  The selected top edge
removes one copy of each, so

```text
B={a,x,y,y},                    m_0(B)=3.
```

For each of the three indexed equal pairs of copies of `y`, its
`P_eq` summand is

```text
m(k,a,x,x,y)=m(a,x,y,y)=3.                         (P23.11z5zzzc4)
```

The equality follows by applying the simple current to the factors
`k,a` and then to all four remaining nontrivial factors; it merely
interchanges `x` and `y=k-x`.  Thus `P_eq>=9`.

If instead four vertices carry the fixed label `x=k/2`, write the fifth
label as `z`.  Then `B={a,x,x,z}`.  Deleting any indexed equal pair of
copies of `x` gives

```text
m(k,a,x,x,z)=m(a,x,x,z)=3,                         (P23.11z5zzzc5)
```

because `x` is fixed by the simple current.  There are six such pairs,
so in this case `P_eq>=18`.  In either graph `g<=6`, and hence

```text
T<=c+2g<=24<20+9<=N+P_eq.                          (P23.11z5zzzc6)
```

Now let `k` be a plus label.  Put `x=k-q`, where `q` is the selected
minus label, and let `n_x` be its multiplicity among the four non-`k`
plus positions.  If the other minus label `a` differs from `q`, then
the two possible companion labels `k-q,k-a` are distinct and

```text
h=n_(k-q)+n_(k-a)<=4.
```

Thus `h>=5` forces `a=q` and `h=2n_x`, with `n_x=3` or four.  The
equal-minus term of `P_eq` is

```text
m(P)=m(q,x,x,z)=3                  if n_x=3,
m(P)=m(q,x,x,x)=3                 if n_x=4,          (P23.11z5zzzc7)
```

where the first line writes the remaining non-`k` label as `z`; pair
the indexed labels `k,x` first to obtain the displayed selected
complement.  In the first case, each of the three equal pairs of copies
of `x` also contributes

```text
m(q,q,k,x,z)=m(q,x,x,z)=3.
```

Hence `P_eq>=12`.  In the second case the six equal pairs contribute
three each, and `P_eq>=21`.  Therefore

```text
n_x=3:       T<=c+12<=24<20+12,
n_x=4:       T<=c+16<=28<20+21.                    (P23.11z5zzzc8)
```

Together with `(P23.11z5zzzb4)`, these estimates prove
`(P23.11z5zzzc1)`.  QED.

At `t=2`, a repeated vertex in the distinguished graph pays its own
high-rank edge.  This is the one-unit refinement missing from the raw
seventeen-dimensional band estimate.

**Corollary 23A9ZZ4 (complete mixed-parity top-label `d=3`, `t=2`
layer).**  Under the hypotheses and notation of Corollary 23A9ZZ2,
assume `t=2`.  Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                          (P23.11z5zzzd1)
```

**Proof.**  Corollary 23A9ZZ2 gives the result for `g<=2` or `h<=2`,
and `(P23.11z5zzzb4)` gives `N>=17` throughout.

Let first `k` be a minus label.  The `k`-orientation has exactly `g`
active cuts.  Since the selected plus labels are `2,k-2`, both plus
parities occur when `k` is odd, while at least two plus labels are even
when `k` is even.  In the latter case all five plus labels cannot be
even: invariant parity would then make the other minus label even and
the whole configuration would be all even.  Thus both plus parities
occur in either case.  For the other minus label, the elementary parity
count `(P23.11z5zzc12)` therefore gives at most six candidate pairs.
Consequently

```text
c<=g+6,                    T<=c+2g<=3g+6.           (P23.11z5zzzd2)
```

Thus `g=3` gives `T<=15<N`.  If `g=4`, the complementary-value graph
has a repeated label `x` incident to an active edge with label
`y=k-x`.  Delete an indexed equal pair of copies of `x`.  If `Z`
denotes the other two plus positions, its `P_eq` contribution and the
rank `D` of any indexed `x,y` cut in that component satisfy

```text
m(k,a,y,Z)=m(k-a,y,Z)
 =m(a,x,Z)
 =D>=1.                                             (P23.11z5zzzd3)
```

More explicitly, after the deletion the three remaining plus labels
are `y,Z`; fuse `k` with `a`, and then apply the simple current
simultaneously to `k-a` and `y`.  The result is the fourfold complement
of the chosen `x,y` cut.  Hence `P_eq>=1`, while
`(P23.11z5zzzd2)` gives `T<=18<=N+P_eq`.

For `g>=5`, the graph classification
`(P23.11z5zzzc2)--(P23.11z5zzzc5)` applies without using `t>=3`.
It gives `g<=6` and `P_eq>=9`.  Therefore

```text
T<=c+2g<=24<17+9<=N+P_eq.                          (P23.11z5zzzd4)
```

Now let `k` be a plus label.  Retain `x=k-q` for the selected minus
label and put `y=k-a` for the other one.  If `h=3`, then `q!=a` and
the three active `k`-pairs use the two distinct companion labels
`x,y`.  One companion label occurs in at least two active indexed
positions; interchange the two minus orientations if necessary and
denote it by `y`.
Deleting an indexed equal pair of copies of `y` gives

```text
m(q,a,k,Z)=m(q,y,Z)
 =D_y>=1,                                           (P23.11z5zzzd5)
```

where `Z` denotes the other two non-`k` positions and `D_y` is the
rank of an active cut using `{k,y}` in the `a`-orientation.  The first
equality pairs `a` with `k`.  Thus `P_eq>=1`, and

```text
T<=c+2h<=18<=17+P_eq.
```

Suppose `h=4`.  If `q=a`, then the equal-minus term pairs the selected
`k,x` first and is exactly the selected complement:

```text
m(P)=m(q,x,R_1,R_2,R_3)=3.                         (P23.11z5zzzd6)
```

If `q!=a`, all four non-`k` positions carry `x` or `y`.  For
multiplicities `1+3`, the three indexed equal pairs on the repeated
side contribute at least one each by `(P23.11z5zzzd5)`.  For
multiplicities `2+2`, delete the equal pair of copies of the selected
label `x`; the same calculation identifies its `P_eq` term with the
selected rank three.  Hence `P_eq>=3` in every `h=4` case, and

```text
T<=c+8<=20<=17+P_eq.                               (P23.11z5zzzd7)
```

Finally `h>=5` has the equal-minus classification from the proof of
Corollary 23A9ZZ3: `h=6` or eight, and `P_eq>=12`.  Since `h<=8`,

```text
T<=c+2h<=28<17+12<=N+P_eq.
```

All distinguished counts have now been covered, proving
`(P23.11z5zzzd1)`.  QED.

The last top-edge profile has only the channels zero and two.  Its cut
count can be evaluated exactly because the labels one and `k-1` reduce
every complementary fourfold rank to two endpoint indicators.

**Lemma 23A9ZZ5 (exact mixed top-edge arithmetic at overlap three).**
Let a mixed-parity, support-disjoint configuration have a maximal top
cut of rank three with `t=1`.  Put `H=k-1`.  Then:

(i) If `k` is a minus label, then `T<=9`, except for

```text
k=6,       minus labels {4,6},
plus labels {1,2,3,3,5},                            (P23.11z5zzzze1)
```

where `T=11`.

(ii) If `k` is a plus label, then `T<=10`.

**Proof.**  Every label in the selected fourfold complement lies in
`[2,k-2]`, by the interval-size argument `(P23.11z5zzza5)`.  In
particular `k>=6`.

For (i), write the other minus label as `a` and the three plus labels
outside the selected pair `{1,H}` as `r_1,r_2,r_3`.  For
`{i,j,l}={1,2,3}`, use brackets for indicator functions and put

```text
W=sum_(i<j)[r_i+r_j=k]{
      [a+r_l in {k-2,k+2}]+2[a+r_l=k]},

E_1=sum_i[|a-r_i|=1][|r_j-r_l|=1],

E_H=sum_i[a+r_i in {k-1,k+1}]
             [r_j+r_l in {k-1,k+1}],

E_0=[a=k-2]N_(r_1,r_2,r_3)^k,

E_2=sum_l[r_l=2]N_(r_i,r_j)^a.                    (P23.11z5zzzze2)
```

These are exact, not upper bounds.  The selected `{1,H}` cut in the
`k`-orientation contributes three.  A residual pair with sum `k` has
complement `{a,1,H,r_l}` and hence rank

```text
N_(a,r_l)^(k-2)+N_(a,r_l)^k
 =[a+r_l in {k-2,k+2}]+2[a+r_l=k],
```

which gives `W`.  In the `a`-orientation, pairs of types
`{1,r_i}`, `{H,r_i}`, `{1,H}`, and `{r_i,r_j}` give respectively
`E_1,E_H,E_0,E_2`.  Thus

```text
T=3+W+E_1+E_H+E_0+E_2.                            (P23.11z5zzzze3)
```

Let `rho` be the number of indexed residual pairs with sum `k`.
Sorting the three residual labels, the complementary-value graph has
the four possible forms displayed in the first two columns below.
Substitution in `(P23.11z5zzzze2)`, together with
`m_0(a,r_1,r_2,r_3)=3`, gives the complete indicator table

```text
rho   residual complementary form       W       E_1+E_H+E_0+E_2

0     no complementary pair             0              <=4

1     {x,k-x,z}                          <=2            <=4
      exceptional {3,3,2}, k=6,a=4       2               6

2     {x,y,y}, x+y=k                    <=2            <=4
      or {x,x,y}

3     {x,x,x}, 2x=k                      <=3            <=1.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzzze4)
```

For completeness, the table is obtained only from the following
mutually exclusive endpoint equalities: an `E_1` indicator uses
`a=r_i+-1` and `r_j=r_l+-1`; an `E_H` indicator uses
`a+r_i=k+-1` and `r_j+r_l=k+-1`; `E_0` fixes `a=k-2`; and an `E_2`
indicator fixes one residual label at two.  In the `rho=1` row insert
`r_j=k-r_i`; five endpoint indicators force simultaneously

```text
a=k-2,       z=2,       r_i=r_j=k/2,       k=6,
```

which is the exceptional row.  Otherwise at most four occur.  In the
`rho=2` row the forms `x,y,y` and `x,x,y` leave at most two indicators
of each boundary type.  In the `rho=3` row all three labels are fixed
at `k/2`; support disjointness eliminates simultaneous `E_1` and
`E_H`, and only one endpoint indicator remains.  When `rho=0`, three
indicators of one boundary type force two residual labels to differ by
one, excluding three of the opposite boundary type; `E_0` and `E_2`
are their endpoint specializations and do not increase the total past
four.  This proves every entry of `(P23.11z5zzzze4)`.

Equations `(P23.11z5zzzze3)--(P23.11z5zzzze4)` give `T<=9`, except
in `(P23.11z5zzzze1)`, where they give `T=3+2+6=11`.

For (ii), take first the selected minus label to be one, so the selected
plus pair is `{k,H}`.  The case in which the selected minus label is
`H` is the same calculation with the boundary labels one and `H`
interchanged and every fusion interval reflected.  Write the other
minus label as `a`, put `y=k-a`, and retain `r_1,r_2,r_3` for the
residual plus labels.  The selected cut again contributes three.
Expanding the remaining cuts exactly as above gives

```text
F=sum_i[r_i=k-2]N_(r_j,r_l)^y
 +sum_(i<j)[|r_i-r_j|=1][|r_l-a|=1]

 +sum_i[r_i=y]{N_(r_j,r_l)^(k-2)+N_(r_j,r_l)^k}

 +sum_i[a+r_i in {k-1,k+1}]N_(r_j,r_l)^(k-1)
 +sum_l[r_l=2]N_(r_i,r_j)^a,

T=3+F.                                             (P23.11z5zzzze5)
```

Let `n` be the multiplicity of `y` among the three residual labels.
Substitute `y=k-a` in `(P23.11z5zzzze5)`.  The same four endpoint
alternatives used in `(P23.11z5zzzze4)` give

```text
n                    0        1        2        3
F                    <=3      <=7      <=6      <=3,
T                    <=6      <=10     <=9      <=6.           (P23.11z5zzzze6)
```

To check the only two larger columns, when `n=1` the two labels outside
`y` can activate at most two adjacent-pair indicators and two reflected
indicators in addition to the two channels of the unique `{k,y}` cut
and one endpoint indicator.  When `n=2`, the two indexed `{k,y}` cuts
have the same unused labels; rank two for both excludes one adjacent
and one reflected indicator by support disjointness, lowering the
total to six.  For `n=0` there is no high-rank `{k,y}` cut, and for
`n=3` every other endpoint condition would force `a=y`, again forbidden
by support disjointness.  This proves `(P23.11z5zzzze6)` and part (ii).
QED.

**Corollary 23A9ZZ6 (complete mixed-parity top-label `d=3` layer).**
Keep the hypotheses and notation of Corollary 23A9ZZ2.  Then

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                          (P23.11z5zzzf1)
```

Consequently every finite mixed-parity top-label chamber with `d>=3`
is closed.

**Proof.**  Corollaries 23A9ZZ3 and 23A9ZZ4 handle `t>=3` and `t=2`.
It remains to take `t=1`.  The exact top profile and
`(P23.11z5zzzb3)` give

```text
N=m_0(B)+m_2(B)>=3+7=10.                           (P23.11z5zzzf2)
```

If `k` is a plus label, Lemma 23A9ZZ5(ii) gives `T<=10<=N`.  If `k`
is a minus label, Lemma 23A9ZZ5(i) gives `T<=9<N` except in
`(P23.11z5zzzze1)`.  Direct fusion there gives

```text
N=10,       P_eq=1,       U_1=2,       U_2=4,
T=11.                                                (P23.11z5zzzf3)
```

The sole equal plus pair is the indexed pair of threes; deleting it
leaves `m(6,4,1,2,5)=1`, so its `P_eq` term pays the missing unit.
Thus `T=N+P_eq` in the exceptional case as well.  This proves
`(P23.11z5zzzf1)`.  Corollary 23A9ZY already handles every `d>=4`,
giving the final assertion.  QED.

The non-top residue of Proposition 23A9ZZ1 is also closed.  The
additional observation is that the multiplicity profile of an invariant
triple is concave until it vanishes.

**Corollary 23A9ZZ7 (complete mixed-parity non-top `d=3` layer).**
Under the hypotheses and notation of Proposition 23A9ZZ1,

```text
T<=N+P_eq.                                          (P23.11z5zzzg1)
```

In particular, all ten cells in `(P23.11z5zzza4)` are paid; no `U`
term is needed.

**Proof.**  Put

```text
a_j=m_(2j)(A).
```

In the tetrahedral notation `(P25.29e3)`, throughout the positive
support,

```text
a_j=U_j-L_j+1,
L_j=max(-j,j-x-y,-x),
U_j=min(j,z,w,z+w-j).                              (P23.11z5zzzg2)
```

The maximum of affine functions is convex and the minimum is concave.
Consequently the successive differences of `a_j` are nonincreasing.
Since `a_0=1` and `a_1>=2`, this gives

```text
a_3=3  ==> a_2>=3,
a_3=2  ==> a_2>=2,
a_3=1  ==> a_2>=2.                                (P23.11z5zzzg3)
```

Combining `(P23.11z5zzzg3)` with `(P23.11z5zzza1)` yields

```text
r=3: N>=3+2*7+3*7+3*3=47,
r=2: N>=3+2*7+2*7+2*3=37,
r=1: N>=3+2*7+2*7+  3=34.                        (P23.11z5zzzg4)
```

Thus all `r=2,3` cells are raw, as are the `r=1,c<=11` cells.  It
remains to treat `r=1,c=12` and the four `r=0` cells.

First suppose `r=1,c=12`.  If `k>=9` and `m_8(A)>0`, then

```text
m_8(B)>=3.                                         (P23.11z5zzzg5)
```

Indeed, write the common three-entry interval for `B` as
`K={L,L+2,L+4}`.  There are three upper-endpoint cases.  If
`L+4<=k-2` and `L>=2`, the three ordered pairs

```text
(L,L+4), (L+2,L+2), (L+4,L)
```

give output eight: their common sum is `2L+4`, which lies in
`[8,2k-8]`.  At the lower endpoint `L=1`, use instead
`(3,5),(5,3),(5,5)`; the last pair is admissible because `k>=9`.

If `K` ends at `k-1`, so `K={k-5,k-3,k-1}`, use

```text
(k-5,k-5),             (k-5,k-3), (k-3,k-5).
```

Their sums are `2k-10,2k-8,2k-8`, respectively, and hence satisfy both
triangle walls for output eight.  This includes `k=9`, where the three
pairs are `(4,4),(4,6),(6,4)`.

Finally, if `K` ends at `k`, the internal pair `(k-4,k-4)` survives.
The strict interval extension used in Proposition 23A9ZZ1 contains
`k-6` in one of the two paired intervals.  According to its orientation,
the two additional ordered pairs are either

```text
(k-6,k-4),                    (k-6,k-2)
```

or their reversals.  Their sums are `2k-10,2k-8`.  At `k=9` these,
together with `(5,5)`, are `(3,5),(3,7),(5,5)` up to reversal, so the
lower endpoint also causes no loss.  This proves `(P23.11z5zzzg5)`,
and hence
`N>=37>36>=T`.

For `k>=9` it remains that `m_8(A)=0`.  The complete tetrahedral table
`(P23.11z5zzt7)` gives

```text
{1,2,3},            {1,k-3,k-2},       {2,k-2,k-2},
{2,k-3,k-1},        {3,k-2,k-1}.       (P23.11z5zzzg6)
```

Four of these triples contain a wall-distance-one label.  If it is the
minus label, all six cuts in the other orientation leave it in their
complements and have rank at most two.  If it is a plus label, its
degree among the six active parity-compatible edges is at most four,
so at least two active cuts omit it and again have rank at most two.
Thus `T<=36-2=34<=N`.  The remaining triple `{2,k-2,k-2}` has exact
profile

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)=(1,3,2,1),
```

and therefore `N>=3+3*7+2*7+3=41`.

For completeness, the exceptional small levels are a finite symbolic
fusion table.  Substitute

```text
i star_k j={|i-j|,|i-j|+2,...,min(i+j,2k-i-j)}
                                                               (P23.11z5zzzg7)
```

in `m_0(B)=3`, `m_6(A)=1`, and `c=12`.  After disjoint support,
mixed parity, and maximality, the complete rows are

```text
k     possible unordered A                         lower N   upper T

6     {1,2,3},{1,3,4},{2,3,5},{3,4,5}                 34        30
      {2,3,3},{3,3,4}                                 48        36

7     {1,2,3},{1,4,5},{3,5,6}                         38        30
      {2,5,5}                                         45        30

8     {1,2,3},{1,5,6},{2,5,7},{3,6,7}                 47        30.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzzg8)
```

These bounds are obtained entry by entry from `(P23.11z5zzzg7)`;
there is no level-five row by `(P23.11z5zzza5)`.  This finishes
`r=1,c=12`.

Now let `r=0`.  The endpoint specialization of `(P23.11z5zzzg2)`
gives exactly

```text
{1,1,2},             {1,k-2,k-1},       {2,k-1,k-1}.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzzg9)
```

In either repeated family, disjoint support places the repeated
wall-distance-one pair on the plus side.  Its exact profile is
`(1,2,1)`, so

```text
N>=3+2*7+7=24,              P_eq>=m_2(B)>=7.        (P23.11z5zzzg10)
```

This pays `c<=10`.  For `c>=11`, each minus orientation has at most six
candidate cuts.  The orientation of the selected cut therefore has at
least `c-6` active cuts.  Only the selected indexed pair removes both
copies of the repeated wall label; each of the other cuts leaves one
in its complement and has rank at most two.  Relative to `3c`, this
saves at least `c-7`, and hence

```text
c=11: T<=33-4=29,
c=12: T<=36-5=31.                                 (P23.11z5zzzg11)
```

Both are at most the lower bound `N+P_eq>=31`.

It remains to pay the distinct triple `{1,k-2,k-1}`.  Put
`H_2=k-2,H_1=k-1`.  Since `c<=8` is raw, suppose `c>=9`.  The boundary
fusion conditions are

```text
1 in x star_k y      iff |x-y|=1,
H_1 in x star_k y    iff |(k-x)-y|=1,
H_2 in x star_k y    iff
   x+y in {k-2,k,k+2} and |x-y|<=k-2.              (P23.11z5zzzg12)
```

Apply these three tests to the selected minus label, intersect the two
remaining intervals by `(P23.11z5zzzg7)`, and impose
`2<=ell<=k-2` on every label of `B`.  The complete dense table is

```text
k       number of (selected minus,B) rows       lower N   upper T

6                         3                         24        26
7                        14                         25        22
8                         9                         29        18
9                         5                         30        14
k>=10                     0                          -         -.
                                                               (P23.11z5zzzg13)
```

This is a symbolic boundary-neighbour table: the three lines in
`(P23.11z5zzzg12)` leave only the displayed affine endpoint choices,
and direct substitution in `(P23.11z5zzzg7)` gives the last two
columns.  At level six the three rows are

```text
q=1,       B={2,3,3,4},       (N,T)=(24,21);
q=4,       B={4,2,3,3},       (N,T)=(24,26);
q=5,       B={2,3,3,4},       (N,T)=(24,21).       (P23.11z5zzzg14)
```

Only the middle row is not raw.  Its equal-minus and equal-plus
deletions give `P_eq=7` (and, independently, `U=11`), so
`26<=24+7`.  Thus every row of `(P23.11z5zzzg13)` is paid.  This
exhausts the ten cells in `(P23.11z5zzza4)` and proves
`(P23.11z5zzzg1)`.  QED.

The precise obstruction to assembling a one-minus boundary theorem by
iteration is already visible before choosing a path basis.  Expand an
arbitrary tensor-ring element as

```text
H=sum_(b>=0) H_b tensor e_b
```

and, with nonexistent endpoint rows interpreted as zero, define

```text
(nabla H)_b=e_1H_b-H_(b-1)-H_(b+1).                  (P23.11z5u)
```

At finite level the same formula is used on `0<=b<=k`, with the forbidden
path-graph branches omitted.

**Lemma 23A9K (higher-boundary tower and nonclosure).**  For every `m>=0`,

```text
(id tensor tau)(Delta^m H)=(nabla^m H)_0.             (P23.11z5v)
```

In particular, a signed remainder with minus labels `q_1,...,q_m` and plus
labels `p_1,...,p_n` has partial character

```text
(nabla^m[
  product_(i=1)^m D_(q_i) product_(j=1)^n S_(p_j)])_0. (P23.11z5w)
```

If `c_j^(k)(b)=(N_1^j)_(0,b)` is the number of length-`j` paths from zero
to `b` in the finite fusion graph (and `c_j(b)` is its ordinary half-line
analogue), then the same identity expands as

```text
(nabla^m H)_0
 =sum_(j=0)^m (-1)^j binom(m,j)e_1^(m-j)
    sum_b c_j^(k)(b)H_b.                              (P23.11z5w1)
```

Thus the `m`th boundary uses the complete alternating row jet through
distance `m`; a first-boundary injection controls only its first two rows.

The identical statements hold in every `SU(2)_k`.  The first-boundary
positivity assertion does not iterate formally: already

```text
nabla D_q=e_q delta_0-e_0 delta_q,                    (P23.11z5x)
```

so `nabla` does not preserve either the componentwise-positive row cone or
the multiplicative cone generated by the `D_q,S_p`.

More strongly, first-boundary positivity does not control the second
boundary even on the symmetric componentwise-positive tensor cone.  In the
ordinary ring, and in every `SU(2)_k` with `k>=3`, put

```text
H=e_1 tensor e_0+e_0 tensor e_1
  +e_2 tensor e_1+e_1 tensor e_2.
```

Then

```text
(nabla H)_0=0,                  (nabla^2 H)_0=-e_3.
```

This `H` is not asserted to be a product of the special generators
`D_q,S_p`; the example rules out only an abstract induction from
one-boundary positivity on a larger positive cone.  Inside the actual
`D/S` cone, Lemma 23A2 gives the corresponding chain-level obstruction:
for `[3^-,3^-,1^+,2^+,2^+]` the tensor product of the factorwise Koszul
complexes has genuine odd invariant homology in outer grade `a^6`.
Consequently independent one-minus resolutions do not assemble into the
required higher-minus resolution.

**Proof.**  Multiplication by
`Delta=e_1 tensor e_0-e_0 tensor e_1` changes the row vector of `H` by
`(P23.11z5u)`, because the fundamental fusion matrix is the adjacency
matrix of the ordinary or finite `A`-path.  Iteration followed by extraction
of the zeroth second-coordinate row proves `(P23.11z5v)`.  Proposition 23
then gives `(P23.11z5w)`.  The two commuting parts of `nabla` are
first-coordinate multiplication by `e_1` and minus the fundamental
path-graph adjacency operator.  Their binomial expansion gives
`(P23.11z5w1)`, and `(P23.11z5x)` is Proposition 24's boundary vector
`(P24.4)`.

For the displayed positive tensor, its only nonzero rows are

```text
H_0=e_1,             H_1=e_0+e_2,             H_2=e_1.
```

The fusion identities `e_1^2=e_0+e_2` and
`e_1e_2=e_1+e_3` give

```text
(nabla H)_0=e_1^2-(e_0+e_2)=0,
(nabla H)_1=e_1(e_0+e_2)-e_1-e_1=e_3.
```

Hence `(nabla^2H)_0=-(nabla H)_1=-e_3`.  These identities remain unchanged
at level `k>=3`.  The final assertion is exactly Lemma 23A2.  QED.

The second boundary nevertheless has an exact exterior Cauchy--Binet
assembly.  It also shows why proving every complementary packet separately
cannot work, even on an actual support-disjoint `D/S` tensor.

**Lemma 23A9O (mixed exterior assembly and packet obstruction).**  For an
indexed plus word `P=(p_1,...,p_n)`, put

```text
X_U=product_(i in U)N_(p_i),             X_empty=I,
```

and, for positive labels `a,b`, define

```text
M_U(a,b)
 = (X_U)_(a,b)(X_(U^c))_(0,0)
   +(X_U)_(0,0)(X_(U^c))_(a,b)
   -(X_U)_(a,0)(X_(U^c))_(b,0)
   -(X_U)_(b,0)(X_(U^c))_(a,0).          (P23.11z5xd1)
```

Then the two-minus boundary defect of Proposition 4 is exactly

```text
E_P(a,b)=1/2 sum_(U subset [n]) M_U(a,b).             (P23.11z5xd2)
```

The same identity holds in every finite fusion ring `SU(2)_k`.
Termwise nonnegativity in `(P23.11z5xd2)` is false even under the
disjoint-support reduction.  In the ordinary ring, take

```text
P=[1,1,1,1,1,1],             a=b=3.
```

If `U` and `U^c` each contain three indexed factors, then

```text
X_U=X_(U^c)=N_1^3,                 M_U(3,3)=-2.       (P23.11z5xd3)
```

There are twenty oriented subsets of this type, so even the entire balanced
cardinality shell is negative:

```text
1/2 sum_(|U|=3)M_U(3,3)=-20.                         (P23.11z5xd3a)
```

Nevertheless the complete defect is positive:

```text
sum_(r in 3 star 3)c_P(r,0)=175,
c_P(3,3)=20,                    E_P(3,3)=155.         (P23.11z5xd4)
```

Thus an exterior-power proof of the all-label second boundary must transfer
capacity between distinct complementary subsets; it cannot be a positive
sum of the natural mixed `2` by `2` determinant packets.

**Proof.**  On the exterior square, the ordered plus transfer is

```text
product_i dGamma(N_(p_i))
 =sum_(U subset [n]) X_U tensor X_(U^c).              (P23.11z5xd5)
```

The whole sum commutes with exchange of the tensor coordinates and hence
preserves the exterior square.  Taking its coefficient from
`e_b wedge e_0` to `e_a wedge e_0`, whose standard squared norm is two,
gives one half of the sum in `(P23.11z5xd1)`.  Proposition 24 identifies
this boundary coefficient with `E_P(a,b)`, proving `(P23.11z5xd2)`.
The argument uses only symmetric commuting fusion matrices and is unchanged
at finite level.

For `(P23.11z5xd3)`, odd path parity gives

```text
(N_1^3)_(0,0)=(N_1^3)_(3,3)=0,
(N_1^3)_(3,0)=(N_1^3)_(0,3)=1,
```

so `(P23.11z5xd1)` is `-1-1=-2`.  Finally,
`V_1^(tensor j)` has multiplicity sums

```text
1, 2, 6, 20
```

at even lengths `j=0,2,4,6`, while the complementary invariant
multiplicities are respectively `5,2,1,1`.  Including the indexed choices
gives

```text
175=1*1*5+15*2*2+15*6*1+1*20*1.
```

The only split contributing to `c_P(3,3)` is three against three, and each
side contains `V_3` once, so it contributes `binom(6,3)=20`.  This proves
`(P23.11z5xd4)`.  QED.

The strict exact C++ diagnostic
`character_ring_iter/analyze_su2_mixed_exterior_packets.cpp` independently
constructs all fusion matrices and finds `(P23.11z5xd3)` as its first
disjoint-support failure through the declared bounds.  Its source SHA-256
is
`c716227b0d78f2e6dfadddbecdb7fb467be68fe0ce9f9027f23f63c69a0346b0`;
the replay is `certificates/su2_mixed_exterior_packets.log`.  The
counterexample is the exact path calculation above, not the bounded search.

Thus `(P23.11b)` is a chain-level realization of the FDC quotient, but its
factorwise tensor product cannot prove outer positivity by homology
concentration, and the minimal all-scalar evaluation differential is too
small.  Any successful Dirac/Koszul construction must retain non-scalar
even `H_2` carriers, allow operations which redistribute rather than consume
the whole active block, or use both.  The fixed-support injection
`(P23.11n)` remains valid; the failure is precisely cross-support capacity.

This supplies a concrete Koszul/Dirac object, but superdimension alone does
not give the required sign.  In particular, a natural attempt to use the
positive product formula for a higher-multiplicity rank-two Jacobi system
fails exactly.

For `m>=1`, put

```text
dnu_m=(x-y)^(2m)dmu(x)dmu(y).                            (P23.12)
```

Split `2m` minus factors into two groups of `m`.  The signed integral is

```text
< product_(i=1)^m D_(q_i),
  product_(plus j)S_(r_j) product_(i=m+1)^(2m)D_(q_i) >_(nu_m). (P23.13)
```

The measure `(P23.12)` is a rank-two `C_2/BC_2` Jacobi weight.  One could
therefore hope that every `m`-fold row product belongs to the nonnegative
cone of its triangular orthogonal basis and that every `S_q` preserves that
cone.  Both necessary statements are false at the first available degrees.

**Proposition 23B (`BC_2` hypergroup-cone obstruction).**  Let
`P_lambda^(m)` be obtained by Gram--Schmidt orthogonalization of the
`USp(4)` characters `Psi_lambda` against `(P23.12)`, with leading
coefficient one and the lower dominance terms taken first.

1.  At `m=1`, this is the ordinary `USp(4)` character basis, and

```text
S_2=Psi_(2,0)-Psi_(1,1)+Psi_(0,0).                      (P23.14)
```

Thus `S_2` has coefficient `-1` and does not preserve its nonnegative cone.

2.  At `m=2`,

```text
P_(1,1)^(2)=Psi_(1,1)+3/5,
<P_(1,1)^(2),P_(1,1)^(2)>_(nu_2)=42/5,                 (P23.15)

<D_1 D_3,P_(1,1)^(2)>_(nu_2)=-14/5.                   (P23.16)
```

Hence the coefficient of `P_(1,1)^(2)` in the two-row half-product
`D_1D_3=Psi_(2,0)` is `-1/3`.

**Proof.**  Formula `(P23.14)` is `(P8.2)` at `q=2`.  For the second
statement use `Psi_(1,1)=xy+1`, `Psi_(2,0)=x^2+xy+y^2-2`, and the semicircle
moments

```text
integral x^2 dmu=1,  integral x^4 dmu=2,
integral x^6 dmu=5.                                  (P23.17)
```

Direct expansion of `(x-y)^4` gives

```text
integral (x-y)^4 dmu^2=10,
integral (x-y)^4 Psi_(1,1)dmu^2=-6,
```

which yields the first line of `(P23.15)`.  The same expansion gives the
norm and pairing in `(P23.15)--(P23.16)`; their quotient is `-1/3`.
QED.

The strict exact rational C++ diagnostic
`character_ring_iter/analyze_su2_bc2_hypergroup.cpp` constructs the
triangular basis directly from the semicircle moments.  The commands with
arguments `1 4` and `2 4` reproduce respectively `(P23.14)` and
`(P23.16)`.  Proposition 23B does not contradict `Q3`: it rules out the
stronger proof in which generic Jacobi-hypergroup positivity is applied
after the half-product split.  Any successful use of `(P23.10)` or
`(P23.13)` must retain cancellations between several orthogonal types.

Thus all label dependence has moved into an explicitly positive quotient;
all cancellation is carried by the single fundamental factor `Delta^m`.
This is the natural place to seek the missing Temperley--Lieb differential
in Proposition 22.

In the ordinary category the positive quotient itself is the character of
one familiar module.  If

```text
A=V_1 tensor 1,                 B=1 tensor V_1,
```

then the symmetric-power decomposition gives

```text
D_q=character Sym^(q-1)(A direct-sum B).                 (P23.18)
```

Thus `(FDC)` is not asking for positivity of an artificial quotient: after
extracting `Delta^m`, every remaining minus factor is an actual polynomial
`SU(2) x SU(2)` module.  The unresolved sign is precisely the Euler index
created by the common virtual factor `(A-B)^m`.

The restriction on `H` in `(FDC)` is essential.  Positivity fails on the
whole symmetric nonnegative tensor cone: with

```text
m=1,                 H=e_1 tensor e_1,
```

one gets `(id tensor tau)(Delta H)=-e_1`.  The strict C++ diagnostic
`character_ring_iter/search_su2_fundamental_difference_cone.cpp` finds this
as its first symmetric-atom counterexample.  A proof must therefore preserve
the complete `D_q,S_q` generator groupings, just as the outer-Turan atom
counterexamples require complete irreducible strings.

For one minus factor, the normal form has an exact source--sink transfer
description.  Expand an arbitrary tensor character as

```text
H=sum_(b>=0) H_b tensor e_b,       H_(-1)=0,
L_b(H)=e_1 H_b-H_(b-1)-H_(b+1).                       (P24.1)
```

**Proposition 24 (fundamental transfer recurrence).**  One has

```text
(id tensor tau)(Delta H)=L_0(H).                       (P24.2)
```

Moreover, if `S_q=e_q tensor e_0+e_0 tensor e_q`, then

```text
L_b(H S_q)=e_q L_b(H)+sum_c N_(q,c)^b L_c(H),          (P24.3)
```

where `N_(q,c)^b` is the `SU(2)` tensor multiplicity.  The divided-difference
generator has the two-point boundary vector

```text
L_b(D_q)= e_q indicator_(b=0)-e_0 indicator_(b=q).      (P24.4)
```

Thus the one-minus case of `(PCP)` is exactly the assertion that positive
transfer by the operators

```text
(T_q L)_b=e_q L_b+sum_c N_(q,c)^b L_c                 (P24.5)
```

always makes the source at `b=0` dominate the sink at `b=q` in the zeroth
row.  The identical recurrence holds on the finite `A_(k+1)` fusion graph.

**Proof.**  Since `tau(e_1e_b)=indicator_(b=1)`, contraction of
`Delta H` gives `e_1H_0-H_1=L_0(H)`.  If `K=HS_q`, then

```text
K_b=e_qH_b+sum_c N_(q,c)^b H_c.
```

Apply `(P24.1)` and use associativity of the fusion coefficients, or
equivalently commutation of the fundamental adjacency matrix with every
fusion matrix `N_q`, to obtain `(P24.3)`.  Finally the `b`th row of `D_q` is
`e_(q-1-b)` for `0<=b<q` and zero otherwise.  The fundamental fusion
recurrence cancels every interior term, leaving exactly `(P24.4)`.  The
finite path graph obeys the same calculation because all indices used in
`D_q` lie below the affine wall.  QED.

Proposition 24 recovers the previously isolated two-minus boundary
domination problem, but now as a positive transfer of one explicit
source--sink pair.  A path injection proving that the source contribution
dominates the sink would settle the complete one-minus partial-character
sector simultaneously in ordinary `SU(2)` and all `SU(2)_k`.

Two unbounded suffix sectors admit an invariant oriented-wedge cone.  For a
row vector `L=(L_b)`, identify its coefficient array with

```text
Omega(L)=sum_(a,b)[e_a]L_b e_a tensor e_b
        in exterior^2(span{e_0,...,e_k}).
```

Under this identification, the transfer in `(P24.5)` is

```text
A_p=dGamma(N_p)=N_p tensor I+I tensor N_p,             (P24.6)
```

and the seed `(P24.4)` is `e_q wedge e_0`.

**Proposition 24A (fundamental-suffix cone).**  For every `q` and `n>=0`,

```text
A_1^n(e_q wedge e_0)
```

is a nonnegative combination of the oriented wedges
`E_(u,v)=e_u wedge e_v`, `u>v`.  Consequently the complete one-minus
partial-character sector with minus label `q` and any number of fundamental
plus factors is nonnegative, ordinarily and in every `SU(2)_k`.

**Proof.**  The fundamental fusion matrix makes only nearest-neighbor
moves.  Starting from `u>v`, a move which would reverse the order must first
make the two labels equal, and that wedge vanishes.  Every surviving move
preserves the orientation and has coefficient one.  At the affine wall,
forbidden boundary moves are simply absent.  Thus `A_1` is entrywise
nonnegative in the `E`-basis.  Formula `(P24.2)` reads the desired
coefficient from `e_a wedge e_0`, which has positive orientation.  QED.

**Proposition 24B (even-suffix, odd-source cone).**  If `q` is odd, then
for arbitrary even labels `2r_1,...,2r_n`,

```text
A_(2r_n)...A_(2r_1)(e_q wedge e_0)
```

has nonnegative terminal coefficients at every `e_a wedge e_0`, ordinarily
and in every `SU(2)_k`.  Hence the corresponding one-minus
partial-character sector is nonnegative.

**Proof.**  Every `N_(2r)` preserves label parity.  On opposite-parity pairs
use the basis

```text
B_(u,v)= e_u wedge e_v  if the larger label u is odd,
B_(u,v)=-e_u wedge e_v  if the larger label u is even.
```

A noncrossing fusion update preserves this sign.  A crossing reverses the
wedge orientation and also changes the parity of the larger endpoint,
producing a second minus sign.  Thus each `A_(2r)` is entrywise nonnegative
in the `B`-basis, including after affine truncation.  The seed
`e_q wedge e_0=B_(q,0)` is positive.  Every nonzero terminal has odd `a`
and likewise equals `B_(a,0)`.  QED.

The label-two operator has a stronger cone on each fixed-parity chain.

**Proposition 24B1 (unbounded `{1,2}` boundary cone).**  Let

```text
K=(V_(1,x) direct-sum V_(1,y))^m
   tensor (V_(2,x) direct-sum V_(2,y))^n.
```

For every `m,n>=0` and every `q>=1`, ordinarily, the boundary character

```text
V_(q,x) tensor K_0-K_q
```

is a nonnegative `SU(2)_x` character.  The same holds in `SU(2)_k` for
`k>=2` and `q<=k`.  Equivalently, there is an equivariant injection

```text
K_q -> V_(q,x) tensor K_0.                            (P24.6a)
```

In particular, `(P24.6a)` proves the one-generator even-plus ray
`K=(V_(2,x) direct-sum V_(2,y))^n` for every `n`, including every
support-disjoint even `q`.

**Proof.**  On either parity chain, fusion by `V_2` moves a label by at
most one chain step, or leaves it fixed.  At `0,1` and at the affine wall,
some of these moves are simply absent.  Hence, for `u>v` of the same
parity, a one-particle move in

```text
A_2=dGamma(N_2)
```

cannot reverse their order: if they are adjacent in the parity chain, a
move toward the other label first produces equality and its wedge is zero.
Thus `A_2` is entrywise nonnegative on the standard oriented wedges
`E_(u,v)=e_u wedge e_v` with `u,v` of the same parity, ordinarily and at
every finite level.

If `q` is even, the seed `E_(q,0)` lies in this cone.  Apply `A_2^n`
first and then use Proposition 24A to apply `A_1^m`.  If `q` is odd and
`m=0`, Proposition 24B applies.  If `q` is odd and `m>0`, the first
fundamental update is

```text
A_1 E_(q,0)
 =E_(q+1,0)+E_(q-1,0)+E_(q,1),                       (P24.6b)
```

with invalid, equal, or affine-wall terms omitted.  Every surviving term
is positive and has same-parity endpoints.  Apply `A_2^n` and then
`A_1^(m-1)`.  The fusion matrices commute, so this reordering covers every
word in the two labels.  For `a>0`, Proposition 24 identifies the
coefficient of `e_a` in the displayed boundary character with the terminal
coefficient of `E_(a,0)`.  Its coefficient of `e_0` is zero by exchange
antisymmetry, as in Proposition 19.  Hence every irreducible coefficient is
nonnegative, and semisimplicity gives `(P24.6a)`.  QED.

For the indexed-size decomposition requested by Lemma 23A9, the pure
label-two specialization reads

```text
K_s=direct-sum_(j=0)^n binom(n,j)m_(2^j)(s)
       V_2^(tensor(n-j)).                              (P24.6c)
```

Proposition 24B1 proves the simultaneous multiplicity inequalities in
`K_q -> V_q tensor K_0`; it is not merely a dimension estimate.

**Corollary 24B2 (two-minus `{1,2}`-suffix sector).**  Every signed
contraction with exactly two minus factors and with every plus label in
`{1,2}` is nonnegative, ordinarily and in every `SU(2)_k`.

**Proof.**  Remove either minus factor, of label `a`.  The remainder has
one minus factor and a plus word over `{1,2}`, so Proposition 24B1 makes
its partial character genuine.  The signed contraction is twice the
coefficient of `e_a` in that partial character, by Propositions 19 and 21.
QED.

The two smallest generators admit a complete ordinary theorem.  Its proof
does not apply support reduction to `s_2d_2`: that operation would create
`d_4` and leave the stated two-generator problem.

**Proposition 24B3 (full ordinary `{1,2}` cone).**  Full `GKS2*` holds,
with arbitrary word length, on the nonnegative linear cone generated by
`V_1` and `V_2` in ordinary `SU(2)`.

Put

```text
d_i=chi_i(x)-chi_i(y),       s_i=chi_i(x)+chi_i(y),
H_(m,n,c)=integral integral d_1^(2m)s_1^(2n)s_2^c dmu(x)dmu(y).
```

Every signed word in the two labels with an even number of minus positions
is either zero or equals some `H_(m,n,c)`, and

```text
H_(m,n,c)>=0                         (m,n,c>=0).      (P24.6e)
```

**Proof.**  Write an irreducible word as

```text
d_1^a d_2^b s_1^p s_2^c,             a+b even.
```

The exact identity `d_2=d_1s_1` turns this into

```text
d_1^(a+b)s_1^(b+p)s_2^c.
```

Put `a+b=2m`.  If `b+p` is odd, simultaneous central translation in both
variables negates the integrand, so its integral is zero.  Otherwise put
`b+p=2n`.  This proves the canonical reduction without pairing `s_2` with
`d_2`.

It remains to prove `(P24.6e)`.  If `X=chi_1(g)`, Haar measure makes `X`
semicircular on `[-2,2]`.  Put `x=X^2/4`.  Then `x` has density

```text
(2/pi)x^(-1/2)(1-x)^(1/2) dx,          0<x<1,
```

and, conditional on `x`, the two signs of `X=+-2sqrt(x)` are equiprobable.
For independent `x,y`, averaging the first two factors of `H_(m,n,c)` over
these signs gives the nonnegative homogeneous function

```text
W_(m,n)(x,y)
 =2^(2rho-1){
    (sqrt(x)-sqrt(y))^(2m)(sqrt(x)+sqrt(y))^(2n)
   +(sqrt(x)+sqrt(y))^(2m)(sqrt(x)-sqrt(y))^(2n)},
rho=m+n.                                             (P24.6f)
```

Write `S=x+y` and `u=(x-y)/S`.  The full coordinate range is

```text
|u|<=U(S),       U(S)=min(1,(2-S)/S),       0<S<2.
```

In the comparison range `0<S<1`, one has `U(S)=1`.  Homogeneity and the
product Jacobi density show that the `S`-marginal after weighting by
`W_(m,n)` is

```text
f_rho(S)
 =C S^rho integral_(-1)^1 G_(m,n)(u)
       sqrt({(2-S)^2-S^2u^2}/{1-u^2}) du,            (P24.6g)
```

where `C>0` and `G_(m,n)(u)>=0` are independent of `S`.  We claim that,
for `rho>=1`,

```text
f_rho(1-S)>=f_rho(S),              0<S<=1/2.         (P24.6h)
```

Indeed, put

```text
R=(1-S)/S,
A_S=(2-S)^2-S^2u^2,
B_S=(1+S)^2-(1-S)^2u^2.
```

The pointwise comparison needed in `(P24.6g)` is

```text
R^(2rho)B_S>=A_S.
```

Since `R>=1` and `rho>=1`, it is enough to use `R^2`.  The expression
`R^2B_S-A_S` is affine decreasing in `u^2`, and at `u^2=1` it is

```text
4(1-S)(R-1)>=0.
```

This proves `(P24.6h)`.

Finally, `s_2=4S-2=2(2S-1)`.  For even `c`, the sign-averaged integrand is
pointwise nonnegative.  For odd `c`, pair `S` with `1-S` on
`0<S<1/2`; its combined contribution is a positive constant times

```text
(1-2S)^c[f_rho(1-S)-f_rho(S)]>=0.
```

The unpaired region `S>1` is also nonnegative.  This proves `(P24.6e)` for
`rho>=1`; for `rho=0` the word is all plus.  Finally expand each factor
from the nonnegative linear cone into `V_1,V_2`.  Multilinearity preserves
the fixed minus-position count and gives a nonnegative combination of the
irreducible words just proved.  QED.

The canonical reduction above remains exact in every `SU(2)_k`, `k>=2`,
because `e_1^2=e_0+e_2`; the central sign argument also survives.  The
continuous Jacobi-density comparison `(P24.6g)--(P24.6h)` does not.  Thus
Proposition 24B3 does **not** prove the uniform finite `{1,2}` theorem,
although Corollary 3 proves every fixed word once its level is beyond the
stabilization threshold.  Corollary 24B2 separately handles every finite
word with two minus factors.  Central translation in the second variable
interchanges `d_1` and `s_1` and fixes `s_2`, so

```text
H_(m,n,c)^(k)=H_(n,m,c)^(k).
```

Moreover `n=0` is Proposition 20, `m=0` is all plus, and even `c` is
pointwise nonnegative in the real spectral realization of the fusion
trace.  The case `m=1` has exactly two minus factors and is Corollary 24B2;
the displayed symmetry gives the case `n=1`.  After these cases, the exact
unresolved family may be taken to be

```text
H_(m,n,2h+1)^(k),        m,n>=2, h>=0, k>=2.         (P24.6i)
```

It contains the full even-character ray

```text
H_(m,m,c)^(k)
 =tau_k tensor tau_k{d_2^(2m)s_2^c},                 (P24.6j)
```

so the one-minus theorem alone cannot settle the finite problem.

The last ray nevertheless has a rank-uniform cyclic normal form.  It
isolates the remaining sign issue in one explicit family of moment minors.

**Lemma 24B4 (finite `V_2`-ray cyclic reduction).**  Put `N=k+2`,
`C(z)=z+z^(-1)`, and

```text
P_+={z:z^N=1},          P_-={z:z^N=-1},
h_m(z)=[4-C(z)^2]^m.
```

For `m>=1,c>=0`, let

```text
R_(k;m,c)=tau_k tensor tau_k{d_2^(2m)s_2^c}.
```

If `N` is odd, then

```text
R_(k;m,c)
 =1/(4N^2) sum_(p,q in P_+)
   [C(p)-C(q)]^2 h_m(p)h_m(q)[2+C(p)C(q)]^c.         (P24.6k)
```

If `N` is even, the right side is instead

```text
1/(8N^2) sum_(epsilon in {+,-}) sum_(p,q in P_epsilon)
   [C(p)-C(q)]^2 h_m(p)h_m(q)[2+C(p)C(q)]^c.         (P24.6l)
```

Equivalently, set

```text
A_j^(epsilon)=sum_(p in P_epsilon)h_m(p)C(p)^j,
D_j^(epsilon)=A_j^(epsilon)A_(j+2)^(epsilon)
                    -A_(j+1)^(epsilon)^2.
```

Each sector sum in `(P24.6k)--(P24.6l)` is exactly

```text
2 sum_(j=0)^c binom(c,j)2^(c-j)D_j^(epsilon).        (P24.6m)
```

Thus the uniform finite `V_2` ray is the single binomially weighted moment-
minor inequality in `(P24.6m)`, with one sector for odd `N` and two sectors
for even `N`.

**Proof.**  Verlinde diagonalization gives the exact spectral formula

```text
tau_k f(e_2)
 =1/(2N) sum_(a^N=1)[2-C(a)]f(1+C(a));              (P24.6n)
```

the identity root may be included because its weight is zero.  In the
double sum use

```text
a=pq,       b=p/q.
```

For odd `N` this is a bijection of `P_+^2`.  For even `N`, its restriction
to each of `P_+^2` and `P_-^2` is two-to-one, and the two images are,
respectively, the pairs whose root exponents have equal and opposite
parity.  They partition all `(a,b)`.  The elementary identities

```text
[2-C(pq)][2-C(p/q)] =[C(p)-C(q)]^2,
C(pq)-C(p/q)       =(p-p^(-1))(q-q^(-1)),
C(pq)+C(p/q)       =C(p)C(q)
```

give `(P24.6k)--(P24.6l)`; the two signs from the even power in the middle
identity cancel.  Expanding `[2+C(p)C(q)]^c` and symmetrizing the two square
terms gives `(P24.6m)`.  QED.

The individual minors in `(P24.6m)` are not all nonnegative, so ordinary
Hankel total positivity does not finish the proof.  For example, when
`N=6`, both cyclic sectors are invariant under `C -> -C`.  Hence
`A_1=A_3=0`, while `A_2>0`, and therefore

```text
D_1=-A_2^2<0.
```

The binomial weighting and the special cyclic moments are load-bearing.
Lemma 24B4 is an exact reformulation, not yet a proof of the finite ray.
The strict exact C++ verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_label2_ray_cyclic.cpp` checks both cyclic
formulas against direct fusion multiplication.  Its bounded replay through
level fourteen, ten minus pairs, and twenty-five plus factors passed all
3,380 ray values and found 2,470 negative individual minors; see
`certificates/su2_label2_ray_cyclic.log`.  This is a cross-check of the
identities and the no-go, not evidence promoted to an all-index proof.

The same root change gives a sharper two-grid form of the entire finite
residual `(P24.6i)`.

**Lemma 24B5 (finite two-generator cyclic Jacobi reduction).**  Put
`N=k+2`, `C(z)=z+z^(-1)`, and

```text
Q_epsilon={a:a^N=epsilon},       epsilon in {+1,-1},
w_(m,n)(T)=(2-T)^m(2+T)^n.
```

Then, for all `m,n>=0` and `c>=0`,

```text
H_(m,n,c)^(k)
 =1/(8N^2) sum_(epsilon in {+1,-1})
    sum_(a,b in Q_epsilon)
      [C(a)-C(b)]^2
      w_(m,n)(C(a))w_(m,n)(C(b))
      [2+C(a)C(b)]^c.                               (P24.6o)
```

If

```text
A_j^(epsilon;m,n)
 =sum_(a in Q_epsilon)w_(m,n)(C(a))C(a)^j,
D_j^(epsilon;m,n)
 =A_j^(epsilon;m,n)A_(j+2)^(epsilon;m,n)
    -{A_(j+1)^(epsilon;m,n)}^2,
```

then equivalently

```text
H_(m,n,c)^(k)
 =1/(4N^2) sum_(epsilon in {+1,-1}) sum_(j=0)^c
    binom(c,j)2^(c-j)D_j^(epsilon;m,n).              (P24.6p)
```

Consequently the full finite `{1,2}` theorem is reduced exactly to

```text
(CJM_sum)  sum_(epsilon in {+1,-1}) sum_(j=0)^(2h+1)
  binom(2h+1,j)2^(2h+1-j)D_j^(epsilon;m,n)>=0
```

for `m,n>=2`, every `h>=0`, and every `N>=4`.  The sectorwise inequalities

```text
(CJM_epsilon)  sum_(j=0)^(2h+1)
  binom(2h+1,j)2^(2h+1-j)D_j^(epsilon;m,n)>=0
```

are sufficient and strictly stronger than the exact target
`(CJM_sum)`; all tested sectors satisfy this stronger form.

**Proof.**  The fundamental finite fusion trace has the exact doubled-root
form

```text
tau_k f(e_1)
 =1/(4N) sum_(z^(2N)=1)[2-C(z^2)]f(C(z)).            (P24.6q)
```

In the double sum set `z=pq`, `w=p/q`.  On each of the two grids
`p^(2N)=+1` and `p^(2N)=-1`, this map is two-to-one; their images partition
all pairs `(z,w)`.  Now put `a=p^2`, `b=q^2`.  Each `(a,b)` has four
preimages, and `a,b` lie in the same `Q_epsilon`.  The identities

```text
[2-C(z^2)][2-C(w^2)] =[C(a)-C(b)]^2,
C(z)-C(w)            =(p-p^(-1))(q-q^(-1)),
C(z)+C(w)            =C(p)C(q),
C(z^2)+C(w^2)        =C(a)C(b)
```

show respectively that the fusion weights give the first square, the
`d_1^(2m)` factors give `(2-C(a))^m(2-C(b))^m`, the
`s_1^(2n)` factors give `(2+C(a))^n(2+C(b))^n`, and
`s_2^c` gives `[2+C(a)C(b)]^c`.  Keeping the two-to-one and four-to-one
factors gives exactly `1/(8N^2)` and proves `(P24.6o)`.  Binomial expansion
of the final factor gives `(P24.6p)`.  The reductions preceding
`(P24.6i)` prove the final equivalence.  QED.

Formula `(P24.6o)` also makes the remaining obstruction precise.  Every
summand outside the last odd power is nonnegative, and a negative summand
requires `C(a)C(b)<-2`.  Thus `(CJM_sum)`, or the stronger
`(CJM_epsilon)`, asks for a cyclic Jacobi transport
from those opposite-sign root pairs to the same-sector pairs with
`2+C(a)C(b)>=0`; individual determinant transport is impossible by the
`D_1<0` example above.

The strict exact analyzer
`character_ring_iter/analyze_su2_two_generator_cyclic.cpp` verifies
`(P24.6o)--(P24.6p)` against direct fusion multiplication and audits the
stronger sectorwise inequality.  Through level fourteen, `m,n<=8`, and
`c<=25`, all 21,632 total values and both of their sector sums were
nonnegative.  The replay is in
`certificates/su2_two_generator_cyclic.log`.  This is bounded support for
`(CJM_epsilon)`, not its missing all-index proof.

The two cyclic sectors have exact reflection symmetries, and their
large-level range is already controlled at a substantially sharper cutoff
than the crude fusion-stability bound.

**Lemma 24B6 (sector symmetry, no-alias chamber, and exterior
recurrence).**  In the notation of Lemma 24B5, write

```text
S_epsilon(m,n,c)
 =sum_(a,b in Q_epsilon)[C(a)-C(b)]^2
   w_(m,n)(C(a))w_(m,n)(C(b))[2+C(a)C(b)]^c.
```

If `N` is odd, then

```text
S_epsilon(m,n,c)=S_(-epsilon)(n,m,c),                (P24.6t)
```

whereas if `N` is even, the same identity holds without changing
`epsilon`.  In particular the two sectors agree when `N` is odd and
`m=n`.

Put `rho=m+n`.  If

```text
N>rho+c+2,                 equivalently k>m+n+c,     (P24.6u)
```

then each sector is nonnegative.  Hence every finite residual
`H_(m,n,c)^(k)` in this no-alias chamber is proved.

Finally, for every `N,epsilon,m,n,c`,

```text
S_epsilon(m+1,n,c)+S_epsilon(m,n+1,c)
 =2S_epsilon(m,n,c+1)+4S_epsilon(m,n,c).             (P24.6v)
```

This last display is an exact Christoffel recurrence, not a positivity
claim.

**Proof.**  The map `a -> -a` sends `C(a)` to `-C(a)` and interchanges
`w_(m,n)` with `w_(n,m)`.  It interchanges `Q_+` and `Q_-` for odd `N`
and preserves each grid for even `N`, proving `(P24.6t)`.

The summand defining `S_epsilon` is a Laurent polynomial of degree at most
`rho+c+2` in each of `a,b`: the Jacobi weight contributes `rho`, the
Vandermonde square contributes two, and the final kernel contributes `c`.
Under `(P24.6u)`, root averaging over either `Q_epsilon` therefore extracts
only the ordinary Laurent constant term.  Both sectors equal the
corresponding continuous-circle double average.  This is the cyclic form
of the ordinary integral `H_(m,n,c)`, which is nonnegative by Proposition
24B3.  This proves the no-alias assertion.

For the recurrence, set `T=C(a)` and `R=C(b)`.  The two possible endpoint
Christoffel increments satisfy

```text
(2-T)(2-R)+(2+T)(2+R)
 =8+2TR=2(2+TR)+4.
```

Multiplication by the remaining common summand and summation over the grid
gives `(P24.6v)`.  QED.

There is also an exact one-vector formulation of the unresolved alias
range.  On `R^N`, let `U_epsilon` be cyclic translation with
`U_epsilon^N=epsilon I`, put `J_epsilon=U_epsilon+U_epsilon^*`, and set

```text
g_(m,n)=(I-U_epsilon)^m(I+U_epsilon)^n e_0,
X_(m,n)=g_(m,n) wedge J_epsilon g_(m,n),
K_epsilon=2I+wedge^2 J_epsilon.
```

With the standard exterior inner product, Fourier normalization gives

```text
S_epsilon(m,n,c)
 =2N^2 <X_(m,n),K_epsilon^c X_(m,n)>.                (P24.6w)
```

Indeed,

```text
<g_(m,n),J_epsilon^j g_(m,n)>
 =A_j^(epsilon;m,n)/N,
```

and the exterior matrix coefficient is the determinant
`D_j^(epsilon;m,n)/N^2`; binomial expansion of `K_epsilon^c` now gives
`(P24.6w)`.  This is a genuine structured spectral-moment problem:
`K_epsilon` is not positive semidefinite in general, since its eigenvalues
are `2+C(a)C(b)`.  Thus `(P24.6w)` is a reduction, not a hidden
positivity proof.  Incrementing `m` or `n` applies respectively
`wedge^2(I-U_epsilon)` or `wedge^2(I+U_epsilon)` to `X_(m,n)`; the sum of
their squared multipliers is `2K_epsilon+4I`, which is the operator form
of `(P24.6v)`.

Two tempting strengthenings of this reduction are false even inside the
hard family.

**Lemma 24B6A (negative first alias and restricted-transport no-gos).**
Let `D=m+n+c+2` and suppose `N<=D<2N`.  If `F(a,b)` denotes the Laurent
summand in `S_epsilon`, then

```text
S_+(m,n,c)+S_-(m,n,c)
 =2N^2{[a^0b^0]F+4[a^Nb^N]F}.                       (P24.6w1)
```

The corner coefficient in `(P24.6w1)` need not be nonnegative.  At
`N=4,m=n=2,c=1`,

```text
[a^0b^0]F=64,       [a^4b^4]F=-16,                  (P24.6w2)
```

so the continuous contribution `2048` is cancelled exactly by the first
alias correction `-2048`.

Nor can all negative pairs at `c=1` always be paid using only the positive
pairs with `C(a)C(b)>=0`.  This fails in the `Q_+` sector at
`N=7,m=2,n=5`.

**Proof.**  Root filtering gives

```text
S_epsilon=N^2 sum_(r,s)epsilon^(r+s)[a^(rN)b^(sN)]F.
```

The sum over the two signs deletes the terms with `r+s` odd.  Since
`D<2N`, only `r,s` in `{-1,0,1}` can occur.  The Laurent summand is
invariant under inversion of either variable and under their exchange, so
the four surviving corner coefficients are equal.  This proves
`(P24.6w1)`.

For `m=n=2,c=1`, put `W(T)=(4-T^2)^2`.  Continuous circle averaging gives

```text
[z^0]W(C(z))=6,       [z^0]W(C(z))C(z)^2=4,
```

and its odd moments vanish.  Hence the moment-minor formula gives constant
coefficient

```text
2{2(6*4)-4^2}=64.
```

At level two, `e_2^2=e_0`; writing
`A=e_2 tensor e_0`, `B=e_0 tensor e_2` gives

```text
(A-B)^4(A+B)=0.
```

Thus the finite value is zero, and `(P24.6w1)` forces the corner
coefficient `-16`.  This proves `(P24.6w2)`.

For the restricted-transport assertion, let `x>y>z` be the three
nonendpoint `Q_+` values at `N=7`.  They are the roots of
`t^3+t^2-2t-1`, isolated by `(P24.6y)`.  With
`w(t)=(2-t)^2(2+t)^5`, the only nonzero selected positive pair is
`{y,z}`, while the only negative-kernel pair is `{x,z}`.  After removing
the common positive factor `8w(z)`, credit minus load is

```text
E=(y-z)^2w(y)(2+yz)+(x-z)^2w(x)(2+xz).              (P24.6w3)
```

Exact rational interval arithmetic on the three isolating intervals gives

```text
-24084669191229575729010427922763841
 /125000000000000000000000000000000
 <=E<=
-11383042764028581341963127060977211
 /62500000000000000000000000000000 <0.              (P24.6w4)
```

Hence that restricted credit pool is insufficient.  The omitted
positive-kernel pair `{x,y}` is precisely the credit used when `n>=m` in
Proposition 24B8 below, so this no-go does not contradict the full
level-five theorem.  QED.

The strict exact verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_c1_transport_nogo.cpp` checks the cubic
sign changes, the pair classification, and the rational enclosure
`(P24.6w4)`; its replay is
`certificates/su2_c1_transport_nogo.log`.

The endpoint weights nevertheless have a uniform shape constraint which
underlies the successful transports below.

**Lemma 24B6B (angular log-concavity and inward slope).**  Write
`T_j=2cos(theta_j)` on either cyclic sector, with the distinct angles
ordered increasingly in `[0,pi]`.  Then

```text
w_j=w_(m,n)(T_j)
 =4^(m+n)sin(theta_j/2)^(2m)cos(theta_j/2)^(2n)     (P24.6w5)
```

is log-concave on the angular arithmetic progression.  In particular,
whenever `i+1<=j-1`,

```text
w_(i+1)w_(j-1)>=w_iw_j.                             (P24.6w6)
```

If `m>=n`, `x>y`, and `|y|<=|x|<2`, then

```text
w_(m,n)(y)/w_(m,n)(x)
 ={(4-y^2)/(4-x^2)}^n
  {(2-y)/(2-x)}^(m-n)>=1.                           (P24.6w7)
```

For `n>=m` the reflected assertion follows by `T -> -T`.

**Proof.**  On `(0,pi)`,

```text
(log w)''=-(m/2)csc(theta/2)^2-(n/2)sec(theta/2)^2<0.
```

Hence successive ratios on every angular arithmetic progression decrease,
which is exactly `(P24.6w6)`; zero endpoint weights follow by continuity.
Formula `(P24.6w7)` is the displayed factorization, whose two ratios are
at least one under the stated ordering.  Reflection interchanges `m,n`.
QED.

Lemma 24B6B supplies weight credit but does not by itself prove
`(CJM_epsilon)`: the kernel and Vandermonde ratios must also be controlled.
The restricted-transport counterexample in Lemma 24B6A shows why that
additional geometric comparison is essential.

For the first odd exponent there is a compact orthogonal-polynomial
criterion.

**Lemma 24B6C (first Jacobi-compression criterion).**  Normalize the
sector measure with masses `w_(m,n)(C(a))/A_0`, and let multiplication by
`T` have leading Jacobi coefficients

```text
T p_0=b_0p_0+a_1p_1,
T p_1=a_1p_0+b_1p_1+a_2p_2.
```

Then

```text
S_epsilon(m,n,1)/(2A_0^2)
 =a_1^2(2+b_0b_1-a_1^2).                            (P24.6w8)
```

Thus the `c=1` sector inequality is equivalent to saying that the
determinant of the leading two-by-two Jacobi compression is at least
`-2`.

**Proof.**  With normalized moments `mu_j=A_j/A_0`,

```text
b_0=mu_1,       a_1^2=mu_2-mu_1^2=D_0/A_0^2,
b_1={mu_3-2b_0mu_2+b_0^3}/a_1^2.
```

Direct substitution gives

```text
D_1/A_0^2=a_1^2(b_0b_1-a_1^2).
```

Since `S_epsilon(m,n,1)=2(2D_0+D_1)`, this is `(P24.6w8)`.
The determinant of the displayed Jacobi block is
`b_0b_1-a_1^2`.  QED.

The criterion is exact but not yet a uniform proof: even in the hard range
`b_0b_1` may be negative, and positivity at `c=1` alone does not control
higher odd powers of the indefinite operator `K_epsilon`.

The first three nontrivial levels close exactly.

**Proposition 24B7 (finite `{1,2}` theorem through level four).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_k` for
`k<=4`, with arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  The canonical reduction preceding `(P24.6i)` leaves only
`H_(m,n,c)^(k)` with `m,n>=1`; even `c` is pointwise and it is enough to
take odd `c>=1`.

At level two, the fundamental spectrum is

```text
X in {sqrt(2),0,-sqrt(2)}.
```

For `d_1^(2m)s_1^(2n)` to be nonzero, either one node is zero and the other
is `+-sqrt(2)`, in which case `s_2=X^2+Y^2-2=0`, or the two nodes are
opposite extrema, in which case `s_1=0`.  Equal nodes have `d_1=0`.
Hence `H_(m,n,c)^(2)=0` for `c>=1`.

At level three, `N=5`.  In the `Q_+` sector of `(P24.6o)`, the endpoint
`T=2` is killed by `(2-T)^m`.  The remaining distinct values are

```text
alpha=2cos(2pi/5),       beta=2cos(4pi/5),
alpha beta=-1.
```

Every nonzero Vandermonde pair therefore has `2+TR=1`.  In `Q_-`, the
endpoint `-2` is killed by `(2+T)^n`, and `T -> -T` gives the same argument
with `m,n` interchanged.  Both sector sums are nonnegative.

At level four, `N=6`.  The surviving values in `Q_+` are `T=+-1`, so again
`2+TR>=1` on every nonzero Vandermonde pair.  In `Q_-`, the values

```text
sqrt(3), 0, -sqrt(3)
```

each have multiplicity two.  Put

```text
A=w_(m,n)(sqrt(3)),     B=w_(m,n)(-sqrt(3)),
C=w_(m,n)(0)=2^(m+n).
```

Then `AB=1`.  Grouping the ordered pairs with odd `c` gives exactly

```text
S_-=24{2^c C(A+B)-4AB}.                              (P24.6x)
```

The two pairs involving zero have squared difference three and kernel
`2`, while the pair `{sqrt(3),-sqrt(3)}` has squared difference twelve
and kernel `-1`; multiplicity and ordering contribute the common factor
eight.  Since `A+B>=2sqrt(AB)=2`, `(P24.6x)` is nonnegative.  Thus both
sectors, and hence every residual word, are proved.  Level one has no
`V_2` and is Proposition 1A.  QED.

The next level has only one negative root pair, and it too admits a uniform
transport.

**Proposition 24B8 (finite `{1,2}` theorem at level five).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_5`, with
arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  Here `N=7`.  In `Q_+`, the endpoint `2` is killed and the
remaining distinct values, each of multiplicity two, are the three roots

```text
a>b>c
```

of

```text
f(T)=T^3+T^2-2T-1.
```

Exact substitution gives the rational isolating intervals

```text
1.246<a<1.247,
-0.446<b<-0.445,
-1.802<c<-1.801.                                  (P24.6y)
```

The only negative kernel is the pair `{a,c}`:

```text
2+ab>0,       2+bc>0,       2+ac<0.
```

Write `q>=1` for the odd `s_2` exponent.  For an unordered pair put

```text
P_(x,y)=(x-y)^2w_(m,n)(x)w_(m,n)(y)|2+xy|^q.
```

Suppose first that `m>=n`.  Since `|b|<a` and `b<a`,

```text
w_(m,n)(b)/w_(m,n)(a)
 ={(4-b^2)/(4-a^2)}^n
  {(2-b)/(2-a)}^(m-n) >=1.
```

The intervals `(P24.6y)` give

```text
|2+ac|<1/4,      2+bc>2,
b-c>1.355,       a-c<3.049.
```

Consequently

```text
P_(b,c)/P_(a,c)
 >8(1.355/3.049)^2>1.                              (P24.6z)
```

Here increasing the odd exponent `q>=1` only improves the displayed
kernel ratio.  Thus `{b,c}` pays the unique negative pair.

If `n>=m`, use `{a,b}` instead.  Now

```text
w_(m,n)(b)/w_(m,n)(c)
 ={(4-b^2)/(4-c^2)}^m
  {(2+b)/(2+c)}^(n-m) >=1,
```

while `(P24.6y)` gives

```text
2+ab>7/5,       a-b>1.691.
```

Hence

```text
P_(a,b)/P_(a,c)
 >(28/5)(1.691/3.049)^2>1.                         (P24.6za)
```

This proves the `Q_+` sector for every `m,n>=1` and every odd `q`.
Reflection `(P24.6t)` proves `Q_-`.  Even `c` and the canonical base cases
were already pointwise, so every word follows.  QED.

The next level closes by a single equal-kernel transport.

**Proposition 24B9 (finite `{1,2}` theorem at level six).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_6`, with
arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  Here `N=8`.  In `Q_+`, after the endpoint weights kill
`T=+-2`, the values are `sqrt(2),0,-sqrt(2)`.  Thus `2+TR>=0` on every
pair.

In `Q_-`, the four distinct values are

```text
u, v, -v, -u,
u^2=2+sqrt(2),       v^2=2-sqrt(2),       uv=sqrt(2),
```

each with the same multiplicity.  The only negative kernel is `{u,-u}`,
for which `2-u^2=-sqrt(2)`.  Pair it with `{v,-v}`, whose kernel is
`2-v^2=sqrt(2)`.  Put `rho=m+n`.  Their endpoint-weight products are

```text
w_(m,n)(v)w_(m,n)(-v)=u^(2rho),
w_(m,n)(u)w_(m,n)(-u)=v^(2rho).
```

After including the squared Vandermonde factors, the ratio of positive
credit to negative load is

```text
{v^2u^(2rho)}/{u^2v^(2rho)}
 =(u^2/v^2)^(rho-1)>=1.                              (P24.6zb)
```

The kernels have equal absolute value, so this proves the comparison for
every odd exponent; all other pairs are nonnegative.  Together with the
base cases, the full level-six assertion follows.  QED.

Level seven again has a single negative root pair.

**Proposition 24B10 (finite `{1,2}` theorem at level seven).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_7`, with
arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  Here `N=9`.  After the endpoint `2` is killed, the distinct
values in `Q_+` are

```text
a>b>c>d,       c=-1,
```

where `a,b,d` are the roots of `T^3-3T+1`.  Exact substitution gives

```text
1.532<a<1.533,
0.347<b<0.348,
-1.880<d<-1.879.                                  (P24.6zc)
```

Only `{a,d}` has negative kernel.  Write `q>=1` for the odd exponent and
use the pair loads `P_(x,y)` defined in Proposition 24B8.

If `m>=n`, pay `{a,d}` with `{b,d}`.  The endpoint-weight ratio satisfies

```text
w_(m,n)(b)/w_(m,n)(a)
 ={(4-b^2)/(4-a^2)}^n
  {(2-b)/(2-a)}^(m-n)
 >2.34.
```

The rational intervals `(P24.6zc)` also give, at `q=1`,

```text
{P_(b,d)/P_(a,d)}/{w_(m,n)(b)/w_(m,n)(a)}
 >(2.226/3.413)^2(1.345/0.883)>0.647.
```

Thus `P_(b,d)/P_(a,d)>2.34(0.647)>1`.  The positive-to-negative
kernel ratio exceeds one, so every larger odd `q` only improves the
comparison.

If `n>=m`, pay `{a,d}` with `{a,b}`.  Now

```text
w_(m,n)(b)/w_(m,n)(d)
 ={(4-b^2)/(4-d^2)}^m
  {(2+b)/(2+d)}^(n-m)
 >8.2,
```

and

```text
{P_(a,b)/P_(a,d)}/{w_(m,n)(b)/w_(m,n)(d)}
 >(1.184/3.413)^2(2.531/0.883)>0.344.               (P24.6zd)
```

Their product is again greater than one, and the kernel ratio again
improves with `q`.  This proves `Q_+`; reflection `(P24.6t)` proves
`Q_-`.  The base cases complete the theorem.  QED.

The strict exact verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_level7_transport.cpp` checks the cubic
root isolations and proves rational lower bounds
`345430145445392/226809671401777>3/2` and
`573582736783056896/200934617892538035>14/5` for the two complete
credit-to-load ratios; see `certificates/su2_level7_transport.log`.

Level eight has two negative-pair types, both paid by explicit reflections.

**Proposition 24B11 (finite `{1,2}` theorem at level eight).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_8`, with
arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  Here `N=10`.  Put `phi=(1+sqrt(5))/2` and `rho=m+n`.
