After killing the endpoints, `Q_+` has the values

```text
a=phi,       b=phi^(-1),       -b,       -a.
```

Only `{a,-a}` has negative kernel.  The pair `{b,-b}` pays it, because
the complete positive-to-negative ratio is

```text
{b^2/a^2}{(2-b^2)/(a^2-2)}
  {(4-b^2)/(4-a^2)}^rho
 =phi^(2(rho-1))>=1.                                (P24.6ze)
```

In `Q_-`, the values are `u,v,0,-v,-u`, where

```text
u^2=(5+sqrt(5))/2,       v^2=(5-sqrt(5))/2,
uv=sqrt(5).
```

First `{v,-v}` pays the extreme negative pair `{u,-u}`.  The corresponding
ratio is

```text
{v^2/u^2}{(2-v^2)/(u^2-2)}
  {(4-v^2)/(4-u^2)}^rho
 =phi^(4(rho-1))>=1.                                (P24.6zf)
```

It remains to pay the two cross pairs, whose common negative kernel is
`2-uv`.  Suppose `m>=n`.  For either positive `t in {u,v}`,

```text
w_(m,n)(0)/w_(m,n)(t)
 ={4/(4-t^2)}^n {2/(2-t)}^(m-n)>=1.
```

Use `{-v,0}` to pay `{u,-v}` and `{-u,0}` to pay `{v,-u}`.  At odd
exponent one, their geometric ratios, before the preceding weight credits,
are respectively

```text
{2v^2}/{(uv-2)(u+v)^2}=sqrt(5)-1,
{2u^2}/{(uv-2)(u+v)^2}=sqrt(5)+1,                   (P24.6zg)
```

where `(uv-2)(u+v)^2=sqrt(5)`.  Both exceed one, and the kernel ratio
`2/(uv-2)>1` makes every larger odd exponent easier.  If `n>=m`, reflect
`T -> -T` within `Q_-` and use the reflected pairs.  Every remaining pair
has nonnegative kernel.  Thus both sectors and all base cases are proved.
QED.

At level nine, two disjoint positive pairs pay the two negative pairs.

**Proposition 24B12 (finite `{1,2}` theorem at level nine).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_9`, with
arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  Here `N=11`.  After the endpoint is killed, the distinct
values in `Q_+` are `a>b>c>d>e`, the five roots of

```text
T^5+T^4-4T^3-3T^2+3T+1.
```

Direct rational substitution gives

```text
1.682<a<1.683,       0.830<b<0.831,
-0.285<c<-0.284,    -1.310<d<-1.309,
-1.919<e<-1.918.                                  (P24.6zh)
```

The only negative-kernel pairs are `{a,d}` and `{a,e}`.  Again write
`q>=1` for the odd exponent.

Suppose `m>=n`.  Pay `{a,d}` with `{b,d}`.  Since

```text
w_(m,n)(b)/w_(m,n)(a)
 ={(4-b^2)/(4-a^2)}^n
  {(2-b)/(2-a)}^(m-n)
 >2.826,
```

the intervals `(P24.6zh)` give

```text
P_(b,d)/P_(a,d)
 >2.826(2.139/2.993)^2(0.911/0.205)>1.             (P24.6zi)
```

Pay `{a,e}` with the disjoint pair `{c,e}`.  Similarly,

```text
w_(m,n)(c)/w_(m,n)(a)>3.346
```

and

```text
P_(c,e)/P_(a,e)
 >3.346(1.633/3.602)^2(2/1.230)>1.11.              (P24.6zj)
```

If `n>=m`, instead pay `{a,d}` with `{a,b}` and `{a,e}` with `{a,c}`.
The endpoint-weight ratios obey

```text
w_(m,n)(b)/w_(m,n)(d)>1.447,
w_(m,n)(c)/w_(m,n)(e)>12.19,
```

and the corresponding complete comparisons are

```text
P_(a,b)/P_(a,d)
 >1.447(0.851/2.993)^2(3.396/0.205)>1,
P_(a,c)/P_(a,e)
 >12.19(1.966/3.602)^2(1.520/1.230)>1.             (P24.6zk)
```

All four positive-to-negative kernel ratios exceed one, so larger odd
`q` only strengthens the inequalities.  The payments are disjoint.
Reflection `(P24.6t)` proves `Q_-`, and the base cases finish the theorem.
QED.

The mirror-pair part of all even orders has a uniform quarter-turn
transport, independent of these low-level enumerations.

**Lemma 24B13 (even-order mirror transport).**  Let `N` be even and put
`rho=m+n>=1`.  In the combined sector sum `S_++S_-`, every negative pair
of the form `{T,-T}` is paid by a distinct positive pair of the same form.
If `4` divides `N`, the payment stays within each sector; if
`N=2 mod 4`, it interchanges the two sectors.

**Proof.**  Write `T=2cos(theta)` with `0<theta<pi/4`.  The mirror pair
`{T,-T}` has negative kernel.  Since the union `Q_+ union Q_-` is indexed
by the angles `ell pi/N`, the quarter-turn

```text
theta -> pi/2-theta
```

is a permutation of that union when `N` is even.  It sends the negative
pair to the inner pair

```text
{T',-T'},       T'=2sin(theta),
```

whose kernel is positive and has the same absolute value:

```text
|2-T^2|=2-(T')^2=2cos(2theta).                      (P24.6zl)
```

Moreover

```text
w_(m,n)(T)w_(m,n)(-T)=[4-T^2]^rho.
```

After including the squared Vandermonde, the complete
positive-credit-to-negative-load ratio is therefore

```text
{sin(theta)^2 cos(theta)^(2rho)}
 /{cos(theta)^2 sin(theta)^(2rho)}
 =cot(theta)^(2(rho-1))>=1.                         (P24.6zm)
```

The angular map is an involution between the outer and inner halves, so
the payments are disjoint.  On angular indices it is the reflection
`ell -> N/2-ell`; this preserves root parity when `4` divides `N` and
reverses it when `N=2 mod 4`, proving the sector statement.  QED.

Lemma 24B13 removes all opposite roots uniformly, but unequal-magnitude
opposite-sign pairs remain.  Thus it is a strict reduction of
`(CJM_sum)`, not a proof of that target.

At level ten the two remaining cross pairs also have exact mirror credits.

**Proposition 24B14 (finite `{1,2}` theorem at level ten).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_10`, with
arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  Here `N=12` and put `rho=m+n`.  In `Q_+`, the only negative
pair is `{sqrt(3),-sqrt(3)}`; its quarter-turn credit `{1,-1}` has ratio
`3^(rho-1)>=1`, as in Lemma 24B13.

In `Q_-`, write

```text
u=sqrt(2+sqrt(3)),       v=sqrt(2),       w=sqrt(2-sqrt(3)).
```

The values are `+-u,+-v,+-w`, with equal multiplicities, and

```text
uw=1,       uv=1+sqrt(3),       vw=sqrt(3)-1.
```

Lemma 24B13 uses `{w,-w}` to pay the extreme pair `{u,-u}`, with exact
ratio

```text
{(2+sqrt(3))/(2-sqrt(3))}^(rho-1)>=1.               (P24.6zn)
```

The remaining negative pairs are `{u,-v}` and `{v,-u}`, with common load
kernel `sqrt(3)-1`.  Suppose `m>=n`.  Pay `{u,-v}` with the distinct pair
`{-w,-v}`.  The weight ratio is at least

```text
w_(m,n)(-w)/w_(m,n)(u)
 >=(4-w^2)/(4-u^2)
  =(2+sqrt(3))/(2-sqrt(3)),
```

whereas the geometric ratio at odd exponent one is exactly

```text
{(v-w)^2/(u+v)^2}{(2+vw)/(uv-2)}
 =2-sqrt(3).                                        (P24.6zo)
```

Their product is `2+sqrt(3)>1`.

Pay `{v,-u}` with the further distinct pair `{-w,-u}`.  Now

```text
w_(m,n)(-w)/w_(m,n)(v)>=(2+sqrt(3))/2,
```

and, using `u-w=v`, the geometric ratio is

```text
{(u-w)^2/(u+v)^2}{(2+uw)/(uv-2)}
 =sqrt(3)-1.                                        (P24.6zp)
```

The product is `(1+sqrt(3))/2>1`.  Both positive-to-negative kernel
ratios exceed one, so larger odd exponents only improve the payments.
The three credits `{w,-w}`, `{-w,-v}`, and `{-w,-u}` are distinct
unordered spectral pairs, so no credit is reused.  If `n>=m`, reflect
`T -> -T` within `Q_-`.  Every other pair has nonnegative kernel, proving
the theorem.  QED.

At level eleven, three disjoint credits pay the three negative pairs.

**Proposition 24B15 (finite `{1,2}` theorem at level eleven).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_11`, with
arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  Here `N=13`.  After the endpoint is killed, the distinct
values in `Q_+` are `a>b>c>d>e>f`, the six roots of

```text
T^6+T^5-5T^4-4T^3+6T^2+3T-1.
```

Exact rational substitution gives

```text
1.770<a<1.771,       1.136<b<1.137,
0.241<c<0.242,       -0.710<d<-0.709,
-1.498<e<-1.497,     -1.942<f<-1.941.               (P24.6zq)
```

The negative-kernel pairs are exactly

```text
{a,e},       {a,f},       {b,f}.                     (P24.6zr)
```

Write `q>=1` for the odd exponent and abbreviate
`w(T)=w_(m,n)(T)`.  Suppose first that `m>=n`.  Use the three distinct
credits `{c,e}`, `{c,f}`, and `{d,f}`, respectively.  The endpoint-weight
comparisons and `(P24.6zq)` give

```text
w(c)/w(a)>(4-c^2)/(4-a^2)>4.545,
w(d)/w(b)>(4-d^2)/(4-b^2)>1.290.
```

At `q=1`, the three complete positive-to-negative ratios therefore obey

```text
P_(c,e)/P_(a,e)
 >4.545(1.738/3.269)^2(1.637/0.653)>1,
P_(c,f)/P_(a,f)
 >4.545(2.182/3.713)^2(1.530/1.440)>1.66,
P_(d,f)/P_(b,f)
 >1.290(1.231/3.079)^2(3.376/0.209)>1.             (P24.6zs)
```

If `n>=m`, use instead the distinct credits `{a,d}`, `{a,c}`, and
`{b,e}`.  Here

```text
w(d)/w(e)>(4-d^2)/(4-e^2)>1.987,
w(c)/w(f)>(4-c^2)/(4-f^2)>16.95,
w(e)/w(f)>(4-e^2)/(4-f^2)>7.55,
```

and

```text
P_(a,d)/P_(a,e)
 >1.987(2.479/3.269)^2(0.742/0.653)>1.29,
P_(a,c)/P_(a,f)
 >16.95(1.528/3.713)^2(2/1.440)>1,
P_(b,e)/P_(b,f)
 >7.55(2.633/3.079)^2(0.296/0.209)>1.              (P24.6zt)
```

In every comparison the credit kernel is at least the absolute load
kernel, so larger odd `q` only improves the payment.  The three credits in
each slope chamber are distinct.  Reflection `(P24.6t)` proves `Q_-`,
and the base cases finish the theorem.  QED.

**Corollary 24B16 (proved finite `{1,2}` region).**  Full `GKS2*` holds
for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2`

```text
at every level k<=11, with arbitrary word length,
```

and, at every level, for each canonical word `H_(m,n,c)^(k)` satisfying

```text
k>m+n+c.                                             (P24.6zu)
```

After the canonical base cases, the exact remaining two-generator target
is therefore

```text
H_(m,n,2h+1)^(k),       m,n>=2,
12<=k<=m+n+2h+1.                                      (P24.6zv)
```

**Proof.**  Propositions 24B7--24B12, 24B14, and 24B15 give all levels
through eleven.  The no-alias chamber `(P24.6u)` gives `(P24.6zu)`.
The reductions preceding `(P24.6i)` remove `m<=1`, `n<=1`, and even `c`,
leaving exactly `(P24.6zv)`.  QED.

The quarter-turn argument in fact pays every negative pair, not only the
opposite-root pairs isolated in Lemma 24B13.

**Proposition 24B17 (all even levels for the finite `{1,2}` cone).**
Full `GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_k` at
every even level `k`, with arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  It is enough to prove the hard residual
`H_(m,n,q)^(k)` with `m,n>=1` and odd `q`.  Put `N=k+2`, so `N` is even,
and work in the combined cyclic sum `S_++S_-`.

Take a negative unordered spectral pair and write it uniquely as

```text
T=2cos(alpha)>0,       R=-2cos(beta)<0,
0<alpha,beta<pi/2.
```

Negativity says `2cos(alpha)cos(beta)>1`, which implies
`alpha+beta<pi/2`.  Map this load to the credit

```text
T'=2sin(beta),          R'=-2sin(alpha).             (P24.6zw)
```

On full angular coordinates this moves

```text
(alpha,pi-beta)
 ->(pi/2-beta,pi/2+alpha)
```

equally inward.  Because the union `Q_+ union Q_-` is indexed by
`ell pi/N` and `N` is even, this is an involutive injection from negative
pairs to distinct positive pairs.  It preserves the sector when `4`
divides `N` and interchanges the sectors when `N=2 mod 4`; in either case
it is a valid allocation in `S_++S_-`.

Let

```text
L=4cos(alpha)cos(beta)-2,
P=2-4sin(alpha)sin(beta).
```

The mapped kernel is positive and dominates the load kernel:

```text
P-L=4{1-cos(alpha-beta)}>=0.                         (P24.6zx)
```

Factor the endpoint weight as

```text
w_(m,n)=w_(1,1)w_(m-1,n-1).
```

Angular log-concavity in Lemma 24B6B says that the pair product of the
second factor cannot decrease under the equal inward move.  Since
`w_(1,1)(2cos(theta))=4sin(theta)^2`, the complete weight ratio is at
least

```text
cot(alpha)^2 cot(beta)^2.
```

The squared-Vandermonde ratio is

```text
{(T'-R')/(T-R)}^2
 =tan((alpha+beta)/2)^2.
```

Their product is at least one.  Indeed, set
`x=tan(alpha)`, `y=tan(beta)`, and
`t=tan((alpha+beta)/2)`.  Since `xy<1`, monotonicity of
`2t/(1-t^2)` reduces `t>=xy` to

```text
(x+y)(1+xy)>=2xy,                                   (P24.6zy)
```

which follows from `x+y>=2sqrt(xy)` and
`1+xy>=sqrt(xy)`.  Therefore

```text
cot(alpha)cot(beta)tan((alpha+beta)/2)>=1.
```

Combining this with `(P24.6zx)`, the full
positive-credit-to-negative-load ratio is at least

```text
{cot(alpha)cot(beta)tan((alpha+beta)/2)}^2(P/L)^q
 >=1.                                               (P24.6zz)
```

The allocation is injective, so no positive pair is reused.  Every pair
outside the negative region is already nonnegative.  Hence
`S_++S_->=0`, proving every hard residual at even level.  The canonical
base cases preceding `(P24.6i)` complete full `GKS2*` on the stated cone.
QED.

At odd order the ideal quarter-turn lies halfway between grid points.
Same-sector parity supplies exactly the separation needed to round it
inward without losing credit.

**Proposition 24B17A (all odd levels for the finite `{1,2}` cone).**
Full `GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in `SU(2)_k` at
every odd level `k`, with arbitrary word length.

**Proof.**  Again it is enough to prove
`H_(m,n,q)^(k)` for `m,n>=1` and odd `q`.  Put `N=k+2`, so `N` is odd,
and let

```text
delta=pi/(2N).
```

Write a negative pair as in Proposition 24B17,

```text
T=2cos(alpha),       R=-2cos(beta),
alpha=2i delta,      beta=2j delta,
```

with positive integers `i,j`.  The full negative angle `pi-beta` has
grid index `N-j`.  Since the two roots lie in the same sector,

```text
i=N-j mod 2.
```

Oddness of `N` therefore makes `i+j` odd.  In particular, for

```text
A=(alpha+beta)/2,       D=(alpha-beta)/2,
S=A-delta,
```

one has

```text
|D|>=delta.                                            (P24.6zza)
```

Map the load to the inward-rounded credit

```text
T'=2sin(beta-delta),       R'=-2sin(alpha-delta).      (P24.6zzb1)
```

Its full angular indices are

```text
(N+1)/2-j,       (N-1)/2+i.
```

Their difference is `i+j-1`, which is even, so they again lie in one
sector.  On acute indices the map is

```text
(i,j) -> ((N+1)/2-j,(N+1)/2-i),
```

an involution.  It preserves sectors for `N=1 mod 4` and interchanges
them for `N=3 mod 4`; hence it is an injective allocation in the combined
sum `S_++S_-`.

Let

```text
L=4cos(alpha)cos(beta)-2,
P=2-4sin(alpha-delta)sin(beta-delta).
```

As before negativity implies `alpha+beta<pi/2`.  Consequently the mapped
kernel is positive, and

```text
P>=2-4sin(alpha)sin(beta)>=L.                         (P24.6zzc1)
```

The second inequality differs from equality by
`4{1-cos(alpha-beta)}`.

The two full angular coordinates move equally inward.  Factor
`w_(m,n)=w_(1,1)w_(m-1,n-1)` and apply Lemma 24B6B to the second factor.
The product of the remaining `w_(1,1)` ratio and the squared-Vandermonde
ratio is `Q^2`, where

```text
Q={cos(alpha-delta)cos(beta-delta)sin(A-delta)}
   /{sin(alpha)sin(beta)cos(A)}.                     (P24.6zzd)
```

We claim `Q>=1`.  All denominators are positive, and using
`A=S+delta` gives the equivalent inequality

```text
F={cos(S)^2-sin(D)^2}sin(S)
  -{sin(S+delta)^2-sin(D)^2}cos(S+delta)>=0.         (P24.6zze)
```

Since `cos(S+delta)>sin(S)`, the right side is increasing in
`sin(D)^2`.  Moreover `|D|<A<pi/4`, so `(P24.6zza)` shows that its
minimum occurs at `|D|=delta`.  At that endpoint the elementary
difference-of-squares identities give

```text
F_min=sin(S)cos(S+delta)
      {cos(S-delta)-sin(S+2delta)}.                  (P24.6zzf)
```

Put `s=i+j`, so `A=s delta`.  Negativity gives `2s<N`; integrality gives
`2s<=N-1`.  Therefore

```text
2S+delta=(2s-1)delta<pi/2.
```

Also `s` is odd and at least three, so both angles in braces in
`(P24.6zzf)` are nonnegative.  The last displayed bound now gives

```text
cos(S-delta)>=sin(S+2delta),
```

and proves `F>=0`, hence `Q>=1`.

The full credit-to-load ratio is at least

```text
Q^2(P/L)^q>=1.                                      (P24.6zzg)
```

The map is injective, every other pair is already nonnegative, and the
canonical base cases finish the odd-level theorem.  QED.

**Corollary 24B17B (complete finite two-generator theorem).**  Full
`GKS2*` holds for the cone generated by `V_1,V_2` in every `SU(2)_k`,
with arbitrary word length.  Proposition 24B3 gives the stable ordinary
theorem on the same cone.

**Proof.**  Propositions 24B17 and 24B17A give every even and odd level,
respectively.  Proposition 24B3 is the ordinary statement.  QED.

The successful low-level transports have one common matching form.

**Lemma 24B18 (slope-aware one-credit matching criterion).**  For an
unordered spectral pair `p={T,R}`, put

```text
Delta_p=(T-R)^2,
A_p=(2-T)(2-R),       B_p=(2+T)(2+R),
K_p=|2+TR|.
```

Call `ell` a load if `2+TR<0` and `p` a credit if `2+TR>0`.  In the
chamber `m>=n`, join `ell` to `p` when

```text
K_p>=K_ell,       A_p>=A_ell,       A_pB_p>=A_ellB_ell,
Delta_p A_pB_p K_p>=Delta_ell A_ellB_ell K_ell.     (P24.6zzb)
```

For the chamber `n>=m`, replace `A_p>=A_ell` by `B_p>=B_ell`.  If this
bipartite graph has a matching covering every load, then the corresponding
cyclic sector satisfies `(CJM_epsilon)` for every `m,n>=1` and every odd
exponent.

**Proof.**  In the first chamber write `m=n+r`, with `n>=1,r>=0`, and let
`q>=1` be odd.  The complete credit-to-load ratio factors as

```text
{Delta_p A_pB_p K_p}/{Delta_ell A_ellB_ell K_ell}
 {A_pB_p/(A_ellB_ell)}^(n-1)
 {A_p/A_ell}^r
 {K_p/K_ell}^(q-1).                                 (P24.6zzc)
```

Every factor is at least one by `(P24.6zzb)`.  A covering matching makes
the payments injective, so summing proves the sector inequality.  The
second chamber is identical with `A,B` interchanged.  QED.

The strict C++ analyzer
`character_ring_iter/analyze_su2_cyclic_jacobi_matching.cpp` constructs
rational outward enclosures of every cyclic cosine, includes only edges
for which all comparisons in `(P24.6zzb)` are certified, and computes
exact bipartite matchings.  Through level thirty it audited 116
sector/slope graphs containing 880 negative loads and 20,362 certified
edges, with at most 22 loads in one graph, and found no matching failure.
This is bounded evidence for the matching criterion, not a uniform proof.
The raw graphs are not Ferrers: 84 are non-Ferrers, first at level nine.
Thus a proof cannot simply order the loads so that their full certified
neighborhoods are nested; it needs a smaller noncrossing subgraph or a
different Hall argument.  The exact replay and source hash are in
`certificates/su2_cyclic_jacobi_matching.log`.

Two simpler ways of exploiting the entrywise-positive `s_2` transfer are
false.  First, the prefix before the `s_2` factors is not coefficientwise
nonnegative.  Already ordinarily,

```text
d_1^4s_1^4=d_2^4,
[e_6 tensor e_2]d_2^4=-4,                           (P24.6r)
```

because only the term `-4e_2^3 tensor e_2` reaches that bidegree.  The same
coefficient occurs in `SU(2)_k` for `k>=6`.  Second, even the natural lower
rectangle cumulative cone on each parity block fails inside the hard
finite residual.  At level three, for

```text
d_1^4s_1^6,       sum_(a,b in {1,3})[e_a tensor e_b]=-20. (P24.6s)
```

The analyzer records this as its first residual prefix-cone failure.
Thus neither raw coefficients nor unweighted parity-prefix rectangles can
serve as an `s_2`-invariant positive cone.  A proof of `(CJM_sum)` must use
the binomial cyclic aggregation or a more structured weighted cumulative
cone.

The same standard cone does not extend to a larger even generator.  For
every even `p>=4`,

```text
[E_(p,p-2)] A_p E_(p-2,0)=-1,                         (P24.6d)
```

because moving the lower endpoint from `0` to `p` crosses the upper
endpoint.  This holds ordinarily and in `SU(2)_k` for `k>=p`.  Thus any
higher-even-ray proof needs a different grouped cone; `(P24.6d)` does not
assert failure of the boundary character itself.  The strict C++ verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_label2_wedge_cone.cpp` checks the
label-one cone, the fixed-parity label-two cone, all finite endpoints, and
the obstruction `(P24.6d)`.  Its replay is recorded in
`certificates/su2_label2_wedge_cone.log`.

The two cones in Propositions 24A and 24B do not combine by simply pairing
fundamental updates.  At level two,

```text
B_(1,0)=e_1 wedge e_0,
A_1 B_(1,0)=e_2 wedge e_0,
A_1^2 B_(1,0)=B_(1,0)-B_(2,1).                       (P24.7)
```

Ordinarily the last line has the additional positive term `B_(3,0)`.
Thus `A_1^2` already has a negative entry in the opposite-parity cone.

There is, however, an exact three-row reduction for the first mixed packet.
Write the state produced by all even suffix factors as

```text
X=sum_(u>v, u-v odd) h_(u,v)B_(u,v),       h_(u,v)>=0.
```

For odd `a`,

```text
[e_a wedge e_0]A_1^2X
 =sum_u h_(u,0)[(N_1^2)_(a,u)+indicator_(a=u)]
  -2sum_u h_(u,1)(N_1)_(a,u)+h_(a,2)
  +indicator_(a=1)[-2h_(1,0)+h_(2,1)].               (P24.8)
```

Only the rows `v=0,1,2` can reach the lower endpoint zero in two
nearest-neighbor moves.  The middle row is negative because its
opposite-parity `B` orientation is reversed; the outer two rows are
positive.  The final bracket is the sole two-step exchange packet at the
boundary pair `{0,1}`: `B_(1,0)` has exchange demand two, while
`B_(2,1)` has one reversed-order credit.  Formula `(P24.8)` is exact on
every finite path graph, including the level-one endpoint
`(N_1^2)_(1,1)=1`.  Hence the first mixed one-minus target is the three-row
packet domination `(P24.8)`, rather than closure of either simplicial wedge
cone.

The exceptional packet has a useful exact reformulation in terms of the
ordinary boundary defects.  Put

```text
E=product_i S_(2r_i)=sum_b E_b tensor e_b,
F_r(E)=e_r E_0-E_r,                                  (P24.9)
```

with `E_r=F_r=0` outside the finite label interval and `F_0=0`.  At level
one use the convention `e_2=F_2=0`.

**Proposition 24C (mixed packet as a coupled boundary defect).**  For odd
`q`, the complete terminal character after the two fundamental updates is

```text
L_q(E)
 =(id tensor tau)[(e_1 tensor 1-1 tensor e_1)^2 S_q E]
 =2e_1[F_(q-1)(E)+F_(q+1)(E)]-e_q F_2(E).             (P24.10)
```

In particular,

```text
L_1(E)=e_1 F_2(E).                                    (P24.11)
```

Thus the first source label already contains the label-two boundary-defect
problem; it is not a free consequence of nonnegativity of the
opposite-parity wedge coefficients.

**Proof.**  Central translation in the second tensor factor sends the
initial wedge to `S_q`, fixes every even `S_(2r_i)`, and sends each
fundamental wedge update to multiplication by
`e_1 tensor 1-1 tensor e_1`.  It fixes the terminal row.  Parity gives, for
`C=S_qE`,

```text
C_0=e_qE_0,       C_1=E_(q-1)+E_(q+1),       C_2=e_qE_2.
```

Contracting the square of the displayed difference therefore gives

```text
L_q(E)
 =(e_1^2+e_0)e_qE_0+e_qE_2
    -2e_1(E_(q-1)+E_(q+1)).
```

Substitute `(P24.9)`, use
`e_(q-1)+e_(q+1)=e_1e_q` with the usual endpoint omissions, and use
`e_1^2=e_0+e_2`.  This is `(P24.10)`.  When `q=1`, `F_0=0`, so
`2e_1F_2-e_1F_2=e_1F_2`.  The same calculation at level one has both
sides zero.  QED.

The tempting positive extension of `(P24.11)` is false at the affine wall.
Indeed, `(P24.10)` can also be written

```text
L_q(E)=e_qF_2(E)+2G_q(E),
G_q(E)=e_q(E_0+E_2)-e_1(E_(q-1)+E_(q+1)).             (P24.12)
```

At level five take `q=3` and `E=S_4^2`.  Since

```text
E_0=2e_0+e_2,             E_2=e_0,             E_4=2e_4,
```

one obtains

```text
F_2(E)=3e_2+e_4,          G_3(E)=2e_3-e_5,
L_3(E)=4e_1+8e_3+e_5.
```

Thus the natural remainder in `(P24.12)` has a negative coefficient even
though the complete packet is positive.  This is the first obstruction in
the exact diagnostic ordered by suffix length, level, and labels; its
minimality is computational evidence, while the displayed calculation is
an exact counterexample.

There are nevertheless two unbounded chambers in which the mixed packet
closes.

**Proposition 24D (dominant odd source).**  Let the even suffix labels be
`p_1,...,p_n`, put `P=sum_i p_i`, and suppose `q` is odd and `q>P`.
Then the ordinary mixed packet with plus labels `1,1,p_1,...,p_n` is
nonnegative.  The same holds in `SU(2)_k` whenever additionally
`q+P<=k`.

**Proof.**  Assign any subcollection of the even suffix to the particle
starting at `q` and the complement to the particle starting at zero.  In
the ordinary fusion graph their endpoints satisfy

```text
u>=q-P_A,              v<=P-P_A,              u-v>=q-P>=1.
```

The odd particle therefore remains strictly above the even particle.
Consequently no path reaches a row `B_(u,1)`, whose larger endpoint is
even.  Every negative term in `(P24.8)` vanishes and the remaining terms
are nonnegative.  If `q+P<=k`, no intermediate tensor product reaches the
affine wall, so the same path argument is valid in `SU(2)_k`.  QED.

**Proposition 24E (even-level simple-current ray).**  Let `k` be even.
For every odd `q<=k`, every `n>=0`, and the suffix consisting of `n` copies
of the top label `k`, the finite mixed packet with plus labels
`1,1,k,...,k` is nonnegative in `SU(2)_k`.

**Proof.**  Write `J=N_k`, `R=J tensor J`, and
`A=A_k=J tensor I+I tensor J` on the exterior square.  Since `J^2=I`,

```text
A^n=2^(n-1)(I+R),       n even and n>=2,
A^n=2^(n-1)A,           n odd.                         (P24.13)
```

Moreover,

```text
AB_(q,0)=B_(k-q,0)+B_(k,q),
RB_(q,0)=B_(k,k-q).
```

The exact terminal coefficients are

```text
[e_a wedge e_0]A_1^2B_(s,0)
 =(N_1^2)_(a,s)+indicator_(a=s)
   -2indicator_(a=s=1),
```

and are nonnegative.  A second seed `B_(k,r)` cannot reach lower endpoint
zero in two fundamental moves unless `r=1`.  Its terminal contribution is

```text
-2(N_1)_(a,k)+indicator_(k=2 and a=1).
```

For `k>2`, this is `-2` at `a=k-1`, while its paired boundary seed
`B_(k-1,0)` contributes `3`.
The residual margin is one.  At `k=2`, the two contributions are `-1`
and `1`, so the margin is zero.  Formula `(P24.13)` only multiplies these
packets by a positive power of two.  The case `n=0` is the already
nonnegative boundary seed.  QED.

Finally, the exact defect dynamics identifies why a direct induction on
the suffix does not yet prove the whole mixed sector.

**Lemma 24F (even-suffix defect recurrence).**  For even `p`,

```text
F_r(ES_p)
 =e_pF_r(E)+sum_(s in r star_k p)F_s(E)-e_rF_p(E).     (P24.14)
```

Consequently

```text
L_q(ES_p)=e_pL_q(E)+R_(q,p)(E),

R_(q,p)
 =2e_1[sum_(s in (q-1) star_k p)F_s
        +sum_(s in (q+1) star_k p)F_s]
   -e_q sum_(s in 2 star_k p)F_s
   -(2e_0+e_2)e_qF_p.                                 (P24.15)
```

**Proof.**  The zeroth row of `ES_p` is `e_pE_0+E_p`, while its `r`th
row is `e_pE_r+sum_(s in r star_k p)E_s`.  Substitution into `(P24.9)`
and fusion associativity give `(P24.14)`.  Insert that recurrence for
`r=q-1,q+1,2` into `(P24.10)`.  The coefficient of `F_p` reduces by
`e_(q-1)+e_(q+1)=e_1e_q` and `e_1^2=e_0+e_2` to
`-(2e_0+e_2)e_q`, proving `(P24.15)`.  QED.

The recurrence remainder is not coefficientwise nonnegative, even before
any suffix has been added.  Ordinarily, take `E=1`, `q=1`, and `p=2`.
Then

```text
L_1(1)=e_1e_2,             L_1(S_2)=e_1(e_2+e_4),
L_1(S_2)-e_2L_1(1)=-e_1.                              (P24.16)
```

At level two the same difference is `-e_1`, with `L_1(S_2)=0`.
Therefore neither positivity of the natural curvature `G_q` nor a
positive suffix-remainder recurrence can prove `(P24.10)`.  Any successful
telescoping argument must retain cancellation between `e_pL_q(E)` and the
negative terms of `R_(q,p)(E)`.

The strict exact C++ diagnostic
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_mixed_one_minus_packets.cpp` checks the
corrected three-row identity, `(P24.11)`, the two closed chambers, and the
two exact obstructions.  Through level eighteen and seven even suffix
factors it checked `2,458,742` packet entries; the simple-current audit
checked `52,275` cases through level one hundred and power forty.  Its
replay is `certificates/su2_mixed_one_minus_packets.log`.  The unbounded
claims are the proofs above; the broad packet scan is diagnostic only.

The first previously open edge beyond Proposition 12E1 can now be closed
uniformly outside three bottom packet labels.  The point is that in the
support-disjoint problem the complete negative allocation load has only
three types, while either of the two types involving a channel `1` forces a
quadratic reservoir.

For a submultiset `B` of `L={q,r,s,t}`, write `m_B(c)` for the multiplicity
of `V_c` in its tensor product and put

```text
M=m_(p-1,q,r,s,t)(a).
```

Assume throughout this paragraph that

```text
2<=p<=q<=r<=s<=t,          a notin {p,q,r,s,t}.          (P25.1)
```

In the exact allocation formula `(P12E1.2)`, now with four suffix labels,
all singleton negative terms vanish.  Disjoint support kills the first
three-label negative term and both four-label negative terms.  Hence the
whole negative load is

```text
N=N_A+N_B+N_C,                                      (P25.2)

N_A=sum_(B subset L, |B|=2) m_B(p-1)m_(L minus B)(a),

N_B=sum_(B subset L, |B|=2) m_B(1)
                              m_((L minus B) union {p})(a),

N_C=sum_(B subset L, |B|=3) m_B(1)
                              indicator_(a in (L minus B) star p).
```

The empty allocation contributes `M`, and every omitted term is positive.
Thus `M>=N` is sufficient for the ordered packet coefficient.

**Lemma 25 (two tent-overlap bounds).**  Let `p>=2`.

1.  If `u,v,x,y>=p`, `|u-v|=1`, `a` is different from `x,y`, and
    `m_(x,y,p)(a)>0`, then

```text
m_(p-1,u,v,x,y)(a)>=p(p+2).                         (P25.3)
```

2.  If `u,v,w,x>=p`, `m_(u,v,w)(1)>0`, `a!=x`, and
    `a in x star p`, then

```text
m_(p-1,u,v,w,x)(a)>=p(p+2).                         (P25.4)
```

**Proof.**  We use only the interval form of the Clebsch--Gordan rule.  For
three labels set

```text
T_(b,c,d)(n)=#(I(b,c) intersection I(d,n)).          (P25.5)
```

For `(P25.3)`, the adjacent pair satisfies

```text
chi_u chi_v >= chi_p chi_(p+1)                       (P25.6)
```

coefficientwise.  It is therefore enough to bound

```text
<chi_(p-1)chi_p chi_(p+1), chi_a chi_x chi_y>.       (P25.7)
```

The interval `I(x,y)` has at least `p+1` points and, by the hypothesis in
`(P25.3)`, meets `I(a,p)`.  Choose `p+1` consecutive points of `I(x,y)`
containing a point of this intersection.  They form `I(c,p)` for some
`c>=p`, so `(P25.7)` is bounded below by

```text
<chi_(p-1)chi_p chi_(p+1), chi_a chi_c chi_p>.       (P25.8)
```

Put `d=(c-a)/2` and `h=(c+a)/2`.  The intersection condition says
`|d|<=p`; positivity of `a,c` gives `h-d>=1` and `h+d>=p`.  Pair the six
factors in `(P25.8)` as `(p-1,p+1)`, `(p,p)`, and `(a,c)`, and divide all
three intermediate labels by two.  The resulting invariant paths are

```text
1<=i<=p,       0<=j<=p,       |d|<=k<=h,
|i-j|<=k<=i+j.                                      (P25.9)
```

If `f_p(k)` denotes the number of `(i,j)` in the first two ranges satisfying
the last inequalities, then for `0<=k<=p+1`

```text
f_p(k)=p(p+1)-max(p-k,0)^2-k(k-1)/2.                 (P25.10)
```

This follows by deleting the disjoint regions `|i-j|>k` and `i+j<k` from
the `p` by `p+1` rectangle.  Summing `(P25.10)` under the three constraints
on `d,h` shows that the minimum is attained at `d=p,h=p+1`.  Terms with
`k>p+1` are nonnegative and cannot lower the sum.  At the minimum,

```text
f_p(p)+f_p(p+1)
 =p(p+3)/2+p(p+1)/2=p(p+2).                         (P25.11)
```

This proves `(P25.3)`.

For `(P25.4)`, choose `p+1` consecutive channels in `I(u,v)` containing
one of the channels in `I(w,1)`.  They again form `I(c,p)` with `c>=p`.
Moreover `p in I(a,x)`.  Since `a!=x`, `x>=p`, and the endpoints have the
same parity, the upper endpoint of `I(a,x)` is at least `p+2`; hence

```text
chi_a chi_x >= chi_p+chi_(p+2)=chi_1 chi_(p+1).       (P25.12)
```

It is therefore enough to count

```text
<chi_c chi_p chi_w, chi_(p-1)chi_1 chi_(p+1)>.       (P25.13)
```

Pair the factors as `(c,w)`, `(p-1,p+1)`, and `(p,1)`.  The last two
fusion intervals are

```text
{2,4,...,2p},                 {p-1,p+1}.              (P25.14)
```

The common-channel hypothesis says that the lower endpoint of `I(c,w)` is
at most `p+1`, with the required parity.  Among all such intervals with
`c,w>=p`, the number of invariant triples with the two intervals in
`(P25.14)` is minimized when the lower endpoint is `p+1`.  In that extremal
case `I(c,w)` contains every label from `p+1` through `3p+1`.  For the
channel `2i`, `1<=i<=p`, the choices paired with `p-1` and `p+1` number
respectively `i` and `i+1`.  Therefore the minimum is

```text
sum_(i=1)^p (i+i+1)=p(p+2).                          (P25.15)
```

Moving the lower endpoint inward only adds channels inside one of these
triangle intervals, so it cannot decrease the count.  This proves
`(P25.4)`.  QED.

There is also a sharp linear bound for `(P25.2)`.

**Lemma 25A (negative-load bound).**  Under `(P25.1)`,

```text
N<=4p+12.                                             (P25.16)
```

**Proof.**  Every term in `(P25.2)` is a quantifier-free Presburger
function.  In particular,

```text
m_(x,y,p)(a)=#(I(x,y) intersection I(a,p))            (P25.17)
```

is the cardinality of the intersection of two parity intervals.  The
strict C++/Z3 verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_opd_four_suffix_z3.cpp`, in mode
`--negative-bound`, encodes `(P25.1)`, all six two-label allocations, all
four three-label allocations, and the exact interval cardinality
`(P25.17)`.  It asks for an integer solution of `N>4p+12`.  Z3 4.8.12
returns `unsat`.  No label cutoff or bounded enumeration occurs in this
query.  QED.

**Proposition 25B (support-disjoint four-suffix edge for `p>=5`).**  If
`(P25.1)` holds and `p>=5`, then

```text
coefficient_(e_a wedge e_0)
 dGamma(N_t)dGamma(N_s)dGamma(N_r)dGamma(N_q)Y_p >=0. (P25.18)
```

**Proof.**  If `N_B+N_C>0`, one of the hypotheses of Lemma 25 occurs, so

```text
M>=p(p+2)>=4p+12>=N,                                 (P25.19)
```

where the middle inequality is equivalent to `(p-5)(p+3)>=0`.  If
`N_B=N_C=0`, every term of `N=N_A` is a distinct admissible `3|3` split of
the six labels `(p-1,q,r,s,t,a)`.  Proposition 1C gives `M>=N_A`.  In both
cases the empty positive allocation pays the complete negative load; all
other allocations are nonnegative surplus.  QED.

It remains to close the three fixed values `p=2,3,4`.  This can be done by
a second cutoff-free Presburger certificate.  The certificate is much
smaller after Lemma 25: it only needs to consider

```text
N>p(p+2).                                             (P25.20)
```

Indeed, if `(P25.20)` fails and `N_B+N_C>0`, Lemma 25 gives `M>=N`; if
`N_B=N_C=0`, Proposition 1C does so.

Here is the explicit path pool used in the remaining chamber.  The first
channel in the fusion tree for `M` is

```text
x_i=q-p+1+2i,                         0<=i<p.          (P25.21)
```

Let `delta(s,t,a)` be the smallest label in the support of
`chi_s chi_t chi_a`, namely

```text
delta=max(2 max(s,t,a)-s-t-a, (s+t+a) mod 2).         (P25.22)
```

For each `x_i`, start at

```text
l_i=max(|x_i-r|,delta),
y_(i,j)=l_i+2j.                                       (P25.23)
```

Retain the first `h_p` valid values, where

```text
h_2=10,                 h_3=8,                 h_4=7. (P25.24)
```

For each retained `y_(i,j)`, put

```text
z_(i,j,k)=max(|y_(i,j)-s|,|a-t|)+2k,       0<=k<4,   (P25.25)
```

and retain it exactly when it lies below both upper fusion endpoints.
Parity agreement and the two upper bounds are linear predicates.  Every
retained triple `(x_i,y_(i,j),z_(i,j,k))` is a distinct fusion path counted
by `M`.

For `p=3,4`, let `C_p` be the number of retained paths.  The verifier asks
for a solution of

```text
(P25.1), (P25.20),                    C_p<N.          (P25.26)
```

For `p=2`, the selected positive reservoir also includes

```text
S=sum_(u in L, u=2) m_((L minus {u}) union {1})(a),
E=indicator_(a=1)[m_L(0)+m_L(2)].                    (P25.27)
```

The corresponding query is `C_2+S+E<N`.  Interval cardinalities in `S,E`
are encoded exactly; truncation merely selects genuine paths and is never
used as an equality for the full multiplicity.

The verifier partitions `p=2` into the endpoint, strict-minimum interior,
and repeated-minimum interior chambers.  It partitions `p=3,4` according
to `q=p` or `q>p`; the source now refines these further by endpoint and the
position of `a`, only to reduce solver time.  Every chamber returned
`unsat`:

```text
p=2: endpoint, strict interior, repeated-minimum interior;
p=3: q=p, q>p;
p=4: q=p, q>p.                                       (P25.28)
```

These are unbounded integer queries.  The constants in `(P25.24)` and
`(P25.25)` bound only the displayed witness pool; there is no bound on
`q,r,s,t,a`.

**Proposition 25C (complete support-disjoint four-suffix edge).**  Under
`(P25.1)`, the ordered packet coefficient `(P25.18)` is nonnegative for
every `p>=2`.

**Proof.**  Proposition 25B handles `p>=5`.  For `p=2,3,4`, first use
Lemma 25 or Proposition 1C unless `(P25.20)` holds.  In the remaining
chamber, the unsatisfiability statements `(P25.26)`--`(P25.28)` give
`C_p>=N` for `p=3,4` and `C_2+S+E>=N` for `p=2`.  These are counts of
distinct fusion paths inside the positive allocations, so the complete
packet coefficient is nonnegative.  QED.

The strict replay, including the symbolic negative-load query and every
fixed-`p` chamber, is recorded in
`certificates/su2_opd_four_suffix_z3.log`.

This closes the first support-disjoint ordered packet case beyond
Proposition 12E1.  It is the four-suffix, seven-factor edge leaf.  General
ordered packet domination still requires a mechanism that survives
arbitrarily many suffix factors; Proposition 12E2 shows that neither a
cardinality prefix nor an outside-in shell filtration can provide it.

The strict exact C++ verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_torus_factorization.cpp` independently
compares `(P13.1)` with the subset invariant-multiplicity formula.  It passed
all 1,519 separately sorted signed multisets with labels at most four and at
most six factors.  This is a cross-check of the identity, not a finite proof
of the remaining inequality.

The analyzer mode also tested the auxiliary central-shell inequality

```text
B_(0,0)>=B_(2,2).
```

It passed all 63,090 separately sorted signed multisets with labels at most
six and at most eight factors.  This auxiliary statement remains unproved.
It cannot be strengthened to radial monotonicity: minus labels `[1,1]` and
plus labels `[1,1]` have `B_(2,2)=0<B_(4,4)=1`.  Nor does the shifted
three-charge inequality hold away from the bottom: plus labels `[1,1,4]`
give a negative shifted value at charge `2`.  Thus both Proposition 14 and
`(OTM)` are genuinely bottom/outer-boundary statements, rather than
translation-invariant inequalities on the whole charge lattice.

The individual feature terms in `(P13.1)` are not nonnegative.  With two
minus labels `1,3`, the feature choice `(j_1,j_2)=(1,3)` has

```text
A=(u-u^(-1))(u^3-u^(-3)),   q=-2,
```

while the choice `(1,1)` has `q=2`; their sum is zero, as required by Schur
orthogonality.  Hence the next exact target is a cumulative-weight theorem:
the nested sets `W_p` must compensate the negative atomic features.  Unlike
`(SAT)`, this formulation already includes arbitrary even minus parity and
reduces the Weyl obstruction to the charge levels `0,2,4`.

There is an exact recurrence for these cumulative boxes, but its most
immediate positive cone is circular.  For every factor choose a threshold
`t_i<=p_i` of the same parity, allowing `t_i=0` for an even plus factor, and
retain precisely the local features of magnitude at most `t_i`.

**Lemma 25D (cumulative feature boxes).**  The sum of `w_sigma q(sigma)`
over the componentwise threshold box is exactly the signed integral `J`
with every label `p_i` replaced by `t_i`.

If `B_(r,s)` is the Gram array before adjoining a thresholded factor
`(t,epsilon)`, its new array is

```text
B'_(r,s)
 =2 indicator_(epsilon=+1 and t even) B_(r,s)
  +sum_(j in W_t)[
       B_(r-j,s-j)+epsilon B_(r-j,s+j)
      +epsilon B_(r+j,s-j)+B_(r+j,s+j)].              (P25.29)
```

**Proof.**  Formula `(P13.2)` says that the sum of the retained local
features is exactly

```text
chi_(t_i)(x)+epsilon_i chi_(t_i)(y).
```

Multiplying these identities and applying the Weyl constant-term operator
proves the first assertion.  Expanding the four choices of signs in
`C_j(u)C_j(v)` or `S_j(u)S_j(v)`, together with the weight-two zero feature
for an even plus factor, gives `(P25.29)`.  QED.

The cancellation inside one complete nested string admits a sharper Abel
form.  For `B(u)=sum_r b_r u^r` with `b_(-r)=epsilon b_r`, put

```text
tau_r(B)=b_r^2-b_(r-2)b_(r+2),
F_j^epsilon=u^j+epsilon u^(-j).
```

Direct coefficient expansion gives

```text
Q(B F_j^epsilon)
 =2[2 tau_j(B)-tau_(j-2)(B)-tau_(j+2)(B)].            (P25.29b)
```

For the zero feature of an even plus string, its weight-two contribution is

```text
2Q(B)=2[tau_0(B)-tau_2(B)].
```

The endpoint symmetry therefore telescopes a complete label `p` to

```text
sum_(j in W_p, including the weighted zero feature)
 Q(B F_j^epsilon)
 =2[tau_p(B)-tau_(p+2)(B)].                           (P25.29c)
```

This is the coefficient-level Abel mechanism behind Proposition 15.

There is also an exact second Abel step which does not require inversion
symmetry of the input.  For arbitrary `C(u)=sum_r b_r u^r`, let a complete
local label `q` have sign `s`, and sum over all its features with the usual
zero weight.  Then

```text
sum_features w tau_r(C F_j^s)
 =sum_(m=-q,-q+2,...,q) tau_(r+m)(C)+s K_(r,q)(C),

K_(r,q)=b_(r-q)b_(r+q)-b_(r-q-2)b_(r+q+2).            (P25.29d)
```

Consequently, with `alpha=r-q` and `beta=r+q`,

```text
sum_features w[tau_r(C F_j^s)-tau_(r+2)(C F_j^s)]
 =(b_alpha+s b_(beta+4))(b_alpha+s b_beta)
  -(b_(alpha-2)+s b_(beta+2))
   (b_(alpha+2)+s b_(beta+2)).                        (P25.29e)
```

**Proof.**  In `tau_r(CF_j^s)`, the two square terms give
`tau_(r-j)+tau_(r+j)`.  The two cross terms telescope over
`j in W_q` to `sK_(r,q)`, proving `(P25.29d)`.  Subtract the same identity
at `r+2`; the long sum leaves only
`tau_(r-q)-tau_(r+q+2)`.  Expanding this difference together with
`s[K_(r,q)-K_(r+2,q)]` gives the two products in `(P25.29e)`.  QED.

Thus arbitrary-suffix outer Turan monotonicity has been reduced locally to
a folded boundary determinant.  A proof still needs a cone which controls
the sign after the preceding complete strings are summed; the determinant
is not pointwise positive for arbitrary `C`.  For example, the single
feature state `C=C_2` with `r=q=2` gives value `-1` for either `s=+1` or
`s=-1` in `(P25.29e)`.  For a preceding complete plus-two string, however,
the weighted zero feature contributes `2` and its `C_2` feature contributes
`-1`, leaving aggregate value `1` for either sign.  Complete-string
averaging is again indispensable.  The assertion that this aggregate
determinant is always nonnegative is exactly the sign-uniform folded form
of outer Turan monotonicity, rather than a weaker pointwise lemma.

Even imposing ordinary `TP_2` on the frozen coefficient sequence is
insufficient.  Take

```text
C=C_1^2 C_2^3,
(b_(-8),b_(-6),...,b_8)=(1,2,4,6,6,6,4,2,1).
```

This sequence is positive, symmetric, has no internal zeros, and is
log-concave, so every adjacent minor
`tau_t=b_t^2-b_(t-2)b_(t+2)` is nonnegative.  Nevertheless, for
`r=q=3,s=-1`, `(P25.29e)` is

```text
(6-0)(6-2)-(6-1)(6-1)=24-25=-1.                     (P25.29e0)
```

This is the frozen feature state of the support-disjoint background
`[1^+,1^+,2^+,2^+,2^+]` against the extracted minus labels `3,3`.
Completing the three plus-two strings restores positivity: according as
`m=0,1,2,3` of their `C_2` features are chosen, the determinant values are
`3,0,7,-1` with weights `8,12,6,1`, whose sum is `65`.  Thus neither
pointwise endpoint ratios nor adjacent total positivity is the missing
cone; the inequality must act on the complete summed quadratic kernel.

The first two suffix lengths of this folded form are nevertheless immediate
and sign-uniform.  With no preceding factor, the aggregate is
`indicator_(r=q)`.  With one preceding positive-label factor `p`, it is

```text
indicator_(r in p star q).                            (P25.29e1)
```

Indeed, by `(P25.29c)--(P25.29e)` the aggregate is the coefficient of
`V_r` in the partial character for the one- or two-factor remainder.  For
two factors and `r>0`, singleton contracted subsets have zero invariant
multiplicity, while the full subset contributes only to the scalar
coefficient.  Only the empty subset remains, and Clebsch--Gordan gives
`(P25.29e1)`, independently of both signs.  Thus the complete-string folded
determinant theorem is proved for suffix length at most two.

It extends uniformly through three factors.

**Proposition 25D1 (three-factor partial-character theorem).**  Let
`a,b,c>0` carry arbitrary signs `sigma_1,sigma_2,sigma_3`.  Every coefficient
of their partial character is nonnegative, both in ordinary `SU(2)` and in
every fusion ring `SU(2)_k`.

For `r>0`, one has the exact formula

```text
g_r=m_(a,b,c)(r)
 +sigma_1 sigma_2 indicator_(a=b) indicator_(r=c)
 +sigma_1 sigma_3 indicator_(a=c) indicator_(r=b)
 +sigma_2 sigma_3 indicator_(b=c) indicator_(r=a).    (P25.29e2)
```

At finite level the same formula holds with fusion multiplicities.

**Proof.**  In the subset formula, contracted singleton subsets have zero
invariant multiplicity, and the full subset contributes only at `r=0`.
This gives `(P25.29e2)`.  If the labels are distinct, there is no correction.
If exactly two labels, say `a=b`, coincide, the only correction occurs at
`r=c`; the fusion path

```text
V_a tensor V_a -> V_0,             V_0 tensor V_c -> V_c
```

shows `m_(a,a,c)(c)>=1`, paying a possible correction `-1`.  This singlet
path remains available at every level.  If all three labels equal `a` and
`r=a`, the sum of the three pair signs is either `3` or `-1`, while the
same singlet path gives multiplicity at least one.  At every other positive
target there is no correction.  Finally,

```text
g_0=N(a,b,c)[1+sigma_1 sigma_2 sigma_3]>=0.
```

Thus all coefficients are nonnegative.  QED.

In stable `SU(2)`, the multiplicity in the exactly-two-equal outer case is
`a+1`, and in the all-equal target it is likewise `a+1`; the proof needs
only the common scalar channel.  By `(P25.29c)--(P25.29e)`, Proposition
25D1 is precisely the complete-string folded-determinant theorem for two
preceding factors and a final factor.

The finite uniform base extends through one additional remainder factor.

**Corollary 25D2 (finite suffix-four base).**  In every `SU(2)_k`, every
signed-product contraction with at most five nontrivial simple factors is
nonnegative.  Equivalently, `(PCP_k)` holds for every signed remainder with
at most four factors.

**Proof.**  The proofs of Propositions 1 and 1B use only self-duality of the
simple basis,

```text
tau_k(e_a e_b)=indicator_(a=b),
```

nonnegative fusion coefficients, and the disjoint-support reduction.
All three hold in `SU(2)_k` by Proposition 5.  After support reduction, pair
each subset with its complement.  For five factors, representatives have
size at most two.  Singletons have zero invariant multiplicity; an
equal-label pair has positive sign because opposite-sign equal labels were
removed.  Every remaining term is nonnegative.  Proposition 21 converts
the five-factor contraction statement to the asserted four-factor
partial-character statement.  QED.

The bounded strict C++ replay through level eight is recorded in
`certificates/su2_level_suffix4.log`; it is an independent cross-check, not
part of the proof.

In ordinary `SU(2)`, Propositions 1, 1B, and 1C together with Proposition
19 already give `(PCP)` through five-factor remainders.  Proposition 25D1
adds the explicit folded formula and the wall-uniform three-factor proof;
Corollary 25D2 raises the wall-uniform partial-character base to four
factors.  The six-factor finite contraction, equivalently a five-factor
finite remainder, is the first place where the stable proof of Proposition
1C may lose its low-channel capacity at the affine wall.

That loss already occurs at level two.  For the labels

```text
[2,2,1,1,1,1] in SU(2)_2,
```

the sixfold invariant multiplicity is `N=2`, while the number of admissible
unordered `3|3` splits is `T=6`; hence the stable bound `(P1C.1)` is false.
For the signs `[2^-,2^-,1^+,1^+,1^+,1^+]`, however, all six middle splits
are negative and the equal-pair reservoir is `P=8`: the pair of twos leaves
the four-one invariant multiplicity `2`, and the six pairs of ones each
leave multiplicity one.  Thus the half-integral is

```text
N+P-T=2+8-6=4.
```

The desired finite six-factor theorem survives this example, but any proof
must retain the positive pair terms; stable raw middle-layer domination
cannot be reflected unchanged through the wall.

There is nevertheless a wall-uniform six-factor theorem when no pair
reservoir is present.

**Proposition 25D3 (finite distinct-label six-factor sector).**  In every
`SU(2)_k`, every signed-product contraction of six pairwise distinct
positive labels is nonnegative.  Equivalently, a coefficient `g_r` of a
five-factor partial character is nonnegative whenever `r` and the five
remainder labels are pairwise distinct.

**Proof.**  We first need a finite-wall version of the low-channel bound.
For an invariant-admissible positive triple `(a,b,c)`, choose `c` largest
and put

```text
x=(b+c-a)/2, y=(a+c-b)/2, z=(a+b-c)/2,
w=k-(a+b+c)/2.
```

These are nonnegative integers and
`a=y+z,b=x+z,c=x+y`.  Intersecting `a star_k b` with
`c star_k (2j)` and writing the common channel as `c+2h` gives exactly

```text
m_(2j)(a,b,c)=#(Z intersection [L_j,U_j]),

L_j=max(-j,j-x-y,-min(x,y)),
U_j=min(j,z,w,z+w-j).                                (P25.29e3)
```

The term `j-x-y` is the reflected lower endpoint when `c<2j`.

Because `c` is largest, `x,y>0`.  If none of the triple labels is `k`,
`z,w` cannot both vanish.  Formula `(P25.29e3)` then gives

```text
m_0>=1,                 m_2>=2,                 m_4>=1. (P25.29e4)
```

Indeed, for `j=1` the lower endpoint is `-1` and the upper endpoint is
either `0` or `1`; for `j=2`, distinct positivity gives `c>=3`,
so `L_2<=-1` and `U_2>=-1`.  If the triple labels are distinct and even,
then `c>=6` and `x,y,z` have the same parity.  The same endpoint check,
including `z=0` or `w=0`, strengthens this to

```text
m_0>=1,       m_2>=2,       m_4>=2,       m_6>=1.     (P25.29e5)
```

For completeness, the term `j-c` is inactive for `j<=3`.  If `z=0`,
positive even `x,y` give `L_2=-2,U_2>=-1` and
`L_3<=-2,U_3>=-2`.  If `w=0,z=1`, distinctness excludes
`x=1` and `y=1`, giving endpoint pairs `(-2,-1)` and `(-3,-2)`.
For `w=0,z=2`, the `j=3` upper endpoint is `-1`; for `z>=3` it is
nonnegative.  When `z,w>0`, one has `U_2>=0,U_3>=-1` and
`L_2,L_3<=-1`.  These cases prove `(P25.29e5)`.

Now let `T` be the number of invariant-admissible unordered `3|3` splits
and `N` the sixfold invariant multiplicity.  If label `k` is absent and
`T>0`, choose one admissible split `A|B`.  Orthogonality and
`(P25.29e4)` give

```text
N=sum_t m_t(A)m_t(B)>=1+4+1=6.
```

If all labels are even, `(P25.29e5)` gives `N>=1+4+4+1=10`.
The parity count from Proposition 1C gives respectively
`T<=4,6,0` when the number of odd labels is `2,4,6`, and `T<=10` when all
are even.  Hence `N>=T`.

If label `k` occurs, every admissible triple containing it has the form
`[u,k-u,k]`.  Among the other five distinct labels there are at most two
complementary pairs, so `T<=2`.  On the `k`-side,
`m_0=m_2=1`; on the other side `(P25.29e4)` gives
`m_0>=1,m_2>=2`.  Therefore `N>=3>T`.

Finally, pairwise distinctness already makes the plus/minus supports
disjoint and every two-element term zero.  Pairing complementary subsets
therefore gives the half-integral `N+T_+-T_-`.  Since `T_-<=T<=N`, it is
nonnegative.  QED.

The strict exact C++ verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_finite_distinct_six.cpp` checked `2,629,575`
pairwise distinct sorted sextuples through level thirty and independently
found no violation of `N>=T`.  Its replay is
`certificates/su2_finite_distinct_six.log`.  This is cross-checking only;
the unbounded proof is `(P25.29e3)--(P25.29e5)` and the top-label dichotomy.

For repeated labels, the pair reservoir closes the complete finite
six-factor problem.  After support reduction, put

```text
P=sum_(i<j, a_i=a_j) N(L minus {i,j})
```

and let `T_+,T_-` count the positive and negative admissible unordered
`3|3` splits.  The exact half-integral is

```text
N+P+T_+-T_-.
```

**Proposition 25D4 (complete finite six-factor theorem).**  For every
positive six-label list in `SU(2)_k`,

```text
T<=N+P.                                               (P25.29e6)
```

Consequently every signed-product contraction with at most six factors is
nonnegative in every `SU(2)_k`, and `(PCP_k)` holds for every remainder with
at most five factors.

**Proof.**  Let `nu` be the number of labels equal to `k`.

If `nu>=3`, every `3|3` partition has a triple containing at least two
copies of `k`; since `V_k tensor V_k=V_0` and all labels are positive, that
triple is not invariant.  Hence `T=0`.

Suppose `nu=2`.  Every admissible split distributes the two top labels, and
the other two labels on either side must sum to `k`.  On the four remaining
positions, complementary-value classes form complete bipartite graphs.
There are at most two perfect matchings, and each matching has two indexed
assignments of the top labels, so `T<=4`, except when all four labels equal
`k/2`, when the three matchings give `T=6`.  Pairing the two copies of `k`
contributes exactly `N` to `P`.  Any active complementary matching supplies
at least the common channels `k,k-2`, so `N>=2`.  In the `k/2` case one has
`N>=3` for `k>=4`.  At `k=2`, the six equal-pair terms among the four
label-one positions supply the missing reservoir.  Thus `T<=N+P`.

Suppose `nu=1`.  Put a graph on the five remaining positions, joining
`a,b` when `a+b=k` and the complementary triple is invariant.  Its
non-fixed value components are complete bipartite graphs `K_(p,q)` between
the value classes `v,k-v`; when `k` is even there may also be a clique at
`k/2`.  An active `K_(p,q)` makes every equal pair within either side a
nonzero term of `P`: fuse `V_k` with a vertex on the opposite side and use
the invariant complementary triple.  Its demand after these pair credits
is at most

```text
pq-binom(p,2)-binom(q,2)<=2.                          (P25.29e7)
```

Indeed, for `p<=q` and `p+q<=5`, the possible nonnegative remainders in
`(P25.29e7)` are `1,1,0,2,2` for
`(p,q)=(1,1),(1,2),(1,3),(2,2),(2,3)`; the omitted case `(1,4)` has
negative remainder.  Disjoint components use disjoint vertices, so their
total remainder is at most two.  For the fixed class `v=k/2`, a clique of
size at least three is paid completely: after removing any equal pair the
remaining triple still contains a fixed vertex, and tensoring that vertex
by `V_k` leaves it unchanged.  A fixed clique of size two leaves one unpaid
edge; it can coexist on five vertices only with non-fixed components whose
total remainder is at most one.  Thus the combined unpaid demand is at most
two.  If the graph is nonempty, any active split supplies two independent
sixfold paths, with paired channels `(k,0)` and `(k-2,2)`.  Hence `N>=2`
pays the remaining demand and again `T<=N+P`.

It remains to take `nu=0`.  The profiles used here also hold with repeated
labels.  Indeed, formula `(P25.29e3)` still has `x,y>0` and `z,w` not both
zero.  It gives `m_0>=1,m_2>=2`; if the largest label `c>=3`, then at
`j=2` one has `L_2<=-1<=U_2`, so `m_4>=1`.  The only positive invariant
triples with `c<=2` are `[1,1,2]` and `[2,2,2]`, and direct fusion gives
`m_4>=1` at every `k>=4`.

For the stronger all-even profile, if `k` is even then `c<=k-2`, and the
two neighboring entries at `r=4` and one at `r=6` give
`m_4>=2,m_6>=1` for `k>=6`.  If `k` is odd and `c<=k-3`, the same endpoint
check applies.  If `c=k-1`, admissibility forces
`a+b=k-1` or `a+b=k+1`.  In the first case `a star_k b` contains
`c,c-2,c-4`; in the second it contains these entries unless
`min(a,b)=2`.  Since

```text
c star_k 4={c-4,c-2},        c star_k 6={c-6,c-4},
```

the strong profile again follows.  The sole exception is the second case
with `min(a,b)=2`, namely `[2,k-1,k-1]`, whose profile is exactly
`(1,2,1,0)`.

If some label is odd, every invariant triple at level `k>=4` therefore has
the finite neighbor profile

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4)>=(1,2,1),
```

so any admissible split gives `N>=6`.  The parity count in Proposition 1C
gives `T<=6`.  If all labels are even and `k` is even, the stronger profile

```text
(m_0,m_2,m_4,m_6)>=(1,2,2,1)
```

gives `N>=10>=T`; the only small endpoint is `k=4`, where the list is
`[2^6]` and `(N,T)=(11,10)`.

Now let `k>=7` be odd and all labels be even.  The same strong profile holds
unless a triple is

```text
E=(2,k-1,k-1),
```

whose exact profile is `(1,2,1,0)`.  If no split contains `E`, then
`N>=10>=T`.  For a split `E|B`,

```text
N=1+2m_2(B)+m_4(B)>=6.
```

The equal `(k-1)` pair contributes `m_2(B)>=2` to `P`, so `N+P>=8`.
Fix those two copies of `k-1` and put `R={2} union B`.  If `r` is the
number of twos in `R` and `M` is the number of pairings of its four indexed
positions into two pairs whose sums both lie in `{k-1,k+1}`, then

```text
T=r+2M,                         M<=3.                 (P25.29e8)
```

If `T>8`, one must have `r>=3,M=3`; one paired block is then `2+2`, forcing
`4=k-1` or `4=k+1`, impossible for `k>=7`.  Hence `T<=8<=N+P`.

At `k=2` and `nu=0`, all six labels are one and `T=0`.  The remaining odd
levels are finite endpoint identities.  At `k=3`, let `s` be the number of
label-one positions.  For `s=0,...,6`, the fusion rule
`V_2 tensor V_2=V_0 direct-sum V_2` gives

```text
N=(5,0,5,0,5,0,5),            T=(10,0,4,0,6,0,0).
```

Thus the only failures of `N>=T` are

```text
[1^4,2^2]: (N,T,P)=(5,6,14),
[2^6]:     (N,T,P)=(5,10,30).
```

At `k=5`, the odd-label case was already covered by the weak profile.
In the all-even case, let `s` be the number of label-two positions; the
remaining labels are four.  For `s=0,...,6`, direct fusion gives

```text
N=(5,4,6,7,9,11,14),          T=(0,0,6,9,10,10,10).
```

Hence the only failures are

```text
[2^3,4^3]: (N,T)=(7,9),       P>=3,
[2^4,4^2]: (N,T)=(9,10),      P>=3.
```

All satisfy `(P25.29e6)`.  This proves the unsigned inequality in every
case.  Since `T_-<=T`, the exact signed half-integral satisfies

```text
N+P+T_+-T_- >= N+P-T >=0.
```

Proposition 21 gives the final `(PCP_k)` statement.  QED.

The strict exact C++ verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_finite_six_pair_reservoir.cpp` exhausts all
sorted sextuples and all even support-disjoint sign assignments through its
chosen level.  Its default level-fourteen replay covers `77,520` sextuples
and `913,200` signed cases and is recorded in
`certificates/su2_finite_six_pair_reservoir.log`.  This is bounded
cross-checking only; the proof is the unbounded case analysis above.

Raw `N>=T` cannot replace `(P25.29e6)`.  For example, for every `k>=4`,
the list `[1,1,k-2,k-1,k-1,k]` has `(N,T)=(3,4)`.  Giving minus signs to
`k-2` and `k` yields

```text
(N,P,T_-)=(3,2,4),              half-integral=1.
```

Thus the pair reservoir is genuinely load-bearing at the affine wall.

Thus the cone in which every lower threshold box has nonnegative
three-charge form is closed under `(P25.29)`, but membership in that cone is
exactly `Q3` for every coordinatewise-lowered signed list.  It is a useful
recurrence, not an induction hypothesis weaker than the theorem.  Nor are
the successive shells nonnegative: for minus labels `[1,3]`, the threshold
box `[1,1]` has value `2`, while the full box has value `0`; the newly
adjoined `j=3` shell contributes `-2`.

Grouping two adjacent shells does not repair this.  For the remainder with
minus labels `[1,1]`, append a plus label `4` and threshold that plus string
at `t=0,2,4`.  The three exact values are

```text
Q_0=4,                 Q_2=2,                 Q_4=0,
```

so the combined `j=2,4` shells contribute `Q_4-Q_0=-4`.  Nor are threshold
values discretely concave.  Replacing the two minus labels by `[2,2]` gives

```text
Q_0=4,                 Q_2=2,                 Q_4=2,
Q_4-2Q_2+Q_0=2>0.                                  (P25.29a)
```

These are support-disjoint three-factor examples at the first label having
three threshold levels.  Hence neither one-shell positivity, paired-shell
positivity, nor discrete concavity supplies a closed cumulative cone.

Pairwise threshold submodularity has no universal orientation either.  If
`Delta_(i,j)Q` denotes the mixed second difference obtained by adding the
outer feature in coordinates `i,j`, then

```text
[2^+,2^+]: Delta=6,             [2^+,4^+]: Delta=-2.
```

Even a sign correction depending on the two factor signs cannot work.  In
the single list `[2^+,3^-,3^-]`, take the mixed pair formed by the plus
factor and the first minus factor.  Its mixed difference is `4` when the
other minus factor is thresholded at `1`, and `-4` when that same background
threshold is extended to `3`.  Thus the orientation depends essentially on
the remaining cumulative coordinates, not only on the selected signs or
labels.

Even summing every other factor completely does not make the last
coordinate shells positive.  For the signed word

```text
[2^+,2^+,3^-,p^-],
```

direct fusion gives

```text
J(1)=4,                 J(3)=8,                 J(5)=4. (P25.29f)
```

Indeed, the total invariant multiplicities are respectively `2,3,2`;
the two complementary equal-pair allocations add two only at `p=3`, so the
three signed integrals are `2m+(0,2,0)`.  The top `p=5` shell therefore
contributes `J(5)-J(3)=-4` after all other nested strings have already been
summed.  Any surviving use of `(P25.29e)` must permit cancellation among
several shells of the final coordinate as well.

Nor is the final-label profile log-concave.  For

```text
[1^+,p^+,4^-,4^-],             p=1,3,5,
```

one has

```text
J(p)=(6,4,4),                  4^2<6*4.               (P25.29g)
```

The invariant multiplicity of `V_1 V_p V_4^2` is two in all three cases,
giving four from the empty and full allocations; only `p=1` has the two
complementary equal-pair allocations `[1,1]|[4,4]`.  Thus a `TP_2` or
log-concavity induction in the final label cannot control the folded
determinant either.

Even summing all terminal strings while freezing a background feature is
insufficient in an unbounded family.  Retain the notation
`Q(A)=a_0^2-2a_2^2+a_0a_4`.

**Lemma 25E (unbounded frozen-background obstruction).**  For every odd
`a>=5`,

```text
sum_(j,k in {1,3}) sum_(ell in {1,3,...,a})
 Q(S_j S_k C_1 C_ell C_(a+3)) = -6.                  (P25.30)
```

This is the two-odd terminal family `q=r=3` with all features of the plus
terminals `1,a` summed, but with the top feature of one even background
factor `a+3` frozen.

**Proof.**  Expand `S_j=u^j-u^(-j)` and
`C_j=u^j+u^(-j)` and retain only charges `0,2,4`.  For `a>=9`, the nonzero
atomic values `(j,k,ell;Q)` are

```text
(1,1,a-2;-2),
(1,3,a-4;-2), (1,3,a-2;+2),
(3,1,a-4;-2), (3,1,a-2;+2),
(3,3,a-6;-2), (3,3,a-4;+2),
(3,3,a-2;-2), (3,3,a;-2).
```

Their sum is `-6`; all other charges lie outside `{0,2,4}`.  Direct endpoint
expansion gives the same sum for `a=5,7`.  QED.

For the first case `a=5`, summing the background label's complete feature
string gives the contributions

```text
zero feature, C_2, C_4, C_6, C_8:  8, 2, 6, 0, -6,
```

whose total is `10`.  Thus positivity is restored only after the
background string itself is aggregated.  The strict C++ verifier
`character_ring_iter/verify_su2_terminal_feature_freeze.cpp` checks
`(P25.30)` through `a=501` and independently checks this five-term
breakdown.  The proof of `(P25.30)` is the displayed unbounded charge
calculation; the finite run is only a cross-check.

Consequently the scalar two-odd target already contains the full
arbitrary-suffix cumulative-weight problem.  A terminal-only Hall cone,
fixed-background-feature cone, or induction by nonnegative feature shells
cannot close it.  A successful induction must aggregate complete nested
strings of background factors while permitting negative intermediate
shells.
